topics.title: Determination of Cure Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL507 - PCPOL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Cure Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cure Time** (also called **Vulcanization Time** or **Optimum Cure Time**, denoted as **t₉₀**) is defined as the time required, at a specified temperature of vulcanization, for a rubber compound (or thermosetting elastomer) to develop **90% of the maximum attainable crosslink density** — i.e., the time needed for the torque to rise from the minimum value $M_L$ to a value equal to $M_L + 0.90(M_H - M_L)$, where $M_H$ is the maximum (plateau) torque on the rheograph.

As per the **ASTM D5289** (Moving Die Rheometer) and **ASTM D2084** (Oscillating Disc Rheometer) standards adopted in KTU practical syllabi, the cure characteristics of an uncured elastomer are monitored in real time under isothermal conditions, and the **optimum cure time t₉₀** is graphically and computationally extracted from the resulting torque–time trace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCPOL507 – Module Experiment 2):**
> Students are required to (i) plot the rheograph (torque vs. time curve) experimentally using a rheometer, (ii) identify $M_L$, $M_H$, $t_{s2}$ (scorch time) and $t_{90}$ (cure time), and (iii) compute the **Cure Rate Index (CRI)** as a derived parameter.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine baking a cake in an oven 🍰:
- The **cake batter** is your *uncured rubber compound* (raw, soft, sticky, useless).
- The **oven heat** is your *vulcanization temperature* (typically 150 °C, 160 °C, or 180 °C).
- The **timer** that you set is roughly your *cure time*.

You do NOT pull the cake out the moment you place it inside (it is still liquid). You also do NOT leave it forever (it burns). There is a **sweet spot** — the time at which the cake is fully set, springy, and structurally perfect.

For rubber, the **rheometer** acts like a tiny "smart oven with a built-in spoon". The spoon stirs the rubber slightly back and forth (oscillation), and measures how much force (torque) is needed to do so. As crosslinks form:
- At first → low torque (soft, unvulcanized).
- Later → torque rises sharply (crosslinks forming).
- Eventually → torque flattens out (fully vulcanized, plateau).

The time when torque reaches 90 % of its peak plateau is the **cure time t₉₀** — your "perfect baking moment."

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Conditions (KTU Lab):**
> - **Test Piece:** Circular uncured rubber pellet (~ 30 mm diameter, ~ 12–15 mm³ volume)
> - **Test Temperature:** 160 °C (most common) or 150 °C
> - **Oscillation Frequency:** 1.7 Hz (100 cpm) for ODR / 1.67 Hz (100 cpm) for MDR
> - **Oscillation Angle:** ± 1° (ODR) / ± 0.5° (MDR)
> - **Chamber Pressure:** ~ 4.5 MPa for MDR

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Curing (Vulcanization)

**Vulcanization** is the process of forming three-dimensional crosslinks between long-chain polymer molecules (typically natural rubber, polyisoprene, SBR, NBR, EPDM, etc.) using a crosslinking agent — historically **sulfur**, but also peroxides, metal oxides, or urethane crosslinkers.

The crosslinking reaction transforms the soft, plastic, thermoplastic-like raw elastomer into a tough, elastic, thermoset network:

$$
\text{Polymer Chain} - H + S_x + H - \text{Polymer Chain} \xrightarrow{\Delta,\ \text{accelerator}} \text{Polymer} - S_x - \text{Polymer} + H_2S \uparrow
$$

As crosslinks form, the **viscosity** of the rubber rises (because molecular motion is now restricted), and therefore the **torque** required to oscillate the sample increases. This rise in torque is the experimental signature of cure.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Interpretation of a Rheograph (Torque–Time Curve)

A typical rheograph has four distinct regions. KTU examiners love asking students to label these:

**Step 1 – Initial Softening (Torque Dip):**
- As the sample heats up, viscosity drops slightly → torque reaches a **minimum value $M_L$** (also called *minimum torque*).
- $M_L$ is an indicator of the **scorch safety** and processability of the compound.

**Step 2 – Induction / Scorch Period:**
- For a brief time, torque remains nearly flat at $M_L$. Crosslinking is slow due to the induction period of the accelerator system.
- The time to reach $M_L + 2$ dN·m is called the **scorch time $t_{s2}$**. If you start molding a part and processing takes longer than $t_{s2}$, the rubber will "scorch" (prematurely cure) inside the machine — disastrous.
- $t_{s2}$ therefore represents **processing safety**.

**Step 3 – Crosslinking Region (Curing Phase):**
- Torque rises steeply as crosslinks proliferate. This is the **vulcanization reaction zone**.

**Step 4 – Plateau / Overcure Region:**
- Torque levels off at the **maximum value $M_H$** (also called *maximum torque*).
- $M_H - M_L$ is proportional to the **stiffness / crosslink density** of the fully cured compound.
- In some compounds (e.g., NR), $M_H$ may slightly decline (reversion) due to crosslink breakage at prolonged heating.

**Step 5 – Determining t₉₀:**
- A horizontal reference line is drawn at:
$$
M_{90} = M_L + 0.90\,(M_H - M_L)
$$
- The point where this line intersects the rising cure curve corresponds to the **optimum cure time $t_{90}$** on the time axis.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Typical Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|-----------|----------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $M_L$ (Minimum Torque) | Read from the lowest point of the rheograph | dN·m | Processability / viscosity of uncured stock |
| 2 | $M_H$ (Maximum Torque) | Read from the highest stable point of the rheograph | dN·m | Stiffness of fully cured stock |
| 3 | $\Delta M = M_H - M_L$ | Difference between max and min torque | dN·m | Proportional to crosslink density |
| 4 | $t_{s1}$ (Scorch Time – Initial) | Time to reach $M_L + 1$ dN·m | minutes (min) | Incipient curing onset |
| 5 | $t_{s2}$ (Scorch Time – Standard) | Time to reach $M_L + 2$ dN·m | minutes (min) | Processing safety limit |
| 6 | $t_{90}$ (Optimum Cure Time) | Time to reach $M_L + 0.90(M_H - M_L)$ | minutes (min) | **Time required for industrial molding** |
| 7 | $t_{95}$, $t_{50}$ | Time to 95 % or 50 % cure | minutes (min) | Used for technical/spec control |
| 8 | Cure Rate Index (CRI) | $\text{CRI} = \dfrac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s2}}$ | $\text{min}^{-1}$ | Speed of vulcanization (higher = faster) |
| 9 | Crosslink Density (theoretical) | $\nu = \dfrac{E}{3\,R\,T}$ (Flory-Rehner, for swelled network) | $\text{mol}\cdot\text{m}^{-3}$ | Used to validate rheometer data |
| 10 | Reversion (%) | $\dfrac{M_H - M_{H,\,\text{at } 2t_{90}}}{M_H - M_L} \times 100$ | % | Tendency of cured stock to degrade |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no `|` symbol in the table?**
> The KTU examiner-friendly note here is to write absolute values and conditions using $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX to prevent the markdown table from breaking. For example, use $\vert M_H - M_{t_{90}} \vert$ instead of $\vert M_H - M_{t_{90}} \vert$ (with literal pipes).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | Why Cure Time Matters |
|--------------------|----------------------|
| **Tyre Manufacturing** | A tyre blank is cured in a press for *exactly* $t_{90}$ — under-cure = blow-outs, over-cure = embrittlement. |
| **Moulded Rubber Seals (O-rings, gaskets)** | Determines mould residence time; controls throughput. |
| **Conveyor Belting & Hoses** | Multi-ply products need $t_{90}$ matched to platen temperature profile. |
| **Footwear Soles (EVA, TPR)** | Direct injection moulding uses $t_{90}$ to set cycle time. |
| **Adhesives & Coatings** | Cure profile determines production line speed. |
| **Aerospace Elastomers (silicone, FKM)** | Over-engineered cure windows; $t_{90}$ defines autoclave cycle. |

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the t₉₀ Expression (Graphical → Analytical)

The cure curve is typically approximated as a first-order kinetic rise. Let us derive the analytical form of $t_{90}$ from the kinetic model and show that it corresponds to the 90 % crosslink density point.

**Step 1 – Crosslinking Kinetics:**
Assume the fraction of remaining unreacted crosslinkable sites decays exponentially:
$$
X(t) = X_0 \cdot e^{-k\,t}
$$
where:
- $X(t)$ = unreacted crosslink sites at time $t$ (mol per unit volume)
- $X_0$ = initial concentration
- $k$ = first-order rate constant of vulcanization (min⁻¹)

**Step 2 – Crosslink Density Built Up to Time $t$:**
The amount of crosslinks formed (cured fraction) at time $t$ is:
$$
\alpha(t) = \dfrac{X_0 - X(t)}{X_0} = 1 - e^{-k\,t}
$$

**Step 3 – Torque Proportional to Crosslink Density:**
Empirically, torque is directly proportional to crosslink density (Flory network theory):
$$
M(t) = M_L + (M_H - M_L)\,\alpha(t) = M_L + (M_H - M_L)\,(1 - e^{-k\,t})
$$

**Step 4 – Apply the 90 % Cure Condition:**
Set $\alpha(t_{90}) = 0.90$:
$$
1 - e^{-k\,t_{90}} = 0.90
$$

**Step 5 – Solve for $t_{90}$:**
$$
e^{-k\,t_{90}} = 0.10
$$

$$
-k\,t_{90} = \ln(0.10) = -\ln(10) \approx -2.3026
$$

$$
t_{90} = \dfrac{\ln(10)}{k} = \dfrac{2.3026}{k}
$$

This is the **closed-form** expression of cure time. Note that $t_{90}$ depends inversely on $k$, which itself follows an **Arrhenius** temperature dependence:
$$
k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R\,T}
$$
where $E_a$ is the activation energy of vulcanization (typically 80–120 kJ/mol for sulfur cures).

**Step 6 – Substitute back into the torque equation:**
$$
M_{90} = M_L + 0.90\,(M_H - M_L)
$$

This is the **graphical reference line** used to read $t_{90}$ directly off the rheometer trace — exactly the KTU exam procedure.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Sample KTU Numerical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Record Format (Representative Data):**
> An uncured NR compound is tested in an ODR at 160 °C. The recorded rheograph yields:
> - $M_L = 8.0$ dN·m
> - $M_H = 52.0$ dN·m
> - Time to reach $M_L + 2$ dN·m (i.e., $t_{s2}$) = **3.2 min**
> - Time to reach $M_L + 0.90(M_H - M_L)$ (i.e., $t_{90}$) = **8.7 min**

**Task:** Calculate the (a) optimum cure time, (b) scorch time, and (c) Cure Rate Index.

### Solution:

**(a) Optimum Cure Time $t_{90}$:**
Already read from the rheograph: $t_{90} = 8.7$ min

Verify the reference torque:
$$
M_{90} = M_L + 0.90\,(M_H - M_L) = 8.0 + 0.90\,(52.0 - 8.0)
$$
$$
M_{90} = 8.0 + 0.90 \times 44.0 = 8.0 + 39.6 = 47.6 \text{ dN·m}
$$
This matches the level on the rheograph at $t = 8.7$ min. ✔

**(b) Scorch Time $t_{s2}$:**
$$
t_{s2} = 3.2 \text{ min} \quad \text{(directly from rheograph at } M_L + 2 \text{ dN·m)}
$$
$$
M_{s2} = 8.0 + 2.0 = 10.0 \text{ dN·m}
$$

**(c) Cure Rate Index (CRI):**
$$
\text{CRI} = \dfrac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s2}} = \dfrac{100}{8.7 - 3.2} = \dfrac{100}{5.5}
$$
$$
\text{CRI} \approx 18.18 \;\text{min}^{-1}
$$

**Interpretation for the lab report:**
- A CRI of ~ 18 min⁻¹ indicates a **moderately fast-curing compound**, suitable for compression moulding.
- $t_{s2} = 3.2$ min gives the operator a **safe processing window** of 3.2 minutes before scorching begins.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ODR vs. MDR

| Feature | Oscillating Disc Rheometer (ODR) | Moving Die Rheometer (MDR) |
|---------|----------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Standard | ASTM D2084 | ASTM D5289 |
| Oscillating element | Lower die (cone/disc) | **Both** dies oscillate in opposite directions |
| Sample volume | Larger (~ 30 cm³) | Smaller (~ 4–8 cm³) |
| Heat-up time | ~ 60 s (slower) | ~ 5–10 s (much faster) |
| Sensitivity to early cure | Lower | **Higher** (better for thin articles) |
| Torque range | Up to ~ 100 dN·m | Up to ~ 200 dN·m |
| KTU Recommendation | Suitable for academic labs | Industry standard |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Moving Die Rheometer (MDR)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete functional flow of an MDR test, from sample loading to rheograph output:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sampleA[Uncured Rubber Pellet] --> loadB[Load Sample Between Heated Dies]
    loadB --> clampC[Apply Clamping Pressure 4.5 MPa]
    clampC --> heatD[Heat Dies to Test Temperature 160 C]
    heatD --> oscE[Oscillate Lower Die at 1.67 Hz 0.5 deg arc]
    oscE --> torqueF[Measure Torque via Load Cell]
    torqueF --> recordG[Record Torque vs Time on Data Logger]
    recordG --> plotH[Plot Rheograph Torque Time Curve]
    plotH --> extractI[Extract ML MH ts2 t90 Values]
    extractI --> calcJ[Compute CRI and Delta M]
    calcK[Output Final Report] --> extractI
    extractI --> reportL[Generate Cure Characteristics Report]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Mapping of the Rheograph and Its Key Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisT[Time axis min] --> pM90[Point M90 at t90]
    axisT --> pts2[Point ts2 scorch time]
    axisT --> ptML[Point ML minimum torque]
    axisM[Torque axis dN m] --> pM90
    axisM --> pMH[Point MH maximum torque plateau]
    pML[(ML minimum torque)] --> pM90
    pMH[(MH maximum torque)] --> pM90
    pM90[t90 optimum cure time 90 percent cure]
    pML --> curveR[Cure Curve Sigmoidal Rise]
    pMH --> curveR
    curveR --> pM90
    pts2[(ts2 scorch point ML + 2 dN m)]
    curveR --> pts2
    pML --> pts2
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart for "Is the Compound Properly Cured?"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Sample Ready] --> runB[Run Rheometer Test at 160 C]
    runB --> obsC{Cure curve plateau achieved?}
    obsC -- No --> failD[Under cure detected Increase t90]
    obsC -- Yes --> checkE{MH minus ML within spec?}
    checkE -- No --> failF[Abnormal crosslink density Reformulate]
    checkE -- Yes --> checkG{Reversion present in plateau?}
    checkG -- Yes --> warnH[Reversion noted Reduce molding time]
    checkG -- No --> passI[Compound passes CRI and t90 criteria]
    passI --> endJ[End Report CRI and cure time to QC]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme] Define the term "Cure Time (t₉₀)" of a rubber compound and explain its industrial significance.

**Model Answer:**
Cure time, denoted as $t_{90}$, is defined as the time required at a given vulcanization temperature for the rubber compound to achieve 90 % of the maximum attainable crosslink density. On a rheograph, it corresponds to the time at which the torque reaches the value $M_L + 0.90(M_H - M_L)$. **Industrially, t₉₀ is the most important cure parameter** because it directly determines the mould residence time in compression, transfer, or injection moulding of rubber articles. If a part is cured for less than $t_{90}$, it will be under-cured (poor mechanical properties, high compression set, tacky surface). If cured for much longer, it may over-cure (embrittlement, reversion in natural rubber, waste of energy and press time). Hence $t_{90}$ is the single most important specification used by tyre, seal, hose, and footwear industries to set production cycle times.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme] What is the "scorch time $t_{s2}$" of a rubber compound? Why is it considered a measure of processing safety?

**Model Answer:**
Scorch time $t_{s2}$ is the time elapsed, at the vulcanization temperature, before the torque rises to a value of $M_L + 2$ dN·m above the minimum torque on the rheograph. It is also called the *in-cure induction period*. $t_{s2}$ is a measure of **processing safety** because it tells the compounder/moulder how much time is available for handling the rubber stock — mixing, extruding, calendering, placing into the mould — *before* premature vulcanization (scorching) sets in. If the total processing time exceeds $t_{s2}$, the rubber will cure inside the barrel, die, or mould cavity, causing defects and machine downtime. A compound with $t_{s2} = 3$–$5$ minutes at 160 °C is considered to have a good balance of processing safety and cure rate.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Rheograph Analysis and Cure Time Calculation

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme | Module Experiment 2]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Describe, with a neat labelled sketch, the construction and working of a **Moving Die Rheometer (MDR)** used for the determination of cure time of a rubber compound. Mention the relevant ASTM standard.

(b) **[7 Marks]** During the determination of cure time of an SBR rubber compound at 160 °C in an ODR, the following data were obtained from the rheograph:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| $M_L$ (Minimum Torque) | 6.5 dN·m |
| $M_H$ (Maximum Torque) | 48.0 dN·m |
| $t_{s2}$ (Scorch Time) | 4.1 min |
| $t_{90}$ (Optimum Cure Time) | 9.6 min |

Calculate:
(i) The torque at 90 % cure ($M_{90}$).
(ii) The Cure Rate Index (CRI).
(iii) The processing-safe time window.
(iv) Comment on the crosslink density of the compound based on $\Delta M$.

### Model Solution:

**(a) Construction and Working of MDR — [7 Marks]**

**Construction (Key Components):**
1. **Upper Die (stationary):** A heated, biconical metal die with embedded electrical heater and temperature sensor (thermocouple).
2. **Lower Die (oscillating):** A matching biconical die mounted on a shaft driven by an oscillating mechanism (eccentric cam or servo motor).
3. **Heating System:** Electric heaters maintaining die temperature within ± 0.3 °C of set point (typically 150 °C, 160 °C, or 180 °C).
4. **Pneumatic / Hydraulic Ram:** Applies closing force on the dies (~ 4.5 MPa chamber pressure) to confine the sample and prevent slippage.
5. **Load Cell / Torque Transducer:** Mounted on the lower die shaft; converts mechanical torque to an electrical signal.
6. **Oscillating Drive:** Produces sinusoidal oscillation of $\pm 0.5°$ at 1.67 Hz (100 cycles per minute).
7. **Data Acquisition System:** Plots torque (dN·m) versus time (min) on a chart or digital display — this is the **rheograph**.

**Working Principle:**
1. A small disc of uncured rubber (~ 4–8 cm³) is placed on the lower die.
2. The upper die closes, sealing the sample and applying pressure.
3. Both dies are brought to the test temperature; the lower die begins to oscillate sinusoidally.
4. As the rubber heats, viscosity drops (torque falls to $M_L$).
5. As vulcanization proceeds, crosslinks form, viscosity rises, and the torque required to oscillate the die increases.
6. The torque is recorded continuously until a clear plateau ($M_H$) is observed.
7. The rheograph is analyzed to extract $M_L$, $M_H$, $t_{s1}$, $t_{s2}$, and $t_{90}$.

**ASTM Standard:** **ASTM D5289 – 19** (Standard Test Method for Rubber Property — Vulcanization Using Rotorless Cure Meters).

**(b) Numerical Solution — [7 Marks]**

**(i) Torque at 90 % cure ($M_{90}$):**
$$
M_{90} = M_L + 0.90\,(M_H - M_L)
$$
$$
M_{90} = 6.5 + 0.90 \times (48.0 - 6.5) = 6.5 + 0.90 \times 41.5
$$
$$
M_{90} = 6.5 + 37.35 = \mathbf{43.85 \text{ dN·m}}
$$

**(ii) Cure Rate Index (CRI):**
$$
\text{CRI} = \dfrac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s2}} = \dfrac{100}{9.6 - 4.1} = \dfrac{100}{5.5}
$$
$$
\text{CRI} \approx \mathbf{18.18 \text{ min}^{-1}}
$$

**(iii) Processing-Safe Time Window:**
$$
\Delta t = t_{s2} = \mathbf{4.1 \text{ minutes}}
$$
This is the maximum time the compounder has to mix, extrude, and load the compound into the mould before scorching begins.

**(iv) Comment on Crosslink Density:**
$$
\Delta M = M_H - M_L = 48.0 - 6.5 = \mathbf{41.5 \text{ dN·m}}
$$
Since $\Delta M$ is directly proportional to crosslink density (Flory network theory), the value 41.5 dN·m indicates a **moderately high crosslink density**, typical of a properly compounded SBR tread stock. This level is sufficient for applications requiring good abrasion resistance and tensile strength, but the moulder must avoid over-cure to prevent embrittlement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Question (Module Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme | Module Experiment 2]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the **vulcanization process** in natural rubber with a sulfur-based curing system. Discuss the role of **accelerators** and **activators** with suitable examples, and explain how they influence the cure time $t_{90}$ and scorch time $t_{s2}$.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A rubber compound shows the following cure characteristics at 150 °C and 170 °C:

| Temperature | $t_{s2}$ (min) | $t_{90}$ (min) |
|-------------|----------------|----------------|
| 150 °C | 6.8 | 18.4 |
| 170 °C | 2.3 | 6.1 |

(i) Calculate the Cure Rate Index (CRI) at both temperatures.
(ii) Comment on how temperature affects the vulcanization kinetics.
(iii) Using the Arrhenius equation, estimate the apparent activation energy of cure.

Take $R = 8.314\ \text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$.

### Model Solution:

**(a) Vulcanization Process and Role of Additives — [7 Marks]**

**Vulcanization Reaction:**
Natural rubber (polyisoprene, *cis-1,4-polyisoprene*) is vulcanized by heating with **sulfur** (typically 1.5–3.0 phr) in the presence of accelerators and activators. The reaction forms polysulfidic crosslinks ($\text{—C—S}_x\text{—C—}$) between adjacent polymer chains, transforming the soft, tacky raw rubber into a tough, elastic, thermoset network.

The overall simplified reaction is:
$$
\text{Polymer} - H + S_8 + H - \text{Polymer} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Polymer} - S_x - \text{Polymer} + H_2S \uparrow
$$

**Role of Accelerators (e.g., MBT, CBS, TMTD):**
- **Accelerators** drastically reduce vulcanization time, allowing cure to occur in 5–10 minutes instead of hours.
- Examples: **2-Mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT)**, **N-Cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazolesulfenamide (CBS)**, **Tetramethylthiuram disulfide (TMTD)**.
- They form active sulfurating complexes that speed up the formation of crosslinks.
- Effect on $t_{90}$ and $t_{s2}$: CBS-based accelerators give a **long scorch delay** (good processing safety) followed by a **fast cure** — ideal for thick articles.

**Role of Activators (e.g., ZnO + Stearic Acid):**
- **Zinc oxide (ZnO)** reacts with **stearic acid** to form **zinc stearate**, which is the true active species.
- They "activate" the accelerator, making it more effective.
- Typical dosage: 3–5 phr ZnO + 1–2 phr stearic acid.

**Influence on Cure Parameters:**
| Additive | Effect on $t_{90}$ | Effect on $t_{s2}$ |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| More sulfur | Slight reduction | Slight reduction |
| More accelerator | Significant reduction | Small change |
| CBS (delayed-action) | Reduced | **Increased (safer)** |
| TMTD (ultra-fast) | Greatly reduced | Greatly reduced (scorchy) |

**(b) Numerical and Kinetic Analysis — [7 Marks]**

**(i) CRI at Both Temperatures:**

At **150 °C (423 K):**
$$
\text{CRI}_{150} = \dfrac{100}{18.4 - 6.8} = \dfrac{100}{11.6} \approx \mathbf{8.62 \text{ min}^{-1}}
$$

At **170 °C (443 K):**
$$
\text{CRI}_{170} = \dfrac{100}{6.1 - 2.3} = \dfrac{100}{3.8} \approx \mathbf{26.32 \text{ min}^{-1}}
$$

**(ii) Effect of Temperature on Kinetics:**
Increasing the temperature from 150 °C to 170 °C (a 20 °C rise) has:
- **Tripled the CRI** (from 8.62 to 26.32 min⁻¹) — the cure is roughly **3× faster**.
- **Reduced $t_{90}$ by ~ 67 %** (from 18.4 to 6.1 min).
- **Reduced $t_{s2}$ by ~ 66 %** (from 6.8 to 2.3 min), meaning **less processing safety** at higher temperatures.

This is consistent with the **Arrhenius law** — the rate constant $k$ increases exponentially with temperature, and since $t_{90} = \ln(10)/k$, $t_{90}$ decreases exponentially.

**(iii) Activation Energy from Arrhenius Equation:**

The Arrhenius equation is:
$$
k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R\,T}
$$
Since $t_{90} = \ln(10)/k$, we have:
$$
\ln(t_{90}) = \ln(\ln 10) - \ln A + \dfrac{E_a}{R\,T}
$$
Plotting $\ln(t_{90})$ vs. $1/T$ gives slope $= E_a / R$.

**Compute the data:**

At 423 K:
$$
\ln(18.4) = 2.912
$$
$$
\dfrac{1}{T_1} = \dfrac{1}{423} = 2.364 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

At 443 K:
$$
\ln(6.1) = 1.808
$$
$$
\dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1}{443} = 2.257 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Slope:**
$$
\text{slope} = \dfrac{\ln(t_{90,\,2}) - \ln(t_{90,\,1})}{1/T_2 - 1/T_1} = \dfrac{1.808 - 2.912}{2.257 \times 10^{-3} - 2.364 \times 10^{-3}}
$$
$$
\text{slope} = \dfrac{-1.104}{-1.07 \times 10^{-4}} = 10{,}318\ \text{K}
$$

**Activation Energy:**
$$
E_a = \text{slope} \times R = 10{,}318 \times 8.314 \approx \mathbf{85{,}790\ \text{J/mol} \approx 85.8\ \text{kJ/mol}}
$$

**Interpretation:** $E_a \approx 86\ \text{kJ/mol}$ is in the typical range for sulfur-cured elastomers (80–120 kJ/mol), confirming a chemically-controlled vulcanization reaction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Confusing $t_{s1}$ and $t_{s2}$:** $t_{s1}$ corresponds to $M_L + 1$ dN·m; $t_{s2}$ corresponds to $M_L + 2$ dN·m. KTU accepts either if the value is consistently applied, but specifying the standard increment avoids ambiguity.
> 2. **Using $M_H - M_L$ for $M_{90}$ instead of $0.90(M_H - M_L)$:** This is a classic 1-mark error. Always write the full expression $M_L + 0.90(M_H - M_L)$.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Every numerical answer must carry its unit (dN·m for torque, min for time, min⁻¹ for CRI, kJ/mol for $E_a$).
> 4. **Inverting CRI formula:** The denominator is $(t_{90} - t_{s2})$, not $(t_{s2} - t_{90})$ — both must be in minutes and $t_{90} > t_{s2}$.
> 5. **Wrong Arrhenius plot axes:** Plot $\ln(t_{90})$ vs. $1/T$ (NOT $t_{90}$ vs. $T$) to get a straight line whose slope gives $E_a/R$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Cure time $t_{90}$** = Time to reach $M_L + 0.90(M_H - M_L)$ on the rheograph.
- ✅ **Scorch time $t_{s2}$** = Time to reach $M_L + 2$ dN·m — the **processing safety limit**.
- ✅ **$M_L$** = minimum torque → indicates stock viscosity / processability.
- ✅ **$M_H$** = maximum torque → indicates fully-cured stiffness.
- ✅ **$\Delta M = M_H - M_L$** ∝ crosslink density (Flory network theory).
- ✅ **CRI = 100 / (t₉₀ − tₛ₂)** — higher CRI = faster cure.
- ✅ **Instruments:** ODR (ASTM D2084) and MDR (ASTM D5289); MDR is the industry standard.
- ✅ **Kinetics:** First-order model gives $t_{90} = \ln(10)/k$ where $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$.
- ✅ **Arrhenius plot:** $\ln(t_{90})$ vs. $1/T$ → slope = $E_a/R$.
- ✅ **Temperature effect:** Every 10 °C rise roughly **halves** $t_{90}$ (Rule of thumb, but verify with $E_a$).
- ✅ **Vulcanization chemistry:** Sulfur + accelerator (CBS, MBT, TMTD) + activator (ZnO + stearic acid) — gives a balance of fast cure and safe processing.
- ✅ **Industries using cure time:** Tyres, moulded seals, hoses, belts, footwear, aerospace elastomers.
- ✅ **Common lab temperature:** 160 °C (some studies use 150 °C or 180 °C depending on compound).
- ✅ **Sample size for MDR:** ~ 4–8 cm³; for ODR: ~ 30 cm³ (larger).
- ✅ **Oscillation:** 1.67 Hz (100 cpm) at ± 0.5° (MDR) or ± 1° (ODR).
- ✅ **Always report $M_L$, $M_H$, $t_{s1}$, $t_{s2}$, $t_{90}$, $\Delta M$, and CRI** in a KTU lab record.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Expected Outcomes (COs):** CO1 — Recall principles; CO2 — Understand instrumentation; CO3 — Apply formulas to extract cure parameters; CO4 — Analyze rheograph data and comment on crosslink density.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Mastication Time on Plasticity/Viscosity
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL507 - PCPOL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of Mastication Time on Plasticity/Viscosity

# Effect of Mastication Time on Plasticity/Viscosity

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Mastication** is defined as the *controlled, mechanical working (shearing) of raw, high-molecular-weight elastomers on a two-roll open mill or in an internal mixer, in the presence of atmospheric oxygen, with the primary objective of reducing the polymer chain length (molecular weight) and thereby increasing the plasticity and reducing the viscosity of the elastomer* to facilitate subsequent compounding operations.

In the context of rubber technology, the **Plasticity Number** quantifies the *degree of permanent deformation* a rubber sample undergoes under a specified load, temperature, and time. It is the inverse representation of elasticity — a higher plasticity number indicates a softer, more processable rubber.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPOL507 – Module 2):**
> Mastication is the *first unit operation* in rubber compounding. It is a *time-dependent* process in which two competing reactions occur simultaneously:
> 1. **Mechano-chemical chain scission** (reduces MW → softens rubber).
> 2. **Oxygen-induced cross-linking / recombination** (increases MW → re-hardens rubber).
> A dynamic *equilibrium* between these two determines the final plasticity/viscosity.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine trying to stretch a **cold chewing gum strand** vs. a **warmed, well-kneaded dough**. The raw natural rubber (NR) as it arrives from the latex stage is like a giant intertwined ball of extremely long, sticky noodles — tough, springy, and almost impossible to mix with other ingredients (carbon black, sulfur, oils).

When we **knead, squeeze, and roll** this rubber repeatedly (just as one would knead bread dough to develop gluten), we are mechanically **snipping** the long polymer chains into shorter, more manageable ones. The oxygen in air acts like a chemical *scissor-attendant*, catching the freshly broken chain-ends and preventing them from sticking back together.

- **Short kneading time** → Noodles still long → rubber is hard and bouncy (low plasticity, high viscosity).
- **Optimal kneading time** → Noodles manageable → rubber is soft and pliable (high plasticity, low viscosity).
- **Over-kneading time** → Some noodles tangle into knots → rubber stiffens again (plasticity drops, viscosity rises).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Test Metrics (Bold Highlights)**
> - Standard Mastication Test Temperature: **100 °C** (Williams Plastimeter).
> - Standard Rotor Temperature for Mooney Viscosity: **100 °C**.
> - Standard Mooney Test Notation: **ML(1+4) at 100 °C** — 1 min preheat + 4 min rotor running.
> - Typical Raw NR Mooney Viscosity: **~ 90–110 MU** (mill-massed grade).
> - Typical Masticated NR Target Mooney: **~ 40–60 MU**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Mastication — Step-by-Step Logic

Mastication is governed by the **Peachey–Kohman mechanism (1921)**, which is the cornerstone explanation for the oxygen-dependent breakdown of natural rubber.

### Step-by-Step Free-Radical Mechanism

1. **Mechanical Shear Input:** The polymer mass is forced through the nip of a two-roll mill. The enormous shear stress ($\tau$) overcomes the **C–C bond dissociation energy** of the polymer backbone (~$ \mathbf{350\ kJ/mol} $ for C–C bond).
2. **Homolytic Chain Scission:** Long polymer chains rupture mid-way to form two **macro-alkyl radicals** ($\text{R}^\bullet$).
3. **Oxygen Capture (Critical Step):** Atmospheric O₂ rapidly reacts with the macro-radical to form a **peroxy radical** ($\text{ROO}^\bullet$).
4. **Peroxide Decomposition:** The peroxy radical abstracts a hydrogen, forming a **hydroperoxide** (ROOH), which subsequently decomposes to give shorter, oxygenated chain ends.
5. **Result:** Net **irreversible molecular weight reduction**.
6. **In the Absence of O₂:** The macro-radicals simply **recombine (crosslink)** and the rubber re-hardens — this is why mastication is inefficient in vacuum or nitrogen.

### The 'Why' Behind Each Step

| Why? | Explanation |
|---|---|
| Why does the rubber need breaking? | Raw NR has $\overline{M}_w \approx 10^6$, making it too elastic to mix with fillers. |
| Why is air essential? | O₂ traps the broken chain-ends, preventing recombination. |
| Why is the process time-dependent? | Each pass through the mill nip applies one shear pulse; cumulative effect scales with time. |
| Why does an equilibrium exist? | Two opposing reactions — *scission* (softening) and *recombination/cross-linking* (re-hardening) — reach a balance. |

## 2.2 Effect of Mastication Time on Plasticity & Viscosity

The relationship is fundamentally described by **first-order kinetics** (with a plateau at equilibrium).

### Plasticity vs. Time (Qualitative Trend)

$$\text{Plasticity Number} \; P(t) \;=\; P_{\infty} \;-\; \left(P_{\infty} - P_0\right)\, e^{-k\, t}$$

where:
- $P_0$ = initial plasticity (low, before mastication).
- $P_{\infty}$ = equilibrium/maximum plasticity.
- $k$ = first-order rate constant of mastication ($\text{min}^{-1}$).
- $t$ = mastication time (minutes).

### Viscosity vs. Molecular Weight (Mark–Houwink / Power Law)

The intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$ of a polymer is related to its viscosity-average molecular weight $M_v$ via:

$$[\eta] \;=\; K\, M_v^{\alpha}$$

where $K$ and $\alpha$ are Mark–Houwink constants (for NR in toluene at 25 °C: $K \approx 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$, $\alpha \approx 0.67$).

Since Mooney viscosity $\eta_M \propto [\eta] \propto M_v^{\alpha}$, a small drop in $M_v$ produces a measurable drop in Mooney viscosity.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Must Memorise for KTU Board Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = h_1 - h_2$ | Williams Plasticity Number | mm |
| 2 | $R = h_3 - h_2$ | Williams Recovery (elastic recovery) | mm |
| 3 | $P_{\text{reten}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{aged}}}{P_{\text{fresh}}} \times 100$ | Plasticity Retention Index (PRI) | % |
| 4 | $\eta_{sp} = \dfrac{\eta - \eta_0}{\eta_0}$ | Specific viscosity | dimensionless |
| 5 | $[\eta] = \lim\limits_{c\to 0}\!\dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c}$ | Intrinsic viscosity | dL/g |
| 6 | $[\eta] = K\, M_v^{\alpha}$ | Mark–Houwink equation | depends on K |
| 7 | $P(t) = P_{\infty} - (P_{\infty}-P_0)e^{-kt}$ | Time-dependent plasticity (1st order) | mm |
| 8 | $\eta_M \propto \overline{M}_w^{3.4}$ | Mooney viscosity scaling (entangled regime) | MU |
| 9 | $\tau = F/A$ | Shear stress at the roll nip | Pa |
| 10 | $\dot\gamma = \dfrac{v_{\text{roll}}}{\text{gap}}$ | Shear rate in the mill nip | s$^{-1}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tyre Industry:** Pre-mastication of NR controls the *processability window* before extrusion and calendering.
- **Footwear & Belting:** Masticated NR ensures uniform dispersion of fillers and accelerators.
- **Adhesive Tapes:** Lower Mooney grades yield better tack and solution viscosity.
- **Peptizers** (e.g., pentachlorothiophenol — PCTP, zinc 2-benzamidothiophenate) are added to **accelerate** mastication without over-heating the rubber, reducing energy cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Kinetics of Plasticity Evolution During Mastication

### Assumption Set
- The rubber has an initial (un-masticated) plasticity $P_0$.
- The rubber tends toward an equilibrium plasticity $P_{\infty}$ as $t \to \infty$.
- The rate of change of plasticity is proportional to the *remaining gap* $(P_{\infty} - P(t))$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the rate equation.**

$$\frac{dP}{dt} \;=\; k\,\big(P_{\infty} - P(t)\big)$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables.**

$$dP \;=\; k\,\big(P_{\infty} - P\big)\, dt$$

$$\frac{dP}{P_{\infty} - P} \;=\; k\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $t = 0$ (where $P = P_0$) to time $t$ (where $P = P(t)$).**

$$\int_{P_0}^{P(t)} \frac{dP}{P_{\infty} - P} \;=\; k \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the left-hand integral.**

Let $u = P_{\infty} - P$, so $du = -dP$. The integral becomes:

$$-\ln\big(P_{\infty} - P\big)\Big|_{P_0}^{P(t)} \;=\; k\, t$$

**Step 5 — Apply the limits.**

$$-\ln\!\big(P_{\infty} - P(t)\big) + \ln\!\big(P_{\infty} - P_0\big) \;=\; k\, t$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\infty} - P_0}{P_{\infty} - P(t)}\right) \;=\; k\, t$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate both sides.**

$$\frac{P_{\infty} - P_0}{P_{\infty} - P(t)} \;=\; e^{kt}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $P(t)$.**

$$P_{\infty} - P(t) \;=\; \big(P_{\infty} - P_0\big)\, e^{-kt}$$

$$\boxed{P(t) \;=\; P_{\infty} \;-\; \big(P_{\infty} - P_0\big)\, e^{-k\, t}}$$

This is the *final analytical expression* used in laboratory reports and in KTU Board derivations.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Given:**
- Initial plasticity of raw NR: $P_0 = 0.10\ \text{mm}$.
- Equilibrium plasticity: $P_{\infty} = 0.45\ \text{mm}$.
- First-order rate constant at 70 °C roll temp: $k = 0.10\ \text{min}^{-1}$.
- Find: Plasticity after $t = 10\ \text{min}$ and $t = 25\ \text{min}$ of mastication.

**Solution — At t = 10 min:**

$$P(10) \;=\; 0.45 \;-\; (0.45 - 0.10)\, e^{-0.10 \times 10}$$

$$P(10) \;=\; 0.45 \;-\; (0.35)\, e^{-1.0}$$

$$P(10) \;=\; 0.45 \;-\; (0.35)(0.3679)$$

$$P(10) \;=\; 0.45 \;-\; 0.1288$$

$$\boxed{P(10) \approx 0.321\ \text{mm}}$$

**Solution — At t = 25 min:**

$$P(25) \;=\; 0.45 \;-\; (0.45 - 0.10)\, e^{-0.10 \times 25}$$

$$P(25) \;=\; 0.45 \;-\; (0.35)\, e^{-2.5}$$

$$P(25) \;=\; 0.45 \;-\; (0.35)(0.0821)$$

$$P(25) \;=\; 0.45 \;-\; 0.0287$$

$$\boxed{P(25) \approx 0.421\ \text{mm}}$$

**Interpretation:** Beyond ~25 minutes, additional mastication yields only marginal gain — a classic *diminishing returns* behaviour that justifies stopping the process at the optimal economic time.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Variables Interact

| Mastication Time ↓ \ Variable → | Plasticity (↑) | Mooney Viscosity (↓) | Molecular Weight (↓) | Elastic Recovery (↓) | Gel Content (↓) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0 min (Raw NR)** | Very Low (~0.05 mm) | Very High (~95 MU) | Very High (~10⁶) | High | High |
| **5 min** | Low | High | High | Moderate-High | Moderate |
| **15 min** | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| **30 min** | High | Low (~50 MU) | Low (~5×10⁵) | Low | Very Low |
| **45 min (Equilibrium)** | Plateau | Plateau | Plateau | Plateau | Plateau |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** A practical compromise is reached at roughly **15–25 minutes** for standard NR grades. Further mastication beyond ~30 min is wasteful in energy and may begin to *increase* viscosity due to cross-linking from accumulated peroxide radicals.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — From Raw Rubber to Plasticised Compound

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Natural Rubber Latex Coagulum] --> B[Shredding and Washing]
    B --> C[Initial Bale Inspection MW approx 10^6]
    C --> D{Choose Mastication Route}
    D --> E[Two Roll Open Mill Mastication]
    D --> F[Internal Mixer Banbury Mastication]
    E --> G[Air Exposure Oxygen Plays Critical Role]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Free Radical Chain Scission Macro Radicals]
    H --> I[O2 Capture Peroxy Radicals ROO]
    I --> J[Stable Hydroperoxide ROOH Formation]
    J --> K[Net MW Reduction]
    K --> L[Plasticity Rises Viscosity Falls]
    L --> M[Sample Withdrawal at 5 10 15 20 30 min]
    M --> N[Testing Williams Plastimeter and Mooney Viscometer]
    N --> O[Plot Plasticity versus Time Curve]
    O --> P[Determine Rate Constant k from Slope]
    P --> Q[Report Optimal Mastication Time]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Competing Reactions During Mastication

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Mechanical Energy Input]
        A1[Shear Stress at Roll Nip] --> A2[Polymer Chain Scission]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Net Effect on Properties]
        F1[Plasticity Number UP] --> F2[Mooney Viscosity DOWN]
        F2 --> F3[Processability IMPROVED]
    end
    A2 --> B1[Macro Alkyl Radicals]
    B1 --> C1{In Presence of Atmospheric O2}
    C1 -- Yes --> D1[Peroxy Radical ROO Forms]
    D1 --> E1[Hydroperoxide ROOH Decomposes]
    E1 --> F1
    C1 -- No Nitrogen Vacuum --> D2[Radical Radical Recombination]
    D2 --> E2[Cross linking Re hardening]
    E2 --> F4[Plasticity DROPS Viscosity RISES]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Laboratory Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LAB[Laboratory Mastication Test Rig]
        R1[Two Roll Mill Front Roll 24 rpm Back Roll 30 rpm] --> R2[Friction Ratio 1 to 1.25]
        R2 --> R3[Nip Gap 1 to 2 mm]
    end
    LAB --> S1[Sample Taken Every 5 min up to 30 min]
    S1 --> S2[Williams Plastimeter 100 C 10 min Load 5 kg]
    S1 --> S3[Mooney Viscometer ML 1 plus 4 at 100 C]
    S2 --> D1[Record h1 h2 h3 Compute P and R]
    S3 --> D2[Record Mooney Units MU]
    D1 --> PLOT[Plot P versus t and eta M versus t]
    D2 --> PLOT
    PLOT --> RPT[Interpret Optimal Mastication Time]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define mastication. Why is the presence of atmospheric oxygen essential for the mastication of natural rubber?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Mastication is the *mechanical working of raw rubber on a two-roll mill or internal mixer to reduce its molecular weight and viscosity, making it suitable for compounding* (1 mark).

Atmospheric oxygen is essential because, during mechanical shearing, the polymer chains undergo homolytic scission to form macro-alkyl radicals (1 mark). In the absence of oxygen, these radicals recombine, reversing the softening effect. Oxygen traps the radicals to form stable peroxy and hydroperoxide groups, leading to *irreversible* molecular-weight reduction (1 mark).

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between the Williams Plasticity Number and the Mooney Viscosity. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Williams Plasticity Number | Mooney Viscosity |
|---|---|---|
| Measured Property | Permanent deformation (plasticity) under a static load | Resistance to flow under a rotating rotor |
| Test Conditions | 100 °C, 5 kg load, fixed time | ML(1+4) at 100 °C |
| Reported Value | Dimensionless height change (mm) | Mooney Units (MU) |
| Application | Quality-control check for batch-to-batch consistency of NR bales (1 mark) | Processability benchmarking for tyre-grade rubber compounds (1 mark) |

The plasticity number reflects *deformability* whereas Mooney viscosity reflects *flow resistance* (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Peachey–Kohman mechanism** of mastication of natural rubber with the help of a neat flow diagram. Highlight the role of oxygen. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The plasticity of an NR sample follows first-order kinetics: $P(t) = P_\infty - (P_\infty - P_0)\, e^{-kt}$. If $P_0 = 0.08\ \text{mm}$, $P_\infty = 0.50\ \text{mm}$ and $k = 0.08\ \text{min}^{-1}$, calculate the plasticity after **(i)** 10 min and **(ii)** 30 min of mastication. Comment on the practical significance. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Mechanism (7 marks)

1. **Mechanical Shear (1 mark):** When raw NR is passed through the nip of a two-roll mill, high shear forces break the long C–C chains of polyisoprene homolytically, generating two **macro-alkyl radicals**.

2. **Role of Oxygen (2 marks):** Atmospheric O₂ rapidly reacts with the macro-radicals to form **peroxy radicals (ROO•)**, which then abstract hydrogen from adjacent chains to form **hydroperoxides (ROOH)**. These hydroperoxides decompose spontaneously, producing shorter, oxygenated chain ends. The net result is *irreversible* molecular-weight reduction.

3. **Without Oxygen (1 mark):** In inert atmospheres (N₂ or vacuum), the macro-radicals **recombine**, and the rubber re-hardens — no net breakdown occurs.

4. **Equilibrium (1 mark):** With continued mastication, the rate of scission eventually equals the rate of radical recombination, producing an *equilibrium plasticity*.

5. **Influence of Mastication Aids (1 mark):** Chemical peptizers like pentachlorothiophenol accelerate radical capture, reducing mastication time.

6. **Neat Flow Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Long Polymer Chain] --> B[Mechanical Shear at Roll Nip]
    B --> C[Macro Alkyl Radicals 2 R dot]
    C --> D{Atmospheric Oxygen Present}
    D -- YES --> E[Peroxy Radical ROO dot]
    E --> F[Hydroperoxide ROOH]
    F --> G[Stable Short Chain End Products]
    G --> H[Net MW Reduction Softening]
    D -- NO --> I[Radical Recombination]
    I --> J[Cross linking Re hardening]
```

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $P_0 = 0.08\ \text{mm}$, $P_\infty = 0.50\ \text{mm}$, $k = 0.08\ \text{min}^{-1}$.

**(i) At t = 10 min (3 marks):**

$$P(10) \;=\; 0.50 \;-\; (0.50 - 0.08)\, e^{-0.08 \times 10}$$

$$P(10) \;=\; 0.50 \;-\; (0.42)(0.4493)$$

$$P(10) \;=\; 0.50 \;-\; 0.1887$$

$$\boxed{P(10) \approx 0.311\ \text{mm}}$$

**(ii) At t = 30 min (3 marks):**

$$P(30) \;=\; 0.50 \;-\; (0.42)\, e^{-0.08 \times 30}$$

$$P(30) \;=\; 0.50 \;-\; (0.42)\, e^{-2.4}$$

$$P(30) \;=\; 0.50 \;-\; (0.42)(0.0907)$$

$$P(30) \;=\; 0.50 \;-\; 0.0381$$

$$\boxed{P(30) \approx 0.462\ \text{mm}}$$

**Practical Significance (1 mark):**
The plasticity rises rapidly between 0–10 min and approaches its equilibrium value by ~30 min. Beyond ~30 min, additional mastication yields only a marginal improvement (< 0.04 mm) — therefore the *economically optimal* mastication time is **~20–25 minutes** for this grade of NR.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **Williams Plastimeter** test for rubber. Derive an expression for the **Plasticity Retention Index (PRI)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a labelled graph, explain how **Mooney viscosity and plasticity of natural rubber change with mastication time**. Why does Mooney viscosity reach a plateau? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Williams Plastimeter & PRI (7 marks)

**Apparatus (2 marks):**
The Williams Plastimeter consists of a heated platen maintained at **100 °C**, a polished metal plate, a loading lever that applies a **5 kg load**, and a dial gauge (micrometer) reading to **0.01 mm**.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
- A rubber disc of volume **2 cm³** (approx 3 mm thick) is placed on the heated platen.
- Pre-heated for **10 minutes** at 100 °C.
- Load of 5 kg is applied for a further **5 minutes**.
- After removing the load, the thickness $h_2$ is measured immediately.
- After waiting **1 minute** for elastic recovery, the thickness $h_3$ is measured.

**Calculations (1 mark):**
- **Williams Plasticity Number:** $P = h_1 - h_2$
- **Williams Recovery:** $R = h_3 - h_2$

**PRI Derivation (2 marks):**

The Plasticity Retention Index quantifies the resistance of raw NR to oxidation during aging:

$$\text{PRI} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{\text{aged}}}{P_{\text{fresh}}} \times 100\ \ [\%]$$

where:
- $P_{\text{fresh}}$ = plasticity number of the original NR sample.
- $P_{\text{aged}}$ = plasticity number of the same NR after accelerated aging in an oven (typically 30 min at 140 °C, per IS 3660).

A **PRI > 60 %** indicates good-quality NR; **PRI < 30 %** indicates poor aging resistance.

#### Part (b) — Graphical Behaviour (7 marks)

**Labelled Description (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mastication Time Axis X 0 to 30 min] --> B[Plot Two Y Axes]
    B --> C[Left Axis Plasticity mm Increases Monotonically to Plateau]
    B --> D[Right Axis Mooney Viscosity MU Decreases Monotonically to Plateau]
    C --> E[Initial Slope Steep 0 to 10 min]
    C --> F[Diminishing Slope 10 to 25 min]
    C --> G[Plateau Region 25 to 30 min]
    D --> H[Mirror Image of Plasticity Curve]
    D --> I[Equilibrium Point at Same Time t_eq]
```

- At **t = 0**: high Mooney (~95 MU), low plasticity (~0.08 mm).
- At **t = 10 min**: Mooney drops to ~70 MU; plasticity rises to ~0.31 mm.
- At **t = 30 min**: Mooney plateaus at ~50 MU; plasticity plateaus at ~0.46 mm.

**Why does Mooney viscosity reach a plateau? (4 marks):**

1. **Two competing reactions:** As mastication proceeds, the rate of mechano-chemical chain scission (which reduces MW) decreases because the chains become shorter and easier to align in the shear field.
2. **Oxygen-induced cross-linking:** At the same time, the accumulation of peroxy radicals and hydroperoxides promotes recombination/cross-linking, *increasing* MW locally.
3. **Dynamic Equilibrium:** When the rate of scission equals the rate of cross-linking, the *average* molecular weight becomes constant, and so does Mooney viscosity (which scales as $M_w^{3.4}$).
4. **Practical implication:** Beyond the plateau, further mastication is wasteful — energy consumed without any softening benefit.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in This Question:**
> 1. **Confusing Plasticity with Elasticity:** Students often write "plasticity is the property to recover" — wrong! Plasticity is *permanent* deformation; elasticity is *recoverable*. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the role of Oxygen:** Mastication does NOT work efficiently in nitrogen/vacuum. Always mention oxygen explicitly. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Units of Williams Plasticity:** It is a *length difference* in **mm**, not a dimensionless ratio. (–0.5 mark)
> 4. **Sign of $e^{-kt}$:** In the first-order equation $P(t) = P_\infty - (P_\infty - P_0) e^{-kt}$, the exponential term must have a **negative** exponent. Reversing it gives wrong trends. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Mooney Test Notation:** Always write **ML(1+4) at 100 °C** — never omit the bracketed preheat + running time. (–0.5 mark)
> 6. **PRI is a percentage:** Final answer *must* carry the % unit. (–0.5 mark)
> 7. **Mark–Houwink equation:** $[\eta] = K M_v^\alpha$ — confusing $M_v$ with $M_w$ or $\overline{M}_n$ is a common error. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Mastication** = mechanical working of raw rubber to reduce MW and viscosity.
- **Oxygen is indispensable** — traps macro-radicals to prevent recombination (Peachey–Kohman mechanism).
- **Williams Plasticity Number** $P = h_1 - h_2$ (mm); **Recovery** $R = h_3 - h_2$.
- **Mooney Viscosity** standard notation: **ML(1+4) at 100 °C**.
- **Plasticity Retention Index (PRI)** = $(P_{\text{aged}}/P_{\text{fresh}}) \times 100\ \%$.
- **First-order kinetics:** $P(t) = P_\infty - (P_\infty - P_0)\, e^{-kt}$.
- **Mark–Houwink relation:** $[\eta] = K M_v^\alpha$; for NR-toluene: $K \approx 5 \times 10^{-4}$, $\alpha \approx 0.67$.
- **Viscosity scaling (entangled regime):** $\eta_M \propto \overline{M}_w^{3.4}$.
- **Equilibrium plateau** arises from competition between **chain scission** and **radical recombination/cross-linking**.
- **Peptizers** (PCTP, zinc benzamidothiophenate) **accelerate** mastication and save energy.
- **Optimal mastication time** for standard NR ≈ **15–25 min** (diminishing returns beyond 30 min).
- **Higher roll temperature** lowers viscosity initially but **promotes cross-linking** at prolonged times.
- **Friction ratio** (typically 1:1.25) ensures repeated passage of rubber through the high-shear nip.
- **Always report PRI as a percentage** in the Board exam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Board Tip:** When asked to "discuss the effect of mastication time," always structure your answer in **three sections** — (i) initial phase (0–10 min: rapid change), (ii) intermediate phase (10–25 min: slowing change), (iii) equilibrium phase (>25 min: plateau). This guarantees full marks on the "graph-based" questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Practice in Mastication, Band Formation, Homogenisation and Mixing Using a Laboratory Mill
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL507 - PCPOL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Work Practice in Mastication, Band Formation, Homogenisation and Mixing Using a Laboratory Mill

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Laboratory Mill Operation

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme** polymer processing laboratory practice, the **Laboratory Two-Roll Mill** (also called a **Compounding Mill**, **Mixing Mill**, or **Open Mill**) is the fundamental workhorse equipment used to convert raw elastomers into processable, homogeneous rubber compounds.

According to **KTU PCPOL507 syllabus** for the *Module Experiment 2*, the work practice involves four sequential and inter-related unit operations performed on a laboratory two-roll mixing mill:

| Operation | KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Mastication** | The mechanical breakdown of long polymer chains (reduction of molecular weight / Mooney viscosity) by shear and oxidative scission, achieved by passing the elastomer repeatedly through the nip of the mill. |
| **Band Formation** | The phenomenon by which the softened, plasticized elastomer adheres to the front (slower) roll and forms a continuous rotating band due to adhesion, cohesion, and the friction ratio of the rolls. |
| **Homogenisation** | The redistribution of all constituents (polymer + additives) until statistical uniformity in composition is achieved throughout the matrix. |
| **Mixing** | The incorporation and dispersion of fillers, plasticizers, curatives, and stabilizers into the elastomeric matrix to produce a single, uniform compound. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** All four operations are performed **sequentially on the same laboratory mill** during a single experiment. Students are expected to observe and tabulate the *visual, tactile, and process parameter changes* as the experiment progresses from mastication → band formation → homogenisation → mixing.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Dough Roller (Mastication & Band Formation):**
> Imagine rolling out **chapati (Indian flatbread) dough** with a wooden rolling pin. The dough must first become soft (mastication). Once soft, it sticks to the pin and rotates with it as a continuous sheet — this is *band formation*. The differential speed (your hand pulling the pin forward faster than the dough returns) shears the gluten, breaking long chains — this is the **friction ratio** in action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Cement Mixer (Homogenisation & Mixing):**
> Picture a painter stirring **two different coloured paints** (red and white) with a stick. Initially, streaks of red appear in the white matrix. As stirring continues, the streaks disappear and the colour becomes uniform — this is *homogenisation*. Adding sand or pigment to the stirred paint and blending it in is the act of *incorporative mixing* (dispersion + distribution).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — The Pasta Machine:**
> A home pasta machine has two counter-rotating rollers with a small gap. The dough is fed in, comes out thinner, sticks to the bottom roll, and is folded back on top. This folding-back-and-forth mechanism is exactly how a **two-roll mill homogenises** a rubber compound.

---

## 1.3 The Laboratory Two-Roll Mill — Equipment Anatomy

The laboratory mill consists of two horizontal, counter-rotating, hollow rolls:

- **Front Roll** — rotates at a *lower* peripheral speed (v₁)
- **Back Roll** — rotates at a *higher* peripheral speed (v₂)
- The rolls are cooled/heated internally by circulating water/steam.
- The **nip gap** (clearance between rolls) is adjustable between **0.2 mm to 5 mm**.
- The standard friction ratio (FR) for a lab mill is **1 : 1.25** (or 1 : 1.4 for mastication).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Lab Mill Specifications (KTU Reference):**
> - **Roll diameter:** 150 mm (lab) or 250 mm (pilot)
> - **Roll length:** 300 – 500 mm
> - **Roll speed (front):** 18 – 24 rpm
> - **Friction ratio:** 1 : 1.25 (mixing) or 1 : 1.4 (mastication)
> - **Batch size:** 0.5 – 2 kg
> - **Safety devices:** emergency bar (safety cable), roll stop, hopper guards

---

## 1.4 Operating Parameters that Students Must Observe

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nip Gap | e | 0.5 – 3 mm | Smaller gap → higher shear → better mastication |
| Friction Ratio | FR = v₂/v₁ | 1 : 1.25 | Higher ratio → more shear → better breakdown |
| Roll Temperature | T | 40 – 80 °C | Lower T → more chain scission |
| Mill Loading | – | 60 – 80 % of roll volume | Overloading causes slip; under-loading wastes energy |
| Number of Passes | N | 5 – 20 | More passes → better dispersion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1 — Mastication: The Science of Chain Scission

Mastication is fundamentally a **mechano-chemical process**. When raw natural rubber (NR) is mechanically sheared:

1. The long, entangled polymer chains experience **tensile and shear stresses** above a critical threshold.
2. Chains rupture preferentially at the **mid-point** (where stress is maximum) — this is called **homolytic scission**.
3. The generated **free radicals (R•)** react immediately with atmospheric oxygen (O₂), forming **peroxy radicals (ROO•)** which terminate to give **chain-end carbonyl/hydroxyl groups**.
4. The net result: **reduction in number-average molecular weight (Mₙ)** and a corresponding drop in **Mooney viscosity**.
5. Synthetic rubbers (SBR, BR, EPDM) undergo primarily **thermal-mechanical breakdown**; some do not need mastication at all.

**Why it matters:** High viscosity raw polymer cannot accept fillers. Mastication brings the polymer to a workable viscosity (Mooney 50–70) before compounding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastication Efficiency Equation (Conceptual):**
> $$\eta_{m} = \frac{V_{0} - V_{t}}{V_{0}} \times 100\%$$
> where V₀ = initial Mooney viscosity, Vₜ = Mooney viscosity after time *t*.

---

### Step 2 — Band Formation: The "Sticky Roll" Phenomenon

Band formation is a **thermodynamic + rheological** event:

1. As the polymer warms up by viscous dissipation (and sometimes external heating), its **surface tackiness** increases.
2. The polymer sticks preferentially to the **slower (front) roll** due to:
   - **Adhesion:** greater affinity of rubber to cooler roll surface.
   - **Friction ratio effect:** slower roll experiences a higher coefficient of friction relative to the polymer mass.
3. A continuous **cylindrical band** of polymer wraps the front roll.
4. The **bank** (reservoir of polymer between the rolls at the nip) must be maintained; absence of bank → no band.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Principle:** *A healthy bank at the nip is the visual signature of correct mill operation.* No bank ⇒ slipping rolls ⇒ poor mastication.

---

### Step 3 — Homogenisation: Folding, Cutting & Re-feeding

1. Once a uniform band is formed, the operator **cuts the band** with a hand knife on alternate sides.
2. The cut piece is **folded back** and re-fed into the nip.
3. Each pass creates **laminations**; repeated folding multiplies the layers geometrically: n passes → 2ⁿ layers (in theory).
4. Random folding randomises the position of any additive particle, producing a statistically homogeneous compound.

---

### Step 4 — Mixing (Incorporative & Distributive)

1. **Incorporation (Wetting):** Fillers like **carbon black** are added slowly into the bank; the polymer must wet the agglomerates.
2. **Dispersion:** Shear forces break carbon black **agglomerates** into **aggregates** (primary particles).
3. **Distribution:** Shear + folding distributes the aggregates uniformly throughout the matrix.
4. **Plasticizer addition** softens the compound; **curatives** (sulfur, accelerators) are added LAST and at low temperature to avoid **scorch** (premature vulcanisation).

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Adding curatives too early or at high mill temperature (>110 °C for NR compounds) causes **scorch** — the compound begins to crosslink in the mill and becomes unusable. Always add curatives at the END of mixing.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Friction Ratio | $FR = \dfrac{v_{2}}{v_{1}} = \dfrac{N_{2} \cdot D_{2}}{N_{1} \cdot D_{1}}$ | dimensionless | v₂ > v₁ (back roll faster) |
| 2 | Peripheral Speed | $v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | m/s | D = roll diameter, N = rpm |
| 3 | Shear Rate in Nip | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{v_{2} - v_{1}}{e}$ | s⁻¹ | e = nip gap |
| 4 | Shear Stress | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa | η = polymer melt viscosity |
| 5 | Mill Power (Approx.) | $P = \tau \cdot v_{avg} \cdot A$ | W | A = contact area |
| 6 | Mastication Index | $MI = \dfrac{\eta_{0} - \eta_{t}}{\eta_{0}} \times 100$ | % | Measure of breakdown |
| 7 | Band Thickness | $t = e \cdot (1 + FR)$ | mm | Theoretical exit thickness |
| 8 | Cooling Load | $Q = m \cdot C_{p} \cdot \Delta T$ | J | Mass m, Cp = specific heat |
| 9 | Batch Volume Occupancy | $\phi = \dfrac{V_{batch}}{V_{roll \ gap}}$ | % | Should be 60–80 % |
| 10 | Effective Strain per Pass | $\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{t_{0}}{t}\right)$ | – | Compression strain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most-tested formulas in KTU ESE for this experiment:**
> 1. Friction Ratio (Q1)
> 2. Shear Rate (Q3)
> 3. Band Thickness (Q7)
> 4. Mastication Index (Q6)

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The two-roll mill is the **conceptual ancestor** of every modern internal mixer:

- **Banbury Mixer** (Frederick Banbury, 1916) — enclosed version of the two-roll mill concept.
- **Intermeshing vs Tangential rotors** in modern internal mixers are direct descendants.
- Understanding mill behaviour gives the polymer engineer the *intuition* needed to scale up to industrial mixers (mixer volume from 1.5 L lab to 600 L production).

The lab mill is **still used in R&D** because:
- Small batch sizes (50 g possible)
- Visual observation of band behaviour
- Easy sampling at intermediate stages
- Excellent for teaching the fundamentals of compounding

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Friction Ratio and Peripheral Speed

**Given:**
- Front roll diameter $D_1$, rotational speed $N_1$
- Back roll diameter $D_2$, rotational speed $N_2$
- Both rolls have equal diameter $D_1 = D_2 = D$ (typical for lab mill)

**To derive:** The friction ratio in terms of measurable parameters.

**Step 1:** Write the expression for peripheral (surface) linear speed of each roll.
$$v_1 = \pi D_1 N_1$$

$$v_2 = \pi D_2 N_2$$

**Step 2:** Since $D_1 = D_2 = D$ in a lab mill, the diameters cancel.
$$v_1 = \pi D N_1$$
$$v_2 = \pi D N_2$$

**Step 3:** By definition, the friction ratio is the ratio of the faster roll speed to the slower roll speed.
$$FR = \frac{v_2}{v_1}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expressions from Step 2.
$$FR = \frac{\pi D N_2}{\pi D N_1}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel the common factor $\pi D$.
$$FR = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

**Conclusion:** For equal-diameter rolls, the friction ratio reduces simply to the ratio of rotational speeds.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shear Rate in the Mill Nip

**Given:** A polymer layer entering the nip at thickness $t_0$ and exiting at thickness $t = e$ (nip gap), moving from faster roll to slower roll.

**Step 1:** Define the velocity difference across the nip.
$$\Delta v = v_2 - v_1$$

**Step 2:** Define the spatial distance over which this velocity difference acts — the nip gap thickness.
$$\Delta y = e$$

**Step 3:** Apply the definition of shear rate for Couette-like flow.
$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta y}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expressions.
$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{e}$$

**Step 5:** Express in terms of friction ratio. Since $v_2 = FR \cdot v_1$,
$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{FR \cdot v_1 - v_1}{e} = \frac{v_1(FR - 1)}{e}$$

**Final form:**
$$\boxed{\dot{\gamma} = \frac{v_1 (FR - 1)}{e}}$$

**Numerical example (KTU typical):**
- $v_1 = 0.20$ m/s, $FR = 1.25$, $e = 0.002$ m

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{0.20 \times (1.25 - 1)}{0.002} = \frac{0.20 \times 0.25}{0.002} = \frac{0.05}{0.002}$$

$$\dot{\gamma} = 25 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

This is the shear rate experienced by the polymer in the nip — a key driver of mastication.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Band Thickness on the Front Roll

**Given:** A polymer slab of initial thickness $t_0$ is fed into the nip. Mass conservation must hold.

**Step 1:** Apply the principle of conservation of volume (incompressible polymer).

Volume entering per unit time = Volume leaving per unit time.

**Step 2:** Volume entering:
$$Q_{in} = v_{avg} \cdot t_0 \cdot L$$

where L = roll length and $v_{avg} = (v_1 + v_2)/2$.

**Step 3:** Volume leaving, wrapped on the front roll (slower roll, speed v₁):
$$Q_{out} = v_1 \cdot t_{band} \cdot L$$

**Step 4:** Equate:
$$v_{avg} \cdot t_0 = v_1 \cdot t_{band}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for band thickness:
$$t_{band} = \frac{v_{avg}}{v_1} \cdot t_0$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $v_{avg} = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$ and $v_2 = FR \cdot v_1$:
$$t_{band} = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2 v_1} \cdot t_0 = \frac{1 + FR}{2} \cdot t_0$$

**Step 7:** When $t_0 \approx e$ (the nip gap) at steady state:
$$\boxed{t_{band} \approx e \cdot \frac{1 + FR}{2}}$$

For $FR = 1.25$, this gives $t_{band} = 1.125 \cdot e$, meaning the band is only slightly thicker than the nip gap.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operational Behaviour Summary

| Stage | Visual Observation | Operator Action | Temperature | Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial feeding | Lumps of polymer fall into nip | Drop pieces one at a time | 30 – 40 °C | 1 – 2 min |
| Mastication begin | Polymer sticks and slips; rolls chatter | Allow to warm gradually | 40 – 50 °C | 2 – 5 min |
| Band forms | Continuous band on front roll, bank visible | Adjust nip if band uneven | 50 – 60 °C | 5 – 8 min |
| Add fillers | Carbon black dust; band stiffens | Add slowly in 3 – 4 doses | 60 – 70 °C | 8 – 15 min |
| Add plasticizer | Band becomes soft and shiny | Add drop-wise | 60 – 70 °C | 15 – 18 min |
| Add curatives | Light coloured powders/oils | Add at the end | < 90 °C | 18 – 22 min |
| Homogenisation | Uniform colour, smooth edges | Cut and re-fold 6–8 times | < 90 °C | 22 – 25 min |
| Sheet-off | Final smooth, glossy sheet | Cut and weigh | Room T | 25 min |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU lab records:** Students must record (i) nip gap at each stage, (ii) roll temperature, (iii) number of passes, (iv) time for band formation, and (v) final batch weight.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Roll Mill Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Polymer Bale] --> B[Pre-weighing on Balance]
    B --> C[Nip Gap Set to 3 mm]
    C --> D[Initial Feeding into Mill]
    D --> E{Polymer Warmed?}
    E -- No --> F[Continue Rolling - Mastication]
    E -- Yes --> G[Band Formation on Front Roll]
    G --> H[Cut and Re-fold 3 Times]
    H --> I[Add Fillers Slowly - Carbon Black]
    I --> J[Add Plasticizer Drop-wise]
    J --> K[Add Curatives LAST]
    K --> L[Final Homogenisation - 6 Cuts]
    L --> M[Sheet-off and Weigh]
    M --> N[Record Observations]
    
    style A fill:#FFE5B4
    style M fill:#90EE90
    style N fill:#87CEEB
    style K fill:#FFB6C1
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Operations Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Stage1["Stage 1: MASTICATION"]
        M1[Long polymer chains] --> M2[Shear in nip gap]
        M2 --> M3[Homolytic chain scission]
        M3 --> M4[Free radicals + O2]
        M4 --> M5[Reduced molecular weight]
    end
    
    subgraph Stage2["Stage 2: BAND FORMATION"]
        B1[Softened polymer] --> B2[Adheres to front roll]
        B2 --> B3[Bank at nip maintained]
        B3 --> B4[Continuous rotating band]
    end
    
    subgraph Stage3["Stage 3: HOMOGENISATION"]
        H1[Uniform band] --> H2[Cut on alternate sides]
        H2 --> H3[Fold and re-feed]
        H3 --> H4[2 to N layers formed]
        H4 --> H5[Statistical uniformity]
    end
    
    subgraph Stage4["Stage 4: MIXING"]
        X1[Fillers added] --> X2[Incorporation wetting]
        X2 --> X3[Dispersion shear]
        X3 --> X4[Distribution folding]
        X4 --> X5[Plasticizer addition]
        X5 --> X6[Curatives final]
        X6 --> X7[Final compound]
    end
    
    Stage1 --> Stage2
    Stage2 --> Stage3
    Stage3 --> Stage4
```

## 4.3 Nip Region Cross-Section Schematic (Text Description)

```
   BACK ROLL (Faster, v2)
  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  ←── v2 →
        ↘   polymer slab
            ↘   ___________
                 \  NIP    /
                  \________/    ←── nip gap "e"
   FRONT ROLL (Slower, v1)  
  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  ←── v1 →
        
        BANK ──►  (rolled material at entry)
        BAND ──►  (continuous sheet on front roll)
```

**Legend:**
- Back roll rotates clockwise, front roll rotates counter-clockwise
- Polymer sticks to front (slower) roll due to higher friction
- Bank = reservoir of polymer at entry; Bank = 60–80 % of roll volume for optimum operation

## 4.4 Schematic — Block Diagram of Mill Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs["RAW INPUTS"]
        I1[Elastomer]
        I2[Fillers - Carbon Black]
        I3[Plasticizers - Oil]
        I4[Curatives - Sulfur, Accelerator]
        I5[Antioxidants]
    end
    
    subgraph MillOps["MILL OPERATIONS"]
        OP1[Mastication]
        OP2[Band Formation]
        OP3[Incorporation]
        OP4[Dispersion]
        OP5[Distribution]
    end
    
    subgraph Outputs["QUALITY OUTPUTS"]
        O1[Homogeneous Compound]
        O2[Smooth Sheet]
        O3[Correct Viscosity]
    end
    
    I1 --> OP1
    OP1 --> OP2
    OP2 --> OP3
    I2 --> OP3
    OP3 --> OP4
    OP4 --> OP5
    I3 --> OP5
    I5 --> OP5
    OP5 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    I4 -.Last addition.-> OP5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Mapping Rationale:** A physical schematic of the two-roll mill with all mechanical details is complex to render in text. The block-level architecture above captures the *functional flow* of materials and operations, which is exactly what KTU examiners reward in lab viva and record books.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define mastication. Why is it necessary for natural rubber but not for most synthetic rubbers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Mastication is the mechanical breakdown of long, entangled polymer chains of raw rubber into shorter chains by the application of shear in the nip of a two-roll mill, resulting in a reduction of Mooney viscosity and molecular weight.

**Reason for NR necessity (1 mark):** Natural rubber (NR) as harvested has a high molecular weight (M̄w ≈ 10⁶) and a high Mooney viscosity (>80), making it difficult to incorporate fillers. Mastication reduces the chain length to a workable level (Mooney ~ 50).

**Reason for synthetic rubbers (0.5 mark):** Synthetic rubbers like SBR, BR, EPDM are polymerised to a lower, controlled molecular weight and are supplied with built-in process oils, so they require little or no mastication before compounding.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the phenomenon of band formation on a two-roll mill.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Band formation is the process by which softened rubber, after mastication, adheres to the surface of the front (slower) roll and forms a continuous, rotating cylindrical sheet (band) around it.

**Three conditions required (2 marks):**
1. **Sufficient mastication/warming** of the polymer to make it tacky.
2. **Adhesion** of the polymer to the roll surface — greater affinity for the slower, cooler front roll.
3. **Maintenance of the bank** at the nip — a reservoir of polymer that continuously feeds into the gap.

**Visual sign (1 mark):** A uniform bank at the nip with a smooth, glossy band on the front roll indicates correct mill operation. Absence of bank leads to roll slippage and poor compounding.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a laboratory two-roll mill. Explain the role of friction ratio in mastication. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A laboratory two-roll mill has rolls of diameter 150 mm and length 300 mm. The front roll rotates at 20 rpm and the back roll at 25 rpm. The nip gap is set to 2 mm. Calculate the (i) friction ratio, (ii) peripheral speeds of both rolls, (iii) shear rate in the nip, and (iv) theoretical band thickness. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A(a)**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A laboratory two-roll mill consists of two parallel, horizontal, hollow steel rolls mounted in a robust cast-iron frame.

- **Rolls:** Diameter 150 mm (typical lab), length 300 mm. They are cored for circulation of heating/cooling water.
- **Drive system:** Geared motor with a fixed gear ratio providing the friction ratio; variable speed drives allow adjustment.
- **Nip adjustment:** A screw mechanism moves the front roll forward/backward to set the nip gap from 0.2 mm to 5 mm.
- **Safety devices:** Emergency trip bar (cable), roll stop push-button, hopper guards.
- **Cutting knife:** Hand-held knife for cutting and folding the band.

**Working (2 marks):**

The elastomer is fed into the nip from above. The back roll rotates faster, dragging the polymer down and forward. The polymer sticks to the slower front roll and forms a continuous band. Repeated passes through the nip apply shear, causing mastication. After mastication, fillers and additives are added progressively.

**Role of friction ratio (2 marks):**

The friction ratio (FR = v₂/v₁) determines the **shear rate** in the nip:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{e} = \frac{v_1 (FR - 1)}{e}$$

A higher friction ratio produces higher shear, accelerating mastication but also generating more heat. For pure mastication, FR ≈ 1.4 is used; for mixing, FR ≈ 1.25 is preferred to avoid scorch.

---

### **Model Answer — Question A(b)**

**Given:**
- D = 150 mm = 0.15 m
- L = 300 mm = 0.30 m
- N₁ = 20 rpm (front roll)
- N₂ = 25 rpm (back roll)
- e = 2 mm = 0.002 m

**(i) Friction Ratio (1.5 marks):**
$$FR = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{25}{20} = 1.25$$

**(ii) Peripheral Speeds (2 marks):**
$$v_1 = \pi D N_1 = \pi \times 0.15 \times \frac{20}{60} = \pi \times 0.05$$
$$v_1 = 0.157 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_2 = \pi D N_2 = \pi \times 0.15 \times \frac{25}{60} = \pi \times 0.0625$$
$$v_2 = 0.196 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Shear Rate in the Nip (2 marks):**
$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{e} = \frac{0.196 - 0.157}{0.002} = \frac{0.039}{0.002}$$
$$\dot{\gamma} = 19.5 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**(iv) Theoretical Band Thickness (1.5 marks):**
$$t_{band} = e \cdot \frac{1 + FR}{2} = 0.002 \times \frac{1 + 1.25}{2} = 0.002 \times 1.125$$
$$t_{band} = 0.00225 \text{ m} = 2.25 \text{ mm}$$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**a)** Discuss in detail the four sequential operations — mastication, band formation, homogenisation, and mixing — performed on a laboratory two-roll mill during rubber compounding. Highlight the safety precautions observed. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Differentiate between **incorporative** and **distributive mixing**. With reference to carbon black dispersion in natural rubber, explain how the two-roll mill achieves both types of mixing. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B(a)**

**(1) Mastication (2 marks):**
The raw polymer is sheared repeatedly through the nip. Long chains rupture at the mid-point (homolytic scission), free radicals react with O₂, and molecular weight drops. Visual: polymer changes from a leathery, tough mass to a soft, plastic, tacky state. Typically takes 5–10 minutes at 40–60 °C with FR = 1.4.

**(2) Band Formation (1.5 marks):**
Once the polymer is sufficiently plasticised, it adheres to the front (slower) roll and forms a continuous band. The bank at the nip is the visual signature. The operator trims the edges to maintain a uniform band.

**(3) Homogenisation (1.5 marks):**
The band is cut with a hand knife on alternate sides, the cut piece is folded, and re-fed. After 6–8 such cycles, the compound is statistically uniform in composition.

**(4) Mixing (1 mark):**
Fillers, plasticizers, antioxidants, and finally curatives are added in a strict sequence. Curatives are added LAST to avoid scorch. Final homogenisation gives a uniform sheet.

**Safety Precautions (1 mark):**
- Never bring hands near the nip.
- Use the emergency trip bar if a hand is drawn in.
- Wear heat-resistant gloves and face shield.
- Operate at safe temperature (< 90 °C for NR compounds with curatives).
- Ensure the roll guard is in place.

---

### **Model Answer — Question B(b)**

**Incorporative Mixing (3 marks):**
Incorporative mixing is the **wetting** of filler agglomerates by the polymer and their initial incorporation into the matrix. It is governed by **stress transfer** and the polymer's ability to wet the filler surface.

**Distributive Mixing (2 marks):**
Distributive mixing is the **spatial redistribution** of filler aggregates throughout the polymer matrix so that the composition is statistically uniform. It does not break agglomerates; it merely spreads them.

**Two-roll mill achieves both (2 marks):**
- **Incorporative mixing** is achieved by the **high shear rate** in the nip gap ($\dot{\gamma} = v_1 (FR-1)/e$). When the polymer-carbon black mixture passes through the narrow nip, the high stress breaks carbon black agglomerates into smaller aggregates and forces polymer to wet them.
- **Distributive mixing** is achieved by the **cutting and folding action** of the operator. Each cut-and-fold cycle doubles the number of layers, statistically randomising the position of any filler aggregate in the matrix.

**Conclusion:** The nip provides **intensive shear (incorporative)** while the cut-fold-re-feed cycle provides **extensive folding (distributive)**. Both actions are essential to produce a usable rubber compound.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students LOSE marks in this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Conflating mastication with mixing** — Mastication is breakdown of polymer chains ALONE. Mixing is incorporation of additives. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are merged.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of oxygen in mastication** — Mastication is mechano-CHEMICAL, not purely mechanical. Atmospheric O₂ must be mentioned for full marks.
> 3. **Wrong unit for shear rate** — Shear rate is in **s⁻¹**, NOT rpm or m/s.
> 4. **Ignoring the safety bar / emergency stop** — At least one safety precaution is mandatory in the answer; omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Adding curatives too early in the practical** — This is a critical practical error; scorched compound = failed experiment.
> 6. **Confusing front and back roll speeds** — Front roll is **slower**; back roll is **faster**. Many students invert this.
> 7. **Not tabulating observations** — Lab records without a properly formatted observation table lose easy marks.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
