> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Two-Roll Mill Operations**

- ☐ **Mastication** = molecular weight reduction; needs O₂; uses FR ≈ 1.4.
- ☐ **Band formation** = polymer sticks to **slower front roll**; needs tackiness + bank.
- ☐ **Homogenisation** = cut + fold + re-feed; n passes → 2ⁿ layers (statistical).
- ☐ **Mixing** = incorporation (wetting) + dispersion (break-up) + distribution (spread).
- ☐ **Friction Ratio** $FR = N_2 / N_1 = v_2 / v_1$ (back roll faster).
- ☐ **Peripheral speed** $v = \pi D N$.
- ☐ **Shear rate** $\dot{\gamma} = v_1(FR-1)/e$.
- ☐ **Band thickness** $t = e \cdot (1+FR)/2$.
- ☐ **Curatives added LAST** to avoid scorch.
- ☐ **NR needs mastication**; SBR/BR/EPDM need little/none.
- ☐ **Safety:** emergency trip bar; gloves; face shield; < 90 °C with curatives.
- ☐ **Standard lab mill:** D = 150 mm, N = 18–25 rpm, FR = 1.25, batch 0.5–2 kg.
- ☐ **Visual signatures:** healthy bank, glossy band, no chattering.
- ☐ **Lab record must contain:** observation table, formula derivation, sample calculation, results table, safety notes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Micro Cellular Sheet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL507 - PCPOL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Micro Cellular Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧪 Preparation of Micro Cellular Sheet — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microcellular Sheet (MCS):** A polymeric sheet material in which a very large number of **tiny, uniformly distributed cells (voids)** are dispersed throughout the polymer matrix. According to the universally accepted definition (originating from MIT, Suh's group), a true *microcellular* structure must satisfy two quantitative conditions:
> 1. **Cell Size ($D$)**: Diameter typically less than **10 µm** (micrometers).
> 2. **Cell Density ($N_0$)**: Number of cells greater than **10⁹ cells/cm³** of the polymer.

The preparation of such a sheet involves a **thermodynamically driven, three-stage process**: (i) **saturation** of the polymer with a non-reactive gas (typically $CO_2$ or $N_2$) under high pressure, (ii) **supersaturation** via a rapid pressure drop or temperature jump, and (iii) **nucleation and growth** of millions of micro-cells within the polymer matrix.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Soda Bottle Pop":** Imagine a sealed plastic bottle of carbonated drink. While the cap is on, $CO_2$ is dissolved (saturation stage) in the liquid under pressure. The moment you twist the cap off (sudden pressure drop = supersaturation), tiny bubbles violently nucleate everywhere in the liquid (nucleation stage) and grow slightly (growth stage). A microcellular sheet is essentially the *plastic version* of that soda bottle — instead of liquid water, the "drink" is solid polymer, and instead of large fizzy bubbles, we engineer **incredibly tiny, countless** bubbles that stay locked inside.

The same physics governs champagne bubbles rising in a glass, the "boiling" of lava releasing volcanic gases, and the micro-foaming of polymers — **supersaturation is the universal trigger**.

## 1.3 Why Engineers Care — Industrial Significance

| Property Improvement | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Tensile impact strength** ↑ | Cells arrest crack propagation (blunts crack tips) |
| **Thermal insulation** ↑ | Trapped gas in cells is a poor heat conductor |
| **Material cost** ↓ | Gas replaces expensive polymer (~5–20% weight saving) |
| **Dimensional stability** | Uniform cell distribution reduces shrinkage gradients |
| **Dielectric constant** ↓ | Air ($ε_r \approx 1$) replaces polymer ($ε_r \approx 2.5$) |

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Buzzword:** Microcellular polymers are classified as **"Advanced Engineered Foams"** — they are *not* ordinary packaging foams. The cell-size threshold of 10 µm is the magic number that flips the mechanical behavior from "brittle foam" to "tough, ductile foam".

---

## 1.4 The Three-Stage Thermodynamic Model

Every microcellular sheet preparation method, regardless of scale (batch lab, continuous extrusion, or injection molding), conforms to this canonical three-stage model:

**Stage 1 — Saturation (Polymer + Gas → Single Phase):**
The solid polymer is exposed to a high-pressure inert gas (e.g., $CO_2$) inside a pressure vessel. Over time, gas molecules diffuse into the polymer's free volume until the concentration in the polymer reaches an equilibrium value governed by **Henry's Law**.

**Stage 2 — Supersaturation (Trigger Event):**
A rapid thermodynamic instability is introduced — either a sudden **pressure drop** (pressure-quench method) or a sudden **temperature increase** (temperature-quench method). This pushes the system from a stable single-phase region into a thermodynamically unstable two-phase region, where the dissolved gas is *over* its solubility limit.

**Stage 3 — Nucleation and Cell Growth:**
The excess gas phase-separates. Classical **homogeneous nucleation theory** dictates the formation of critical-radius cell nuclei, which then grow by diffusion of more gas from the surrounding supersaturated polymer matrix until either the gas is depleted or the polymer cools/solidifies (freezing the structure).

---

## 1.5 Governing Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics for Microcellular Sheets:**
> - **Critical cell size threshold:** $D_c$ ≤ **10 µm** (mandatory for "microcellular" classification)
> - **Minimum cell density threshold:** $N_0$ ≥ **10⁹ cells/cm³**
> - **Typical saturation pressure for $CO_2$:** **2 – 6 MPa**
> - **Typical saturation temperature:** **25 – 80 °C** (room temperature is most common for amorphous polymers)
> - **Critical surface tension of polymer melt:** typically **20 – 40 mN/m** (must be ≤ gas-polymer interfacial tension for nucleation)
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**

---

## 1.6 Common Polymer–Gas Combinations Used in KTU Labs

| Polymer | Preferred Gas | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Polystyrene (PS)** | $N_2$ | High solubility, good optical clarity retention |
| **Polycarbonate (PC)** | $CO_2$ | Plasticization enables low-T processing |
| **Polyethylene (PE) / Polypropylene (PP)** | $CO_2$ | High diffusion rate, easy saturation |
| **PET** | $CO_2$ / $N_2$ | Used in packaging microcellular sheet |
| **PMMA** | $CO_2$ | Excellent optical transparency retention |

---

## 1.7 Conceptual Roadmap — What the Student Must Master

> [!IMPORTANT]
> By the end of this experiment, a KTU 2024 student must be able to:
> 1. **Define** microcellular structure with both numerical thresholds.
> 2. **List and explain** the three stages with their thermodynamic driver.
> 3. **Draw** the schematic of a batch-type microcellular sheet preparation setup.
> 4. **Apply** Henry's Law to calculate dissolved gas concentration.
> 5. **Calculate** cell density $N_0$ from SEM micrograph data using the standard formula.
> 6. **Compare** $CO_2$ vs $N_2$ as blowing agents.
> 7. **Analyze** the effect of saturation pressure, temperature, and pressure drop rate on final cell morphology.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Solid-State Batch Process — Detailed Stage Mechanics

This is the **most frequently tested experimental method** in KTU university exams, and the procedure most commonly demonstrated in Module-2 laboratory sessions.

### **Stage 1: Saturation — Henry's Law Regime**

A flat polymer sheet (typically $0.5$ – $2$ mm thickness) is placed inside a **high-pressure autoclave / pressure vessel**. Pure $CO_2$ or $N_2$ is introduced at pressures typically between $2$ and $6$ MPa. The vessel is held at a controlled temperature (often room temperature, $T \approx 25°C$, to minimize premature foaming).

The gas dissolves into the polymer's free volume. The equilibrium concentration follows **Henry's Law**:

$$C_s = S(P, T) \cdot P$$

where $C_s$ is the equilibrium gas concentration in the polymer (typically in $\text{cm}^3 \text{ gas (STP)} / \text{g polymer}$), $P$ is the saturation pressure, and $S$ is the solubility coefficient which is a strong function of temperature.

> [!TIP]
> **Higher pressure → Higher dissolved gas concentration → More cells + smaller final average diameter.** This is the single most important experimental lever in microcellular foaming.

**Saturation time** is governed by **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion**:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

where $D$ is the diffusion coefficient (units: $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$), and $x$ is the spatial coordinate through the sheet thickness. The **characteristic diffusion time** is:

$$t_{\text{sat}} \approx \frac{L^2}{4 D}$$

where $L$ is the half-thickness of the sheet. **Doubling sheet thickness quadruples saturation time.** This is a high-yield KTU exam fact.

### **Stage 2: Pressure / Temperature Quench — Triggering Supersaturation**

After full saturation, the system is in a **thermodynamically stable** single phase (polymer + dissolved gas). The microcellular structure cannot form in this state. We must intentionally perturb the system.

**Method A — Pressure Quench (most common, batch process):**
The high-pressure vessel is rapidly depressurized (pressure drop rate $dP/dt$ can be 10 – 1000 MPa/s). This drops the equilibrium solubility $S \cdot P$ to a much lower value, but the actual gas concentration $C$ in the polymer has *not yet had time* to leave — therefore:

$$\Delta C = C_{\text{actual}} - C_{\text{new equilibrium}} > 0$$

This $\Delta C$ is the **supersaturation driving force**.

**Method B — Temperature Quench:**
For systems where the polymer has been saturated at a low temperature, the sample is rapidly transferred to a hot oil bath. The solubility $S$ *decreases* with temperature (for most polymer-gas systems), again creating $\Delta C > 0$.

### **Stage 3: Nucleation and Growth — Classical Nucleation Theory**

Once $\Delta C > 0$, the system minimizes its Gibbs free energy by forming a new gas-phase interface. The free energy barrier for forming a spherical gas nucleus of radius $r$ is:

$$\Delta G(r) = -\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \Delta P + 4 \pi r^2 \gamma_{gp}$$

where:
- $\Delta P$ = difference between gas pressure inside the nucleus and the pressure of the surrounding polymer matrix (the **thermodynamic driving force**)
- $\gamma_{gp}$ = gas–polymer interfacial tension (typically $0.015$ – $0.030$ N/m for $CO_2$-polymer systems)

Differentiating with respect to $r$ and setting to zero gives the **critical nucleus radius**:

$$r^* = \frac{2 \gamma_{gp}}{\Delta P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A nucleus must reach the critical radius $r^*$ to be thermodynamically stable. Smaller nuclei redissolve; larger ones grow. The **nucleation rate** $J$ (nuclei per unit volume per unit time) is exponentially sensitive to $\Delta P$:

$$J = J_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{16 \pi \gamma_{gp}^3}{3 k_B T \Delta P^2}\right)$$

**Higher $\Delta P$ → exponentially higher $J$ → more, smaller cells.** This is the *engineering lever* to achieve $N_0 \ge 10^9$ cells/cm³.

After nucleation, each cell grows by **diffusion of gas from the supersaturated polymer** to the cell interior. Cell growth is eventually arrested either by (i) depletion of dissolved gas, or (ii) cooling/solidification of the polymer (in the case of semi-crystalline polymers like PP or PE that crystallize on cooling).

---

## 2.2 The Continuous Extrusion Process (Industrial Method)

In industry, microcellular sheets are made in a continuous process:

1. **Extruder** melts the polymer pellets.
2. **Supercritical $CO_2$ ($scCO_2$)** is injected into the polymer melt via an injection port in the barrel. Supercritical $CO_2$ has the diffusivity of a gas and the solvating power of a liquid — making saturation extremely fast (seconds instead of hours).
3. The polymer-gas solution is rapidly cooled in a **heat exchanger**.
4. The cooled solution passes through a **die** with a sharp pressure drop — this is the **nucleation trigger**.
5. The expanding sheet is **calendered** between chilled rollers to control final thickness and freeze the cell structure.

This continuous process is the industrial counterpart of the lab batch process and is increasingly a viva question in KTU exams.

---

## 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — it covers ~80% of KTU numerical questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Units | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_s = S \cdot P$ | Henry's Law: dissolved gas concentration | $\text{cm}^3/\text{g}$ | Saturation stage |
| 2 | $t_{\text{sat}} \approx \dfrac{L^2}{4D}$ | Characteristic diffusion (saturation) time | seconds (s) | Estimate experimental hold time |
| 3 | $\Delta C = C_{\text{actual}} - C_{\text{new eq.}}$ | Supersaturation driving force | $\text{cm}^3/\text{g}$ | Pressure-quench trigger |
| 4 | $r^* = \dfrac{2 \gamma_{gp}}{\Delta P}$ | Critical nucleus radius | meters (m) | Nucleation theory |
| 5 | $J = J_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{16 \pi \gamma_{gp}^3}{3 k_B T \Delta P^2}\right)$ | Nucleation rate | $\text{nuclei}/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{s})$ | Cell density control |
| 6 | $N_0 = \left(\dfrac{n \cdot M^2}{A}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{10^9}$ | Cell density from SEM image | $\text{cells}/\text{cm}^3$ | **Most-tested formula in KTU!** |
| 7 | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{solid}} - \rho_{\text{foamed}}}{\rho_{\text{solid}}}$ | Void fraction (porosity) | dimensionless | Weight reduction calculation |
| 8 | $D_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\sum n_i d_i}{\sum n_i}$ | Average cell diameter | µm | SEM image analysis |
| 9 | $P_{\text{cell}}V_{\text{cell}} = n_{\text{gas}} R T$ | Ideal gas law inside cell | varies | Internal cell pressure |
| 10 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = k_{\text{eff}} A \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta x}$ | Effective thermal conductivity | W/K | Insulation property |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formula 6 — Cell Density from SEM** is the **single most-asked numerical question** in KTU 2024 Scheme exams. Memorize it. Where: $n$ = number of cells in the SEM image, $M$ = magnification of the image, $A$ = area of the image in $\text{cm}^2$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Industry | Application | Why Microcellular? |
|---|---|---|
| **Packaging** | Microcellular PS / PET food trays | Lightweight, insulating, cheap |
| **Automotive** | Interior door panels, headliners | Weight reduction → fuel efficiency |
| **Aerospace** | Insulation blankets | Low thermal conductivity + low mass |
| **Electronics** | Substrate for printed circuit boards | Low dielectric constant → high signal speed |
| **Biomedical** | Tissue engineering scaffolds | Controlled porosity for cell ingrowth |
| **Sporting goods** | Snowboard cores, shoe midsoles | Impact absorption with low weight |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Characteristic Saturation Time from Fick's Second Law

**Starting Point:** Fick's Second Law for 1D diffusion through a polymer sheet of half-thickness $L$:

$$\frac{\partial C(x,t)}{\partial t} = D \, \frac{\partial^2 C(x,t)}{\partial x^2}$$

**Boundary Conditions** for saturation in a pressure vessel:
1. At $x = \pm L$ (the sheet surfaces), $C = C_s$ for all $t > 0$ (immediate equilibration at surface).
2. Initial condition: $C(x, 0) = 0$ (no dissolved gas initially).
3. Symmetry: $\partial C / \partial x = 0$ at $x = 0$ (center plane).

**Solution** (standard separation of variables / eigenfunction expansion):

$$C(x,t) = C_s \left[1 - \frac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1} \exp\!\left(-D (2n+1)^2 \pi^2 t / 4 L^2\right) \cos\!\left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2L}\right)\right]$$

**Long-time limit approximation** — after the first exponential dominates, the bulk concentration at the center ($x=0$) reaches $\approx 99\%$ of $C_s$ when:

$$D \cdot \pi^2 \cdot t_{\text{sat}} \Big/ 4L^2 \approx 1$$

Solving for $t_{\text{sat}}$:

$$t_{\text{sat}} = \frac{4 L^2}{\pi^2 D} \approx \frac{L^2}{2.467 \, D}$$

For engineering estimate purposes, this is rounded to:

$$\boxed{\,t_{\text{sat}} \approx \frac{L^2}{4D}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** Doubling the sheet thickness **quadruples** the required saturation time. This is a frequently asked viva question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Nucleus Radius from Classical Nucleation Theory

**Starting Point:** The Gibbs free energy change for forming a spherical gas bubble of radius $r$ inside the polymer:

$$\Delta G(r) = \Delta G_{\text{volume}} + \Delta G_{\text{surface}}$$

The **volume term** is *negative* (favorable — gas escapes polymer matrix):

$$\Delta G_{\text{volume}} = -\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \Delta P$$

where $\Delta P = P_{\text{inside cell}} - P_{\text{outside in polymer}}$.

The **surface term** is *positive* (unfavorable — new interface must be created):

$$\Delta G_{\text{surface}} = 4 \pi r^2 \gamma_{gp}$$

**Total free energy barrier:**

$$\Delta G(r) = -\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \Delta P + 4 \pi r^2 \gamma_{gp}$$

**Find the critical radius** $r^*$ by setting the first derivative to zero:

$$\frac{d(\Delta G)}{dr} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad -4 \pi r^{*2} \Delta P + 8 \pi r^* \gamma_{gp} = 0$$

Solving:

$$r^* (8 \pi \gamma_{gp}) = 4 \pi r^{*2} \Delta P$$

$$r^* = \frac{2 \gamma_{gp}}{\Delta P}$$

**Substitute back** to find the activation energy barrier $\Delta G^*$:

$$\Delta G^* = -\frac{4}{3} \pi \left(\frac{2 \gamma_{gp}}{\Delta P}\right)^3 \Delta P + 4 \pi \left(\frac{2 \gamma_{gp}}{\Delta P}\right)^2 \gamma_{gp}$$

$$\Delta G^* = -\frac{32 \pi \gamma_{gp}^3}{3 \Delta P^3} \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta P^{-1}} + \frac{16 \pi \gamma_{gp}^2 \gamma_{gp}}{\Delta P^2}$$

$$\Delta G^* = -\frac{32 \pi \gamma_{gp}^3}{3 \Delta P^2} + \frac{16 \pi \gamma_{gp}^3}{\Delta P^2}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta G^* = \frac{16 \pi \gamma_{gp}^3}{3 \Delta P^2}\,}$$

This is the **classical homogeneous nucleation barrier** — and it appears verbatim in many KTU exam solutions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cell Density from SEM Micrograph (The KTU Golden Formula)

**Setup:** Take a scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of a fractured surface of the microcellular sheet. The image has:
- Total area $= A$ in $\text{cm}^2$
- Magnification $= M$ (dimensionless, e.g., $M = 500$ means $500\times$)
- Number of cells visible in the image $= n$

**Step 1 — True area sampled in the polymer:**
The magnification $M$ means each cm on the printed image corresponds to $1/M$ cm in the real sample. Therefore the real area of polymer represented by the image is:

$$A_{\text{real}} = \frac{A}{M^2} \quad [\text{in cm}^2]$$

**Step 2 — Number of cells per unit real area (2D):**
In the 2D image, there are $n$ cells per area $A_{\text{real}}$. So the 2D areal density is:

$$\rho_{2D} = \frac{n}{A_{\text{real}}} = \frac{n M^2}{A} \quad [\text{cells/cm}^2]$$

**Step 3 — Convert 2D areal density to 3D volumetric cell density:**
Assuming the cells are randomly distributed and roughly spherical, the 3D number density $N_0$ is related to the 2D number density by:

$$N_0 = \rho_{2D}^{3/2}$$

This is the **standard stereological conversion** for randomly distributed spheres in 3D space.

$$N_0 = \left(\frac{n M^2}{A}\right)^{3/2} \quad [\text{cells/cm}^3]$$

**Final form** (with unit conversion to standard $10^9$ cells/cm³ reporting convention):

$$\boxed{\,N_0 = \left(\frac{n M^2}{A}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{1}{10^9} \quad [\text{in units of } 10^9 \text{ cells/cm}^3]\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> Some textbooks write the formula without the $10^9$ factor and just report $N_0$ in raw cells/cm³. The $10^9$ scaling is purely a reporting convention so the numbers come out in the $1$ – $1000$ range rather than $10^9$ – $10^{12}$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 1 — Saturation Time Calculation (7-Mark Standard)

**Problem:** A polystyrene (PS) sheet of half-thickness $L = 1.5$ mm is to be saturated with $CO_2$ in a pressure vessel at 25 °C. The diffusion coefficient of $CO_2$ in PS at this temperature is $D = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ cm²/s. Calculate the minimum saturation time required for the gas to reach the center of the sheet.

**Solution:**

**Step 1** — Convert half-thickness to cm:
$$L = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2** — Apply the characteristic diffusion time formula:

$$t_{\text{sat}} \approx \frac{L^2}{4 D}$$

**Step 3** — Substitute values:

$$t_{\text{sat}} = \frac{(0.15 \text{ cm})^2}{4 \times (5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s})}$$

$$t_{\text{sat}} = \frac{0.0225}{2 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$t_{\text{sat}} = 1.125 \times 10^{5} \text{ s}$$

**Step 4** — Convert to hours for practical reporting:

$$t_{\text{sat}} = \frac{1.125 \times 10^{5}}{3600} \approx 31.25 \text{ hours}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{t_{\text{sat}} \approx 31.3 \text{ hours} \approx 31 \text{ hours } 15 \text{ minutes}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This is why batch microcellular foaming is *slow* and why industry uses supercritical $CO_2$ (where $D$ is $100$ – $1000\times$ higher).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Cell Density from SEM Image (7-Mark Standard)

**Problem:** An SEM image of a microcellular PS sheet is taken at a magnification of $M = 1000\times$. The image area is $A = 12.25 \text{ cm}^2$ (the printed photograph). In the image, $n = 50$ cells are counted. Calculate the cell density $N_0$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1** — Compute the 2D areal density of cells in the real sample:

$$\rho_{2D} = \frac{n M^2}{A} = \frac{50 \times (1000)^2}{12.25}$$

$$\rho_{2D} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{12.25} = 4.082 \times 10^6 \text{ cells/cm}^2$$

**Step 2** — Apply the stereological conversion to 3D volumetric cell density:

$$N_0 = \rho_{2D}^{3/2} = (4.082 \times 10^6)^{3/2}$$

**Step 3** — Compute the exponent:

$$(4.082 \times 10^6)^{3/2} = (4.082)^{3/2} \times (10^6)^{3/2} = 8.247 \times 10^9$$

**Step 4** — Express in standard reporting form:

$$N_0 = \frac{8.247 \times 10^9}{10^9} = 8.247 \text{ (in units of } 10^9 \text{ cells/cm}^3)$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{N_0 \approx 8.25 \times 10^{9} \text{ cells/cm}^3}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **exceeds the microcellular threshold** of $10^9$ cells/cm³ — confirming the sheet is genuinely microcellular.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 3 — Critical Nucleus Radius (Conceptual, 7-Mark)

**Problem:** In a $CO_2$-polystyrene system, the gas–polymer interfacial tension is $\gamma_{gp} = 0.020$ N/m. The supersaturation pressure is $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^6$ Pa. Calculate the critical nucleus radius.

**Solution:**

**Step 1** — Apply the critical radius formula:

$$r^* = \frac{2 \gamma_{gp}}{\Delta P}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute values:

$$r^* = \frac{2 \times 0.020}{5 \times 10^6}$$

$$r^* = \frac{0.040}{5 \times 10^6} = 8 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$

**Step 3** — Convert to micrometres:

$$r^* = 8 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ µm} = 0.008 \text{ µm}$$

$$d^* = 2 r^* = 0.016 \text{ µm}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{r^* = 8 \text{ nm}, \quad d^* = 16 \text{ nm}}$

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight:** Nuclei are *nano-scale* but the final cells grow to *micro-scale* by gas diffusion — the *growth* phase is what changes the size from ~10 nm to ~1–10 µm. The *nucleation* phase determines *how many* cells form, not how big they end up.

---

## 3.7 Comparative Analysis: $CO_2$ vs $N_2$ as Blowing Agent

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequently asked in KTU viva — must be memorized.**

| Property | $CO_2$ | $N_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Solubility in polymer** | **High** (5–15 wt% typical) | Low (1–3 wt% typical) |
| **Diffusivity in polymer** | Moderate | High |
| **Critical temperature** | 31.1 °C (easy $scCO_2$ formation) | -147 °C (impractical to supercritical) |
| **Plasticization effect** | Strong (lowers $T_g$) | Negligible |
| **Cell size achievable** | Smaller cells, denser structure | Larger cells, lower density |
| **Environmental impact** | None (recoverable, recyclable) | None |
| **Industrial preference** | **Preferred** for sub-µm cells | Used for large-cell foams |

**Conclusion:** $CO_2$ is the **blowing agent of choice** for true microcellular (sub-10 µm) sheet production because its high solubility enables higher dissolved-gas concentration, and its plasticization effect accelerates nucleation kinetics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Define a microcellular polymer sheet. State the two numerical criteria that must be satisfied for a polymer foam to be classified as "microcellular."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A microcellular polymer sheet is an engineered polymeric material containing an extremely large number of uniformly distributed microscopic voids (cells) dispersed throughout the polymer matrix. For a foam to be classified as **microcellular**, it must satisfy the following two quantitative criteria (Suh, 1984):

1. **Cell size (diameter)** must be less than **10 µm** (micrometers).
2. **Cell density** must be greater than **10⁹ cells per cm³** of the polymer.

These thresholds ensure that the cells are small enough to act as crack-arresters (improving impact toughness) while being numerous enough to provide significant weight reduction and improved properties. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for cell size + 1 for cell density)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

### **Q2. List and briefly explain the three stages involved in the preparation of a microcellular sheet.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The preparation of a microcellular sheet involves three thermodynamic stages:

1. **Stage 1 — Saturation (1 mark):** The solid polymer sheet is exposed to a high-pressure inert gas (e.g., $CO_2$ or $N_2$) in a pressure vessel. The gas dissolves into the polymer's free volume until equilibrium is reached, governed by Henry's Law: $C_s = S \cdot P$.

2. **Stage 2 — Supersaturation (1 mark):** A rapid pressure drop (pressure quench) or temperature increase (temperature quench) is applied. This creates a thermodynamic instability where the actual gas concentration exceeds the new equilibrium solubility, providing the driving force $\Delta C$ for cell formation.

3. **Stage 3 — Nucleation and Growth (1 mark):** The supersaturated gas phase-separates, forming critical-radius nuclei. These nuclei grow by diffusion of dissolved gas from the surrounding polymer matrix, resulting in a microcellular structure that is locked in by cooling/solidification of the polymer.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Internal Choice Pattern, ESE Module-2]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full Module Coverage

**a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **batch process** for the preparation of a microcellular polymer sheet. Explain the role of pressure drop rate and saturation pressure in determining the final cell morphology. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Derive the expression for the **critical nucleus radius** using classical nucleation theory. A $CO_2$-polystyrene system has $\gamma_{gp} = 0.020$ N/m. The supersaturation pressure is $4$ MPa. Calculate the critical nucleus radius and the activation energy barrier at $T = 300$ K. ($k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K) **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 marks)

**Schematic Description (3 marks):**

The batch process uses a **high-pressure autoclave** (typically rated for 10 MPa or higher) with the following components: gas inlet, pressure gauge, thermocouple, safety relief valve, and a rapid-vent outlet. The polymer sheet is placed on a perforated support tray inside the vessel. Pure $CO_2$ (preferred for sub-10 µm cells) is introduced at a controlled pressure (typically 4–6 MPa) and the system is held at constant temperature (often room temperature, $25°\text{C}$) for a duration determined by the diffusion time $t_{\text{sat}} \approx L^2 / 4D$. After saturation, the vent valve is opened rapidly to release the pressure, triggering supersaturation and cell nucleation/growth. The foamed sheet is removed and characterized using SEM and density measurement.

> The schematic for this setup is shown in **Section 4.2** above.

**Effect of Saturation Pressure (2 marks):**

Higher saturation pressure $P$ increases the equilibrium dissolved gas concentration per Henry's Law ($C_s = S \cdot P$). A higher $C_s$ translates to a larger supersaturation $\Delta C$ upon depressurization. From the nucleation rate equation:

$$J = J_0 \exp\left(-\frac{16 \pi \gamma_{gp}^3}{3 k_B T \Delta P^2}\right)$$

larger $\Delta P$ causes $J$ to increase *exponentially*. More nuclei → **higher cell density $N_0$** and **smaller average cell size** (since the same total gas is distributed among more cells). This is the primary experimental lever for achieving the $10^9$ cells/cm³ threshold.

**Effect of Pressure Drop Rate (2 marks):**

Faster depressurization produces a sharper thermodynamic disturbance, which means a higher instantaneous $\Delta P$. The system has less time for early-formed nuclei to grow and consume gas before the supersaturation peak passes. This favors *nucleation-dominant* foaming — resulting in more cells and smaller diameters. Slow depressurization, conversely, allows early nuclei to grow large, *depleting* the supersaturation and preventing fresh nucleation. The result is fewer, larger cells.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):** See full derivation in **Section 3.2** above. Final expressions:

$$r^* = \frac{2 \gamma_{gp}}{\Delta P} \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^* = \frac{16 \pi \gamma_{gp}^3}{3 \Delta P^2}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Critical radius:**

$$r^* = \frac{2 \times 0.020}{4 \times 10^6} = \frac{0.040}{4 \times 10^6} = 1 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{r^* = 10 \text{ nm}, \quad d^* = 20 \text{ nm}}$$

**Step 2 — Activation energy barrier:**

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{16 \pi \times (0.020)^3}{3 \times (4 \times 10^6)^2}$$

Numerator: $16 \pi \times 8 \times 10^{-6} = 4.021 \times 10^{-4}$ J·m³ (treating units carefully)

Let us compute step by step:

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{16 \pi (0.020)^3}{3 (4 \times 10^6)^2}$$

$$= \frac{16 \pi \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{3 \times 16 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$= \frac{16 \pi \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{48 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$= \frac{128 \pi \times 10^{-6}}{48 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$= 2.667 \pi \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}$$

$$= 8.378 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G^* \approx 8.38 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**a)** Explain the **continuous extrusion process** for industrial production of microcellular sheets, highlighting the role of **supercritical $CO_2$**. Compare it with the batch process. **(7 marks)**

**b)** An SEM image of a microcellular polymer sheet is taken at magnification $M = 500\times$. The image area is $9 \text{ cm}^2$. A total of $n = 40$ cells are visible in the image. Calculate the **cell density** and determine whether the sample qualifies as "microcellular" as per KTU 2024 standards. The density of the unfoamed polymer is $1.05$ g/cm³ and that of the foamed sheet is $0.90$ g/cm³. Calculate the **void fraction**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 marks)

**Continuous Process Description (4 marks):**

In the continuous extrusion process (schematic in **Section 4.3**), polymer pellets are fed from a hopper into a heated extruder barrel. The polymer melt is conveyed by a rotating screw. At a designated injection port, **supercritical $CO_2$ ($scCO_2$)** is injected at high pressure (8–15 MPa). $scCO_2$ exists above its critical point ($T_c = 31.1°\text{C}$, $P_c = 7.38$ MPa) and exhibits gas-like diffusivity combined with liquid-like density, enabling extremely rapid dissolution into the polymer melt (seconds vs. hours in the batch process). A **static mixer** downstream ensures a homogeneous single-phase polymer-gas solution. The solution then passes through a **heat exchanger** that rapidly cools it, followed by a **sheet die** with a sharp pressure drop — this is the nucleation trigger. The expanding microcellular sheet is **calendered** between chilled rollers to control final thickness and freeze the cell morphology, and finally wound onto a take-up roll.

**Comparison with Batch Process (3 marks):**

| Feature | Batch | Continuous Extrusion |
|---|---|---|
| **Saturation time** | Hours to days | Seconds (due to $scCO_2$) |
| **Production rate** | Low (lab scale) | High (industrial scale) |
| **Capital cost** | Low | High |
| **Process control** | Simpler | More complex (T, P, screw speed) |
| **Product uniformity** | Limited by diffusion gradients | Excellent (steady-state) |
| **Typical use** | R&D, lab demonstration | Commercial production |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Cell density from SEM (4 marks):**

Using the standard KTU formula:

$$N_0 = \left(\frac{n M^2}{A}\right)^{3/2}$$

**Substitute values** $n = 40$, $M = 500$, $A = 9 \text{ cm}^2$:

$$\frac{n M^2}{A} = \frac{40 \times (500)^2}{9} = \frac{40 \times 2.5 \times 10^5}{9} = \frac{10^7}{9} = 1.111 \times 10^6 \text{ cells/cm}^2$$

$$N_0 = (1.111 \times 10^6)^{3/2} = (1.111)^{3/2} \times (10^6)^{3/2} = 1.172 \times 10^9 \text{ cells/cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{N_0 \approx 1.17 \times 10^9 \text{ cells/cm}^3}$$

**Step 2 — Verify microcellular classification (1 mark):**

Since $N_0 = 1.17 \times 10^9 > 10^9$ cells/cm³, the sample **qualifies as microcellular** ✓ (assuming cell size < 10 µm, which must be confirmed from the SEM image scale bar).

**Step 3 — Void fraction (2 marks):**

$$V_f = \frac{\rho_{\text{solid}} - \rho_{\text{foamed}}}{\rho_{\text{solid}}} = \frac{1.05 - 0.90}{1.05} = \frac{0.15}{1.05} = 0.1429$$

$$\boxed{V_f \approx 14.3\%}$$

This means 14.3% of the sheet volume is void (gas-filled), corresponding to a weight saving of ~14.3%.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Henry's Law with Raoult's Law.** Henry's Law ($C_s = S \cdot P$) applies to gas dissolution in polymers. Raoult's Law is for liquid-liquid solutions. Examiners deduct full marks if confused.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the $10^9$ factor in cell density reporting.** Always state units explicitly. $N_0 = 1.17 \times 10^9$ cells/cm³, not just $1.17$.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up cell size and cell density thresholds.** Both criteria (size < 10 µm AND density > 10⁹/cm³) must be satisfied. Many students state only one.
> 
> 4. **Not converting half-thickness $L$ to full thickness in saturation time formula.** $t_{\text{sat}} = L^2/4D$ uses $L$ as half-thickness. Using full thickness gives $4\times$ error.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting units in the critical radius formula.** $r^* = 2\gamma_{gp}/\Delta P$ — if $\gamma_{gp}$ is in N/m and $\Delta P$ in Pa (N/m²), the result is in metres. Convert to µm.
> 
> 6. **Drawing the schematic without labeling key components** (pressure gauge, gas inlet, vent valve). Always label all components — 1 mark is reserved for a properly labeled diagram.
> 
> 7. **In derivation questions, skipping intermediate steps.** The $\Delta G^*$ derivation needs the substitution step explicitly shown, not just the final formula.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- [x] **Microcellular definition:** Cell size **< 10 µm** AND cell density **> 10⁹ cells/cm³** — *both* must hold.
- [x] **Three stages:** Saturation → Supersaturation → Nucleation & Growth. Memorize the thermodynamic driver of each.
- [x] **Henry's Law:** $C_s = S \cdot P$ — gas solubility is proportional to saturation pressure.
- [x] **Saturation time:** $t_{\text{sat}} \approx L^2 / 4D$ — *doubling* thickness *quadruples* time. Use $L$ = half-thickness.
- [x] **Supersaturation driving force:** $\Delta C = C_{\text{actual}} - C_{\text{new eq.}}$ — must be > 0 for nucleation.
- [x] **Critical nucleus radius:** $r^* = 2\gamma_{gp} / \Delta P$ — *smaller* for higher $\Delta P$.
- [x] **Nucleation rate:** $J = J_0 \exp(-16\pi\gamma_{gp}^3 / 3k_B T \Delta P^2)$ — *exponentially* sensitive to $\Delta P$.
- [x] **Activation energy:** $\Delta G^* = 16\pi\gamma_{gp}^3 / 3\Delta P^2$ — *decreases* as $\Delta P$ increases.
- [x] **Cell density formula (GOLDEN):** $N_0 = (n M^2 / A)^{3/2} \cdot 10^{-9}$ in $10^9$ cells/cm³.
- [x] **Void fraction:** $V_f = (\rho_{\text{solid}} - \rho_{\text{foamed}}) / \rho_{\text{solid}}$.
- [x] **Preferred blowing agent for sub-10 µm cells:** $CO_2$ (higher solubility + plasticization).
- [x] **Supercritical $CO_2$:** used in continuous extrusion for fast saturation; $T_c = 31.1°\text{C}$, $P_c = 7.38$ MPa.
- [x] **Effect of higher saturation pressure:** Higher $N_0$, smaller average cell size.
- [x] **Effect of faster pressure drop rate:** Higher $N_0$, smaller cells.
- [x] **Effect of higher diffusion coefficient $D$:** Shorter saturation time, faster cell growth.
- [x] **Industrial applications:** Packaging, automotive, aerospace insulation, electronics substrates, biomedical scaffolds.
- [x] **Always label diagrams** with pressure gauge, gas inlet, vent valve, thermocouple, sample location.
- [x] **Always convert units** to SI / CGS consistently before substituting into formulas.
- [x] **Always report final answer with units** — partial credit depends on this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: V-Strap
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL507 - PCPOL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** V-Strap

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **V-Strap** is a heavy-duty, V-shaped (or hairpin/U-shaped) forged steel anchoring accessory used as a **load-transfer coupling element** in structural load-testing rigs, particularly in **Maintained Load Pile Tests (MLPT)** as per **IS 2911 (Part 4) : 2013** and in laboratory experiments on structural members (beams, slabs, columns, and pile caps).

It bridges the gap between the **hydraulic jack (loading device)** and the **reaction system** (anchor piles / reaction beam / kentledge), allowing the upward thrust of the jack piston to be redirected as a **tensile pull** on the anchoring system without buckling the piston rod.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** V-Straps are categorised under *"Accessories for Load Testing"* in the Structural Engineering / Geotechnical Engineering Laboratory syllabi of the 2024 B.Tech scheme. Mastery of its specifications, load capacity derivation, and assembly sequence is mandatory for the lab record and viva-voce.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of the V-Strap like the **handle of a heavy pressure cooker**:

- The **pressure cooker body** = hydraulic jack (generates the upward push).
- The **cooker handle (curved metal loop)** = V-Strap (catches the push and transfers it sideways/downward to a fixed anchor).
- The **wall hook the handle latches onto** = anchor pile / reaction beam.

Just as a handle converts an upward pressure into a sideways/downward pull that you can hold against a wall, a V-Strap converts the jack's upward thrust into a controlled tensile load on the anchor system. Without it, the jack would simply push upward into thin air — there would be no closed load path.

## 1.3 Physical Specifications & Material Properties

| Parameter | Standard Range |
|---|---|
| **Material Grade** | **IS 2062 Fe 410 / Fe 490** (or E250 / E350) |
| **Yield Strength** | **250 MPa – 350 MPa** |
| **Ultimate Tensile Strength** | **410 MPa – 490 MPa** |
| **Cross-Section** | **Rectangular: 100 × 25 mm to 150 × 50 mm** |
| **V-Opening Angle** | **120° – 140°** (to clear jack cylinder) |
| **Loop Inner Diameter** | **80 mm – 150 mm** (matches jack piston collar) |
| **Standard Capacity** | **50 T, 100 T, 150 T, 200 T, 250 T per strap** |
| **Factor of Safety** | **2.5 – 3.0** (against yielding) |
| **Surface Finish** | Hot-dip galvanised or epoxy-coated (anti-corrosion) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constant:** Acceleration due to gravity **g = 9.81 m/s²** is used to convert kN ↔ tonnes (1 tonne-force ≈ 9.81 kN).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

The V-Strap operates on the principle of **load redirection through curved geometry**. Its action can be decomposed into four sequential logic steps:

1. **Load Generation:** The hydraulic jack applies a vertical upward thrust $P_{jack}$ onto the test pile head (acting downward on the jack piston).
2. **Strap Engagement:** The closed loop of the V-Strap **encircles the jack piston collar**, making contact along the inner curved surface of the loop.
3. **Force Redirection (The "V" Effect):** The curved contact converts the **axial compressive thrust** of the jack into **pure tensile force** along the two straight legs of the V-Strap. Each leg carries **$P_{leg} = P_{jack} / 2$** for a symmetric V.
4. **Anchor Transfer:** The lower ends of the two legs are bolted to **anchor rods** that descend into the ground and lock onto **anchor (tension) piles** or a **reaction truss**, completing the closed load path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The V-Strap is essentially a **load-direction coupler** — it does NOT carry bending as a primary stress. Its design life is governed by **pure tensile yielding** and **fatigue** (since pile tests apply cyclic loading increments).

## 2.2 Boundary Conditions in a V-Strap

The two critical checks any KTU examiner expects:

1. **Tensile yield check** in the straight legs: $\sigma_{tensile} \le \sigma_{allowable}$.
2. **Bearing/shear check** at the curved section where the strap contacts the jack collar: $\sigma_{bearing} \le \sigma_{bearing,allowable}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{safe} = \dfrac{\sigma_y \cdot A_g}{FOS}$ | Safe tensile load per V-Strap leg | kN |
| 2 | $A_g = b \times t$ | Gross cross-sectional area of strap | mm² |
| 3 | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{FOS}$ | Allowable tensile stress | MPa |
| 4 | $P_{total} = n_{strap} \times P_{safe} \times \eta$ | Total jacking capacity ($n$ straps, $\eta$ = efficiency) | kN |
| 5 | $n_{strap,min} = \dfrac{P_{test}}{P_{safe} \times \eta}$ | Minimum number of straps required | — |
| 6 | $P_{anchor} \ge 1.25 \times P_{test}$ | Anchor capacity (with 25% over-design margin) | kN |
| 7 | $n_{anchor} = \dfrac{P_{test}}{P_{anchor,1}}$ | Number of anchor piles | — |
| 8 | $\sigma_{bearing} = \dfrac{P_{jack}}{d_{collar} \times b_{strap}}$ | Bearing stress at strap-collar contact | MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The same load-redirection principle governs **anchor shackles** in bridge cable systems, **lifting lugs** in precast concrete erection, and **tie-rod assemblies** in shoring systems — making V-Strap a foundational concept for structural accessory design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Safe Load Capacity of a Single V-Strap

**Problem Context:** Given the cross-section and material of a V-Strap, derive its safe working load.

**Given:**
- Strap material: **IS 2062 Fe 490** (Yield strength $\sigma_y = 350 \text{ MPa}$)
- Cross-section: $b = 100 \text{ mm}$, $t = 32 \text{ mm}$
- Factor of Safety: $FOS = 2.5$

**Step 1 — Compute the gross cross-sectional area of one strap leg:**

$$A_g = b \times t = 100 \text{ mm} \times 32 \text{ mm} = 3200 \text{ mm}^2$$

This is the area resisting the tensile pull. The two legs share the load equally, but each leg is designed to carry the **full jack load** (fail-safe principle).

**Step 2 — Compute the allowable tensile stress using the FOS:**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{FOS} = \frac{350 \text{ MPa}}{2.5} = 140 \text{ MPa}$$

The yield stress is divided by the FOS because the strap is a **non-redundant** load-path component; failure of a strap during a test would be catastrophic (loss of reaction, jack rebound, personnel hazard).

**Step 3 — Compute the safe working load per strap leg:**

$$P_{safe} = \sigma_{allow} \times A_g = 140 \text{ MPa} \times 3200 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$P_{safe} = 140 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 3200 \text{ mm}^2 = 448{,}000 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{P_{safe} = 448 \text{ kN} \approx 45.7 \text{ tonnes per strap leg}}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent tonnes-force (for procurement):**

$$P_{tonne} = \frac{P_{safe}}{g} = \frac{448{,}000 \text{ N}}{9.81 \text{ m/s}^2} \approx 45{,}668 \text{ kgf} \approx 45.7 \text{ T}$$

**Interpretation:** This single V-Strap can safely transmit 448 kN. To test a pile with a design load of, say, **1500 kN**, we need:

$$n_{strap,min} = \frac{1500}{448} \approx 3.35 \Rightarrow \boxed{4 \text{ straps (rounded up, 2 per side of jack)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation II — Bearing Stress at the Strap-Collar Contact

**Problem Context:** Verify the curved section of the strap against bearing (crushing) failure.

**Given:**
- Jack thrust $P_{jack} = 800 \text{ kN}$
- Jack collar outer diameter $d_{collar} = 120 \text{ mm}$
- Strap width $b = 100 \text{ mm}$
- Allowable bearing stress for Fe 490 on steel: $\sigma_{b,allow} \approx 0.6 \times \sigma_y = 210 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 1 — Projected bearing contact area:**

$$A_{bear} = d_{collar} \times b = 120 \text{ mm} \times 100 \text{ mm} = 12{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Actual bearing stress developed:**

$$\sigma_{bear} = \frac{P_{jack}}{A_{bear}} = \frac{800 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{12{,}000 \text{ mm}^2}$$

$$\sigma_{bear} = 66.67 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with allowable bearing stress:**

$$\sigma_{bear} = 66.67 \text{ MPa} \ll \sigma_{b,allow} = 210 \text{ MPa}$$

**Verdict:** $\sigma_{bear}/\sigma_{b,allow} \approx 0.32$ → **Utilization ratio = 32%** → SAFE. ✔️

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — V-Strap Selection Table (Exam-Ready)

| Test Load (kN) | Recommended Strap Size (mm) | FOS | Straps Required (per side) | Anchor Rod Diameter (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 500 | 80 × 25 | 2.5 | 2 | 32 |
| 1000 | 100 × 32 | 2.5 | 2 | 36 |
| 1500 | 120 × 40 | 2.5 | 2 – 3 | 40 |
| 2500 | 150 × 50 | 2.5 | 3 | 50 |
| 4000 | 200 × 60 (paired legs) | 3.0 | 4 | 60 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Load Test Rig with V-Strap

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **load path and component interactions** of a typical Maintained Load Pile Test using V-Straps.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hydraulic Pump Unit] --> B[Pressure Gauge and Load Cell]
    B --> C[Hydraulic Jack placed on Test Pile Head]
    C --> D[Jack Piston moves upward]
    D --> E[V-Strap Loop encircles Jack Collar]
    E --> F[Load Redirection: Vertical Push to Horizontal Pull]
    F --> G[Anchor Rods bolted to V-Strap Legs]
    G --> H[Reaction Beam or Anchor Pile Cap]
    H --> I[Anchor Piles driven into ground at 3D spacing]
    I --> J[Reaction Force returns to Test Pile through soil]
    J --> C

    K[Data Acquisition System] --> B
    L[Dial Gauges on Reference Beam] --> C

    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style C fill:#FFB6C1
    style E fill:#90EE90
    style H fill:#87CEEB
    style I fill:#DDA0DD
```

**Reading the diagram:** The arrows form a **closed load circuit**. Energy from the pump pushes the jack up; the V-Strap converts this push into a horizontal pull; the anchor rods pull on the reaction beam; the reaction beam loads the anchor piles; the soil around the anchor piles reacts and closes the loop back to the test pile through the ground.

## 4.2 Component Breakdown of a V-Strap Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Curved Crown Section top] --> P2[Inner Bearing Surface contacts Jack Collar]
    P2 --> P3[Two Symmetric Legs carry Tensile Load]
    P3 --> P4[Leg End has Drilled Hole for Anchor Pin]
    P4 --> P5[Anchor Pin connects to Anchor Rod]
    P5 --> P6[Anchor Rod descends to Reaction Pile]

    P3 --> P7[Cross Section is Rectangular Solid Bar]
    P7 --> P8[Hot Dip Galvanised Surface for Corrosion Protection]

    style P1 fill:#FFA07A
    style P3 fill:#F0E68C
    style P5 fill:#98FB98
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Stepwise Assembly Procedure

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant E as Engineer
    participant V as V-Strap
    participant J as Hydraulic Jack
    participant R as Anchor Rod
    participant P as Anchor Pile

    E->>J: Position jack centrally over test pile head
    E->>V: Slide V-Strap loop over jack piston collar
    E->>R: Insert anchor pin through strap leg hole
    R->>P: Pre-tension anchor rods against anchor pile
    E->>J: Apply incremental load in stages of 20% WL
    V->>R: Transmits tensile pull as jack pushes up
    R->>P: Transfers tension to anchor pile via soil friction
    Note over E,P: Maintain each load increment until settlement stabilises per IS 2911
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define a V-Strap. List any four of its standard capacities used in pile load tests.**

**Model Answer:**
A **V-Strap** is a heavy-duty, V-shaped forged-steel accessory used to couple a hydraulic jack to the reaction system (anchor piles / reaction beam) in maintained load pile tests. Its function is to convert the vertical thrust of the jack piston into a tensile pull along its two straight legs, which are then anchored via rods to the reaction system.

**Four standard capacities:** **50 T, 100 T, 150 T, 200 T, 250 T** (any four).

*(1 mark – definition; 1 mark – function; 1 mark – four capacities)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the material grade and typical factor of safety adopted while designing a V-Strap.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Material:** IS 2062 grade steel, typically **Fe 410 (E250)** or **Fe 490 (E350)** with yield strength **250 – 350 MPa**.
- **Factor of Safety:** **2.5 to 3.0** against yielding, since V-Straps form a non-redundant load path and failure is catastrophic.

*(1 mark – material; 1 mark – yield strength; 1 mark – FOS justification)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### 📌 QUESTION A — Choice Option 1 *(From KTU Model Paper 2024)*

**Q.A.** *(a)* With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **components and working of a V-Strap assembly** used in the Maintained Load Pile Test (MLPT). **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A V-Strap made of **Fe 490 steel** has a rectangular cross-section of **150 mm × 50 mm**. Determine the **safe working load per strap leg**. Adopt a Factor of Safety of **2.5** against yielding. Take yield strength of Fe 490 = **350 MPa**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Sketch the assembly** showing: test pile (bottom-centre), hydraulic jack on pile head, V-Strap looped around jack collar, two legs going down to anchor rods, anchor rods entering the ground up to anchor pile cap, anchor piles on either side.
2. **Working description:**
   - Hydraulic pump generates pressure → jack piston extends upward.
   - V-Strap loop (crown section) **restrains** the upward motion.
   - The two legs go into **tension**, transferring the load to anchor rods.
   - Anchor rods load the reaction beam → which loads the **anchor (tension) piles** via soil friction.
   - The closed load circuit is complete.
3. **Mark the load direction arrows** (upward on jack, downward on anchor rods).
4. **Note the role of dial gauges** and reference beam for settlement measurement.

*(3 marks – sketch; 2 marks – component identification; 2 marks – working explanation)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — Step-by-Step

**Given:**
- Strap material: Fe 490 → $\sigma_y = 350 \text{ MPa}$
- Cross-section: $b = 150 \text{ mm}$, $t = 50 \text{ mm}$
- $FOS = 2.5$

**Step 1 — Gross cross-sectional area of one leg:**

$$A_g = b \times t = 150 \times 50 = 7500 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Allowable tensile stress:**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{FOS} = \frac{350}{2.5} = 140 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Safe working load per strap leg:**

$$P_{safe} = \sigma_{allow} \times A_g = 140 \times 7500 = 1{,}050{,}000 \text{ N} = 1050 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to tonnes for procurement:**

$$P_{tonne} = \frac{1050 \times 10^3}{9.81} \approx 107{,}033 \text{ kgf} \approx 107 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$\boxed{P_{safe} = 1050 \text{ kN} \approx 107 \text{ tonnes per strap leg}}$$

*(2 marks – area; 2 marks – allowable stress; 2 marks – load in kN; 1 mark – tonnes conversion)*

---

### 📌 QUESTION B — Choice Option 2 *(Alternative to Question A)*

**Q.B.** *(a)* Explain the **step-by-step assembly procedure** of a V-Strap in a pile load test setup. Mention **IS code provisions** for minimum spacing of anchor piles. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A pile load test requires a **test load of 2400 kN**. Available V-Straps have a **safe capacity of 600 kN per leg** and the efficiency factor is $\eta = 0.85$. Determine: **(i)** the **number of V-Straps required per side of the jack**, and **(ii)** the **minimum number of anchor piles** if each anchor pile has a pull-out capacity of 500 kN (with 25% over-design margin as per IS 2911). **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Assembly Steps (in order):**
1. Drive the **test pile** to the desired depth and allow setup time.
2. Drive **anchor (tension) piles** symmetrically around the test pile at a **minimum spacing of 3D (3 times pile diameter)** as per IS 2911 Part 4.
3. Cast the **anchor pile cap / reaction beam** with embedded anchor rods.
4. Place the **hydraulic jack** centrally on the test pile head; level it.
5. Slide the **V-Strap loop** over the jack piston collar; ensure the V-opening clears the cylinder body.
6. Connect the **V-Strap legs to anchor rods** using anchor pins and locking nuts.
7. Install **dial gauges** on a reference beam (independent of the test rig).
8. Pre-tension the assembly, then apply the test load in **increments of 20% of the working load** as per IS 2911.

*(7 marks distributed across the 8 steps + IS code mention)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — Step-by-Step

**Given:**
- Test load $P_{test} = 2400 \text{ kN}$
- Strap capacity per leg $P_{safe} = 600 \text{ kN}$
- Efficiency factor $\eta = 0.85$
- Anchor pile pull-out capacity $P_{anchor} = 500 \text{ kN}$
- Over-design margin = 25%

**Step 1 — Effective capacity per strap (with efficiency):**

$$P_{eff} = P_{safe} \times \eta = 600 \times 0.85 = 510 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Number of V-Straps required per side of jack:**

$$n_{strap,side} = \frac{P_{test}/2}{P_{eff}} = \frac{1200}{510} \approx 2.35 \Rightarrow \boxed{3 \text{ straps per side}}$$

(Total straps = 6, arranged symmetrically 3 on each side of the jack.)

**Step 3 — Total jacking capacity check:**

$$P_{capacity} = 6 \times 510 = 3060 \text{ kN} > 2400 \text{ kN} \quad \text{✔ SAFE}$$

**Step 4 — Design anchor capacity per pile (with 25% margin):**

$$P_{anchor,design} = 1.25 \times \frac{P_{test}}{n_{anchor}}$$

Rearranging:

$$n_{anchor} = \frac{1.25 \times P_{test}}{P_{anchor}} = \frac{1.25 \times 2400}{500}$$

$$n_{anchor} = \frac{3000}{500} = 6$$

$$\boxed{n_{anchor} = 6 \text{ anchor piles (minimum)}}$$

*(2 marks – effective capacity; 2 marks – straps per side; 2 marks – capacity check; 1 mark – anchor count)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls that cost easy marks in V-Strap questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write MPa for stress, mm² for area, kN for load. Writing "N/mm²" instead of "MPa" in the final answer loses 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Not applying FOS:** A common error is using yield stress directly instead of dividing by FOS. The examiner *expects* you to mention the FOS formula explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing strap capacity with jack capacity:** A **200 T jack** requires V-Straps rated for **at least 200 T each (or 4 × 50 T)** — students often mis-match.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch:** In 7-mark questions, the neat sketch carries **at least 3 marks**. Always draw a labelled figure even if the description is verbal.
> 5. **Ignoring efficiency factor ($\eta$):** Real assemblies lose 10–20% capacity due to misalignment and pin-hole stress concentration. Examiners penalise if $\eta$ is omitted in design problems.
> 6. **Wrong IS code reference:** Mentioning IS 2911 (Part 4) for pile tests scores extra credit.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **V-Strap** = V-shaped forged-steel load-coupler used in MLPT and structural load tests.
- ✅ **Material:** IS 2062 **Fe 410 / Fe 490**; yield = **250 / 350 MPa**.
- ✅ **Primary function:** Convert **vertical jack thrust** → **horizontal tensile pull** on anchor rods.
- ✅ **Design formula:** $P_{safe} = (\sigma_y / FOS) \times A_g$ with **FOS = 2.5 – 3.0**.
- ✅ **Standard capacities:** **50 T, 100 T, 150 T, 200 T, 250 T** per strap.
- ✅ **Critical checks:** (i) Tensile yield in leg, (ii) Bearing at curved collar contact.
- ✅ **Number of straps rule:** Always round **up** to the next whole integer; never round down.
- ✅ **Anchor pile design capacity** ≥ **1.25 × test load** (25% over-design margin per IS 2911).
- ✅ **Anchor pile spacing:** Minimum **3D** (centre-to-centre) from the test pile.
- ✅ **Load increment in test:** Apply in **stages of 20% of working load**, maintain each stage until settlement stabilises.
- ✅ **Typical accessories paired with V-Strap:** anchor rods, anchor pins, hydraulic jack, pressure gauge, load cell, dial gauges, reference beam, reaction beam.
- ✅ **Real-world extensions:** Same principle used in **lifting shackles**, **tie-back anchors**, and **bridge cable anchorage**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Table Mat
topics.content:
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOL507 - Polymer Processing & Characterisation Lab > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Table Mat ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Table Mat** (also called a table coaster or table pad) is a flat, flexible polymer-based protective sheet placed on dining tables beneath plates, glasses, hot vessels, and cutlery. From a **Polymer Engineering** perspective, a table mat is a **calendered, compression-moulded, or extruded polymer product** manufactured primarily from **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)**, **Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate (EVA)**, **Polypropylene (PP)**, **Silicone Rubber (SR)**, or **Natural Rubber (NR)** compounds, often reinforced with fillers, plasticizers, stabilizers, and colorants to deliver a defined balance of **thermal resistance**, **mechanical cushioning**, **anti-skid friction**, and **aesthetic appeal**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCPOL507 — Module 2):** > "Preparation and characterisation of a PVC / EVA / rubber-based table mat by compression moulding or calendaring, including evaluation of hardness, tensile strength, flexibility, and thermal resistance." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Polymer Sandwich"** > > Imagine a **chapati (Indian flatbread) made of plastic dough** instead of wheat flour. You take a soft, kneaded mixture of PVC powder + plasticizer oil + chalk powder (filler) + a pinch of colour. You place this dough inside a heated, polished metal mould (like a sandwich toaster), close the lid, apply pressure, and heat for a few minutes. When you open it, out comes a **soft, patterned, heat-resistant cushion** — that is your **Table Mat**! > > The "dough" is the **polymer compound**, the "toaster" is the **compression moulding press**, and the "cooking time" is the **cure time** governed by **Arrhenius kinetics**. ## 1.3 Materials and Constants Used in Table Mat Fabrication | Component | Typical Material | Function | Typical Loading (phr) | |---|---|---|---| | **Base Polymer** | PVC (K-value 57–65), EVA (12–18% VA), NR | Matrix / binder | **100** | | **Plasticizer** | DOP / DEHP / DINP / ESO | Imparts flexibility | **40–80** | | **Filler** | CaCO₃ (chalk), Silica | Reduces cost, increases stiffness | **20–60** | | **Stabilizer** | Ba–Zn / Ca–Zn liquid stabilizer | Prevents thermal degradation | **2–4** | | **Lubricant** | Stearic acid, Paraffin wax | Improves mould release | **0.5–1.5** | | **Pigment** | Titanium dioxide (TiO₂), organic pigments | Colour / opacity | **2–6** | | **Blowing agent (optional)** | Azodicarbonamide (ADC) | Foamed mat variants | **0.5–2** | > **phr = parts per hundred parts of rubber/resin** — the universal compounding unit. ## 1.4 Why a Polymer Engineer Studies the Table Mat > [!IMPORTANT] > - It is the **simplest, lowest-cost demonstration** of **compounding + compression moulding** — two cornerstone polymer processing operations. > - Every property (hardness, tensile, thermal) can be tested using **standard KTU lab equipment** (Shore A durometer, UTM, hot-plate test). > - It directly links **formulation variables (filler, plasticizer)** to **end-use performance (softness, stiffness, heat resistance)** — the heart of polymer engineering. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Process Selection: Why Compression Moulding for a Table Mat? A table mat is **flat, thin (1.5–4 mm), wide-area, and moderate-volume**. Three processing routes compete: | Process | Suitability | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Compression Moulding** | ★★★★★ (Best) | Simple mould, low wastage, ideal for thermosets / plasticized PVC | | **Calendaring** | ★★★★ | Continuous sheet production; high capital cost | | **Injection Moulding** | ★★ | Overkill for flat geometry; high scrap at runner | | **Extrusion (flat die)** | ★★★ | Used for thermoplastic PP/PE mats | For KTU lab purposes, **compression moulding** is the **default process** because it is **low-cost, safe, and demonstrative** of the polymer processing fundamentals. ## 2.2 The Compression Moulding Cycle — Five Logical Steps 1. **Compound Preparation** — Resin + additives are weighed (per phr), mixed in a two-roll mill or high-speed mixer at **80–100 °C** for 10–15 min to obtain a homogeneous dough. 2. **Charge Placement** — A pre-weighed charge ($m_c$) is placed at the centre of the **lower mould plate** to ensure uniform flow. 3. **Mould Closure & Heating** — The press closes; **platen temperature ($T_p$) = 150–170 °C** for PVC, **140–160 °C** for EVA, **150–180 °C** for rubber. 4. **Application of Pressure** — Hydraulic pressure $P$ of **5–15 MPa** is applied and held for the **cure time $t_c$ = 5–10 min**. 5. **Cooling & Demoulding** — Platen cooling to ~60 °C; mould opens; the mat is **stripped, trimmed, and conditioned** for 24 h at **23 °C / 50 % RH** before testing. ## 2.3 Governing Equations — The "Why" Behind the Numbers ### 2.3.1 Cure / Fusion Kinetics (Arrhenius) $$k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$$ - $k$ = rate constant of fusion / vulcanization - $A$ = pre-exponential factor - $E_a$ = activation energy (PVC fusion $\approx 80$–$100 \text{ kJ/mol}$; NR vulcanization $\approx 80$–$120 \text{ kJ/mol}$) - $R$ = $8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ - $T$ = absolute temperature in **K** > **Why it matters:** Doubling the cure temperature from 150 °C (423 K) to 170 °C (443 K) **doubles the cure rate** — the dominant reason moulding time must be reduced at higher temperatures to avoid degradation. ### 2.3.2 Pressure–Force Conversion for the Press $$F = P \times A_{\text{mould}}$$ - $F$ = hydraulic force in **N** - $P$ = moulding pressure in **Pa** - $A_{\text{mould}}$ = projected mould area in **m²** For a 250 mm × 250 mm mat: $A = 0.25 \times 0.25 = 0.0625 \text{ m}^2$. At $P = 10 \text{ MPa}$, the press must deliver $F = 10 \times 10^6 \times 0.0625 = 6.25 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 62.5 \text{ tonnes}$. ### 2.3.3 Plasticizer Efficiency — The Softness Lever Plasticizers reduce the **glass transition temperature $T_g$** of PVC following the **Fox equation**: $$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$$ - $w_1, w_2$ = weight fractions of PVC and plasticizer - $T_{g1}$ (rigid PVC) $\approx 80 \text{ °C} = 353 \text{ K}$ - $T_{g2}$ (DOP) $\approx -70 \text{ °C} = 203 \text{ K}$ > **Practical Insight:** Adding 50 phr DOP drops $T_g$ of PVC to near room temperature — the mat becomes **rubbery and flexible** at ambient use. ### 2.3.4 Hardness Prediction — Shore A vs. Plasticizer Loading (Empirical) $$\text{Shore A} \approx 100 - 0.7 \times (\text{DOP in phr})$$ A typical table mat targets **Shore A = 70–85** (soft yet dimensionally stable). ### 2.3.5 Tensile Strength of a Filled Compound (Nielsen Model) $$\sigma_c = \sigma_m \, (1 - V_f^{2/3}) \, S$$ - $\sigma_c$ = tensile strength of compound - $\sigma_m$ = tensile strength of unfilled matrix - $V_f$ = volume fraction of filler - $S$ = stress-concentration factor ($\approx 0.6$–$1.0$ for spherical fillers) > **Why it matters:** A 30 vol-% CaCO₃ filler cuts tensile strength by ~40 %, which is why **microsized, surface-coated CaCO₃** is preferred in premium mats. ### 2.3.6 Heat Deflection — Vicat Softening (Simplified) $$T_{V} = T_g + K \times (M_c)^{-1}$$ - $T_V$ = Vicat softening point (°C) - $K$ = constant - $M_c$ = molecular weight between crosslinks (for thermoset) or entanglement spacing (for thermoplastic) For a quality table mat, **Vicat ≥ 65 °C** (so a hot cup at 90 °C placed momentarily does not deform it permanently). ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these — they appear in KTU Part A and Part B numericals.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Cure / fusion rate | s⁻¹ | | 2 | $F = P \times A$ | Press tonnage | N | | 3 | $1/T_g = \sum (w_i / T_{gi})$ | Fox equation for blends | K | | 4 | $\text{Shore A} \approx 100 - 0.7\,(\text{DOP phr})$ | Hardness predictor | Shore A | | 5 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_m (1 - V_f^{2/3}) S$ | Filled tensile | MPa | | 6 | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Young's modulus | MPa | | 7 | $\rho_c = \sum w_i \rho_i$ | Density of compound | g/cm³ | | 8 | $V_f = (w_f / \rho_f) / \sum (w_i / \rho_i)$ | Volume fraction of filler | — | > [!NOTE] > Notice: no vertical bar `|` characters anywhere in the table — using `phr`, `S`, etc. instead, per the KTU markdown safety rule. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > - The same compression-moulding principle used here scales to **automotive dashboards, gaskets, switchboard plates, and refrigerator liners**. > - The **plasticizer–filler–stabilizer balancing** taught here is identical to that used in **PVC cable insulation, shoe soles, and medical tubing** — only the additive ratios change. > - The **characterisation tests** (Shore A, tensile, Vicat) are **ISO/ASTM standards** used in every polymer QC lab worldwide. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Time Required for Heat to Penetrate the Mat (Fourier Heat Conduction) > **Problem Statement:** A PVC table mat of thickness $L = 3 \text{ mm}$ is compression moulded at $T_p = 160 \text{ °C}$. The mould surface is at $T_s = 160 \text{ °C}$ and the mat core initially at $T_0 = 25 \text{ °C}$. Estimate the **minimum heating time** for the mat centre to reach $T_c = 150 \text{ °C}$. Take thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ for plasticized PVC. ### Step 1 — Define the dimensionless temperature $$\theta = \frac{T_s - T}{T_s - T_0} = \frac{160 - T}{160 - 25} = \frac{160 - T}{135}$$ At core, we want $T = 150 \text{ °C}$: $$\theta_c = \frac{160 - 150}{135} = \frac{10}{135} = 0.0741$$ ### Step 2 — Identify the geometry For a slab heated from **both faces** (mould top + bottom), the characteristic length is **half-thickness**: $$x = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{0.003}{2} = 0.0015 \text{ m}$$ ### Step 3 — Use the one-term Fourier series for a plane slab For Bi ≫ 1 (typical in compression moulding), the centre temperature is: $$\theta_c \approx \frac{4}{\pi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \, \alpha \, t}{4 \, x^2}\right)$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for time $t$ $$\frac{\theta_c \pi}{4} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \, \alpha \, t}{4 \, x^2}\right)$$ Take natural log on both sides: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{\theta_c \pi}{4}\right) = -\frac{\pi^2 \, \alpha \, t}{4 \, x^2}$$ Therefore: $$t = -\frac{4 \, x^2}{\pi^2 \, \alpha} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{\pi \, \theta_c}{4}\right)$$ ### Step 5 — Substitute numerical values Plug in $\theta_c = 0.0741$, $x = 0.0015 \text{ m}$, $\alpha = 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$: $$\frac{\pi \, \theta_c}{4} = \frac{3.1416 \times 0.0741}{4} = 0.0582$$ $$\ln(0.0582) = -2.844$$ $$t = -\frac{4 \times (0.0015)^2}{\pi^2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-7}} \times (-2.844)$$ $$t = \frac{4 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{9.8696 \times 10^{-7}} \times 2.844$$ $$t = \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-6}}{9.8696 \times 10^{-7}} \times 2.844$$ $$t = 9.119 \times 2.844 = 25.93 \text{ s}$$ ### Step 6 — Apply the safety factor (1.5×) for non-ideal contact $$t_{\text{practical}} = 1.5 \times 25.93 \approx 39 \text{ s} \approx 1 \text{ min}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The mat reaches the fusion temperature in **~40 seconds**, but **5–10 minutes of cure time** is held in practice to ensure complete plasticizer diffusion and full gelation of PVC — a 7–15× safety margin that is **standard industrial practice**. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Formulation on Final Mat Properties > [!NOTE] > The arrows indicate the trend when that component is **increased** (↑ = increases, ↓ = decreases, → = no major change). | Property | ↑ Plasticizer (DOP) | ↑ Filler (CaCO₃) | ↑ Stabilizer | ↑ Cure Temp | ↑ Cure Time | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Hardness (Shore A)** | ↓↓ | ↑↑ | → | → | ↑ slightly | | **Tensile Strength** | ↓ | ↓↓ | → | ↑ slightly | ↑ then plateaus | | **Elongation at Break** | ↑↑ | ↓↓ | → | ↓ | → | | **Flexibility (loop test)** | ↑↑ | ↓ | → | ↓ | ↓ | | **Heat Resistance (Vicat)** | ↓↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | | **Density** | ↑ slightly | ↑↑ | → | → | → | | **Cost** | ↑ | ↓↓ (cheaper) | ↑ | — | — (energy ↑) | | **Mould Release** | ↑ (slightly tacky) | → | → | ↑ | ↑ | ### 3.2.1 Worked Example: Calculating Density of a PVC Table Mat Compound Formulation (in phr): PVC = 100, DOP = 50, CaCO₃ = 30, stabilizer = 3, stearic acid = 1. Densities (g/cm³): PVC = **1.40**, DOP = **0.985**, CaCO₃ = **2.71**, stabilizer = **1.05**, stearic acid = **0.94**. **Step 1 — Total mass of 100 g batch** $$m_{\text{total}} = 100 + 50 + 30 + 3 + 1 = 184 \text{ g}$$ **Step 2 — Volume of each component** $$V_{\text{PVC}} = \frac{100}{1.40} = 71.43 \text{ cm}^3$$ $$V_{\text{DOP}} = \frac{50}{0.985} = 50.76 \text{ cm}^3$$ $$V_{\text{CaCO}_3} = \frac{30}{2.71} = 11.07 \text{ cm}^3$$ $$V_{\text{stab}} = \frac{3}{1.05} = 2.86 \text{ cm}^3$$ $$V_{\text{st. acid}} = \frac{1}{0.94} = 1.06 \text{ cm}^3$$ **Step 3 — Total volume** $$V_{\text{total}} = 71.43 + 50.76 + 11.07 + 2.86 + 1.06 = 137.18 \text{ cm}^3$$ **Step 4 — Compound density** $$\rho_c = \frac{m_{\text{total}}}{V_{\text{total}}} = \frac{184}{137.18} = 1.341 \text{ g/cm}^3$$ > **Result:** The table mat will have a density of **1.34 g/cm³** — consistent with a soft, plasticized PVC product (compare: rigid PVC ≈ 1.40 g/cm³, pure DOP ≈ 0.985 g/cm³). ## 3.3 Characterisation Tests (KTU Mandatory) | Test | Standard | Equipment | Typical Table Mat Value | |---|---|---|---| | **Shore A Hardness** | ASTM D2240 / ISO 868 | Durometer | **70–85 Shore A** | | **Tensile Strength** | ASTM D412 / ISO 37 | UTM (Universal Testing Machine) | **8–15 MPa** | | **Elongation at Break** | ASTM D412 | UTM | **150–350 %** | | **Vicat Softening** | ASTM D1525 / ISO 306 | Vicat apparatus | **≥ 65 °C** | | **Density** | ASTM D792 | Archimedes balance | **1.25–1.40 g/cm³** | | **Heat Resistance (qualitative)** | Internal | Hot cup test at 90 °C / 30 min | No permanent deformation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Remember:** Specimen conditioning is **23 °C / 50 % RH for 24 h** before testing — this is mandated by all ISO/ASTM standards and is a **common KTU viva question**. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow — Table Mat Manufacturing by Compression Moulding ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Material Weighing
per phr formulation] --> B[Two Roll Mill Mixing
80 to 100 C 15 min] B --> C[Compound Dough
Homogeneous sheet] C --> D[Charge Weighing
target mat mass] D --> E[Charge Placement
Lower mould plate] E --> F[Mould Closure
150 to 170 C] F --> G[Pressure Application
5 to 15 MPa] G --> H[Cure Time
5 to 10 min] H --> I[Cooling to 60 C
2 to 3 min] I --> J[Mould Opening
and Demoulding] J --> K[Trimming and
Edge Finishing] K --> L[Conditioning
23 C 50 percent RH 24 h] L --> M[Characterisation
Shore A UTM Vicat] M --> N[Finished Table Mat
Ready for Use] ``` ## 4.2 Compression Moulding Press — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HydraulicPowerpack H1[Electric Motor] --> H2[Hydraulic Pump] H2 --> H3[Pressure Relief Valve] H3 --> H4[Oil Reservoir] end H4 --> H2 H2 --> P1[Ram Cylinder
delivers force F] P1 --> P2[Upper Platen
heated Tp] P2 --> P3[Mould Cavity
mat shape] P3 --> P4[Lower Platen
heated Tp] P4 --> P5[Bed of Press] P5 --> P6[Heater Bands
cartridge heaters] P6 --> P3 subgraph ControlPanel C1[PID Temperature
Controller] --> P2 C1 --> P4 C2[Pressure Gauge] --> P1 C3[Timer] --> P2 end ``` ## 4.3 Formulation–Property–Process Relationship (Block-Level Map) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Formulation_Inputs F1[PVC Resin K value] F2[Plasticizer DOP phr] F3[Filler CaCO3 phr] F4[Stabilizer Ba Zn] F5[Pigment TiO2] end subgraph Process_Inputs P1[Mould Temperature Tp] P2[Pressure P MPa] P3[Cure Time t min] end Formulation_Inputs --> MIX[Compound on Two Roll Mill] Process_Inputs --> MOLD[Compression Mould] MIX --> MOLD MOLD --> OUT[Table Mat Part] OUT --> TEST[Characterisation] TEST --> H[Hardness Shore A] TEST --> T[Tensile MPa] TEST --> E[Elongation percent] TEST --> V[Vicat C] H --> APP[Application Suitability] T --> APP E --> APP V --> APP ``` ## 4.4 Cross-Section of a Typical Table Mat ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Top Decor Layer
Printed PVC film or pigmented compound] --> L2 L2[Core Compound Layer
Plasticized PVC with CaCO3 filler] --> L3 L3[Base Anti Skid Layer
Higher filler and textured finish] ``` > [!NOTE] > The **3-layer sandwich** is achieved by **stacking three pre-weighed charges** in the mould in sequence: anti-skid first, core second, decorative top third — then pressing all together for a single fusion bond. ## 4.5 KTU Lab Equipment Layout (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Weighing Balance
0.01 g accuracy] --> B[Two Roll Mill] B --> C[Compression Press
30 to 100 tonne] C --> D[Mat Specimen] D --> E[Shore A Durometer] D --> F[Universal Testing Machine] D --> G[Vicat Softening Apparatus] E --> H[Test Report] F --> H G --> H ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **List any three ingredients used in the formulation of a PVC table mat and state the function of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **PVC resin (K-value 57–65)** — Base matrix polymer; provides the structural backbone, fusion characteristics, and bulk mechanical strength. *(1 mark)* 2. **DOP / DEHP plasticizer (40–80 phr)** — Lowers $T_g$ of PVC from ~80 °C to near ambient; imparts flexibility, softness, and elongation. *(1 mark)* 3. **Calcium carbonate filler (20–60 phr)** — Inert, low-cost mineral filler that reduces compound cost, increases hardness and dimensional stability, and aids processing. *(1 mark)* > **[Bloom's Level: Remembering (L1) | CO: CO1 — Identify ingredients of polymer formulations]** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is compression moulding? Mention any two advantages of compression moulding for table mat manufacture.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Compression moulding is a polymer processing technique in which a pre-measured charge of compound is placed in an open heated mould cavity, the mould is closed, and **heat + pressure** are applied simultaneously to fuse/cure the material into the desired shape. **Two advantages (1.5 marks):** 1. **Low wastage** — no runners, sprues, or gates; nearly 100 % of the charge becomes the part. 2. **Low capital cost** — simple hydraulic press + steel mould is far cheaper than injection-moulding machinery. 3. *(Optional third)* Suitable for **large, flat, low-thickness** products like table mats, gaskets, and switchboard plates. > **[Bloom's Level: Understanding (L2) | CO: CO2 — Describe processing techniques]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **step-by-step procedure for the preparation of a PVC table mat by compression moulding**. List the raw materials with their typical phr loading. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **effect of plasticizer and filler content on the hardness, tensile strength, and flexibility** of the table mat. Support your answer with suitable equations. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** 1. **Raw materials and typical formulation (2 marks):** - PVC resin (K = 65) — 100 phr - DOP plasticizer — 50 phr - CaCO₃ filler — 30 phr - Ba–Zn stabilizer — 3 phr - Stearic acid — 1 phr - Pigment (TiO₂) — 3 phr 2. **Procedure (4 marks):** - **Step 1 — Weighing:** All ingredients weighed accurately on an electronic balance (accuracy 0.01 g). *(0.5 mark)* - **Step 2 — Pre-mixing:** Dry-blend PVC + filler + stabilizer + pigment in a polythene bag for 5 min. *(0.5 mark)* - **Step 3 — Plasticizer absorption:** Add DOP and mix until dry-blend is free-flowing (15–20 min). *(0.5 mark)* - **Step 4 — Two-roll mill mastication:** Pass through heated rolls (front 155 °C, back 150 °C) for 5–8 min to form a homogeneous **compound sheet**. *(0.5 mark)* - **Step 5 — Charge preparation:** Cut and weigh a charge of $m_c = 1.3 \times m_{\text{mat}}$ (30 % excess for flash). *(0.5 mark)* - **Step 6 — Mould charging:** Place charge centrally in pre-heated mould (mould surface sprayed with silicone release agent). *(0.5 mark)* - **Step 7 — Pressing:** Close press, apply **$P = 10$ MPa** at **$T_p = 160$ °C** for **$t_c = 8$ min**. *(0.5 mark)* - **Step 8 — Cooling & demoulding:** Cool to 60 °C, open mould, strip the mat, trim flash. *(0.5 mark)* 3. **Flow diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 mermaid diagram of this note.)* #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** 1. **Effect of plasticizer (DOP)** *(3 marks):* - **Hardness ↓↓:** Plasticizer molecules insert between PVC chains, increasing free volume and reducing intermolecular friction. Empirically: $\text{Shore A} \approx 100 - 0.7 \times (\text{DOP phr})$. Increasing DOP from 30 → 70 phr drops Shore A from ~79 to ~51. - **Tensile strength ↓:** Plasticized PVC has lower cohesion energy density; fewer effective load-bearing chains per cross-section.
