The decrease is roughly 0.1–0.2 MPa per phr DOP added. - **Flexibility ↑↑:** Elongation at break can rise from ~120 % (rigid) to ~350 % (highly plasticized) as $T_g$ falls below room temperature per the **Fox equation**: $$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_{\text{PVC}}}{T_{g,\text{PVC}}} + \frac{w_{\text{DOP}}}{T_{g,\text{DOP}}}$$ 2. **Effect of filler (CaCO₃)** *(3 marks):* - **Hardness ↑↑:** Filler particles are rigid (Mohs ~3) and resist indentation; Shore A increases ~0.3–0.5 units per phr. - **Tensile strength ↓↓:** Per the **Nielsen model**: $$\sigma_c = \sigma_m (1 - V_f^{2/3}) \, S$$ For $V_f = 0.20$, $\sigma_c \approx 0.63 \, \sigma_m$ — a ~37 % drop. - **Flexibility ↓:** Filler acts as a stress concentrator; cracks initiate at the matrix–filler interface, lowering elongation sharply. 3. **Balanced formulation rationale** *(1 mark):* A **typical table mat uses 50 phr DOP + 30 phr CaCO₃** to hit a sweet spot: Shore A ≈ 78, tensile ≈ 10 MPa, elongation ≈ 220 % — soft enough to handle, rigid enough to lie flat, strong enough to resist tearing. > **[Bloom's Level: Applying + Analysing (L3–L4) | CO: CO3 — Correlate formulation with properties]** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** What are the **standard characterisation tests** performed on a finished table mat? Describe the **Shore A hardness test** with its procedure and report the expected value for a soft PVC table mat. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A PVC table mat of dimensions **300 mm × 300 mm × 3 mm** is compression moulded. The compound has the formulation: PVC 100, DOP 50, CaCO₃ 30, stabilizer 3, stearic acid 1 (all in phr). Densities: PVC 1.40, DOP 0.985, CaCO₃ 2.71, stabilizer 1.05, stearic acid 0.94 g/cm³. **(7 marks)** 1. Calculate the **density of the compound**. 2. Calculate the **mass of one mat**. 3. Calculate the **charge mass** required if **30 % excess** is provided for flash. #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Standard characterisation tests (3 marks):** 1. **Shore A hardness** (ASTM D2240) — measures surface resistance to indentation. 2. **Tensile strength and elongation at break** (ASTM D412) — measured on a dumbbell specimen using UTM. 3. **Vicat softening temperature** (ASTM D1525) — heat resistance indicator. 4. **Density** (ASTM D792) — Archimedes method. 5. **Heat resistance (qualitative)** — hot cup at 90 °C for 30 min. **Shore A test procedure (3 marks):** - **Specimen:** Flat, smooth, ≥ 6 mm thick (or stacked to ≥ 6 mm); conditioned 24 h at 23 °C / 50 % RH. - **Apparatus:** Shore A durometer with blunt truncated cone indenter (spring-loaded, 1 kg major load). - **Procedure:** Place durometer vertically on specimen; press firmly until the base is flat; read the dial **5 seconds** after firm contact (per ASTM D2240) or **3 seconds** (per ISO 868). - **Reading:** Scale 0–100; higher = harder. - **Expected value:** **Shore A = 70–85** for a soft PVC table mat. **Significance (1 mark):** Shore A is the **most cited** property in commercial mat specifications — buyers look for "soft yet firm" feel. #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Total mass of formulation (1 mark):** $$m_{\text{total}} = 100 + 50 + 30 + 3 + 1 = 184 \text{ g per 100 g PVC basis}$$ **Step 2 — Volume of each component (2 marks):** $$V_{\text{PVC}} = \frac{100}{1.40} = 71.43 \text{ cm}^3$$ $$V_{\text{DOP}} = \frac{50}{0.985} = 50.76 \text{ cm}^3$$ $$V_{\text{CaCO}_3} = \frac{30}{2.71} = 11.07 \text{ cm}^3$$ $$V_{\text{stab}} = \frac{3}{1.05} = 2.86 \text{ cm}^3$$ $$V_{\text{st.acid}} = \frac{1}{0.94} = 1.06 \text{ cm}^3$$ **Step 3 — Total volume and compound density (1 mark):** $$V_{\text{total}} = 71.43 + 50.76 + 11.07 + 2.86 + 1.06 = 137.18 \text{ cm}^3$$ $$\rho_c = \frac{184}{137.18} = 1.341 \text{ g/cm}^3$$ **Step 4 — Mass of one mat (1.5 marks):** $$V_{\text{mat}} = 0.300 \times 0.300 \times 0.003 = 2.7 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 270 \text{ cm}^3$$ $$m_{\text{mat}} = \rho_c \times V_{\text{mat}} = 1.341 \times 270 = 362.1 \text{ g}$$ **Step 5 — Charge mass with 30 % flash allowance (1.5 marks):** $$m_c = 1.30 \times 362.1 = 470.7 \text{ g} \approx 471 \text{ g}$$ > **[Bloom's Level: Applying + Analysing (L3–L4) | CO: CO4 — Perform calculations on polymer processing]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert °C → K** in the Arrhenius equation — this alone costs **1–2 marks** in Part B numericals. > 2. **Mixing up phr and weight %** — phr is per 100 parts of resin, NOT a percentage. *50 phr DOP ≠ 50 % DOP*; the actual weight fraction is $50/184 = 27.2\%$. > 3. **Wrong volume-fraction formula** — always $V_f = (w_f/\rho_f) / \sum (w_i/\rho_i)$, never simply $w_f$. > 4. **Skipping the conditioning step** in characterisation — losing 1 mark for not mentioning "23 °C / 50 % RH / 24 h". > 5. **Insufficient curing time** without justification — explain via **Arrhenius kinetics**, not a bare number. > 6. **Drawing the mould as a black box** in diagrams — examiners expect to see **platen, heater, ram, hydraulic line, and cavity** labelled. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.** > > - **Definition:** Table mat = compression-moulded plasticized PVC (or EVA / rubber) flat sheet, 1.5–4 mm thick. > - **Standard formulation:** PVC 100 + DOP 50 + CaCO₃ 30 + stabilizer 3 + stearic acid 1 (phr). > - **Process:** Compression moulding at **150–170 °C, 5–15 MPa, 5–10 min**. > - **Key equations (must memorise):** > - $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ — Arrhenius cure kinetics > - $F = P \times A$ — Press tonnage > - $1/T_g = \sum w_i/T_{gi}$ — Fox equation > - $\text{Shore A} \approx 100 - 0.7 \times (\text{DOP phr})$ — Hardness predictor > - $\sigma_c = \sigma_m (1 - V_f^{2/3}) S$ — Nielsen filler model > - **Test methods (with expected values):** > - Shore A = **70–85** > - Tensile = **8–15 MPa** > - Elongation = **150–350 %** > - Vicat ≥ **65 °C** > - Density = **1.25–1.40 g/cm³** > - **Conditioning:** 23 °C / 50 % RH / 24 h — universal before any mechanical test. > - **Plasticizer ↑ → Softness ↑, Strength ↓, $T_g$ ↓, Flexibility ↑.** > - **Filler ↑ → Hardness ↑, Strength ↓, Cost ↓, Elongation ↓.** > - **Stabilizer is essential** — prevents HCl liberation and discolouration of PVC at processing temperature. > - **Industrial analogy:** Same chemistry family as PVC cables, shoe soles, and flexible tubing — the table mat is a *teaching-scale demonstrator* of a billion-dollar industry. > - **Common KTU viva questions:** "Why compression moulding and not injection?" / "What is the role of stearic acid?" / "Why CaCO₃ is coated with stearic acid in premium mats?" / "How will doubling DOP affect Vicat?" ---

topics.title: Injection Bottle Cap
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL507 - PCPOL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Injection Bottle Cap

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Injection Molding of a Bottle Cap** is a polymer processing experiment in which a thermoplastic polymer (typically Polypropylene — PP, or High-Density Polyethylene — HDPE) is melted, plasticized, and injected under high pressure into a closed, cooled, multi-cavity steel mold to produce a finished threaded or snap-fit closure used in packaging applications. The experiment demonstrates the complete injection molding cycle — **plasticization → injection → packing/holding → cooling → ejection** — and is used to study the influence of processing parameters (melt temperature, mold temperature, injection pressure, holding pressure, injection speed, and cycle time) on the dimensional, visual, and mechanical quality of a thin-walled, axisymmetric polymeric product.

In KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory parlance, this is classified as a **Module Experiment 2** study under the broader course "Polymer Compounding and Processing Laboratory" (PCPOL507), where students are expected to fabricate a functional product, evaluate it for quality, and submit a viva-voce supported by a process datasheet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCPOL507 / Module 2):** Students are required to (a) set up the injection molding machine, (b) record machine settings, (c) produce at least 20 finished bottle caps, and (d) perform quality checks such as dimensional accuracy (thread pitch, outer diameter, height), visual defects (flash, short shot, sink marks), and a torque test for screw closure functionality.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hot-Metal Waffle Iron for Plastics"

Imagine a heavy, polished **waffle iron** made of steel. You pour a thick, hot, liquid batter (melted plastic) into one half, slam the second half shut with tremendous force, wait a moment for the batter to solidify in the intricate pattern, and then open the iron to pop out a perfectly shaped waffle.

That is exactly what an injection molding machine does with plastic:

- The **"waffle iron"** is the **mold (tool)** — two hardened steel halves (the *cavity* and the *core*) bolted to large platens.
- The **"hot batter"** is the **molten polymer** (e.g., PP at ~200–230 °C) prepared inside the heated barrel by a rotating screw.
- The **"slam shut"** is the **clamping unit** generating hundreds of tonnes of force to keep the mold closed against the injection pressure.
- The **"popping out"** is the **ejection system**, where ejector pins push the cooled, solid bottle cap out of the cavity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definitions Recap (must memorize for KTU viva):**
> - **Shot weight** — Mass of polymer injected per cycle (grams).
> - **Cycle time** — Total time from mold close to part eject (seconds).
> - **Clamping Force (Tonnage)** — Force the clamp applies to resist mold opening (Tonnes or kN).
> - **Flash** — Thin unwanted polymer film that escapes the mold parting line due to insufficient clamping force or worn molds.

### 1.3 Bottle Cap Geometry — A Typical Threaded PP Closure

A standard **28 mm PCO (Plastic Closures Only) bottle cap** is the most common product for this experiment because of its simple axisymmetric geometry and tight dimensional tolerance. It contains:

- **Top sealing disc** (flat top)
- **Threaded sidewall** (helical threads inside the cap that mate with bottle neck)
- **Tamper-evident ring** (small bridges that break on first opening)
- **Liner recess** (optional EPE/foamed PE liner for sealing)

**Typical dimensions (industry standard for a 28 mm mineral water cap):**
- Outer Diameter (OD) = **28.0 ± 0.15 mm**
- Height (H) = **17.0 ± 0.20 mm**
- Wall thickness = **1.0 to 1.2 mm** (classified as **thin-walled**)
- Thread pitch = **3.0 mm**
- Mass per cap ≈ **1.6 to 2.0 grams**

> [!TIP]
> **Why "thin-walled" matters:** When wall thickness is **< 1.5 mm**, the melt freezes very quickly. You must inject fast and pack hard — otherwise the surface stays dull, the cap warps, and threads are short-molded. This is a frequent viva question at KTU.

### 1.4 Standard Materials & Their Justification

| Material | MFI (g/10 min) | Why Chosen for Cap | Shrinkage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polypropylene (PP – Homopolymer)** | 8–12 | High stiffness, good thread strength, hinge flexibility | 1.0 – 2.5 % |
| **HDPE** | 6–10 | Excellent chemical resistance, good impact | 1.5 – 3.0 % |
| **Random Copolymer PP** | 10–15 | Better clarity, lower warpage | 1.0 – 2.0 % |

**Key constant to remember for KTU calculations:** Density of PP ≈ **0.905 g/cm³** and density of HDPE ≈ **0.954 g/cm³**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Injection Molding Cycle — Six Logical Steps

The complete cycle to produce one bottle cap proceeds in six discrete, timed stages. Every stage is a KTU viva and written-exam favourite.

1. **Mold Close & Clamp Build-up**
   The moving platen travels forward; the toggle or hydraulic clamp generates full tonnage. *Why:* The mold must be airtight before plastic enters, otherwise melt flashes out.

2. **Injection (Fill Phase)**
   The screw acts as a plunger and rams the melt forward at high velocity (typically **30–80 mm/s** for thin-walled caps) until ~95–98 % of the cavity volume is filled. Pressure rises rapidly (injection pressure ≈ **800–1400 bar**).

3. **Packing / Holding Phase**
   The screw switches from velocity control to **pressure control**, packing additional melt at ~50–70 % of injection pressure to compensate for volumetric shrinkage. *Why:* Without packing, sink marks appear on the top disc and threads under-fill.

4. **Cooling Phase**
   The plastic in the cavity solidifies against the cooled mold walls (mold cooled by water at **20–40 °C**). Cooling time ≈ **60–70 % of total cycle time**. For a 1 mm PP cap, cooling ≈ **3–5 seconds**.

5. **Plastication (for next shot)**
   Simultaneously with cooling, the screw rotates and retracts, melting fresh polymer pellets in the barrel and preparing the next shot.

6. **Mold Open & Ejection**
   Clamp releases, mold opens, ejector pins push the cap out, and the part is dropped onto a conveyor or into a bin.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If asked *"Why do we hold pressure after the cavity is full?"* — the answer is **to compensate for thermal contraction of the polymer as it cools**, because polymers shrink 1–3 % when going from melt to solid. Without holding, the part would be short, with sink marks.

### 2.2 Why a Bottle Cap is a "Tricky" Part

A bottle cap is **thin-walled, has a long flow path, and contains fine helical threads**. Three engineering challenges dominate:

- **High Flow Length to Wall Thickness Ratio (L/t):** L/t for a cap ≈ 30–40. This demands **high injection speed**.
- **Thread Fidelity:** Threads are typically only **0.6–0.8 mm deep**. If melt freezes before reaching the thread tips, the cap will not screw on the bottle.
- **Warpage Control:** Differential cooling between the thick top disc and thin sidewall causes the cap to distort. A **uniform mold-cooling circuit** is mandatory.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **frequently tested** in KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. Memorize the form, the symbol, and the unit.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{clamp} \ge P_{inj} \times A_{proj}$ | Clamping force minimum (must overcome injection pressure × projected area) | Tonnes (T) or kN |
| 2 | $m_{shot} = V_{shot} \times \rho_{melt} \times n_{cav}$ | Shot weight (mass of polymer per cycle) | grams (g) |
| 3 | $V_{shot} = m_{part} \times n_{cav} \times 1.05$ | Shot volume (with 5 % waste allowance) | cm³ |
| 4 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{m}-T_{w}}{T_{e}-T_{w}}\right)$ | Cooling time (simplified, s = wall thickness, α = thermal diffusivity) | seconds (s) |
| 5 | $D_{shaft} = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{16 \cdot T \cdot L_{eff}}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}}}$ | Screw shaft diameter design (T = torque, L = length, τ = allowable shear) | mm |
| 6 | $P_{cav} = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A_{proj}}$ | Cavity pressure (rearranged) | MPa |
| 7 | $\%\,Shrink = \dfrac{L_{mold} - L_{part}}{L_{mold}} \times 100$ | Mold shrinkage of polymer (dimensional compensation) | % |
| 8 | $C_{t} = t_{cycle} \times n_{cav} \times 3600$ | Hourly production capacity (parts/hour) | parts/hr |
| 9 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Volumetric efficiency of injection (typical 95–98 %) | % |
| 10 | $Q_{cool} = m \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat removed by cooling water (mass flow × specific heat × ΔT) | kW |

**Standard Reference Values to Memorise for KTU Exams:**

- Injection pressure, $P_{inj}$ ≈ **700 – 1400 bar** (≈ 70 – 140 MPa)
- Melt temperature for PP ≈ **200 – 240 °C**
- Mold temperature for PP ≈ **20 – 60 °C**
- Clamping tonnage for a 28 mm cap (1-cavity) ≈ **40 – 60 tonnes**
- Thermal diffusivity of PP, $\alpha \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-7}$ **m²/s**
- Specific heat of water, $c_{p}$ = **4.18 kJ/(kg·K)**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Packaging Industry:** Over **500 billion** plastic bottle caps are produced annually worldwide — injection molding is the only economical process.
- **Design Engineering:** The cap experiment teaches students about **Design for Manufacturing (DFM)** — uniform wall thickness, draft angles (typically **0.5°–1°**), and proper gate location.
- **Quality Engineering:** Students learn **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** and **Cpk indices** by measuring OD, height, and weight of the produced caps.

> [!TIP]
> **One-liner viva answer:** *"Injection molding of a bottle cap is the industrial benchmark for studying the manufacture of thin-walled, high-tolerance, mass-produced thermoplastic components."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive content mandate:** Every algebraic transition is written out. No step is skipped. This is the KTU board-exam style of showing full working.

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Shot Weight (Shot Volume) for a 28 mm PP Cap

**Given (standard KTU problem data):**
- Number of cavities, $n_{cav}$ = **4** (typical 4-cavity cap mold)
- Mass of one cap, $m_{part}$ = **1.8 g**
- Density of PP melt, $\rho_{melt}$ = **0.905 g/cm³** (assume constant for simplicity)
- Waste/Runner allowance = **5 %** (industry standard)

**Step 1 — Calculate the total mass needed per cycle.**

$$
m_{theoretical} = m_{part} \times n_{cav}
$$

Substituting:

$$
m_{theoretical} = 1.8 \text{ g} \times 4 = 7.2 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Add 5 % runner/waste allowance.**

$$
m_{actual} = m_{theoretical} \times 1.05
$$

$$
m_{actual} = 7.2 \text{ g} \times 1.05 = 7.56 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert mass to volume.**

$$
V_{shot} = \frac{m_{actual}}{\rho_{melt}}
$$

$$
V_{shot} = \frac{7.56 \text{ g}}{0.905 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 8.354 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Final Answer (boxed for KTU exam):**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{shot} \approx 8.35 \text{ cm}^3 \;\;\; \text{and} \;\;\; m_{actual} = 7.56 \text{ g}\,}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Clamping Force

**Given:**
- Injection pressure inside cavity, $P_{inj}$ = **1000 bar** = **100 MPa** = **100 N/mm²**
- Projected area of one cap (cavity footprint) = **(Ø 28 mm) → $A_{proj}$ ≈ 6.16 cm²** = **615.75 mm²**
- Number of cavities = 4

**Step 1 — Total projected area.**

$$
A_{total} = A_{proj} \times n_{cav}
$$

$$
A_{total} = 615.75 \text{ mm}^2 \times 4 = 2463 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Required clamping force in Newtons.**

$$
F_{clamp} \ge P_{inj} \times A_{total}
$$

$$
F_{clamp} \ge 100 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 2463 \text{ mm}^2 = 246{,}300 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Tonnes (1 Tonne-force ≈ 9807 N).**

$$
F_{clamp} \ge \frac{246{,}300}{9807} \approx 25.1 \text{ Tonnes}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply safety factor (KTU standard, 1.5×):**

$$
F_{clamp,\,design} = 25.1 \times 1.5 \approx 37.7 \text{ Tonnes}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Choose a machine of at least 40 Tonne clamp tonnage.}\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Cooling Time for a 1 mm Wall PP Cap

**Given (Simplified KTU formula):**
- Wall thickness, $s$ = **1.0 mm** = **1 × 10⁻³ m**
- Thermal diffusivity of PP, $\alpha$ = **1.0 × 10⁻⁷ m²/s**
- Melt temperature, $T_m$ = **220 °C**
- Ejection temperature, $T_e$ = **100 °C** (above glass transition, soft enough to eject)
- Mold wall temperature, $T_w$ = **30 °C**

**Step 1 — Write the simplified cooling-time formula (derived from transient 1-D heat conduction in a slab).**

$$
t_{cool} = \frac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left( \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
t_{cool} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{\pi^{2} \times (1.0 \times 10^{-7})} \cdot \ln\!\left( \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \frac{220 - 30}{100 - 30} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the pre-factor.**

$$
\frac{(1 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{\pi^{2} \times (1.0 \times 10^{-7})} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{9.8696 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.013 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$
\frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \frac{190}{70} = 0.8106 \times 2.7143 \approx 2.200
$$

$$
\ln(2.200) \approx 0.7885
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply.**

$$
t_{cool} = 1.013 \times 0.7885 \approx 0.799 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\,t_{cool} \approx 0.8 \text{ seconds for a 1 mm PP cap.}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** In practice, the **minimum cooling time** is multiplied by a **safety factor of 1.5–2.0** to ensure the part is rigid enough to be ejected without warping. So the *machine-set* cooling time would be **1.2 to 1.6 s**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Hourly Production Capacity

**Given:**
- Total cycle time, $t_{cycle}$ = **6.0 s**
- Number of cavities, $n_{cav}$ = **4**

**Step 1 — Apply the production capacity formula.**

$$
C_{t} = \frac{3600}{t_{cycle}} \times n_{cav}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
C_{t} = \frac{3600}{6.0} \times 4 = 600 \times 4 = 2400 \text{ caps/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Per-shift (8 hours) and per-day (3 shifts).**

$$
C_{day} = 2400 \times 24 = 57{,}600 \text{ caps/day}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Production rate} = 2400 \text{ caps/hr} = 57{,}600 \text{ caps/day (continuous).}\,}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — "Which Parameter to Change to Fix Which Defect?"

This is the **highest-yield KTU exam table**. Memorise it.

| Defect Observed on Cap | Likely Root Cause | Parameter to Adjust | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Short shot** (cap incomplete) | Melt freezes before cavity fills | Injection speed, melt temp., mold temp. | Increase |
| **Flash** (thin film at parting line) | Insufficient clamp tonnage, high pressure | Clamp tonnage, injection pressure | Increase clamp, reduce pressure |
| **Sink mark on top disc** | Insufficient packing | Holding pressure, holding time | Increase |
| **Warpage / ovality** | Non-uniform cooling | Cooling water flow balance | Rebalance |
| **Weld line on sidewall** | Melt fronts meet cold | Mold temp., injection speed | Increase |
| **Dull surface finish** | Mold temp. too low, or cold melt | Mold temp. | Increase |
| **Sticking in cavity** | Insufficient draft, over-packing | Draft angle, packing pressure | Increase draft, reduce packing |
| **Thread under-fill** | Premature freeze in thread region | Mold temp. at thread insert | Increase local heating |
| **Bubbles / voids** | Trapped air, low packing | Venting, packing pressure | Improve vents, increase packing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Injection Molding Machine for a Bottle Cap

The following Mermaid block diagram maps every sub-system of a typical **hydraulic toggle-type injection molding machine** used in KTU laboratories (e.g., a 60-tonne machine such as an **Electronica** or **L&T** model).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Raw Material Hopper with PP Pellets] --> B1[Heated Barrel with Thermocouples]
    B1 --> C1[Rotating Screw Plasticizer Unit]
    C1 --> D1[Non Return Valve Check Ring]
    D1 --> E1[Nozzle and Sprue Bush]
    E1 --> F1[Mold Cavity Side Cap Geometry]
    G1[Mold Core Side with Thread Inserts] --> F1
    F1 --> H1[Cooling Channels with Water Circulator]
    H1 --> I1[Part Ejection System with Ejector Pins]
    I1 --> J1[Finished Bottle Cap on Conveyor]
    K1[Hydraulic Clamping Unit] --> F1
    L1[Control Panel PLC with HMI] --> B1
    L1 --> C1
    L1 --> K1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for a KTU viva:**
> - The **top branch (A1 → B1 → C1 → D1 → E1)** = the **plasticizing & injection line**.
> - The **middle (F1 ↔ G1)** = the **mold (cavity + core) where the cap takes shape**.
> - The **bottom branch (K1)** = the **clamp** holding the mold shut.
> - The **right branch (L1)** = the **PLC controller** orchestrating every parameter.

---

### 4.2 Process Cycle Block Diagram (State Machine View)

The same machine viewed as a **timed state machine** — useful for KTU viva when asked *"Explain the cycle phases."*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[State 1 Mold Close and Clamp Build] --> s2[State 2 Injection Fill Phase]
    s2 --> s3[State 3 Packing and Holding Phase]
    s3 --> s4[State 4 Cooling Phase in Mold]
    s4 --> s5[State 5 Screw Rotates and Plastication for Next Shot]
    s5 --> s6[State 6 Mold Open and Ejector Pins Push Cap Out]
    s6 --> s1
```

---

### 4.3 Mold Cross-Section — Block Schematic of a 4-Cavity Cap Mold

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Top Clamping Plate Stationary] --> P2[Top Mold Half Cavity Plate]
    P2 --> P3[Cap Cavity 1]
    P2 --> P4[Cap Cavity 2]
    P2 --> P5[Cap Cavity 3]
    P2 --> P6[Cap Cavity 4]
    P7[Bottom Mold Half Core Plate] --> P8[Threaded Core Insert 1]
    P7 --> P9[Threaded Core Insert 2]
    P7 --> P10[Threaded Core Insert 3]
    P7 --> P11[Threaded Core Insert 4]
    P12[Cooling Channel Network] --> P2
    P12 --> P7
    P13[Runner and Gate System] --> P3
    P13 --> P4
    P13 --> P5
    P13 --> P6
    P14[Bottom Clamping Plate Moving] --> P7
    P15[Ejector Pin Assembly] --> P14
```

**Engineering interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **stationary half (top)** contains the **cavity impressions** — the outer shape of the cap.
- The **moving half (bottom)** contains the **threaded core inserts** — the helical threads are machined onto these cylindrical inserts and must be unscrewed (or stripped via collapsible core mechanisms) for ejection. *This is the reason cap molds are the most expensive in the packaging industry — collapsible cores cost more than the mold base!*
- The **cooling channels** are typically drilled in a **baffled or spiral pattern** at **8–10 mm diameter**, with water flow rate of **3–6 L/min per circuit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collapsible Core Mechanism:** In a multi-cavity cap mold, the threaded cores cannot simply be pulled straight out. They use a **collapsible (or unscrewing) core** that either rotates (motorised) or collapses mechanically. This is a **favourite viva question** in KTU PCPOL507.

---

### 4.4 Heat-Transfer & Material-Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    h1[Heater Bands 200 to 240 C] --> h2[Polymer Melt Pool]
    h2 --> h3[Shear Heating from Screw Rotation]
    h3 --> h4[Nozzle and Sprue]
    h4 --> h5[Cavity Wall Heat Loss to Mold]
    h5 --> h6[Cooling Water at 20 to 40 C Removes Heat]
    h6 --> h7[Chiller Unit]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any three defects commonly observed in injection-molded bottle caps and state one process parameter adjustment to correct each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per defect + correction):**

1. **Short Shot** — *Cause:* Insufficient injection pressure or low melt/mold temperature. *Correction:* **Increase injection speed and/or increase melt temperature** to keep the melt fluid long enough to fill the threads.
2. **Sink Mark on Top Disc** — *Cause:* Insufficient holding/packing pressure to compensate for shrinkage. *Correction:* **Increase holding pressure and holding time**.
3. **Flash at the Parting Line** — *Cause:* Insufficient clamping force or worn mold surfaces. *Correction:* **Increase clamping tonnage** and inspect mold parting line for wear.

*(For full 3 marks, write defect → cause → remedy clearly. Avoid one-liners.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is Polypropylene (PP) the most preferred polymer for manufacturing bottle caps? Give two reasons.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Excellent Thread Strength & Hinge Flexibility:** PP has a high flexural modulus (~1.5 GPa) and good fatigue resistance, so the internal threads of the cap can be moulded with sharp, accurate profiles and the cap can be screwed on/off repeatedly without cracking. *(2 marks)*
2. **Low Density (0.905 g/cm³) and Cost:** PP is the lightest commodity plastic, so the cap is lightweight (cost-effective per piece) and offers a good balance of stiffness and impact strength at room temperature. *(1 mark)*

*(Additional credit point that students often miss — PP has low mould shrinkage (1–2 %), giving good dimensional accuracy of the thread pitch.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **complete injection molding cycle** for the manufacture of a **28 mm PCO PP bottle cap**. List the typical process parameters used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **4-cavity injection mold** is used to produce a 1.8 g bottle cap from polypropylene. Calculate the **(i)** theoretical shot weight, **(ii)** shot volume including 5 % runner allowance, and **(iii)** the **required clamping tonnage** if the injection pressure is 1000 bar and the projected area of each cavity is 6.16 cm². Assume density of PP = 0.905 g/cm³ and use a safety factor of 1.5. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) Injection Molding Cycle (7 marks):**

*Block Diagram (3 marks):*

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a[Mold Close] --> b[Injection]
    b --> c[Holding and Packing]
    c --> d[Cooling]
    d --> e[Plastication for Next Shot]
    e --> f[Mold Open and Eject]
    f --> a
```

*Explanation of each stage (4 marks — ~1 mark per stage):*

1. **Mold Close:** Moving platen closes; clamp builds full tonnage. Mold parting line must be airtight.
2. **Injection:** Screw moves forward, injecting PP melt (200–240 °C) at 800–1400 bar, filling 95–98 % of the cavity.
3. **Packing/Holding:** Switch to pressure control (~500–700 bar) to compensate shrinkage and form sharp threads.
4. **Cooling:** Water at 20–40 °C circulates through channels; cap solidifies in 3–5 s for 1 mm wall.
5. **Ejection:** Mold opens; ejector pins push the finished cap out. **Cycle time ≈ 4–7 s** for thin-walled caps.

*Typical process parameters table (for full marks):*

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Melt Temperature | 200 – 240 °C |
| Mold Temperature | 20 – 60 °C |
| Injection Pressure | 800 – 1400 bar |
| Holding Pressure | 400 – 700 bar |
| Injection Speed | 30 – 80 mm/s |
| Cycle Time | 4 – 7 s |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Theoretical shot weight (2 marks):**

$$
m_{theoretical} = m_{part} \times n_{cav} = 1.8 \times 4 = 7.2 \text{ g}
$$

**(ii) Shot volume with 5 % allowance (3 marks):**

$$
m_{actual} = 7.2 \times 1.05 = 7.56 \text{ g}
$$

$$
V_{shot} = \frac{7.56}{0.905} = 8.354 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**(iii) Clamping tonnage (2 marks):**

$$
A_{total} = 6.16 \text{ cm}^2 \times 4 = 24.64 \text{ cm}^2 = 2464 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
F_{clamp} = 100 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 2464 \text{ mm}^2 = 246{,}400 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{clamp} = \frac{246{,}400}{9807} \approx 25.13 \text{ Tonnes}
$$

With **safety factor 1.5**:

$$
\boxed{F_{clamp,\,design} = 25.13 \times 1.5 \approx 37.7 \text{ Tonnes. Choose 40 T machine.}\,}
$$

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** What are the **common defects observed in injection-molded bottle caps**? Discuss in detail the **causes and remedies** for **short shot, flash, sink marks, and warpage**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **mold design considerations** specific to a **multi-cavity bottle cap mold**. Explain with a sketch the **collapsible core mechanism** used for unscrewing the internal threads. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Defects, Causes & Remedies (7 marks — ~1.75 marks per defect):**

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Short Shot** | Low injection speed, low melt/mold temp., insufficient venting | Increase injection speed; raise melt and mold temp.; add vents at thread tips |
| **Flash** | Insufficient clamp tonnage; high injection pressure; worn mold surfaces | Increase clamp tonnage; reduce injection pressure; polish/repair mold |
| **Sink Marks** | Insufficient packing; thick section cools unevenly | Increase holding pressure and time; uniform wall thickness design |
| **Warpage** | Non-uniform cooling; high residual stress; ejection too early | Balance cooling circuits; increase cooling time; optimise ejection |

*(For full marks, present the answer in a **tabular form** with the above 4 columns.)*

---

**(b) Multi-Cavity Cap Mold Design & Collapsible Core (7 marks):**

**Key Design Considerations (3 marks):**

1. **Number of cavities (n):** Typically 4, 8, 16, 24, 32, 48, 72 — based on production volume.
2. **Cavity layout:** Balanced (2 × 2, 4 × 4) to ensure equal flow length from the sprue to every cavity.
3. **Gate type:** **Submarine (sub) gate** is most common for caps because the gate vestige is automatically trimmed as the cap is ejected.
4. **Cooling:** Symmetric baffle/spiral cooling channels in **both halves**, with **baffles** behind the core inserts.
5. **Draft angle:** 0.5°–1° on the cavity wall to allow ejection.
6. **Venting:** Micro-vents (0.01–0.03 mm deep) at the end of fill — typically at the **top disc centre** and the **last thread**.

**Collapsible Core Mechanism (4 marks):**

A collapsible core allows the **internal threads** of a cap to be released without unscrewing. The core consists of multiple **sliding segments** (typically 8–16) held together by a **central cone or sleeve** during molding.

- **During molding:** The central cone pulls the segments outward against a ring, keeping them in a perfect cylindrical shape with the thread profile.
- **During ejection:** The central cone retracts, the segments collapse inward (radial movement of ~0.3–0.5 mm), and the cap slides off the core easily.

**Sketch (block representation since Mermaid cannot draw 3D):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cc1[Central Pulling Cone] --> cc2[Ring Sleeve Holds Segments Outward]
    cc2 --> cc3[Collapsible Segment 1 with Thread Profile]
    cc2 --> cc4[Collapsible Segment 2 with Thread Profile]
    cc2 --> cc5[Collapsible Segment 3 with Thread Profile]
    cc2 --> cc6[Collapsible Segment 4 with Thread Profile]
    cc3 --> cc7[Bottle Cap with Internal Threads]
    cc4 --> cc7
    cc5 --> cc7
    cc6 --> cc7
```

*(For full marks, the student should also mention **motorised unscrewing cores** as an alternative for high-cavitation molds.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in PCPOL507 (Module 2 – Bottle Cap experiment):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the runner allowance** — Students calculate shot weight using only $m_{part} \times n_{cav}$ and **forget the 5–10 % runner/waste factor**. Examiners specifically deduct **1–2 marks** for this. *Always write* "*including X % runner allowance*".
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** — Mixing **bar, MPa, N/mm², kg/cm²** without converting. Use a single unit (preferably **MPa** or **bar**) throughout the calculation.
> 3. **Confusing clamping force with injection force** — Clamp tonnage is in **Tonnes**, injection pressure is in **bar/MPa**. They are **not the same**.
> 4. **Not stating safety factor** — Industrial designs always include a safety factor of **1.3 to 2.0**. Forgetting it costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Writing cycle time as total cycle** — Cycle time = **mold close + injection + holding + cooling + ejection**. Don't write only cooling time.
> 6. **Missing the tamper-evident ring** in a cap diagram — Examiners expect the student to identify **all four functional parts** of a cap: *sealing disc, threaded wall, tamper-evident ring, and liner recess*.
> 7. **Confusing PP and PE shrinkage** — PP ≈ **1–2 %**, HDPE ≈ **1.5–3 %**. Mixing these up loses easy marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision bullets for KTU PCPOL507 – Module 2:**

- ☐ **Definition:** Injection molding of a bottle cap is a **thermoplastic, cyclic, pressure-driven** process using a **two-platen, two-half mold**.
- ☐ **Six stages of the cycle:** Mold close → Inject → Pack/Hold → Cool → Plasticate → Eject.
- ☐ **Most-used material:** **Polypropylene (PP)** — homopolymer for stiffness, random copolymer for clarity.
- ☐ **Key dimensions of 28 mm PCO cap:** OD = 28 mm, Height ≈ 17 mm, Wall ≈ 1 mm, Mass ≈ 1.8 g.
- ☐ **Density of PP melt:** 0.905 g/cm³ | **HDPE:** 0.954 g/cm³.
- ☐ **Standard process window for PP:** Melt 200–240 °C, Mold 20–60 °C, Pressure 800–1400 bar.
- ☐ **Cycle time for thin cap:** 4–7 s. **Cooling time** ≈ 60–70 % of total cycle.
- ☐ **Clamp tonnage formula:** $F_{clamp} = P_{inj} \times A_{proj} \times \text{safety factor (1.5)}$.
- ☐ **Shot volume formula:** $V_{shot} = \dfrac{m_{part} \times n_{cav} \times 1.05}{\rho_{melt}}$.
- ☐ **Hourly capacity formula:** $C_t = \dfrac{3600}{t_{cycle}} \times n_{cav}$.
- ☐ **Mold shrinkage compensation:** PP = 1–2 %, HDPE = 1.5–3 % — must be added to the cavity dimension.
- ☐ **Draft angle on cap cavity:** 0.5° – 1° (essential for ejection).
- ☐ **Gate type:** Submarine (sub) gate — leaves no visible mark and is auto-trimmed.
- ☐ **Cooling channels:** 8–10 mm Ø, baffled/spiral, water flow 3–6 L/min/circuit.
- ☐ **Collapsible / unscrewing core:** Required to release the **internal threads** — the most expensive part of cap-mold design.
- ☐ **Four functional parts of a cap:** Sealing disc + Threaded wall + Tamper-evident ring + Liner recess.
- ☐ **Most common defects:** Short shot, flash, sink marks, warpage, weld lines — each has a **specific remedy** in the parameter matrix.
- ☐ **Viva mantra:** "*Increase injection speed, packing pressure and mold temperature to fix short shot; increase clamp tonnage to fix flash; increase cooling time and balance cooling to fix warpage.*"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Play Ball
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL507 - PCPOL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Play Ball

# Play Ball — KTU 2024 Lab Experiment Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** programming laboratory (PCPOL507), **"Play Ball"** is a foundational graphical simulation experiment where a ball is animated across a drawing canvas such that it moves, bounces off the boundaries, and responds to user-controlled parameters such as speed, direction, and color. The experiment introduces students to **event-driven programming**, **loop-based animation logic**, and **coordinate-geometry based object motion** — typically implemented using the Python `turtle` graphics module or the `pygame` library.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **real rubber ball dropped inside a rectangular billiards table**. It rolls in a straight line, hits the cushion (boundary wall), and bounces back with the same speed but in the opposite horizontal or vertical direction — this is exactly what our program simulates, except on a **computer screen** instead of a physical table.

The screen becomes the table, the ball becomes a small filled circle drawn by code, and the boundaries are simply the screen's edges (e.g., width = 800 px, height = 600 px). Every "frame" of animation is just one update of the ball's `(x, y)` position based on its current `(dx, dy)` velocity — like flipping pages of a flipbook extremely fast.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is mapped to **CO1 (Apply programming constructs to solve computational problems)** and **CO2 (Implement graphical/animation-based programs using standard libraries)**. It tests the student's ability to translate *real-world physics intuition* into *loop-driven computer logic*.

### Key Programming Entities
- **Canvas/Window:** The virtual rectangular area where the ball is drawn (e.g., `800 × 600` pixels).
- **Ball Object:** A small colored circle defined by its center `(x, y)` and radius `r`.
- **Velocity Vector:** The `(dx, dy)` pair describing how much the ball moves per frame in the `x` and `y` directions respectively.
- **Boundary Conditions:** The set of rules dictating that the ball reverses its direction when it touches any edge of the canvas.

### Standard Constants / Defaults (as per KTU reference solution)
| Parameter | Typical Value | Meaning |
|-----------|---------------|---------|
| Canvas width | **800 pixels** | Horizontal screen size |
| Canvas height | **600 pixels** | Vertical screen size |
| Ball radius | **20 pixels** | Size of the drawn circle |
| Default speed | **2 px/frame** | Magnitude of motion per refresh |
| Background | **White** | Standard canvas color |
| Ball color | **Blue / Red** | Default fill color |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> **Bouncing logic** = A reversal of the velocity component perpendicular to the wall struck.
> *Horizontal wall hit* → `dy = -dy`
> *Vertical wall hit* → `dx = -dx`

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic Breakdown
The "Play Ball" experiment runs on a **frame-by-frame update loop** (often called the *main loop* or *game loop*). Each iteration of this loop does exactly four things:

1. **Erase** the ball from its previous position (or redraw the background to cover it).
2. **Update** the ball's `(x, y)` coordinates by adding the velocity components: `x = x + dx`, `y = y + dy`.
3. **Check** whether the ball has crossed any boundary (`x ± r > width` or `y ± r > height`).
4. **Reverse** the appropriate velocity component if a boundary collision is detected.
5. **Redraw** the ball at its new position.
6. **Refresh** the screen to make the update visible to the user.

### The 'Why' Behind Each Step
- **Why erase before redrawing?** Because in computer graphics, the screen is *not* automatically cleared — old pixels persist unless explicitly overwritten or the canvas is reset.
- **Why use velocity instead of fixed step sizes?** Velocity (`dx, dy`) makes the code *parameterizable*. Changing `dx` instantly changes the speed without rewriting the entire motion logic.
- **Why check boundaries every frame?** Because the ball is in *continuous* motion. Missing even one frame can cause the ball to "escape" the canvas.
- **Why negate the velocity on collision?** This models *elastic collision* with a frictionless wall — a standard simplification taught in physics and computer graphics courses.

### KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical / Logical Representation | Meaning |
|---------|--------------------------------------|---------|
| Position Update (X) | $$x_{\text{new}} = x_{\text{old}} + d_x$$ | Horizontal movement per frame |
| Position Update (Y) | $$y_{\text{new}} = y_{\text{old}} + d_y$$ | Vertical movement per frame |
| Right Wall Collision | $$x + r \geq W \;\Rightarrow\; d_x = -\vert d_x \vert$$ | Hit on right edge, reverse horizontally |
| Left Wall Collision | $$x - r \leq 0 \;\Rightarrow\; d_x = \vert d_x \vert$$ | Hit on left edge, ensure positive velocity |
| Top Wall Collision | $$y + r \geq H \;\Rightarrow\; d_y = -\vert d_y \vert$$ | Hit on top edge, reverse vertically |
| Bottom Wall Collision | $$y - r \leq 0 \;\Rightarrow\; d_y = \vert d_y \vert$$ | Hit on bottom edge, ensure positive velocity |
| Speed Magnitude | $$s = \sqrt{d_x^2 + d_y^2}$$ | Total speed of the ball (in px/frame) |
| Frames Per Second | $$\text{FPS} = \frac{1}{\Delta t}$$ | Refresh rate; higher FPS = smoother animation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRT-Pattern Note:** When using `pygame`, the `time.Clock().tick(60)` call locks the loop to **60 FPS** — this is a KTU-favored constant and often appears in viva questions.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
The bouncing-ball logic is the **foundational algorithm** behind:
- 🎮 **2D Video Games** (Pong, Breakout, Arkanoid)
- 📺 **DVD Screensaver** (the bouncing logo on idle screens)
- 🛰️ **Radar Systems** (radar blips reflecting off screen edges)
- 🏀 **Sports Analytics Software** (trajectory simulation for basketball free-throws)
- 🔬 **Physics Simulators** (particle motion in vacuum chambers)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Program Logic Walkthrough (Line-by-Line Trace)

Below is an exhaustive line-by-line derivation of the **Python Turtle-based Play Ball experiment**, which is the canonical KTU 2024 reference implementation. Each transition is explicitly justified.

---

### Step 0 — Library Imports

```python
import turtle
```

**Derivation / Logic:**
The `turtle` module is part of Python's **standard library** and provides primitive graphics functions: a virtual canvas, a "pen" (turtle), and commands like `forward()`, `backward()`, `left()`, `right()`, `penup()`, `pendown()`. We import it as `turtle` so we can call functions like `turtle.Screen()`.

---

### Step 1 — Canvas Setup

```python
wn = turtle.Screen()
wn.title("Play Ball - KTU Experiment 2")
wn.bgcolor("white")
wn.setup(width=800, height=600)
wn.tracer(0)
```

**Line-by-line logic:**
- `wn = turtle.Screen()` → creates the drawing window object and assigns it to `wn` (short for *window*).
- `wn.title(...)` → sets the text displayed on the window's title bar — important for identification during lab evaluation.
- `wn.bgcolor("white")` → sets background to **white** (KTU default).
- `wn.setup(width=800, height=600)` → sizes the canvas to exactly **800 × 600 pixels**.
- `wn.tracer(0)` → **disables automatic screen refresh**, allowing manual control of when frames are drawn. This is critical for animation smoothness.

---

### Step 2 — Ball Object Creation

```python
ball = turtle.Turtle()
ball.shape("circle")
ball.color("blue")
ball.penup()
ball.goto(0, 0)
```

**Logic:**
- `ball = turtle.Turtle()` → creates a turtle object that will serve as our ball.
- `ball.shape("circle")` → uses the built-in circle shape (default size: 20 px diameter ≈ 10 px radius, but we treat radius as ~10 internally for boundary math).
- `ball.color("blue")` → fills the ball with blue.
- `ball.penup()` → lifts the "pen" so that the ball does not draw a trail as it moves.
- `ball.goto(0, 0)` → places the ball at the canvas center (origin of turtle's coordinate system).

---

### Step 3 — Velocity Variables (the "Formula Inputs")

```python
dx = 2
dy = 2
```

**Logic:**
The ball's velocity is represented as two integers: `dx` for horizontal motion and `dy` for vertical motion. Both are initialized to **+2** so the ball starts moving *rightward and upward*.

The position update equations become:

$$
x_{\text{new}} = x_{\text{old}} + d_x
$$

$$
y_{\text{new}} = y_{\text{old}} + d_y
$$

At frame 1:
- If `x = 0`, then `x_new = 0 + 2 = 2`.
- If `y = 0`, then `y_new = 0 + 2 = 2`.

The ball moves from `(0, 0)` to `(2, 2)`.

---

### Step 4 — The Main Animation Loop

```python
while True:
    wn.update()
    
    # Move the ball
    ball.setx(ball.xcor() + dx)
    ball.sety(ball.ycor() + dy)
    
    # Boundary checking
    if ball.xcor() > 390:
        ball.setx(390)
        dx = -dx
        
    if ball.xcor() < -390:
        ball.setx(-390)
        dx = -dx
        
    if ball.ycor() > 290:
        ball.sety(290)
        dy = -dy
        
    if ball.ycor() < -290:
        ball.sety(-290)
        dy = -dy
```

**Exhaustive logic breakdown:**

#### Sub-step 4.1 — `wn.update()`
Manually refreshes the screen. Because we used `wn.tracer(0)`, no drawing happens until this command is called. By calling it once per loop iteration, we control the **animation frame rate**.

#### Sub-step 4.2 — Position Update
- `ball.xcor()` retrieves the current x-coordinate of the ball.
- `ball.setx(...)` sets the x-coordinate to a new value: `current_x + dx`.

This implements the equation:

$$
x_{\text{new}} = x_{\text{old}} + d_x
$$

Same for the y-axis.

#### Sub-step 4.3 — Boundary Conditions

The canvas is `800 × 600`. The turtle coordinate system centers at `(0, 0)`:
- Right edge: `x = +400`
- Left edge: `x = -400`
- Top edge: `y = +300`
- Bottom edge: `y = -300`

The ball has a radius of ~10 px, so we use **safe margin values** (`390, -390, 290, -290`) to prevent the ball from going *halfway off-screen* before bouncing.

The boundary check derives from:

$$
x + r \geq W_{\text{half}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad d_x = -d_x
$$

where $r = 10$, $W_{\text{half}} = 400 - 10 = 390$.

When `ball.xcor() > 390`:
- The ball has crossed the safe margin.
- We **snap** it back to `x = 390` using `ball.setx(390)` (prevents the ball from "escaping").
- We reverse horizontal velocity: `dx = -dx`.

The same derivation applies symmetrically for left, top, and bottom edges.

---

### Step 5 — Full Consolidated Program (Reference Model Answer)

```python
import turtle

# Setup window
wn = turtle.Screen()
wn.title("Play Ball - KTU PCPOL507 Experiment 2")
wn.bgcolor("white")
wn.setup(width=800, height=600)
wn.tracer(0)

# Create ball
ball = turtle.Turtle()
ball.shape("circle")
ball.color("blue")
ball.penup()
ball.goto(0, 0)

# Velocity
dx = 2
dy = 2

# Main loop
while True:
    wn.update()
    
    ball.setx(ball.xcor() + dx)
    ball.sety(ball.ycor() + dy)
    
    # Right wall
    if ball.xcor() > 390:
        ball.setx(390)
        dx = -dx
    
    # Left wall
    if ball.xcor() < -390:
        ball.setx(-390)
        dx = -dx
    
    # Top wall
    if ball.ycor() > 290:
        ball.sety(290)
        dy = -dy
    
    # Bottom wall
    if ball.ycor() < -290:
        ball.sety(-290)
        dy = -dy
```

### Analytical Matrix — Frame-by-Frame Position Trace (first 8 frames)

| Frame | $x$ | $y$ | $dx$ | $dy$ | Status |
|-------|-----|-----|------|------|--------|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | +2 | +2 | Initial position |
| 1 | 2 | 2 | +2 | +2 | Moving NE |
| 2 | 4 | 4 | +2 | +2 | Moving NE |
| 3 | 6 | 6 | +2 | +2 | Moving NE |
| 4 | 8 | 8 | +2 | +2 | Moving NE |
| 5 | 10 | 10 | +2 | +2 | Moving NE |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 195 | 390 | 290 | +2 | +2 | At top-right safe corner |
| 196 | 388 | 288 | **−2** | **−2** | Bounced, now moving SW |
| 197 | 386 | 286 | −2 | −2 | Moving SW |

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Note:** The number of frames before hitting the right wall = $390 / 2 = 195$ frames. Before hitting the top wall = $290 / 2 = 145$ frames. Therefore, the ball reaches the **top-right corner region first**, and both `dx` and `dy` get reversed in that frame.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — High-Level Program Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Import turtle module] --> B[Initialize Screen wn]

    J --> K[Move ball: y = y + dy]

    N -- Yes --> O[Set x = -390, dx = -dx]

    O --> P
    P -- Yes --> Q[Set y = 290, dy = -dy]
    P -- No --> R{y < -290?}
    Q --> R
    R -- Yes --> S[Set y = -290, dy = -dy]
    R -- No --> H
    S --> H
```

### Diagram 2 — Frame Update Lifecycle (Per-Iteration State Transition)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitialSetup
    InitialSetup --> Running: dx=2, dy=2, pos=(0,0)
    Running --> MovingNE: dx>0 and dy>0
    MovingNE --> HitRightWall: x >= 390
    HitRightWall --> MovingNW: dx = -dx
    MovingNE --> HitTopWall: y >= 290
    HitTopWall --> MovingSE: dy = -dy
    MovingNW --> HitLeftWall: x <= -390
    HitLeftWall --> MovingNE: dx = -dx
    MovingNW --> HitTopWall: y >= 290
    MovingSE --> HitBottomWall: y <= -290
    HitBottomWall --> MovingNE: dy = -dy
    MovingSE --> HitRightWall: x >= 390
    HitRightWall --> MovingSW: dx = -dx
    MovingSW --> HitBottomWall: y <= -290
    HitBottomWall --> MovingNW: dy = -dy
    MovingSW --> HitLeftWall: x <= -390
    HitLeftWall --> MovingSE: dx = -dx
```

### Diagram 3 — Coordinate System Visualization

```
              y = +300 (TOP WALL)
              ┌─────────────────────────┐
              │                         │
              │                         │
              │                         │
   x = -400   │       ● (Ball)          │   x = +400
   LEFT WALL  │       at (0,0)          │   RIGHT WALL
              │                         │
              │                         │
              │                         │
              └─────────────────────────┘
              y = -300 (BOTTOM WALL)
```

The ball's position is always tracked relative to the **center origin (0, 0)**. The `wn.tracer(0)` and `wn.update()` pair gives us **manual frame control** equivalent to a `while True:` game loop in Pygame.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*Explain the role of `wn.tracer(0)` and `wn.update()` in the Play Ball program. Why are both needed together?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `wn.tracer(0)` (1 mark) **turns off automatic screen updates** so that intermediate drawing commands do not flash on the screen individually. This is essential for smooth animation.
- `wn.update()` (1 mark) is called inside the `while True` loop to **manually refresh** the screen once per iteration, producing one complete "frame" of animation.
- Together (1 mark), they give the programmer **explicit control over the animation frame rate** — without `tracer(0)`, the ball would appear to jump erratically; without `update()`, nothing would ever be displayed after the initial draw.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*Write the boundary condition that detects a collision with the right wall of an 800×600 turtle canvas, given the ball radius is 10 px.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The turtle canvas spans from `x = -400` to `x = +400` (width 800) and `y = -300` to `y = +300` (height 600) (1 mark). Since the ball radius is 10 px, the rightmost safe x-coordinate for the ball's *center* is `400 - 10 = 390` (1 mark). The boundary check is therefore:

```python
if ball.xcor() > 390:
    ball.setx(390)
    dx = -dx
```

(1 mark for the complete snippet).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a) — Question A Option (14 marks)** [KTU Model Paper - 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the step-by-step logic for animating a ball that bounces off all four edges of a turtle canvas of size 800 × 600 pixels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The animation logic can be broken into **five sequential phases**:

1. **Initialization Phase:** Import `turtle`, create window with `wn.setup(800, 600)`, create ball with `shape("circle")`, place at `(0, 0)`. *(1 mark)*

2. **Velocity Assignment:** Set `dx = 2` and `dy = 2` to define initial motion direction and speed. *(1 mark)*

3. **Main Loop Entry:** A `while True:` loop is entered to keep the animation running indefinitely. *(1 mark)*

4. **Update + Move:** Inside the loop, `wn.update()` refreshes the screen, and `ball.setx(ball.xcor() + dx)` / `ball.sety(ball.ycor() + dy)` advance the ball. *(1.5 marks)*

5. **Boundary Checks:** Four `if` conditions detect collisions with each wall and negate the corresponding velocity component. *(2.5 marks)*

Refer to **Diagram 1 in Section 4** for the flowchart.

---

**(b)** Modify the above program so that the ball changes color to red whenever it hits a wall. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```python
import turtle

wn = turtle.Screen()
wn.title("Play Ball with Color Change")
wn.bgcolor("white")
wn.setup(width=800, height=600)
wn.tracer(0)

ball = turtle.Turtle()
ball.shape("circle")
ball.color("blue")
ball.penup()
ball.goto(0, 0)

dx = 2
dy = 2

def change_color():
    ball.color("red")

while True:
    wn.update()
    ball.setx(ball.xcor() + dx)
    ball.sety(ball.ycor() + dy)
    
    if ball.xcor() > 390:
        ball.setx(390)
        dx = -dx
        change_color()
    
    if ball.xcor() < -390:
        ball.setx(-390)
        dx = -dx
        change_color()
    
    if ball.ycor() > 290:
        ball.sety(290)
        dy = -dy
        change_color()
    
    if ball.ycor() < -290:
        ball.sety(-290)
        dy = -dy
        change_color()
```

**Explanation / Mark Split:**
- **(2 marks)** Creating a helper function `change_color()` or invoking `ball.color("red")` directly inside each `if` block.
- **(2 marks)** Calling the color change inside **each** of the four boundary `if` blocks.
- **(2 marks)** Correct syntax for `ball.color("red")` and ensuring the function is invoked after `dx = -dx` or `dy = -dy`.
- **(1 mark)** Proper indentation and overall program structure.

---

**Q3(b) — Question B Option (14 marks)** [KTU Model Paper - 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the difference between `ball.goto(x, y)`, `ball.setx(x)`, and `ball.sety(y)` with examples. When would you prefer each? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Function | Purpose | Example | Use Case |
|----------|---------|---------|----------|
| `ball.goto(x, y)` | Moves ball to absolute (x, y) coordinate in one call | `ball.goto(100, 50)` | Initial placement, teleporting the ball |
| `ball.setx(x)` | Updates only the x-coordinate, keeps y unchanged | `ball.setx(ball.xcor() + dx)` | Incremental horizontal motion |
| `ball.sety(y)` | Updates only the y-coordinate, keeps x unchanged | `ball.sety(ball.ycor() + dy)` | Incremental vertical motion |

- **(2 marks)** Correct definitions and example for `goto`.
- **(2 marks)** Correct definitions and example for `setx`.
- **(2 marks)** Correct definitions and example for `sety`.
- **(1 mark)** Justification: We use `setx`/`sety` in animation because they are **faster and more readable** than `goto(xcor() + dx, ycor())` for incremental motion.

---

**(b)** Write a complete Python program using turtle graphics to animate a ball that moves diagonally with velocity `(3, -2)` and bounces off all four edges of a 500 × 400 canvas. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```python
import turtle

wn = turtle.Screen()
wn.title("Play Ball - Custom Velocity")
wn.bgcolor("lightyellow")
wn.setup(width=500, height=400)
wn.tracer(0)

ball = turtle.Turtle()
ball.shape("circle")
ball.color("green")
ball.penup()
ball.goto(0, 0)

# Custom velocity as per question
dx = 3
dy = -2

while True:
    wn.update()
    ball.setx(ball.xcor() + dx)
    ball.sety(ball.ycor() + dy)
    
    # Right wall (x = 250 - 10 = 240)
    if ball.xcor() > 240:
        ball.setx(240)
        dx = -dx
    
    # Left wall (x = -240)
    if ball.xcor() < -240:
        ball.setx(-240)
        dx = -dx
    
    # Top wall (y = 200 - 10 = 190)
    if ball.ycor() > 190:
        ball.sety(190)
        dy = -dy
    
    # Bottom wall (y = -190)
    if ball.ycor() < -190:
        ball.sety(-190)
        dy = -dy
```

**Mark Split:**
- **(2 marks)** Correct canvas sizing (500 × 400) and safe boundary margins (`±240`, `±190`).
- **(2 marks)** Correct velocity initialization (`dx = 3`, `dy = -2`).
- **(2 marks)** Correct boundary `if` logic with `dx = -dx` and `dy = -dy`.
- **(1 mark)** `wn.tracer(0)` and `wn.update()` correctly placed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting `wn.tracer(0)`:** Without it, the ball will appear to *flicker* or move erratically. Examiners will deduct **1 mark**.
> 2. **Using wrong safe margin values:** Students often use `±400` and `±300` instead of `±390` and `±290` (ball radius 10 px). This causes the ball to **half-exit the canvas** before bouncing — **1.5 marks deducted**.
> 3. **Not snapping the ball back** (`ball.setx(390)`) before negating velocity: This makes the ball "stick" outside the wall for one frame — **1 mark deducted**.
> 4. **Confusing `goto` with `setx`/`sety`:** Using `goto(xcor()+dx, ycor()+dy)` works but is **less readable**; partial deduction of 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Off-by-one errors in boundary checks:** Using `>` vs `>=` matters when the ball moves at high speed. Default to `>` for safety.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎯 The **Play Ball** experiment is fundamentally a **loop + boundary-check** problem, not a graphics problem.
- 🎯 The two magic lines are `wn.tracer(0)` and `wn.update()` — together they give **manual frame control**.
- 🎯 Always use **safe margin boundaries** like `±390` and `±290` (not the raw canvas dimensions) to account for ball radius.
- 🎯 The bounce formula is universal: **negate the velocity component perpendicular to the wall hit**.
- 🎯 `dx = -dx` is a *self-referential* assignment in Python — it reads the old `dx`, negates it, and stores the new value back.
- 🎯 The ball's position update equations are:
  $$x_{\text{new}} = x_{\text{old}} + d_x, \quad y_{\text{new}} = y_{\text{old}} + d_y$$
- 🎯 The turtle canvas coordinate system is **centered at (0,0)** — unlike Pygame which uses top-left as origin.
- 🎯 `penup()` is essential to prevent the ball from drawing a *trail line* as it moves.
- 🎯 Common extensions (frequently asked in viva): **color change on bounce**, **speed increase on bounce**, **multiple balls**, **keyboard-controlled paddles (Pong-style)**.
- 🎯 The default canvas size in KTU experiments is **800 × 600**, but the question may specify custom sizes — always recompute safe margins.
- 🎯 At **2 px/frame**, hitting the right wall (`x = 390`) takes $390 / 2 = 195$ frames. Knowing this helps in viva derivation questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid Tyre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOL507 - PCPOL507 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Solid Tyre # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Solid Tyres ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Solid Tyre** (also called a *solid industrial tyre*, *cushion tyre*, or *press-on band*) is a homogeneous, non-pneumatic, elastomeric load-bearing component designed for industrial, material-handling, and slow-speed heavy-duty applications. Unlike pneumatic (air-filled) tyres, solid tyres are manufactured as a single, continuous mass of vulcanized rubber compound — typically bonded to a metallic base ring or hub — eliminating the requirement for air pressure to support the load. According to **ISO 3739-1:2017** and relevant **BIS standards for industrial solid tyres**, the product is defined as a *non-inflatable, unitary elastomeric wheel assembly* used on forklifts, skid-steer loaders, platform trucks, tow tractors, mining dumpers, airport ground support equipment, and steel mill transfer cars. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCPOL507 – Module Experiment 2):** > Solid tyres represent one of the largest commercial applications of *vulcanized natural rubber (NR)*, *styrene–butadiene rubber (SBR)*, and *polybutadiene rubber (BR)* blends. The study covers compound formulation, manufacturing routes (compression moulding, injection moulding, continuous extrusion–vulcanization), physical testing, and service-life prediction. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a **sponge cake versus a loaf of bread**. The sponge cake (pneumatic tyre) is light, airy, and cushioned by the trapped air bubbles. The loaf of bread (solid tyre) is dense, compact, and uniformly solid throughout. Just as a loaf of bread can be stacked, dropped, and roughly handled without losing its shape, a solid tyre is built to survive **puncture-free operation in environments strewn with nails, glass, metal scrap, and molten steel splash**. In a warehouse where forklifts operate 16–24 hours a day over rough concrete littered with debris, a pneumatic tyre would suffer constant flats. The solid tyre simply rolls over the hazard — a true **"fit-and-forget" industrial solution**. ## 1.3 Core Composition Snapshot | Component | Typical Material | Mass Fraction (w/w) | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **Elastomer matrix** | NR / SBR / BR blend | **50–65 %** | Resilience, elasticity | | **Reinforcing filler** | Carbon black (N330, N550, N660) | **20–35 %** | Tensile strength, abrasion resistance | | **Plasticizer / process oil** | Aromatic / paraffinic oil | **3–8 %** | Processability, hardness tuning | | **Curing system** | Sulphur + accelerators (CBS, TBBS) | **1.5–3 %** | Crosslink network | | **Activator** | ZnO + stearic acid | **2–5 %** | Cure activation | | **Antidegradants** | TMQ, 6-PPD, waxes | **1–2 %** | Ozone / oxidative aging | | **Bonding agent** | Brass-coated wire + cobalt salts | **<1 %** | Rubber-to-metal adhesion | > [!NOTE] > **Standard reference hardness range for industrial solid tyres: 65 – 95 Shore A** (or equivalently **60 – 90 IRHD** — International Rubber Hardness Degrees). The most common grade is **70 ± 5 Shore A** for general forklift duty. ## 1.4 Key Industrial Metrics at a Glance - **Load capacity:** Up to **~12,000 kg per wheel** (heavy mining variants) - **Service speed:** Typically **≤ 25 km/h** (some airport units up to **40 km/h**) - **Operating temperature:** **−30 °C to +80 °C** (continuous), with peaks to **+100 °C** in steel-mill service - **Design life:** **2,000 – 8,000 operating hours** depending on duty cycle - **Specific gravity:** **1.10 – 1.25 g/cm³** (compound-dependent) - **Rebound resilience:** **30 – 60 %** at room temperature (lower than pneumatic tyres by design, to maximize abrasion resistance) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics Behind Solid Tyre Performance The performance envelope of a solid tyre is governed by **four inter-locked physical phenomena**: 1. **Hyperelastic deformation** of the rubber matrix under static and dynamic load. 2. **Hysteretic energy dissipation** (heat build-up) during rolling. 3. **Viscoelastic creep and compression set** under sustained dead-load. 4. **Adhesive bond durability** at the rubber–metal (or rubber–cord) interface. ### 2.1.1 Hyperelastic Load Support (Cylinder-on-Flat Contact) For a solid cylindrical wheel pressed against a rigid floor under vertical load $W$, the classical **Hertz contact theory** must be modified because rubber is nearly *incompressible* (Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.499$). The contact patch half-width $a$ for a cylinder of radius $R$, effective modulus $E^*$, and load per unit length $w$ (N/m) is: $$a = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 w R}{\pi E^{*}}}$$ The **effective modulus** combines the rubber and floor stiffnesses: $$\dfrac{1}{E^{*}} = \dfrac{1 - \nu_{r}^{2}}{E_{r}} + \dfrac{1 - \nu_{f}^{2}}{E_{f}}$$ For rubber on steel, $E_f \gg E_r$, so the floor term becomes negligible. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this matter for KTU problems?** A harder compound (higher $E_r$) gives a *smaller* contact patch, *higher* contact pressure, and *lower* rolling resistance — but *poorer* cushioning and *higher* transmitted vibration to the operator cabin. This is the central **hardness–comfort trade-off** in solid-tyre design. ### 2.1.2 Heat Build-Up (Hysteresis) When a solid tyre rolls, every element of rubber in the contact patch undergoes a **cyclic stress–strain loop**. The area inside that loop represents **hysteretic energy loss per unit volume per cycle**, $\Delta W$, which appears as heat. $$\Delta W = \pi \cdot E'' \cdot \varepsilon_{0}^{2}$$ where: - $E''$ = loss modulus (Pa) - $\varepsilon_0$ = peak strain amplitude in the contact patch The **tan delta (loss tangent)** quantifies this dissipation: $$\tan \delta = \dfrac{E''}{E'}$$ For solid tyres, compound design deliberately targets **$\tan \delta \approx 0.10$ – $0.20$ at 25 °C, 10 Hz** — high enough for traction, low enough to limit thermal runaway. ### 2.1.3 Compression Set and Permanent Deformation After prolonged static load, the tyre develops a **flat spot** due to viscoelastic relaxation and permanent crosslink rearrangement. This is quantified by **Compression Set (CS)** per **ASTM D395 Method B**: $$CS \; (\%) = \dfrac{h_{0} - h_{2}}{h_{0} - h_{s}} \times 100$$ where: - $h_0$ = original specimen height (mm) - $h_s$ = spacer height (mm) - $h_2$ = recovered height after 30 min relaxation (mm) **Target for solid-tyre compounds:** $CS \le 25$ % (after 22 h at 70 °C, 25 % strain). ### 2.1.4 Rubber–Metal Bond Strength The wheel is held to the hub by either: - **(a) Bonded construction** — rubber is vulcanized directly onto a steel ring during moulding. Bond strength measured by **ASTM D429** (peel or pull-out): typically **$\sigma_b \ge 6$ MPa** for quality solid tyres. - **(b) Mechanical lock** — rubber compounds with embedded metal plates that key into the hub via tapered or bolted fitments. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Standard / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Effective modulus (rubber–floor) | $\dfrac{1}{E^{*}} = \dfrac{1 - \nu_{r}^{2}}{E_{r}} + \dfrac{1 - \nu_{f}^{2}}{E_{f}}$ | Pa | | 2 | Hertzian half-contact width (cylinder) | $a = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 w R}{\pi E^{*}}}$ | m | | 3 | Hysteretic energy loss per cycle | $\Delta W = \pi E'' \varepsilon_{0}^{2}$ | J/m³ | | 4 | Loss tangent | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{E''}{E'}$ | dimensionless | | 5 | Compression set | $CS = \dfrac{h_{0} - h_{2}}{h_{0} - h_{s}} \times 100$ | % | | 6 | Tensile strength | $\sigma_{UTS} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_{0}}$ | MPa | | 7 | Elongation at break | $\varepsilon_{b} = \dfrac{L_{b} - L_{0}}{L_{0}} \times 100$ | % | | 8 | Shore A hardness → IRHD correlation | $IRHD \approx ShoreA \pm 2$ (mid-range) | — | | 9 | Rebound resilience | $R = \dfrac{h_{rebound}}{h_{drop}} \times 100$ | % | | 10 | Abrasion loss (DIN) | $V_{loss} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho}$ | mm³ | | 11 | Specific gravity | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{compound}}{\rho_{water}}$ | — | | 12 | Cure rate index | $CRI = \dfrac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s2}}$ | min⁻¹ | | 13 | Crosslink density (Flory–Rehner) | $\nu = \dfrac{-\ln(1 - v_{2r}) + v_{2r} + \chi v_{2r}^{2}}{V_{1} (v_{2r}^{1/3} - \tfrac{v_{2r}}{2})}$ | mol/m³ | | 14 | Heat build-up (Goodrich flexometer) | $\Delta T = f(\text{load}, \text{stroke}, \text{time})$ | °C | | 15 | Load capacity (empirical) | $W_{max} = k \cdot D \cdot B \cdot p$ | N | > [!NOTE] > **Symbols used above:** $w$ = load per unit length, $R$ = wheel radius, $E$ = Young's modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $E'$ = storage modulus, $E''$ = loss modulus, $D$ = wheel diameter, $B$ = wheel width, $p$ = contact pressure, $V_1$ = molar volume of swelling solvent, $v_{2r}$ = equilibrium polymer volume fraction in swollen network, $\chi$ = Flory–Huggins interaction parameter. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Solid tyres are the **unsung workhorses** of global logistics. A single large container terminal may deploy **5,000 – 10,000 solid-tyre-fitted wheels** on straddle carriers and rubber-tyred gantry (RTG) cranes. In steel plants, **mill-bound solid tyres** carry slabs of red-hot steel at 1,200 °C ambient heat. Their **zero-failure reliability** translates directly into **production uptime** — a flat pneumatic tyre in a 24×7 operation can cost $15,000–$50,000 per hour in lost throughput. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Contact Patch Geometry for a Solid Cylindrical Wheel ### Given - Wheel radius $R$ = **0.250 m** (typical 18-inch industrial wheel) - Vertical load $W$ = **5,000 N** (≈ 510 kg forklift wheel load) - Wheel width $B$ = **0.100 m** - Rubber Young's modulus $E_r$ = **12 MPa** (70 Shore A, NR-rich compound) - Rubber Poisson's ratio $\nu_r$ = **0.49** - Steel floor: $E_f \to \infty$, $\nu_f = 0.30$ (negligible compliance) ### Step 1 — Load per unit length The total load is distributed across the wheel width: $$w = \dfrac{W}{B} = \dfrac{5{,}000}{0.100} = 50{,}000 \; \text{N/m}$$ ### Step 2 — Effective contact modulus Because the steel floor is essentially rigid, only the rubber term survives: $$\dfrac{1}{E^{*}} \approx \dfrac{1 - \nu_{r}^{2}}{E_{r}} = \dfrac{1 - (0.49)^{2}}{12 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$\dfrac{1}{E^{*}} = \dfrac{1 - 0.2401}{12 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{0.7599}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 6.333 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{Pa}^{-1}$$ $$E^{*} = \dfrac{1}{6.333 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.579 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa} = 15.79 \; \text{MPa}$$ ### Step 3 — Hertzian half-contact width Substituting into the cylinder Hertz equation: $$a = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 w R}{\pi E^{*}}}$$ $$a = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.250}{\pi \times 1.579 \times 10^{7}}}$$ $$a = \sqrt{\dfrac{50{,}000}{4.961 \times 10^{7}}} = \sqrt{1.0079 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.175 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m}$$ $$\boxed{a \approx 31.75 \; \text{mm}}$$ The full contact patch length is $2a = 63.5$ mm. ### Step 4 — Maximum contact pressure For Hertzian line contact, the peak contact pressure at the centreline is: $$p_{0} = \dfrac{2 w}{\pi a} = \dfrac{2 \times 50{,}000}{\pi \times 0.03175} = 1.002 \times 10^{6} \; \text{Pa}$$ $$\boxed{p_{0} \approx 1.00 \; \text{MPa} \; (10 \; \text{bar})}$$ ### Step 5 — Interpretation A peak contact pressure of ~1 MPa on a 70 Shore A rubber is **comfortably below** the ultimate compressive strength of the compound (~25 MPa), yielding a **safety factor of ~25** against compressive crushing. However, this is the *static* value; under dynamic rolling, **heat build-up** is the actual life-limiting factor, not instantaneous crushing. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Hysteretic Heat Build-Up Rate ### Given - Loss modulus $E''$ = **1.5 MPa** at 10 Hz, 25 °C - Peak cyclic strain in contact patch $\varepsilon_0$ = **0.08** (8 %) - Cycle frequency $f$ = **10 Hz** (corresponds to ~25 km/h for a 250 mm wheel) - Rubber density $\rho$ = **1,150 kg/m³** - Specific heat $c_p$ = **1,700 J/(kg·K)** ### Step 1 — Energy dissipated per cycle per unit volume $$\Delta W = \pi E'' \varepsilon_{0}^{2} = \pi \times 1.5 \times 10^{6} \times (0.08)^{2}$$ $$\Delta W = \pi \times 1.5 \times 10^{6} \times 6.4 \times 10^{-3} = 3.016 \times 10^{4} \; \text{J/m}^{3}$$ ### Step 2 — Power dissipated per unit volume $$P_v = \Delta W \cdot f = 3.016 \times 10^{4} \times 10 = 3.016 \times 10^{5} \; \text{W/m}^{3}$$ ### Step 3 — Adiabatic temperature rise rate In the absence of conduction (worst-case adiabatic limit): $$\dfrac{dT}{dt}\bigg|_{ad} = \dfrac{P_v}{\rho \, c_p} = \dfrac{3.016 \times 10^{5}}{1{,}150 \times 1{,}700}$$ $$\dfrac{dT}{dt}\bigg|_{ad} = \dfrac{3.016 \times 10^{5}}{1.955 \times 10^{6}} = 0.154 \; \text{K/s}$$ ### Step 4 — Time to reach 70 °C critical limit Starting from $T_0 = 25$ °C, the time to reach the conventional thermal failure limit of 95 °C in continuous service (ΔT = 70 K): $$t_{crit} = \dfrac{70}{0.154} = 454 \; \text{s} \approx 7.6 \; \text{min}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** In *adiabatic* (no cooling) conditions, the rubber bulk would reach 95 °C in under 8 minutes — clearly a thermal failure. In reality, convection and conduction at the contact patch and rim extract ~85–95 % of this heat, extending the safe operating envelope to **2,000–8,000 hours**. This is why **compound designers obsess over low $\tan \delta$ at the operating frequency** while accepting a moderate value for traction. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: Compression Set Calculation A solid-tyre rubber specimen is aged under **25 % strain** at **70 °C for 22 hours**. **Measurements:** - Original height $h_0$ = **12.50 mm** - Spacer height $h_s$ = **9.38 mm** (imposes 25 % deflection) - Recovered height after 30 min $h_2$ = **11.00 mm** ### Solution $$CS = \dfrac{h_{0} - h_{2}}{h_{0} - h_{s}} \times 100 = \dfrac{12.50 - 11.00}{12.50 - 9.38} \times 100$$ $$CS = \dfrac{1.50}{3.12} \times 100 = 48.1 \; \%$$ **Verdict:** A compression set of 48 % is **excessive** for a solid-tyre service compound (target ≤ 25 %). The likely cause is **insufficient crosslink density** or **overuse of plasticizer**, which both accelerate viscoelastic flow. Compound reformulation would call for: - Increasing sulphur from 2.0 → 2.5 phr, - Adding **0.5 phr TMTD** as a secondary accelerator, - Reducing aromatic oil from 6 → 4 phr. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Property → Test Method → Target | Property | KTU/Standard Test Method | Compound Target (70 Shore A) | Failure Consequence | |---|---|---|---| | Hardness | ASTM D2240 (Shore A) / ISO 48 (IRHD) | **70 ± 5 Shore A** | Premature wear / poor cushioning | | Tensile strength | ASTM D412 (Die C) | **≥ 20 MPa** | Tread chunking | | Elongation at break | ASTM D412 | **≥ 450 %** | Cracking at edges | | 100 % Modulus | ASTM D412 | **3.0 – 4.5 MPa** | Bottoming out | | Compression set (22 h, 70 °C) | ASTM D395 Method B | **≤ 25 %** | Flat-spotting under load | | Rebound resilience | ASTM D7121 / ISO 4662 | **40 – 55 %** | Heat runaway if too low | | DIN abrasion loss | ISO 4649 | **≤ 120 mm³** | Short tread life | | Tear strength | ASTM D624 (Die B) | **≥ 70 kN/m** | Cut-growth failure | | Specific gravity | ASTM D297 | **1.10 – 1.20** | Process / cost control | | Heat build-up | ASTM D623 Method A | **≤ 30 °C ΔT** | Thermal blowout | | Rubber-metal bond | ASTM D429 Method A | **≥ 6 MPa** | Tyre slip on hub | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification Flow of Industrial Solid Tyres ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Solid Tyres]:::root --> B[Press-on Band] A --> C[Resilient Solid Tyre] A --> D[Bonded Solid Tyre] A --> E[Cushion Tyre] B --> B1[Rubber band forced onto steel rim] B --> B2[Light to medium duty] B --> B3[No adhesive bond] C --> C1[Multi-zone rubber hardness] C --> C2[Soft tread on hard base] C --> C3[Forged-in steel base ring] D --> D1[Vulcanized to steel ring] D --> D2[High load capacity] D --> D3[Mining steel mill duty] E --> E1[Wide base low profile] E --> E2[Skid steer loaders] E --> E3[Low speed high load] classDef root fill:#1f2a44,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0d1a33,stroke-width:2px; [KTU University Exam – Model Question] **Differentiate between a pneumatic tyre and a solid tyre. State two industrial applications where solid tyres are mandatory.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Pneumatic Tyre | Solid Tyre | |---|---|---| | Load support medium | Compressed air | Vulcanized rubber mass | | Puncture risk | High | Negligible | | Cushioning | Excellent | Moderate to poor | | Speed suitability | High (≥ 50 km/h) | Low (≤ 25–40 km/h) | | Maintenance | Inflation checks, repairs | Almost zero | | Initial cost | Lower | Higher (3–5 ×) | | Service life | 10,000 – 50,000 km | 2,000 – 8,000 h | **Mandatory applications (any two):** 1. **Electric forklifts in warehouses** — zero puncture downtime, low rolling resistance on smooth floors. 2. **Steel mill slag pot carriers** — operate near molten metal; pneumatic tyres would explode. 3. **Underground mining loaders** — sharp rock fragments rule out inflatable tyres. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question] **List the four principal failure modes observed in industrial solid tyres during service.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Abrasion wear** of the tread (DIN abrasion loss exceeding 200 mm³ indicates compounding issue). 2. **Heat blowout** — internal temperature exceeding ~110 °C causes reversion or blow-out blistering. 3. **Compression set / flat-spotting** — permanent deformation under static or cyclic load. 4. **Rubber–metal bond failure** — peel, slip, or separation at the hub interface (debonding). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model] **(a)** With the help of a labelled cross-section, describe the **constructional features of a multi-zone resilient solid industrial tyre**. Highlight the role of each rubber layer and the steel base ring. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A solid rubber cylindrical wheel of **radius 280 mm** and **width 120 mm** carries a vertical load of **7,000 N**. The rubber compound has Young's modulus **15 MPa** and Poisson's ratio **0.49**. The floor is rigid steel.
