Calculate: - (i) the load per unit length, - (ii) the effective contact modulus, - (iii) the half-contact width, - (iv) the peak contact pressure. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A **(a) Constructional Features — Multi-Zone Resilient Solid Tyre (7 marks)** A modern multi-zone resilient solid tyre consists of **three distinct rubber layers** co-vulcanized with a forged steel base ring, providing a graduated hardness profile from the outer rolling surface to the inner mounting face. | Zone | Hardness (Shore A) | Function | |---|---|---| | **Outer Tread** | 60 – 65 | High resilience, abrasion resistance, traction | | **Mid Cushion** | 50 – 55 | Vibration absorption, load homogenization | | **Base Compound** | 85 – 90 | Stiff load-bearing core, bondability to steel | | **Steel Ring** | — | Forged medium-carbon steel; mechanical & chemical bond | The **steel base ring** is grit-blasted, degreased, coated with a **brass or cobalt-nickel adhesive system**, then overmoulded with the rubber compound. During vulcanization, **sulphur from the rubber diffuses into the brass coating**, forming a **Cu_xS transition layer** (~5 nm thick) that gives the legendary durability of vulcanized rubber-to-brass bonds. This multi-zone design gives the tyre **50 – 70 %** of the cushioning of a pneumatic tyre while maintaining **puncture-proof reliability**. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** - $R$ = 0.280 m, $B$ = 0.120 m, $W$ = 7,000 N - $E_r$ = 15 MPa, $\nu_r$ = 0.49 - $E_f \to \infty$ **Step (i) — Load per unit length:** $$w = \dfrac{W}{B} = \dfrac{7{,}000}{0.120} = 58{,}333.33 \; \text{N/m}$$ **Step (ii) — Effective modulus:** $$\dfrac{1}{E^{*}} = \dfrac{1 - (0.49)^{2}}{15 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{1 - 0.2401}{15 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{0.7599}{15 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$E^{*} = \dfrac{15 \times 10^{6}}{0.7599} = 1.974 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa} = 19.74 \; \text{MPa}$$ **Step (iii) — Half-contact width:** $$a = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 w R}{\pi E^{*}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 58{,}333.33 \times 0.280}{\pi \times 1.974 \times 10^{7}}}$$ $$a = \sqrt{\dfrac{65{,}333.33}{6.201 \times 10^{7}}} = \sqrt{1.0534 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.246 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m}$$ $$\boxed{a \approx 32.46 \; \text{mm}}$$ **Step (iv) — Peak contact pressure:** $$p_{0} = \dfrac{2 w}{\pi a} = \dfrac{2 \times 58{,}333.33}{\pi \times 0.03246}$$ $$p_{0} = \dfrac{116{,}666.67}{0.10197} = 1.144 \times 10^{6} \; \text{Pa}$$ $$\boxed{p_{0} \approx 1.14 \; \text{MPa}}$$ **Conclusion:** The peak Hertzian contact pressure of 1.14 MPa is comfortably within the safe compressive load-bearing range of the 70 Shore A rubber tread compound (ultimate compressive strength ~25 MPa; safety factor ≈ 22). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model] **(a)** Discuss the **compound formulation strategy** for an industrial solid tyre. Explain the role of the elastomer blend, the reinforcing filler system, the plasticizer, and the cure system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A rubber specimen from a solid-tyre compound is tested for **compression set** per **ASTM D395 Method B**. The original height is **12.00 mm**, the spacer height is **9.00 mm**, and the recovered height (after 30 min) is **10.40 mm**. Calculate the compression set and comment on its suitability for service. Suggest two compound modifications if it is unsatisfactory. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B **(a) Compound Formulation Strategy (7 marks)** A typical solid-tyre compound is engineered around a **balanced elastomer blend**, a **high-structure carbon black**, a **process oil**, and an **efficient sulphur cure system**. The recipe below represents a representative 70 Shore A industrial compound (values in **parts per hundred rubber, phr**): | Ingredient | phr | Role | |---|---|---| | Natural Rubber (RSS-3 / SMR-10) | 70 | Resilience, green tack, tear strength | | Polybutadiene Rubber (BR) | 30 | Improves abrasion resistance, lowers heat build-up | | Carbon Black N330 (HAF) | 50 | Reinforcement, tensile, wear | | Aromatic Process Oil | 6 | Plasticizer, processing aid | | Zinc Oxide | 4 | Cure activator | | Stearic Acid | 2 | Cure activator / dispersant | | CBS (N-cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazole sulphenamide) | 1.2 | Primary accelerator (delayed action) | | Sulphur | 2.2 | Crosslinker | | 6-PPD (antiozonant) | 1.5 | Ozone/oxidation protection | | TMQ (polymerized 2,2,4-trimethyl-1,2-dihydroquinoline) | 1.0 | Heat aging protection | | Cobalt Naphthenate (bonding promoter) | 0.5 | Rubber–brass adhesion | **Design rationale:** - **NR + BR blend (70:30):** Pure NR gives high resilience but is prone to cut growth; BR contributes a low glass-transition temperature ($T_g \approx -90$ °C) and low rolling resistance, improving wear and reducing heat build-up. - **N330 carbon black at 50 phr:** Provides the classic balance of tensile strength, abrasion resistance, and processability. Lower-structure blacks (N550, N660) are used in softer, more resilient variants. - **Aromatic oil (6 phr):** Reduces compound viscosity, aids filler dispersion, and slightly lowers hardness. Excess oil causes high compression set and migration. - **Sulphur + CBS (semi-EV system):** Delayed-action sulphenamide accelerators provide scorch safety during processing while delivering a fast, complete cure in the press. The **semi-EV ratio** (S:accelerator ≈ 1.8) gives predominantly polysulphidic crosslinks — desirable for tear strength and dynamic flex life. - **Antidegradants:** Critical for warehouse, outdoor, and steel-mill service where ozone and thermal aging are aggressive. **(b) Compression Set Calculation (7 marks)** **Given:** - $h_0$ = 12.00 mm - $h_s$ = 9.00 mm - $h_2$ = 10.40 mm **Applying the ASTM D395 Formula B equation:** $$CS = \dfrac{h_{0} - h_{2}}{h_{0} - h_{s}} \times 100$$ $$CS = \dfrac{12.00 - 10.40}{12.00 - 9.00} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.60}{3.00} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{CS = 53.33 \; \%}$$ **Comment on suitability:** A compression set of **53.3 %** is **highly unsatisfactory** for a solid-tyre service compound. The industry target is **CS ≤ 25 %** (after 22 h at 70 °C, 25 % strain). The present compound would develop excessive **flat-spotting** under dead-load, leading to ride-quality degradation, vibration, and accelerated bond-line fatigue. **Probable root causes and compound modifications:** 1. **Insufficient crosslink density** — Increase sulphur from ~2.2 → 2.5 phr **and** add 0.3 – 0.5 phr **TMTD (tetramethylthiuram disulphide)** as a secondary accelerator. This raises the effective crosslink density without shortening scorch time excessively. 2. **Excess plasticizer migration** — Reduce aromatic oil from 6 → 3 – 4 phr, and substitute with a **lower-volatility, higher-viscosity paraffinic oil** to minimize post-cure blooming and creep. 3. *(Optional third measure)* Partially replace NR with a higher-elastic-recovery grade, or add **0.5 – 1.0 phr of peroxide co-agent (e.g., HVA-2)** to introduce supplementary **C–C crosslinks** that are thermally stable and resistant to permanent set. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Box** > 1. **Compression set formula** — Students commonly swap numerator and denominator or omit the spacer height. KTU examiners deduct **2 full marks** for incorrect denominator usage. > 2. **Hertz contact equation** — Use the **cylinder-on-flat** formula, NOT the **sphere-on-flat** formula. The factor is **4wR/πE\*\*** for a cylinder, not **(4WR/3πE\*\*)⁽¹/³⁾** as for a sphere. > 3. **Forgetting the spacer** in the compression set equation is the most common error — it is a **3-mark** mistake in KTU papers. > 4. **Units** — Always convert mm → m for contact-patch calculations; otherwise answers are off by factors of 10⁶. KTU examiners are unforgiving on unit consistency. > 5. **Direction of inequality** in design targets — a student writing "hardness ≤ 70 Shore A" instead of "hardness 70 ± 5 Shore A" loses 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Solid Tyre (PCPOL507, Module Experiment 2)** - [ ] Solid tyre = **homogeneous, non-pneumatic, vulcanized elastomeric wheel** for industrial use. - [ ] Standard industrial hardness: **70 ± 5 Shore A** (≈ 65 – 75 IRHD). - [ ] Common elastomer system: **NR (70) + BR (30)** — gives the best resilience–abrasion balance. - [ ] Reinforcing filler: **Carbon black N330, 45 – 55 phr**. - [ ] Cure system: **Semi-EV sulphur + CBS accelerator** for scorch-safe, fast cure. - [ ] Bonding to steel ring: **brass plating + cobalt salt adhesion promoter**; bond strength ≥ **6 MPa** (ASTM D429). - [ ] Hertzian half-contact width for cylinder: $a = \sqrt{4wR/\pi E^{*}}$. - [ ] Effective modulus: $1/E^{*} = (1-\nu_r^2)/E_r + (1-\nu_f^2)/E_f$. - [ ] Peak Hertzian pressure: $p_0 = 2w/(\pi a)$. - [ ] Hysteretic loss: $\Delta W = \pi E'' \varepsilon_0^2$ — drives heat build-up. - [ ] Loss tangent target: **$\tan \delta \approx 0.10$ – 0.20** at 10 Hz, 25 °C. - [ ] Compression set target: **CS ≤ 25 %** at 22 h / 70 °C / 25 % strain. - [ ] Four failure modes: **abrasion, heat blow-out, compression set, rubber–metal debonding**. - [ ] Operating envelope: **≤ 25 km/h**, **−30 °C to +80 °C**, loads up to **~12,000 kg/wheel**. - [ ] Service life: **2,000 – 8,000 h**, depending on duty cycle and ambient. - [ ] Manufacturing route: **compression moulding** (most common) or **injection moulding** (for press-on bands). - [ ] Always cite **ASTM D412, D395, D2240, D429, D623, D624, D297** and **ISO 48, 4649, 4662** in lab answers. - [ ] Moulding temperature: **160 – 180 °C**; cure time: **25 – 45 minutes** depending on section thickness.

topics.title: Sponge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL507 - PCPOL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sponge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sponge Rubber** is a cellular, expanded elastomeric material manufactured by incorporating a chemical **blowing agent** into a rubber compound, which upon thermal decomposition liberates a large volume of gas. This gas is trapped within the viscous, semi-cured rubber matrix, generating a closed-cell or open-cell porous internal architecture. The cured product exhibits a low apparent density, high compressibility, excellent resilience, and superior thermal/acoustic insulation characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment titled *"Preparation of Sponge Rubber"* is a standard Module-II experiment in Polymer Technology / Rubber Technology laboratory courses. It tests the student's understanding of **compounding, vulcanization chemistry, blowing agent kinetics, and cellular structure formation**.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature:

$$\text{Sponge Rubber} = f(\text{Base Polymer} + \text{Blowing Agent} + \text{Vulcanizing System} + \text{Heat})$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bread-Baking Analogy (Real-World Intuition)**
>
> Imagine you are baking a cake. You mix a runny batter (liquid dough), add **baking powder** (the blowing agent), and pour it into a mould. When you place the mould inside a hot oven:
>
> 1. The heat causes the **baking powder to decompose**, releasing **CO₂ gas bubbles**.
> 2. Simultaneously, the batter **sets/rises** (becomes solid) due to baking, trapping the bubbles inside.
> 3. The final product is a soft, porous, spongy cake full of air pockets.
>
> **Sponge rubber is made in EXACTLY the same way**:
> - The **rubber compound** is the batter.
> - The **blowing agent** (commonly azodicarbonamide/ADC) is the baking powder.
> - The **vulcanization system** (sulfur + accelerator) is the oven heat that "sets" the structure.
> - The trapped **N₂/CO₂ gas bubbles** are the CO₂ in the cake.
> - The cured cellular rubber is the finished sponge cake.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | KTU Significance |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| **Cell Size** | **0.1 – 1.0 mm** | Determines cushioning grade |
| **Apparent Density** | **0.1 – 0.6 g/cm³** | Lower density = higher expansion |
| **Expansion Ratio** | **3:1 to 10:1** | Vol. of sponge / Vol. of base rubber |
| **Blowing Agent (ADC) Decomposition Temp** | **~200 °C** | Must match cure onset |
| **Closed-Cell Content** | **> 80% (closed), < 20% (open)** | Affects water absorption |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **apparent density** $\rho_a$ of sponge rubber is governed by:
> $$\rho_a = \frac{m_{\text{rubber}}}{V_{\text{sponge}}} \quad [\text{g/cm}^3]$$
> where $V_{\text{sponge}} \gg V_{\text{rubber}}$ due to the gas-filled voids.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Sponge Rubber

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to differentiate between **Open-Cell** and **Closed-Cell** sponges. Memorize the table below.

| Feature | Closed-Cell Sponge | Open-Cell Sponge |
|---|---|---|
| **Cell Structure** | Each gas pocket is sealed by a polymer membrane | Cells are interconnected; gas can flow |
| **Density** | Higher (0.3 – 0.6 g/cm³) | Lower (0.1 – 0.3 g/cm³) |
| **Water Absorption** | Negligible (floats on water) | High (acts like a true sponge) |
| **Applications** | Gaskets, insulation, marine buoyancy | Mattresses, cushioning, filters |
| **Expansion Ratio** | Moderate (3:1 – 5:1) | High (up to 10:1) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown (Step-by-Step Logic)

The formation of sponge rubber is a **race against time** between two parallel processes. The KTU examiner tests whether the student can articulate this "synchronization" concept.

### Step 1: Compounding Stage
- **Base polymer** (Natural Rubber / NR, or Styrene-Butadiene Rubber / SBR) is masticated on a two-roll mill to reduce its viscosity and improve filler dispersion.
- **Blowing agent** (most commonly **Azodicarbonamide**, ADC) is added and dispersed uniformly throughout the rubber matrix.
- **Activator** (typically **Zinc Oxide, ZnO** + **Stearic Acid**) and **vulcanizing agents** (Sulfur + accelerator like CBS — *N*-Cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazole sulfenamide) are incorporated.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Compounding:** Uniform dispersion of ADC is critical. If the blowing agent is uneven, the sponge will have **uneven cell density** — dense regions and hollow regions, leading to mechanical failure.

### Step 2: Heating Stage — The "Race Against Time"
When the compounded rubber is placed in a heated mould (typically **150 – 180 °C**), **two reactions kick off simultaneously**:

**Reaction A — Gas Generation (Blowing):**
$$\text{Azodicarbonamide (ADC)} \xrightarrow{\Delta \, (\sim 200\,°C)} \text{N}_2 \uparrow + \text{CO} \uparrow + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow + \text{NH}_3 \uparrow + \text{cyanuric acid residue}$$

The gas evolution rate must be matched with the curing rate. This is known as the **"Scorch-Blow Balance"** in rubber technology.

**Reaction B — Crosslinking (Vulcanization):**
$$\text{R–SH + S}_8 \xrightarrow{\text{Accelerator, ZnO, Heat}} \text{R–S}_x\text{–R (crosslinked network)}$$

Sulfur forms **polysulfidic bridges** (-Sₓ-) between adjacent polymer chains, converting the soft, plastic rubber into a tough, elastic 3-D network.

### Step 3: The "Synchronization" Principle
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **vulcanization rate** and the **blowing rate** MUST be synchronized. 
> - If vulcanization is **too fast**: The rubber "sets" before the gas can expand → produces a **dense, non-expanded product** (poor sponge).
> - If blowing is **too fast**: Gas escapes before the rubber sets → produces a **collapsed, porous, weak product** (no dimensional stability).
> - **Ideal case**: The rubber viscosity drops slightly (allowing expansion), THEN sets rapidly, trapping the gas.

### Step 4: Cooling and Demoulding
After the cure cycle (typically 8 – 15 minutes at 160 °C), the sponge is removed from the mould. The internal gas pressure gradually equilibrates with atmospheric pressure (especially for closed-cell types), and the sponge retains its expanded cellular structure permanently.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Important formatting note:** In all formulas below, the vertical bar `|` is intentionally avoided in table cells to preserve markdown table integrity. The notation $\vert$ is used for absolute / modulus values.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_a = \dfrac{m_r}{V_s}$ | Apparent density of sponge | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | Quality control |
| 2 | $E_r = \dfrac{V_s}{V_r}$ | Expansion ratio | Dimensionless | Exam standard |
| 3 | $C = \dfrac{V_{\text{gas}}}{V_{\text{voids}}}$ | Cell closure ratio | Dimensionless | Open vs closed cell |
| 4 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Half-life of blowing agent | seconds | Decomposition kinetics |
| 5 | $V_{\text{gas}} = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$ | Ideal gas law for cell volume | $\text{cm}^3$ | Theoretical yield |
| 6 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Compressive stress on sponge | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | Mechanical testing |
| 7 | $\text{CRR} = \dfrac{h_0 - h_f}{h_0} \times 100\%$ | Compression Recovery Ratio | % | Resilience testing |
| 8 | $\Delta T = T_{\text{decomp}} - T_{\text{activation}}$ | Activity differential (ZnO lowers ADC decomp. temp) | °C | Activator role |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Sponge rubber is **not just a lab curiosity**. It is a high-volume industrial product with critical applications:
> - **Automotive Industry:** Door seals, gaskets, anti-vibration mounts (closed-cell EPDM sponge).
> - **Footwear Industry:** Soles, insoles, sport-shoe midsoles.
> - **Construction:** Expansion joints, acoustic insulation panels, weather-stripping.
> - **Marine:** Buoyancy fenders, life-vest fillings (closed-cell, water-resistant).
> - **Medical:** Orthopedic cushioning, prosthetics liners.
> - **Domestic:** Mattresses, pillows, scrubbing sponges, sealing tapes.

## 2.4 Why Azodicarbonamide (ADC) is the Industry-Standard Blowing Agent

| Property | ADC (Azodicarbonamide) | Sodium Bicarbonate | OBSH (Oxy-bis-benzene sulfonyl hydrazide) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gas Yield (ml/g)** | **220** | 120 | 130 |
| **Decomposition Temp (°C)** | **~200** | ~150 | ~160 |
| **Cell Structure Control** | Excellent | Poor | Good |
| **Toxicity of Residue** | Low (food-grade approved) | None | Moderate |
| **Cost** | Low | Very low | High |
| **KTU Preferred?** | **YES (Most common exam answer)** | Sometimes (for NR latex foam) | Specialty applications |

> [!NOTE]
> The **"ZnO Activation Effect"** is a KTU favourite. Pure ADC decomposes at ~200 °C, but adding **ZnO as an activator** lowers the decomposition temperature to ~150 – 170 °C, perfectly matching the vulcanization window of natural rubber.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Experimental Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Laboratory Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU lab protocol** for the preparation of sponge rubber. Examiners expect the student to write this in a precise, sequential, past-tense/imperative format.

### Stage 1: Material Formulation (Recipe for 100 g batch)

The following **KTU standard formulation** is used. Memorize the role of each ingredient.

| Ingredient | Quantity (phr*) | Function in the Compound |
|---|---|---|
| Natural Rubber (RSS-4 grade) | 100.0 | Base polymer (matrix former) |
| Sulfur | 2.5 | Vulcanizing agent (crosslinker) |
| Zinc Oxide (ZnO) | 5.0 | Activator (lowers ADC decomp. temp) |
| Stearic Acid | 1.5 | Co-activator + processing aid |
| CBS Accelerator | 0.8 | Cure rate accelerator |
| Azodicarbonamide (ADC) | 5.0 | **Blowing agent (gas source)** |
| Paraffin Oil | 5.0 | Plasticizer / softener |
| Antioxidant (e.g., BHT) | 1.0 | Prevents premature ageing |

> *phr = parts per hundred parts of rubber

### Stage 2: Two-Roll Mill Compounding

**Step 1 — Mastication (5 min):**
Pass Natural Rubber through the nip of a clean, warmed (50 °C) two-roll mill for 5 minutes. This reduces the Mooney viscosity and breaks down gel content.

**Step 2 — Stearic Acid addition (2 min):**
Add stearic acid and band it uniformly on the rubber sheet.

**Step 3 — ZnO + Sulfur + CBS addition (3 min):**
Add these powders in a slow, even stream to avoid lumping. Cut and fold the sheet 3–4 times for homogeneous mixing.

**Step 4 — ADC addition (2 min):**
Add azodicarbonamide LAST (to minimize premature decomposition from mill friction heat). Ensure **uniform dispersion** — this is the most critical step for uniform cell size.

**Step 5 — Paraffin Oil + Antioxidant (2 min):**
Add plasticizer and antioxidant. Sheet out at ~2 mm thickness.

### Stage 3: Moulding and Curing

**Step 6 — Mould Loading:**
Cut the compounded sheet into a pre-weighed disc (e.g., 20 g) and place it in a clean, pre-heated **circular mould cavity** of known volume (e.g., 50 mm diameter × 10 mm depth).

**Step 7 — Cure Cycle:**
Close the mould and place in a **hydraulic compression press** pre-heated to **160 ± 5 °C**.

**Step 8 — Application of Pressure (Bumps):**
Apply **initial contact pressure** for 2 minutes. Then "bump" the press (open briefly to release trapped air, then re-close) 2–3 times. This prevents voids in the surface.

**Step 9 — Decompression for Expansion (KEY STEP):**
After ~4 minutes, **partially release the pressure** to ~half the original clamping force. This allows the trapped N₂/CO₂ to **expand** the partially-vulcanized rubber. Hold for 3–5 minutes.

**Step 10 — Final Cure:**
Re-apply full pressure for the remaining cure time (typically total cure = 10–12 minutes at 160 °C, determined by a separate **Monsanto Rheometer** test).

**Step 11 — Demoulding:**
Open the mould and carefully extract the expanded sponge rubber disc. Allow to cool at room temperature for 24 hours before testing.

### Stage 4: Characterization & Testing

**Test 1 — Apparent Density ($\rho_a$):**
Measure mass ($m$) and volume ($V_s$) of the cured sponge. Calculate:
$$\rho_a = \frac{m}{V_s} \quad [\text{g/cm}^3]$$

**Test 2 — Expansion Ratio ($E_r$):**
$$E_r = \frac{V_s}{V_r} = \frac{V_s \cdot \rho_r}{m} \quad \text{where } \rho_r \approx 0.92 \text{ g/cm}^3 \text{ for NR}$$

**Test 3 — Visual Cell Inspection:**
Cut a cross-section and observe under low-power microscope (10×) to count cells per cm² and verify uniform dispersion.

**Test 4 — Water Absorption (Open vs Closed Cell test):**
Immerse in water for 24 hours. **Closed-cell sponge floats** and gains < 5% weight; **open-cell sponge sinks** and gains 30–200% weight.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A sponge rubber disc has mass $m = 18.5$ g. The mould cavity volume was $V_r = 20$ cm³. The expanded sponge volume was measured as $V_s = 78$ cm³. Calculate the **apparent density** and **expansion ratio**. Given base NR density $\rho_r = 0.92$ g/cm³.

**Step 1 — Apparent Density:**
$$\rho_a = \frac{m}{V_s} = \frac{18.5 \text{ g}}{78 \text{ cm}^3}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_a = 0.237 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Density Check (using base rubber mass):**
The volume the rubber would occupy if it were solid is:
$$V_r = \frac{m}{\rho_r} = \frac{18.5}{0.92} = 20.11 \text{ cm}^3$$
This matches the mould cavity volume (~20 cm³) — confirming mass conservation. ✓

**Step 3 — Expansion Ratio:**
$$E_r = \frac{V_s}{V_r} = \frac{78}{20} = 3.9$$

$$\boxed{E_r = 3.9 : 1 \quad (\text{i.e., sponge is } 3.9 \text{ times the volume of base rubber})}$$

**Step 4 — Void Fraction Calculation:**
$$\phi_{\text{voids}} = \frac{V_s - V_r}{V_s} = \frac{78 - 20}{78} = 0.744$$

$$\boxed{\phi_{\text{voids}} = 74.4\%}$$

This means **74.4% of the sponge volume is empty space (gas-filled cells)** — a hallmark of a well-expanded sponge.

## 3.3 Troubleshooting Matrix (KTU Viva Voce Reference)

| Defect Observed | Probable Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| Sponge has dense, un-expanded core | Insufficient blowing agent or cure too fast | Increase ADC to 6–7 phr; lower accelerator |
| Sponge collapsed (shrinks on cooling) | Cure too slow relative to blowing; gas escaped | Increase sulfur + accelerator; reduce ADC slightly |
| Uneven cell size | Poor dispersion of ADC on mill | Improve milling time; add ADC earlier in smaller increments |
| Hard, stiff sponge | Over-cure / excessive sulfur | Reduce cure time by 2 min; check sulfur level |
| Surface cracks or splits | Pressure released too early or uneven mould temp | Maintain pressure until rubber is gelled; pre-heat mould uniformly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Manufacturing Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **entire sponge rubber manufacturing pipeline** — from raw polymer to finished cellular product. This is the standard KTU diagram expected in theory exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Natural Rubber / SBR] --> B[Step 1: Mastication on Two-Roll Mill]
    B --> C[Step 2: Add Stearic Acid and ZnO Activator]
    C --> D[Step 3: Add Sulfur and CBS Accelerator]
    D --> E[Step 4: Add Azodicarbonamide Blowing Agent]
    E --> F[Step 5: Add Plasticizer and Antioxidant]
    F --> G[Step 6: Sheet Out Compound at 2 mm Thickness]
    G --> H[Step 7: Cut and Load into Pre-heated Mould]
    H --> I[Step 8: Close Mould in Compression Press at 160 C]
    I --> J[Step 9: Apply Initial Pressure for 2 min and Bump]
    J --> K[Step 10: Partial Pressure Release for Gas Expansion]
    K --> L[Step 11: Final Full Pressure Cure for 10 min]
    L --> M[Step 12: Open Mould and Demould Sponge Disc]
    M --> N[Step 13: Cool at Room Temperature for 24 h]
    N --> O[Step 14: Quality Testing - Density Expansion Ratio Cell Count]
    O --> P[Finished Sponge Rubber Product - Closed or Open Cell]
```

## 4.2 Synchronization Concept Diagram (Why Both Reactions Must Match)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[BLOWING REACTION]
        direction TB
        B1[ADC Blowing Agent] -->|Heat 160 to 200 C| B2[Decomposition]
        B2 --> B3[Release of N2 and CO2 Gas]
        B3 --> B4[Gas Pressure Inside Viscous Rubber]
    end
    subgraph S2[VULCANIZATION REACTION]
        direction TB
        V1[Sulfur plus CBS plus ZnO] -->|Heat 160 C| V2[Crosslink Formation]
        V2 --> V3[Rubber Viscosity Increases]
        V3 --> V4[Rubber Becomes Elastic Solid]
    end
    B4 --> RACE{RACE BETWEEN BLOW AND CURE}
    V4 --> RACE
    RACE -->|Blow too fast| FAIL1[Collapsed Sponge - Gas Escapes]
    RACE -->|Cure too fast| FAIL2[Dense Un-expanded Sponge]
    RACE -->|Balanced synchronization| WIN[Perfect Cellular Sponge Structure]
```

## 4.3 Cellular Structure Comparison (Open vs Closed Cell)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CLOSED[CLOSED CELL SPONGE]
        direction LR
        C1[Cell A - Sealed] --- C2[Cell B - Sealed]
        C2 --- C3[Cell C - Sealed]
        C3 --- C4[Cell D - Sealed]
        C4 --- C5[Each cell is isolated by polymer wall]
    end
    subgraph OPEN[OPEN CELL SPONGE]
        direction LR
        O1[Cell A - Open] --- O2[Cell B - Open]
        O2 --- O3[Cell C - Open]
        O3 --- O4[Cell D - Open]
        O4 --- O5[Cells interconnected allowing gas and liquid flow]
    end
    CLOSED --> APP1[Application: Buoyancy Gaskets Insulation]
    OPEN --> APP2[Application: Cushioning Mattresses Filters]
```

## 4.4 Equipment Schematic (Two-Roll Mill Used in Compounding)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MILL[Two Roll Compounding Mill]
        direction TB
        R1[Front Roll - Slower 18 rpm] --> NIP[Mill Nip Gap 1 to 2 mm]
        R2[Back Roll - Faster 22 rpm] --> NIP
        NIP --> BAND[Band of Compound Wraps on Front Roll]
        BAND --> CUT[Operator Cuts and Folds Sheet]
        CUT --> BAND
    end
    MILL --> OUT[Homogeneously Mixed Rubber Compound Ready for Moulding]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram-Tip:** When asked to "draw a labelled diagram" in the exam, always include: (1) the two counter-rotating rolls with speed ratio indicated, (2) the **nip** (the wedge-shaped region between rolls), (3) the **bank** (the rolling stock of rubber at the nip), and (4) arrows showing the direction of rotation. This guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term "blowing agent" and explain its role in the preparation of sponge rubber with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **blowing agent** is a chemical compound that, upon thermal decomposition, liberates a large volume of gas (such as N₂, CO₂, or NH₃). This gas is trapped within a polymer matrix to create a cellular, expanded structure.

In sponge rubber preparation, the most widely used blowing agent is **Azodicarbonamide (ADC)**, with the chemical formula $\text{H}_2\text{N–CO–N=N–CO–NH}_2$. When the compounded rubber is heated to ~150–200 °C, ADC decomposes as:

$$\text{ADC} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{N}_2 \uparrow + \text{CO} \uparrow + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow + \text{NH}_3 \uparrow$$

The evolved gas (mainly N₂) creates millions of tiny bubbles inside the viscous, partially-cured rubber. Simultaneously, vulcanization sets the rubber, **trapping the gas permanently** and producing a lightweight, cellular sponge structure. The decomposition is activated by **Zinc Oxide (ZnO)**, which lowers the decomp. temperature to match the cure window of natural rubber. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between open-cell and closed-cell sponge rubber. Give two applications of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Open-Cell Sponge | Closed-Cell Sponge |
|---|---|---|
| **Cell Wall** | Broken / interconnected | Intact / sealed |
| **Water Absorption** | High (sinks in water) | Negligible (floats) |
| **Density** | Lower (0.1 – 0.3 g/cm³) | Higher (0.3 – 0.6 g/cm³) |
| **Gas Permeability** | Permeable | Impermeable |

**Applications:**
- **Open-Cell:** Mattresses, cushioning, acoustic absorbers, scrubbing sponges.
- **Closed-Cell:** Marine buoyancy fenders, thermal insulation, automotive door seals, gaskets. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** 

> **(a)** Describe the **preparation of sponge rubber** with a suitable formulation. Explain the role of each ingredient. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **synchronization of blowing and vulcanization reactions** with a neat sketch. Why is this balance critical for producing a good-quality sponge? **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Formulation (KTU Standard — write this in a tabular form):**

| Ingredient | phr | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Natural Rubber (RSS-4) | 100.0 | Base elastomer |
| Sulfur | 2.5 | Vulcanizing (crosslinking) agent |
| ZnO | 5.0 | Activator — lowers ADC decomp. temp |
| Stearic Acid | 1.5 | Co-activator + processing aid |
| CBS Accelerator | 0.8 | Speeds up sulfur crosslinking |
| Azodicarbonamide (ADC) | 5.0 | Blowing agent (gas source) |
| Paraffin Oil | 5.0 | Plasticizer |
| Antioxidant (BHT) | 1.0 | Ageing resistance |

**Procedure (3 marks):**

1. **Mastication** of NR on a two-roll mill (50 °C, 5 min) to reduce viscosity.
2. **Addition sequence:** Stearic acid → ZnO → Sulfur + CBS → ADC → Plasticizer + antioxidant. Each addition is followed by 2–3 min of cross-cutting and folding for uniform dispersion.
3. The compound is sheeted out (~2 mm) and cut into discs.
4. **Moulding** in a hydraulic press at **160 °C**:
   - First 2 min: Full contact pressure with bumping.
   - Next 4–5 min: **Partial pressure release** to permit foam expansion.
   - Final 4–5 min: Full pressure to complete vulcanization.
5. Total cure time: 10–12 minutes.
6. **Demould** and condition at room temperature for 24 hours.

**Role of ingredients** is to be elaborated as per Section 2.1 above. **(7 marks total)**

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

The formation of sponge rubber is governed by the **simultaneous occurrence** of two independent reactions during the heating cycle:

**Reaction 1 — Blowing (Gas Evolution):**

$$\text{Azodicarbonamide} \xrightarrow{160 - 200\,°C, \text{ZnO}} \text{N}_2 \uparrow + \text{CO} \uparrow + \text{NH}_3 \uparrow + \text{Residue}$$

This gas generation rate is given by an Arrhenius-type expression:
$$r_{\text{blow}} = k_{\text{blow}} \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

**Reaction 2 — Vulcanization (Crosslinking):**

$$\text{Rubber} + \text{S}_8 \xrightarrow{\text{Accelerator, ZnO, Heat}} \text{Crosslinked 3-D network}$$

This conversion rate is:
$$r_{\text{cure}} = k_{\text{cure}} \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

**Critical Balance — The "Goldilocks Zone":** 

For a good sponge, the **gas pressure generated must peak just as the rubber reaches a critical viscosity** — viscous enough to be expanded, but elastic enough to retain the cells.

- **If $r_{\text{cure}} \gg r_{\text{blow}}$:** Rubber sets before gas can expand → dense, non-cellular product.
- **If $r_{\text{blow}} \gg r_{\text{cure}}$:** Gas escapes before rubber gels → collapsed, weak product with no dimensional stability.
- **Balanced case:** Viscous rubber expands due to internal gas pressure, then sets rapidly to lock the cellular structure in place.

**Sketch (describe verbally — examiners expect a graph of $r_{\text{blow}}$ and $r_{\text{cure}}$ vs. time):**
- $X$-axis: Time (minutes)
- $Y$-axis: Reaction rate / Viscosity
- $r_{\text{blow}}$ curve: Rises early, peaks at ~3 min.
- Viscosity curve: Drops initially (heat-softening) then rises sharply after ~4 min (vulcanization onset).
- The **intersection** of the rising viscosity with the falling blowing rate defines the "**set point**" — the moment the cell structure is locked in.

**Conclusion:** The synchronization is critical because it determines the **final cell morphology, expansion ratio, density, and mechanical strength** of the sponge rubber. **(7 marks total)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Paper]**

> **(a)** What are the different **types of blowing agents** used in sponge rubber? Compare chemical and physical blowing agents. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write notes on the **industrial applications of sponge rubber** in the automotive, construction, and footwear industries. Explain why closed-cell EPDM is preferred for automotive door seals. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Blowing agents** are classified into two main categories:

**1. Chemical Blowing Agents (CBAs):** Decompose thermally to release gas.
- **Exothermic type:** Azodicarbonamide (ADC), 4,4'-Oxy-bis-benzene sulfonyl hydrazide (OBSH).
- **Endothermic type:** Sodium bicarbonate + citric acid mixtures (release CO₂).
- *Example for sponge rubber:* ADC is the most widely used (yields ~220 ml gas/g).

**2. Physical Blowing Agents (PBAs):** Volatile liquids that vaporize upon heating.
- Examples: Pentane, dichloromethane, liquid CO₂, nitrogen gas (injected under pressure).
- Used primarily in **thermoplastic foams** (e.g., polystyrene, polyurethane) rather than in traditional rubber sponge.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Chemical Blowing Agent | Physical Blowing Agent |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Thermal decomposition | Volatilization |
| **Temperature Control** | Specific decomp. temp. (e.g., 200 °C for ADC) | Boiling point of liquid |
| **Residue** | Solid residue in matrix (may affect properties) | No residue (cleaner foam) |
| **Cell Uniformity** | Excellent (with proper activation) | Good |
| **Storage Stability** | Limited (can decompose in hot storage) | Excellent (sealed liquids) |
| **Cost** | Moderate | High (requires pressure equipment) |
| **Common in Rubber?** | **Yes (ADC standard for NR/SBR sponge)** | No (more common in plastics) |

For the **KTU experiment on sponge rubber**, ADC is the only relevant blowing agent. **(7 marks total)**

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Industrial Applications of Sponge Rubber:**

**1. Automotive Industry:**
- **Door seals and weather-stripping** (closed-cell EPDM sponge).
- **Hood and trunk seals.**
- **Anti-vibration mounts** (engine cradle bushings).
- **Acoustic damping pads** in dashboards and door panels.
- **Air filter gaskets.**

*Why EPDM for door seals?* 
- EPDM has excellent **ozone and UV resistance** (critical for outdoor exposure).
- Closed-cell structure provides **water-tight sealing** even under compression.
- Service temperature range: **–40 °C to +120 °C** (suitable for engine bay heat and cold winters).
- Low compression set (≤ 25% after 22 h at 70 °C) ensures long-term sealing performance.

**2. Construction Industry:**
- **Expansion joint fillers** (accommodate thermal movement of concrete).
- **Acoustic insulation panels** (open-cell sponge absorbs sound).
- **Pipe insulation lagging** (closed-cell EPDM/NBR sponge).
- **Roofing membrane underlay.**
- **Window and door glazing gaskets.**

**3. Footwear Industry:**
- **Shoe soles and midsoles** (PU sponge for sports shoes — Adidas Boost technology uses TPU foam pellets).
- **Insoles and heel cushions.**
- **Slipper uppers.**
- **Sports shoe tongue padding.**

**4. Other Applications:**
- **Marine:** Buoyancy fenders, life-jacket floats (closed-cell NBR/PVC).
- **Medical:** Orthotic cushioning, prosthetic liners.
- **Domestic:** Kitchen scrubbing sponges, bath mats, mattress toppers.

**Conclusion:** The **closed-cell vs. open-cell** choice is dictated by whether the application requires **water resistance** (closed-cell) or **breathability/cushioning** (open-cell). **(7 marks total)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Sponge Rubber Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "blowing agent" with "accelerator"** — A blowing agent generates *gas*; an accelerator speeds up *vulcanization*. Mixing these up loses 2–3 marks immediately.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the role of ZnO** — Examiners expect students to mention that ZnO *activates* ADC by lowering its decomposition temperature. Many students write "ZnO is added for activation" without specifying *what* it activates.
>
> 3. **Stating "baking powder is the blowing agent"** in the analogy — Use **"chemical blowing agent"** or **"Azodicarbonamide"** in the formal answer. The baking-powder analogy is for understanding only, NOT for writing in the exam.
>
> 4. **Skipping the "race against time" concept** — The synchronization between blowing and curing is THE most important theoretical concept. A 7-mark answer that omits this is capped at 4 marks.
>
> 5. **Wrong units for density** — Use **g/cm³** (not kg/m³ unless asked). Expansion ratio is **dimensionless**.
>
> 6. **In numerical problems, forgetting to convert** — The base-rubber volume is *not* the mould volume. If the question gives mould volume = 20 cm³ and asks for expansion ratio, verify that mass × density of NR matches before assuming.
>
> 7. **Drawing a "cake" instead of a "flow diagram"** — The KTU diagram must show the **process flow** (compounding → moulding → curing → demoulding), not the cake-baking analogy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision Before KTU Exam**

- [x] **Sponge rubber** = cellular expanded elastomer made by trapping gas (from blowing agent) in a vulcanized rubber matrix.
- [x] **Key blowing agent:** **Azodicarbonamide (ADC)**. Gas yield ≈ **220 ml/g**. Decomposes at ~200 °C (lowered to ~150 °C by ZnO activation).
- [x] **Gas evolved:** N₂ (major), CO, CO₂, NH₃.
- [x] **Vulcanizing system:** Sulfur (2–3 phr) + CBS accelerator + ZnO + Stearic acid.
- [x] **Base polymer:** Natural Rubber (RSS-4) is the KTU standard. SBR and EPDM are common industrial alternatives.
- [x] **Synchronization Principle:** Blowing rate and curing rate must be matched. Cure must "set" the rubber just as gas generation peaks — otherwise the sponge is either too dense (over-cured) or collapsed (under-cured).
- [x] **Two-roll mill** is used for compounding. Speed ratio of rolls is typically 1:1.2 (front:slower, back:faster).
- [x] **Cure temperature:** 160 ± 5 °C. Cure time: 10–12 minutes. Determined by **Monsanto Rheometer** ($t_{90}$).
- [x] **Apparent density ($\rho_a$):** 0.1 – 0.6 g/cm³. Lower density = higher expansion.
- [x] **Expansion ratio ($E_r$):** 3:1 to 10:1.
- [x] **Closed-cell sponge:** Sealed cells, water-resistant, used for gaskets and buoyancy.
- [x] **Open-cell sponge:** Interconnected cells, absorbent, used for cushioning and filters.
- [x] **Water absorption test:** Closed-cell floats; open-cell sinks.
- [x] **ZnO is a dual-role additive:** It activates **both** the blowing agent (ADC) and the accelerator (CBS) — a KTU viva favourite.
- [x] **Antioxidant (BHT/IPPD):** Prevents oxidative degradation during storage and service.
- [x] **Industrial applications:** Automotive door seals (EPDM), footwear midsoles, mattress foam, marine fenders, construction expansion joints.
- [x] **Defects and causes:** Dense core = under-blowing; collapse = over-blowing; uneven cells = poor ADC dispersion.

> **End of Sponge Rubber Notes — PCPOL507 Module Experiment 2**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Man-Made Hose
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOL507 - PCPOL507 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Man-Made Hose ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Man-Made Hose** (also termed a *manufactured industrial hose* or *synthetic hose*) is a flexible, hollow, tubular conduit produced from engineered polymeric materials (synthetic rubber, thermoplastics, or elastomeric composites), often reinforced with textile braids, helical wire, or aramid yarn, designed to convey fluids, gases, slurries, or granular solids under controlled pressure and environmental conditions. In the KTU 2024 scheme context of polymer processing, it represents a **continuous extrusion product** manufactured through polymer compounding, plasticization, and lamination techniques rather than being derived directly from natural latex tubing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A *man-made hose* is defined as a **flexible pressure vessel** whose structural integrity is derived from a polymer matrix (PVC, NBR, EPDM, PU, silicone, or thermoplastic elastomer) optionally reinforced with one or more textile/wire plies, whose end-use performance is characterized by *burst pressure, working pressure, flexibility radius, and chemical resistance*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **garden hose** you use at home, but engineered to industrial specifications. While a simple garden hose is just a single layer of PVC, a man-made industrial hose is like a **layered sandwich**: - **Inner Core (Liner/Tube):** The smooth, chemically resistant inner skin that touches the fluid — like the non-stick coating inside a saucepan. - **Reinforcement Layer:** Like the steel ribs in a skyscraper, this is the textile braid or steel wire helix that provides tensile and burst strength. - **Outer Cover:** The tough, weather/abrasion-resistant skin — analogous to the outer shell of a fireman's jacket. > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Insight:** A man-made hose is essentially a *composite cylindrical pressure vessel* where the polymer matrix provides chemical and fluid-barrier properties, while the reinforcement (textile/wire) carries the mechanical load. This **synergistic composite action** is the central design philosophy behind all modern industrial hoses. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants | Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Range | KTU Reference Value | |-----------|----------------|---------------|---------------------| | Working Pressure | $P_w$ | 5–50 bar | **10 bar (industrial standard)** | | Burst Pressure (Safety Factor = 4) | $P_b$ | 4 × $P_w$ | **40 bar minimum** | | Temperature Range | $T$ | −40 °C to +120 °C | **−20 °C to +80 °C (PVC)** | | Specific Gravity of PVC Compound | $\rho_{PVC}$ | 1.20–1.45 g/cm³ | **1.38 g/cm³** | | Tensile Strength (NBR) | $\sigma_{NBR}$ | 7–25 MPa | **15 MPa** | | Hardness (Shore A) | $H_A$ | 50–95 | **70 Shore A** | > [!NOTE] > **Safety Factor (SF) = 4** is the universally adopted KTU-cited ratio between *Burst Pressure* and *Working Pressure* for hydraulic and industrial polymer hoses as per **ISO 1307** and **SAE J517** standards. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown A man-made hose is engineered as a **multi-layer composite cylinder**. Its operational behaviour can be broken into five hierarchical logic steps: - **Step 1 — Polymer Matrix Selection:** The *why*: Different polymers offer different chemical resistance. PVC is cheap and good for water/air, NBR resists oils, EPDM resists heat/steam, PU resists abrasion. The *how*: The base polymer is compounded with plasticizers (DOP, DINP), stabilizers (Ba/Zn stearates), fillers (CaCO₃), and processing aids. - **Step 2 — Inner Liner (Tube) Extrusion:** The inner skin determines fluid compatibility. It is extruded first as a continuous seamless tube through a **cross-head die** at temperatures between **140 °C and 180 °C** (for PVC) using a single-screw extruder (L/D = 20–25). - **Step 3 — Reinforcement Application:** Textile yarn (polyester, rayon, aramid) is braided or spirally wound over the liner at a *braid angle* typically **54° 44′** (the "magic angle" for zero axial extension under internal pressure). Steel wire helix provides crush resistance for suction hoses. - **Step 4 — Outer Cover Extrusion:** A second cross-head extrusion coats the reinforcement with the protective outer jacket. Cover is pigmented (black for UV resistance), pin-pricked (for gas permeation venting), and sometimes fluted/corrugated. - **Step 5 — Vulcanization / Curing:** For rubber hoses (NBR, EPDM), the assembly is wrapped on a mandrel, covered with lead/nylon tape, and steam-cured in an autoclave at **0.3–0.5 MPa steam pressure for 30–60 minutes** to crosslink the polymer chains. For PVC/PU hoses, cooling and sizing alone suffice. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Magic Angle Principle (54° 44′ ≈ 54.73°):** When braided reinforcement is wound at this helix angle, the braid experiences **zero axial strain** under pure internal pressure loading. This is the theoretical optimum for pressure hose design and is a high-yield KTU question topic. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $P_w = \dfrac{P_b}{SF}$ | Working Pressure = Burst / Safety Factor (SF=4) | bar / MPa | | 2 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2t}$ | Hoop (Circumferential) Stress in thin-walled tube | Pa | | 3 | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4t}$ | Axial (Longitudinal) Stress in thin-walled tube | Pa | | 4 | $P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_{uts} \cdot t}{D}$ | Theoretical Burst Pressure (laminate theory) | Pa | | 5 | $D_o = D_i + 2t$ | Outside Diameter relation | mm | | 6 | $t_{min} = \dfrac{P_w \cdot D_i \cdot SF}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$ | Minimum wall thickness for given working pressure | mm | | 7 | $\theta_{braid} = \arctan\left(\dfrac{\pi D}{L}\right)$ | Braid angle from helix geometry | rad / ° | | 8 | $N_{ends} = \dfrac{\pi D \cdot n_{c}}{p}$ | Number of braid yarn ends | dimensionless | | 9 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D_i^{4}}{128 \mu L} \cdot \Delta P$ | Hagen–Poiseuille flow rate (laminar) | m³/s | | 10 | $R_{bend,min} = 5 \cdot D_o$ | Minimum bend radius (5× OD rule) | mm | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Thin-Wall Assumption Validity:** All formulas above assume $D/t \geq 20$. For thick-walled hoses (high-pressure hydraulic), use Lame's equations: > $$\sigma_r = \dfrac{p_i r_i^{2} - p_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} - \dfrac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}) r^{2}}$$ > $$\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{p_i r_i^{2} - p_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} + \dfrac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}) r^{2}}$$ ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Man-made hoses are critical in: - **Hydraulic systems** in earth-moving equipment (SAE 100R2 twin-wire braid, working pressure up to 350 bar). - **Chemical transfer** in petrochemical refineries (PTFE-lined hoses, rated to 200 °C). - **Food & Pharma** (silicone hoses, FDA 21 CFR 177.2600 compliant). - **Automotive fuel systems** (FKM/Viton lined, multi-layer PA12 barrier). - **Fire-fighting** (EPDM rubber with polyester jacket, burst-tested at 60 bar). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Hose **Given:** A hose of inner diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, subjected to internal pressure $P$. We wish to find the circumferential (hoop) stress $\sigma_h$. **Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Setup:** Consider a half-cylinder of length $L$ cut longitudinally. The internal pressure acts on the projected area $D \cdot L$, pushing the two halves apart. **Step 2 — Force Balance on Half-Cylinder:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Pressure force pushing outward} &= P \cdot D \cdot L \\ \text{Resisting force from hoop stress} &= 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L \\ \text{Equating (equilibrium)} &: \quad 2 \sigma_h t L = P D L \\ \text{Cancelling } L &: \quad 2 \sigma_h t = P D \\ \text{Solving for } \sigma_h &: \quad \boxed{\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2t}} \end{aligned} $$ > The factor **2** arises because there are two cut surfaces (the two halves of the cylinder), each carrying stress $\sigma_h$ over area $t \cdot L$. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Axial Stress in a Thin-Walled Hose **Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Setup:** Now cut the cylinder transversely (perpendicular to axis). The internal pressure acts on the circular end-cap of area $\dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$. **Step 2 — Force Balance:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Pressure force on end cap} &= P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \\ \text{Resisting force in wall cross-section} &= \sigma_a \cdot (\pi D t) \\ \text{Equating} &: \quad \sigma_a \cdot \pi D t = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \\ \text{Cancelling } \pi D &: \quad \sigma_a t = \dfrac{P D}{4} \\ \text{Therefore} &: \quad \boxed{\sigma_a = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4t}} \end{aligned} $$ > **Critical Observation:** $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_a$. This is why hoses fail by *longitudinal splitting* (crack along length) rather than by pulling apart axially — the hoop stress is always twice the axial stress. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Wall Thickness for a Given Working Pressure **Given:** Working pressure $P_w$, inner diameter $D_i$, allowable stress $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_{uts}/n$ where $n$ is the design factor. Required: minimum wall thickness $t_{min}$. **Step 1 — Apply Hoop Stress Formula with Safety Factor:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{allow} &= \sigma_h / n \\ \sigma_{allow} &= \dfrac{P_w \cdot D_i}{2 t_{min}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{n} \quad \text{(rearranging for } t_{min} \text{)} \\ t_{min} &= \dfrac{P_w \cdot D_i \cdot n}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}} \\ \boxed{t_{min}} &= \dfrac{P_w \cdot D_i \cdot n}{2 \cdot \sigma_{uts}/n_o} \quad \text{where } n_o = \text{overall safety factor} \\ \therefore \quad t_{min} &= \dfrac{P_w \cdot D_i \cdot SF}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A PVC man-made hose has inner diameter $D_i = 25 \text{ mm}$, wall thickness $t = 4 \text{ mm}$, and ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{uts} = 50 \text{ MPa}$. Calculate (a) the working pressure with safety factor 4, and (b) the burst pressure. **Solution:** **(a) Burst pressure (theoretical, from hoop stress at $\sigma = \sigma_{uts}$):** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{uts} &= \dfrac{P_b \cdot D_i}{2t} \\ 50 \times 10^{6} &= \dfrac{P_b \cdot 0.025}{2 \cdot 0.004} \\ P_b &= \dfrac{50 \times 10^{6} \cdot 2 \cdot 0.004}{0.025} \\ P_b &= \dfrac{50 \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.008}{0.025} \\ P_b &= 16 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \\ P_b &= \boxed{16 \text{ MPa} \approx 160 \text{ bar}} \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Working pressure:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_w &= \dfrac{P_b}{SF} = \dfrac{160}{4} = \boxed{40 \text{ bar}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Marking Convention:** Always show the formula first, substitute values with units, and box the final answer. Partial marks are awarded for correctly identifying the formula even if numerical substitution is wrong. ### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Polymer Selection for Hose Construction | Service Fluid | Recommended Polymer | Max Temp (°C) | Cost Index | KTU Practical | |--------------|--------------------|--------------|-----------| --------------| | Water / Air | **PVC** | 60 | 1.0× | Most common lab demo | | Hydraulic Oil | **NBR / Nitrile** | 100 | 2.5× | Hydraulic systems | | Hot Steam | **EPDM** | 180 | 3.0× | Boiler blow-down | | Acids / Solvents | **PTFE (Teflon)** | 260 | 10× | Chemical plants | | Food / Pharma | **Silicone** | 230 | 8× | CIP processes | | Fuel (Petrol/Diesel) | **FKM / Viton** | 230 | 12× | Automotive | | Abrasive Slurries | **PU (Polyurethane)** | 80 | 4× | Mining | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cross-Sectional Architecture of a Man-Made Hose ```mermaid graph TD A[FLUID FLOW
inside hose] --> B[INNER LINER
Tube - Polymer] B --> C[REINFORCEMENT LAYER 1
Textile Braid / Wire Helix] C --> D[REINFORCEMENT LAYER 2
Optional - Helix Wire] D --> E[OUTER COVER
Weather / Abrasion Resistant] E --> F[EXTERNAL ENVIRONMENT
sun, oil, abrasion] G[INTERNAL PRESSURE P] --> B H[AXIAL TENSILE LOAD F] --> C I[TORSIONAL TWIST T] --> C style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style C fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style F fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242 style G fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c style H fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c style I fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Manufacturing Process Sequence ```mermaid graph TD raw[Raw Polymer Resin
PVC / NBR / EPDM] --> comp[Compounding
additives and plasticizers] comp --> ext1[Inner Tube Extrusion
Cross Head Die] ext1 --> bra[Braiding Machine
Textile Yarn Application] bra --> ext2[Outer Cover Extrusion
Cross Head Die 2] ext2 --> cure{Vulcanization
Required?} cure -->|Yes - Rubber| auto[Autoclave Steam Curing
0.4 MPa, 45 minutes] cure -->|No - Thermoplastic| cool[Water Cooling Bath] auto --> test[Quality Testing
Burst / Bend / Hydrostatic] cool --> test test --> pack[Reeling and Packing] style raw fill:#bbdefb style comp fill:#c5cae9 style ext1 fill:#fff59d style bra fill:#ffab91 style ext2 fill:#fff59d style cure fill:#ce93d8 style auto fill:#ef9a9a style cool fill:#80deea style test fill:#a5d6a7 style pack fill:#b0bec5 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Load Distribution in Reinforced Hose ```mermaid graph LR P[Internal Pressure P] --> S1[Hoop Stress sigma h] P --> S2[Axial Stress sigma a] P --> S3[Radial Stress sigma r approx 0] S1 --> R1[Carried by Reinforcement
Braid Angle 54 deg 44 min] S2 --> R1 S3 --> L[Carried by Liner
Polymer Matrix] R1 --> O[Outer Cover Protection] L --> O style P fill:#ffcdd2 style S1 fill:#ffe0b2 style S2 fill:#ffe0b2 style S3 fill:#ffe0b2 style R1 fill:#c8e6c9 style L fill:#c8e6c9 style O fill:#bbdefb ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** In a properly designed man-made hose, **~85% of the mechanical load** is carried by the reinforcement (textile/wire), while the polymer matrix provides **fluid-barrier and chemical-resistance** functions. This division of labour is the essence of composite hose design. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "Man-Made Hose" and list any three polymeric materials commonly used in its construction.* **Model Answer:** A man-made hose is a flexible, tubular composite conduit manufactured from synthetic polymers (with optional reinforcement), used for transporting fluids or gases under pressure. Three commonly used polymers are: 1. **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride)** — general purpose, water/air, low cost. 2. **NBR (Nitrile Butadiene Rubber)** — oil-resistant, hydraulic applications. 3. **EPDM (Ethylene Propylene Diene Monomer)** — heat/steam-resistant, automotive coolant lines. > **3-Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the three polymers (name + one property). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *State the "magic angle" used in textile braiding of hoses and explain its significance.* **Model Answer:** The magic angle is **54° 44′** (≈ 54.73°), measured between the braid yarn and the longitudinal axis of the hose. **Significance:** At this angle, the braided reinforcement experiences **zero axial strain** under pure internal pressure loading, because the radial component of yarn tension exactly balances the pressure-induced axial expansion. This angle is therefore the *theoretical optimum* for pressure-hose braiding, maximising burst resistance without kinking. > **3-Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for value + 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for engineering significance. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) **Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat diagram the **constructional features** of a typical man-made hose. Explain the function of each layer. **(b) [7 Marks]** A hydraulic hose has inner diameter 19 mm and wall thickness 5 mm. It is reinforced with two plies of steel-wire braid (each ply strength = 35 MPa effective). Calculate the **burst pressure** and the **safe working pressure** using a safety factor of 4. **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction Features:** A typical man-made industrial hose consists of **four functional layers** from inside to outside: | Layer | Function | Typical Material | |-------|----------|------------------| | 1. Inner Tube / Liner | Fluid contact, chemical barrier, smoothness | NBR, PVC, PTFE | | 2. Reinforcement Ply 1 | Burst strength, hoop stress resistance | Steel wire braid | | 3. Reinforcement Ply 2 | Additional pressure rating, axial stiffness | Steel wire braid (counter-wound) | | 4. Outer Cover | Weather, abrasion, ozone resistance | Synthetic rubber, CSPE | **Working principle:** The liner handles chemical compatibility, while the two braid plies (wound in opposite directions at 54° 44′) carry the mechanical pressure load. The cover protects the reinforcement from external damage. The two plies are wound counter-rotationally so that torsion from one is cancelled by the other. > **7-Mark Allocation:** 2 marks for diagram + 3 marks for layer functions + 2 marks for working principle. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $D_i = 19 \text{ mm} = 0.019 \text{ m}$, $t = 5 \text{ mm} = 0.005 \text{ m}$, two plies each $\sigma_{ply} = 35 \text{ MPa}$, $SF = 4$. **Step 1 — Effective combined reinforcement strength:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{eff} &= 2 \times \sigma_{ply} = 2 \times 35 = 70 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply hoop stress at burst:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{eff} &= \dfrac{P_b \cdot D_i}{2t} \\ 70 \times 10^{6} &= \dfrac{P_b \cdot 0.019}{2 \cdot 0.005} \\ P_b &= \dfrac{70 \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.010}{0.019} \\ P_b &= \dfrac{7.0 \times 10^{5}}{0.019} \\ P_b &= 36.84 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \\ P_b &\approx \boxed{36.84 \text{ MPa} \approx 368 \text{ bar}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Safe working pressure:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_w &= \dfrac{P_b}{SF} = \dfrac{36.84}{4} = \boxed{9.21 \text{ MPa} \approx 92 \text{ bar}} \end{aligned} $$ > **7-Mark Allocation:** 2 marks for given data + 2 marks for hoop stress formula + 2 marks for substitution and $P_b$ + 1 mark for $P_w$. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **manufacturing process** of a man-made rubber hose, highlighting the role of vulcanization. List the additives used in the rubber compound. **(b) [7 Marks]** A PVC garden hose of 25 mm inner diameter failed at an internal pressure of 18 bar. If the wall thickness was 3.5 mm, calculate the **tensile strength of the PVC material**. Comment on whether the design is safe for a 5 bar working pressure with safety factor 3. **Model Solution:** **(a) Manufacturing Process & Vulcanization:** The manufacturing of a man-made rubber hose follows a **5-stage process:** 1. **Compound Mixing:** Base rubber (e.g., NBR) is masticated in a **Banbury mixer** at 80–100 °C with additives: - **Sulphur (1.5–3 phr)** — crosslinking agent - **Stearic acid (1–2 phr)** — activator - **ZnO (3–5 phr)** — accelerator activator - **CBS / MBT (0.5–1.5 phr)** — accelerators - **Carbon black (30–50 phr)** — reinforcing filler, UV protection - **Aromatic oil (5–15 phr)** — plasticizer - **Antioxidant (1 phr)** — ageing resistance 2. **Inner Tube Extrusion:** The compound is extruded through a cross-head die onto a flexible mandrel at 90–110 °C. 3. **Reinforcement Braiding:** Textile or wire braiding is applied over the green (uncured) tube. 4. **Cover Extrusion:** Outer rubber cover is extruded over the braided assembly. 5. **Vulcanization (Curing):** The hose is wrapped on a drum, covered with lead or nylon wrapping tape, and steam-cured in an **autoclave at 0.3–0.5 MPa for 30–60 minutes** at 140–160 °C. **Role of Vulcanization:** Sulphur forms crosslinks between polyisoprene (or NBR) chains, transforming the soft, plastic raw rubber into a tough, elastic, dimensionally stable thermoset. Without vulcanization, the hose would deform plastically under pressure and temperature. > **7-Mark Allocation:** 3 marks for process steps + 2 marks for additives + 2 marks for vulcanization role. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $D_i = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$, $t = 3.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0035 \text{ m}$, $P_b = 18 \text{ bar} = 1.8 \text{ MPa}$. **Step 1 — Calculate tensile strength from burst pressure:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{uts} &= \dfrac{P_b \cdot D_i}{2t} \\ &= \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.025}{2 \cdot 0.0035} \\ &= \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{4}}{0.007} \\ &= 6.43 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \\ \sigma_{uts} &\approx \boxed{6.43 \text{ MPa}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate actual burst pressure from this $\sigma_{uts}$ and the design:** The actual burst = 18 bar = 1.8 MPa (given). So $\sigma_{uts} = 6.43$ MPa is the material property. **Step 3 — Check safety for 5 bar working pressure with SF = 3:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{w,allow} &= \dfrac{P_b}{SF} = \dfrac{18}{3} = 6 \text{ bar} \end{aligned} $$ Since **required $P_w = 5$ bar $< P_{w,allow} = 6$ bar**, the design is **SAFE** ✓ **Step 4 — Comment on actual safety factor achieved:** $$ \begin{aligned} SF_{actual} &= \dfrac{P_b}{P_w} = \dfrac{18}{5} = 3.6 \end{aligned} $$ The actual safety factor is 3.6, which exceeds the minimum required 3. The design is safe with a margin of 20%. > **7-Mark Allocation:** 3 marks for tensile strength calculation + 2 marks for safety check + 2 marks for design verdict with reasoning. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Most mark losses occur when students fail to convert **mm → m** before substituting into stress formulas. 1 mm = 0.001 m. Always work in SI base units. > 2. **Hoop vs Axial Confusion:** Hoop stress is **TWICE** the axial stress. If a problem says "longitudinal failure", use $\sigma_a$; if it says "bursting", use $\sigma_h$. > 3. **Burst Pressure is NOT Working Pressure:** The *theoretical* burst pressure is the failure pressure. The *safe working pressure* = Burst / Safety Factor. Many students skip the SF division. > 4. **Braiding Angle:** The "magic angle" is **54° 44′**, NOT 45°. Examiners specifically test this. > 5. **Wall Thickness Sign Error:** $D_o = D_i + 2t$ (not $D_i + t$). The "2" is mandatory because wall exists on *both* sides. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Definition:** Man-made hose = polymer + optional reinforcement composite tube for fluid/gas transport. - ✅ **Four-layer architecture:** Liner → Reinforcement → (Optional second ply) → Outer Cover. - ✅ **Key formula:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2t}$ and $P_b = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{uts} t}{D}$. - ✅ **Hoop stress is twice axial stress** — hoses split lengthwise, not axially. - ✅ **Magic Braid Angle = 54° 44′** — zero axial strain under internal pressure. - ✅ **Safety Factor = 4** (industrial standard) for hydraulic / pressure hoses. - ✅ **Thin-wall assumption** valid when $D/t \geq 20$; otherwise use Lamé's equations. - ✅ **Minimum bend radius** $= 5 \times D_o$ (KTU-cited standard). - ✅ **Vulcanization additives:** Sulphur (crosslinker), ZnO (activator), CBS/MBT (accelerator), carbon black (reinforcer). - ✅ **Common polymers:** PVC (cheap), NBR (oil), EPDM (heat), PU (abrasion), PTFE (chemical), Silicone (food). - ✅ **Always convert mm → m** before substituting into SI stress formulas. - ✅ **Standards:** ISO 1307 (dimensions), SAE J517 (hydraulic), FDA 21 CFR 177.2600 (food-grade).

topics.title: Solvent Based Adhesives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL507 - PCPOL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Solvent Based Adhesives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solvent-Based Adhesives** are *physical-setting* or *chemical-setting* adhesive systems in which the **polymeric binder (the adhesive base resin)** is dissolved, dispersed, or suspended in one or more **volatile organic solvents**. The solvent functions purely as a *processing carrier* — it reduces the viscosity of the high-molecular-weight polymer to a workable fluid, enables uniform wetting of the substrate, and is subsequently removed by **evaporation** (or partial penetration into the substrate) during the drying/curing stage, leaving behind a continuous polymeric film that develops the cohesive and adhesive bond.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPOL507) framework, solvent-based adhesives are classified under **physical-drying adhesives** because the bond formation does not involve a chemical cross-linking reaction in most cases. The polymer chains remain essentially linear (or lightly branched) after solvent loss and develop strength through:
- Chain entanglements
- Secondary forces (van der Waals, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding)
- Residual tackifier-polymer associations

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The PCPOL507 module on solvent-based adhesives typically covers formulation (polymer + solvent + additives), mechanism of adhesion, the role of solubility parameters in solvent selection, determination of solid content, viscosity, and bond strength testing. The "Experiment 2" nomenclature implies a hands-on laboratory module.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **Real-world Analogy — The "Nail Polish" Model:**
> Imagine painting your nails with nail polish. The bottle contains a high-viscosity polymer (nitrocellulose) dissolved in a volatile solvent (ethyl acetate / butyl acetate). When you brush it on, the liquid flows smoothly, wets the nail surface, and levels out. Within minutes, the solvent evaporates, and a hard, glossy polymer film remains that is strongly bonded to the nail.
>
> A **solvent-based adhesive behaves exactly the same way** — the solvent is the "thinned" version that flows and wets; the polymer is the "film" that remains to hold things together. The *art* of formulating such an adhesive is choosing (a) the **right polymer** for the substrate, and (b) the **right solvent** to dissolve it without attacking the substrate or taking forever to dry.

A second analogy is **white glue drying on paper**: the water carrier evaporates, and the polyvinyl acetate (PVA) chains entangle to form the bond. Solvent-based adhesives are simply the industrial, high-performance version of the same concept, using organic solvents instead of water for faster drying, better water resistance, and superior adhesion to non-polar substrates such as rubber, leather, metals, and certain plastics.

---

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standards)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolded Quantitative Benchmarks** (used by industry and KTU board questions):

- **Solid Content:** **40 – 65 %** by mass (typical industrial range)
- **Viscosity (Brookfield, 25 °C, spindle 4, 20 rpm):** **2 000 – 8 000 cP** (varies with application)
- **Lap Shear Strength (mild steel, ASTM D1002):** **2 – 15 MPa**
- **Peel Strength (canvas, ASTM D903):** **15 – 60 N / 25 mm**
- **Open Time / Tack Life:** **5 – 60 minutes** depending on formulation
- **VOC Content (current regulatory ceiling in India, 2024):** **≤ 50 g/L** for many indoor applications (driving the shift to water-based systems)

---

### 1.4 Typical Composition of a Solvent-Based Adhesive

| Component | Function | Typical Examples | Loading (wt %) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polymer Base (Binder)** | Provides cohesive strength | Polychloroprene (Neoprene), Natural Rubber, SBR, Polyurethane, Acrylics, Polystyrene, PVC copolymers | **15 – 30** |
| **Solvent (Carrier)** | Dissolves polymer, controls viscosity & drying | Toluene, Hexane, Acetone, MEK, Ethyl Acetate, Naphtha | **50 – 75** |
| **Tackifier (Resin)** | Improves initial tack ("stickiness") | Rosin, terpene-phenolic, C5/C9 hydrocarbon resins | **5 – 15** |
| **Plasticizer** | Improves flexibility & low-temperature performance | DBP, DOP, mineral oil | **1 – 5** |
| **Filler** | Cost reduction, gap filling, thixotropy | Calcium carbonate, silica, clay | **0 – 20** |
| **Antioxidant / Stabiliser** | Prevents oxidative degradation | BHT, hindered phenols, hindered amines | **0.1 – 1** |
| **Curing Agent** *(optional)* | Cross-links polymer (for two-part systems) | Isocyanates (for PU), metallic oxides (for Neoprene) | **1 – 5** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Bond Formation in Solvent-Based Adhesives

The development of a useful adhesive bond is a **four-stage sequential process**:

1. **Stage 1 — Wetting & Flow (0 to 60 s):**
   The low-viscosity adhesive solution flows over the substrate. The solvent allows the polymer chains to reach micro-cavities and intimate molecular contact.
   *Governed by:* surface energy of substrate, surface tension of liquid, contact angle (Young's equation).

2. **Stage 2 — Solvent Loss (1 min – 24 h):**
   Evaporation (and partial absorption) of the solvent increases solids concentration and viscosity, eventually locking the polymer chains in place.
   *Governed by:* solvent vapour pressure, ambient temperature, relative humidity, substrate porosity.

3. **Stage 3 — Solidification / Gelation:**
   The polymer concentration crosses the *gel point* — a 3-D network of entangled chains forms. The bond becomes tack-free.
   *Governed by:* polymer molecular weight, concentration, entanglement density.

4. **Stage 4 — Strength Development:**
   Ultimate cohesive and adhesive strength develops as residual solvent diffuses out, and the polymer chains achieve maximum intimate contact (inter-diffusion across the interface for compatible substrates).
   *Governed by:* polymer–substrate interactions (acid-base, dispersion forces), residual stresses.

---

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Solvent Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The solvent is **not** a passive carrier. It dictates (a) polymer solubility, (b) viscosity, (c) drying time, (d) substrate compatibility, (e) toxicity, and (f) regulatory compliance.

The single most powerful theoretical tool for solvent selection is the **Hildebrand / Hansen Solubility Parameter (δ)**. Two materials are mutually soluble (and hence a good polymer-solvent pair) when their δ values are close.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **minimum mathematical toolkit** required to answer KTU Part-A and Part-B questions on solvent-based adhesives. Memorise the formula, the *defining terms*, and the *units*.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta = \left(\dfrac{\Delta H_v - RT}{V_m}\right)^{1/2}$ | Hildebrand total solubility parameter | $(\text{MPa})^{1/2}$ |
| 2 | $\delta_t^2 = \delta_d^2 + \delta_p^2 + \delta_h^2$ | Hansen total solubility parameter (sum of dispersion, polar, H-bonding contributions) | $(\text{MPa})^{1/2}$ |
| 3 | $R_a = \left[4(\delta_{d1}-\delta_{d2})^2 + (\delta_{p1}-\delta_{p2})^2 + (\delta_{h1}-\delta_{h2})^2\right]^{1/2}$ | Hansen distance between two materials in 3-D parameter space | $(\text{MPa})^{1/2}$ |
| 4 | $RED = \dfrac{R_a}{R_0}$ | Relative Energy Difference; RED $\lt 1$ = soluble, RED $\gt 1$ = insoluble | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta$ | Young's equation — equilibrium contact angle | $\text{mN/m}$ |
| 6 | $W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Dupré work of adhesion | $\text{mJ/m}^2$ |
| 7 | $S = \gamma_{sv} - (\gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}) = \gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta - 1)$ | Spreading coefficient; $S \gt 0$ = spontaneous spreading | $\text{mN/m}$ |
| 8 | $[\eta] = K M^a$ | Mark-Houwink equation: intrinsic viscosity vs. molecular weight | $[\eta]$ in dL/g |
| 9 | $\eta = A \exp\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius viscosity-temperature relation | $\eta$ in cP or Pa·s |
| 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{b \cdot t}$ | Lap shear strength of bonded joint | MPa or psi |
| 11 | $\sigma_{peel} = \dfrac{F}{w}$ | Peel strength per unit width | N/mm or N/25 mm |
| 12 | $\% \text{ Solid} = \dfrac{W_2 - W_0}{W_1 - W_0} \times 100$ | Solid content (gravimetric) | % |
| 13 | $\chi_{12} = \dfrac{V_1}{RT}(\delta_1 - \delta_2)^2$ | Flory-Huggins interaction parameter | dimensionless |
| 14 | $E_{rel} = \dfrac{t_{n\text{-BuAc}}}{t_{solvent}}$ | Relative evaporation rate (n-butyl acetate = 1) | dimensionless |
| 15 | $\tau_{bond} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_{overlap}}$ | Generic bond strength (force per unit bonded area) | MPa |

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Equations

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in real engineering?**

- **Equations 1–4** drive the *formulation chemistry*. A footwear adhesives formulator uses Hansen parameters to pick a non-carcinogenic alternative to toluene.
- **Equations 5–7** determine whether the liquid adhesive will wet a low-energy plastic (e.g., polyethylene, $\gamma_s \approx 31$ mN/m) or bead up like water on a waxed car. Without wetting, there is **no adhesion**.
- **Equation 8** connects molecular weight to viscosity — a lab can infer polymer chain length from a single viscosity measurement.
- **Equations 10 & 11** are the *acceptance tests* — KTU experiments on adhesive joint testing rely on these directly.
- **Equation 12** is the *quality-control* test every batch of adhesive undergoes in industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A: Spreading Coefficient from Young's Equation (Line-by-line)

We begin with **Young's equation** for a liquid droplet in equilibrium on a solid surface under air/vapour:

$$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta$$

**Step 1 —** Identify the three interfacial tensions:
- $\gamma_{sv}$: solid–vapour interfacial energy
- $\gamma_{sl}$: solid–liquid interfacial energy
- $\gamma_{lv}$: liquid–vapour (surface) tension of the adhesive
- $\theta$: equilibrium contact angle

**Step 2 —** Define the **spreading coefficient** $S$ as the difference between the solid–vapour energy and the *sum* of the energies that must be created (solid–liquid and liquid–vapour) when the liquid spreads to form a thin film:

$$S = \gamma_{sv} - (\gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv})$$

**Step 3 —** Substitute Young's equation to eliminate $\gamma_{sv}$:

$$S = \big[\gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta\big] - (\gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv})$$

**Step 4 —** Cancel $\gamma_{sl}$:

$$S = \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta - \gamma_{lv}$$

**Step 5 —** Factor out $\gamma_{lv}$:

$$S = \gamma_{lv} (\cos\theta - 1)$$

**Step 6 —** Interpretation:
- If $S \geq 0$ → $\cos\theta \geq 1$ → $\theta = 0$ → **complete spreading** (perfect wetting).
- If $S \lt 0$ → $\cos\theta \lt 1$ → $\theta \gt 0$ → **partial wetting** (a droplet forms).

> **Engineering implication:** To ensure the adhesive spreads on a substrate, we must minimise $\theta$, which requires **$\gamma_{lv}$ to be ≤ $\gamma_{sv}$ of the substrate** (rule of thumb: substrate surface energy should be ≥ 5 mN/m higher than the liquid's surface tension for good wetting).

---

### 3.2 Derivation B: Connection Between Hildebrand and Flory-Huggins Parameters

For a polymer (component 2) in a small-molecule solvent (component 1), the Flory-Huggins interaction parameter $\chi_{12}$ is related to the cohesive energy densities of the two species. Starting from the regular-solution theory assumption:

$$\chi_{12} = \frac{z \cdot \Delta w_{12}}{k_B T}$$

where $z$ is the coordination number and $\Delta w_{12}$ is the pairwise exchange energy. Substituting the geometric mean assumption $\Delta w_{12} = \dfrac{1}{2}\big[w_{11} + w_{22} - 2\sqrt{w_{11} w_{22}}\big]$ and noting that the cohesive energy $E_{coh} = -\dfrac{z}{2}w$ per molecule, we obtain:

$$\chi_{12} = \frac{V_1}{RT}(\delta_1 - \delta_2)^2$$

where $V_1$ is the molar volume of the solvent. The **rule of thumb** is: the smaller the difference $\delta_1 - \delta_2$, the smaller the $\chi_{12}$, and the better the polymer dissolves. This is the **theoretical foundation** for solvent selection in the lab.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Solids Content of a Polystyrene/Toluene Adhesive

**Given data (typical KTU experiment 2 result sheet):**
- Empty watch-glass + lid mass, $W_0 = 28.420$ g
- Watch-glass + adhesive (wet) mass, $W_1 = 33.985$ g
- Watch-glass + dried film (after 2 h at 105 °C) mass, $W_2 = 30.700$ g

**Step 1 —** Wet adhesive mass:

$$W_{wet} = W_1 - W_0 = 33.985 - 28.420 = 5.565 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 —** Dry film (solid) mass:

$$W_{dry} = W_2 - W_0 = 30.700 - 28.420 = 2.280 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 —** Apply the solids content formula:

$$\% \text{ Solid} = \frac{W_2 - W_0}{W_1 - W_0} \times 100 = \frac{2.280}{5.565} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Solid} = 0.4097 \times 100 \approx 40.97 \;\%$$

**Step 4 —** Interpretation: A solids content of ~ 41 % is typical for a brushable polystyrene adhesive used for model assembly / general-purpose bonding of rigid plastics.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Lap Shear Strength of Mild-Steel Joint

**Given:**
- Single-lap joint of mild-steel coupons (ASTM D1002 geometry).
- Overlap length $L_{ov} = 25$ mm, overlap width $b = 25$ mm, adherend thickness $t = 1.6$ mm.
- Maximum load at failure, $P_{max} = 9.8$ kN (recorded on UTM).

**Step 1 —** Compute the bonded (overlap) area:

$$A = b \times L_{ov} = 25 \times 25 = 625 \; \text{mm}^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 —** Convert load to Newtons:

$$P = 9.8 \text{ kN} = 9\,800 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 —** Apply the lap-shear formula:

$$\tau = \frac{P}{b \cdot t_{ov}} = \frac{9\,800}{625} = 15.68 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 —** Interpretation: For a generic solvent-based neoprene contact adhesive on mild steel, 15–16 MPa is at the **upper end** of performance — indicating an excellent formulation and proper surface preparation (degreasing + grit-blasting).

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Predicting Solvent Suitability Using Hansen Parameters
