**Goal:** Decide whether *ethyl acetate* or *hexane* is a better solvent for *polystyrene* ($\delta_d = 18.5$, $\delta_p = 4.5$, $\delta_h = 2.9$ MPa$^{1/2}$, $R_0 = 12.7$ MPa$^{1/2}$).

| Solvent | $\delta_d$ | $\delta_p$ | $\delta_h$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ethyl acetate | 15.8 | 5.3 | 7.2 |
| Hexane | 14.9 | 0.0 | 0.0 |

**Step 1 —** Compute $R_a$ for **ethyl acetate** vs. polystyrene:

$$R_a = \big[4(18.5-15.8)^2 + (4.5-5.3)^2 + (2.9-7.2)^2\big]^{1/2}$$

$$R_a = \big[4(7.29) + 0.64 + 18.49\big]^{1/2} = \big[29.16 + 0.64 + 18.49\big]^{1/2}$$

$$R_a = (48.29)^{1/2} = 6.95 \; \text{MPa}^{1/2}$$

**Step 2 —** RED for ethyl acetate:

$$RED = \frac{6.95}{12.7} = 0.547 \lt 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{SOLUBLE}$$

**Step 3 —** Compute $R_a$ for **hexane** vs. polystyrene:

$$R_a = \big[4(18.5-14.9)^2 + (4.5-0)^2 + (2.9-0)^2\big]^{1/2}$$

$$R_a = \big[4(12.96) + 20.25 + 8.41\big]^{1/2} = (80.50)^{1/2} = 8.97 \; \text{MPa}^{1/2}$$

**Step 4 —** RED for hexane:

$$RED = \frac{8.97}{12.7} = 0.706 \lt 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{SOLUBLE (marginally)}$$

**Step 5 —** Conclusion: Both are predicted to dissolve polystyrene, but **ethyl acetate is the better choice** (lower RED = stronger interaction, faster dissolution, clearer film). This is consistent with laboratory practice — polystyrene cement is conventionally made with ethyl acetate / toluene, not hexane.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes of Solvent-Based Adhesive Joints

| Failure Mode | Visual Signature | Root Cause | Preventive Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Adhesive failure** | Clean substrate, adhesive on one side only | Poor wetting / wrong solvent | Increase contact time, use a primer, raise substrate $\gamma_s$ |
| **Cohesive failure** | Adhesive split, residue on **both** substrates | Polymer strength too low, plasticiser migration | Increase molecular weight, add reinforcing resin |
| **Substrate failure** | Substrate itself tears | Adhesive stronger than substrate (rare) | Acceptable; indicates excellent bond |
| **Mixed failure** | Patchy — both adhesive and cohesive areas | Borderline formulation | Tighter QC on solids & viscosity |
| **Environmental creep** | Bond fails under sustained load at elevated T | Plasticiser loss, oxidative degradation | Add antioxidant, crosslink with isocyanate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Solvent-Based Adhesive Manufacture, Application, and Bond Formation

The following Mermaid diagram traces the *entire life-cycle* of a solvent-based adhesive — from raw-material selection through quality control to bond formation on a substrate. This is the **process-flow schematic** that maps the interactions between formulation chemistry, rheology control, application engineering, and final testing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Selection] --> A1[Polymer Resin]
    A --> A2[Solvent System]
    A --> A3[Tackifier Resin]
    A --> A4[Additives Package]

    A1 --> B[Dissolution Reactor]
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A4 --> B

    B --> B1{Stirring 4-8 h at 25-40 C}
    B1 -->|Clear solution| C[Filtration Mesh 100 micron]
    B1 -->|Cloudy / gel| B2[Re-dissolve / reformulate]

    C --> D[In-Process QC Stage]
    D --> D1[Solid Content 40 to 65 percent]
    D --> D2[Viscosity Brookfield 2000 to 8000 cP]
    D --> D3[Appearance and colour check]

    D -->|Pass| E[Packaging in Airtight Container]
    D -->|Fail| B2

    E --> F[End User Application]
    F --> F1[Substrate Preparation Degrease and Abrade]
    F1 --> F2[Adhesive Application Brush or Spray]
    F2 --> F3[Drying Stage 1 Open Time 5 to 30 min]
    F3 --> F4[Mating of Substrates Under Pressure]
    F4 --> F5[Drying Stage 2 Solvent Evaporation 24 to 72 h]
    F5 --> G[Final Bond Inspection]
    G --> G1[Lap Shear Test]
    G --> G2[Peel Test]
    G --> G3[Visual Inspection]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Adhesion Mechanism Hierarchy

This second diagram maps the *molecular and macroscopic* mechanisms that together produce the final bond. Each layer feeds the next.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Level 1 Molecular Wetting] --> L2[Level 2 Interdiffusion of Chains]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Mechanical Interlocking]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Chemical Adsorption]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 Electrostatic Bridging]
    L5 --> L6[Level 6 Weak Boundary Layer Avoidance]
    L6 --> OUT[Final Joint Strength]
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic: Adhesive-Joint Test Geometry (Lap-Shear)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Top Adherend Mild Steel 25 by 100 mm] -->|overlap 25 mm| S2[Bottom Adherend Mild Steel 25 by 100 mm]
    S2 --> ADH[Adhesive Film 0.1 to 0.3 mm thick]
    ADH --> S1
    S1 --> F1[Force P Applied by UTM]
    S2 --> F2[Reaction Support]
    F1 --> CALC[Calculate tau = P divided by A overlap]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Effect of Solvent Choice on Adhesive Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SOL[Solvent Choice] --> A1[Solubility Parameter Match]
    SOL --> A2[Evaporation Rate]
    SOL --> A3[Toxicity and VOC]
    SOL --> A4[Cost]

    A1 --> OUT1[Polymer Dissolution Completeness]
    A2 --> OUT2[Open Time and Drying Speed]
    A3 --> OUT3[Worker Safety and Compliance]
    A4 --> OUT4[Commercial Viability]

    OUT1 --> FINAL[Final Adhesive Performance]
    OUT2 --> FINAL
    OUT3 --> FINAL
    OUT4 --> FINAL
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCPOL507]**
*Define a solvent-based adhesive. List its four major components and state the role of each.*

> **Model Answer (Examiner's Key, 3 marks):**
>
> A **solvent-based adhesive** is an adhesive formulation in which the polymeric binder is dissolved or dispersed in a volatile organic solvent; the solvent evaporates after application, leaving a continuous polymer film that forms the bond.
>
> **Four major components and roles:**
>
> 1. **Polymer base (binder):** Provides cohesive strength — e.g., polychloroprene, polyurethane, natural rubber, SBR.
> 2. **Solvent (carrier):** Dissolves the polymer, reduces viscosity, controls drying — e.g., toluene, hexane, acetone, MEK, ethyl acetate.
> 3. **Tackifier resin:** Improves initial tack (stickiness) before full cure — e.g., rosin, terpene-phenolic, C5/C9 hydrocarbon resins.
> 4. **Additives:** Plasticisers, antioxidants, fillers — adjust flexibility, stability, and cost.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCPOL507]**
*State Young's equation and explain how the contact angle governs the wetting of a substrate by an adhesive.*

> **Model Answer (Examiner's Key, 3 marks):**
>
> $$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta$$
>
> where $\gamma_{sv}$, $\gamma_{sl}$, $\gamma_{lv}$ are the solid–vapour, solid–liquid, and liquid–vapour interfacial energies, and $\theta$ is the equilibrium contact angle.
>
> **Role of $\theta$:**
> - $\theta \lt 90°$ → adhesive partially wets the substrate (acceptable for bonding).
> - $\theta \to 0°$ → complete spreading (ideal).
> - $\theta \gt 90°$ → poor wetting; the adhesive beads up and the bond is weak.
>
> Practical rule: for adequate wetting, the substrate's surface energy must exceed the adhesive's surface tension by at least **5 – 10 mN/m**. Surface preparation (degreasing, corona treatment, abrasion) is therefore critical for low-energy substrates like polyolefins.

---

### 5.2 KTU Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Module Experiment 2 — KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B carries 14 marks. Per the KTU ESE pattern, students answer **one of two alternatives**, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Sub-parts typically combine theory + numerical or theory + experiment.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q-A(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PCPOL507]**
*"Describe the mechanism of bond formation in a solvent-based adhesive. Discuss the four sequential stages with reference to the role of surface energy, viscosity, and solvent loss."*

> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> 1. **Stage 1 – Wetting (1 mark):** Low-viscosity adhesive solution flows over the substrate. Governed by Young's equation; contact angle $\theta$ must be small. Surface preparation of substrate is critical.
>
> 2. **Stage 2 – Solvent Loss (2 marks):** Solvent evaporates (and partially absorbs into porous substrates) → concentration of polymer rises → viscosity increases → the film reaches the "set" point. Drying time depends on solvent vapour pressure, ambient T, RH, and ventilation.
>
> 3. **Stage 3 – Gelation / Solidification (2 marks):** Polymer concentration crosses the gel point; chain entanglements lock the structure. The bond becomes tack-free. Molecular weight and entanglement density are key variables.
>
> 4. **Stage 4 – Strength Development (2 marks):** Residual solvent diffuses out; polymer chains inter-diffuse across the interface (auto-adhesion); secondary forces (van der Waals, H-bonding) reach maximum. Ultimate bond strength is the sum of cohesive strength of the polymer and the adhesive force at the interface.
>
> *Examiner tip:* Always mention that *wetting precedes adhesion* — no bond forms without intimate molecular contact.

---

**Q-A(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PCPOL507]**
*"A polystyrene-based adhesive is prepared by dissolving 25 g of polystyrene in 75 g of a mixed solvent (toluene : ethyl acetate = 1 : 1). Calculate the solids content. In a lap-shear test on mild steel (overlap 25 mm × 25 mm), the joint fails at 7.5 kN. Calculate the lap-shear strength. Comment on whether this is a good bond for a structural application."*

> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Solids content (3 marks):**
>
> $$\% \text{ Solid} = \frac{W_{polymer}}{W_{polymer} + W_{solvent}} \times 100$$
>
> $$\% \text{ Solid} = \frac{25}{25 + 75} \times 100 = 25 \;\%$$
>
> **Step 2 — Lap-shear strength (3 marks):**
>
> $$A = b \times L_{ov} = 25 \times 25 = 625 \; \text{mm}^2$$
>
> $$\tau = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{7\,500 \; \text{N}}{625 \; \text{mm}^2} = 12.0 \; \text{MPa}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Comment (1 mark):**
>
> A lap-shear strength of **12 MPa** is **good for a general-purpose** adhesive on mild steel and is acceptable for many non-structural applications. However, for true *structural* applications (load-bearing joints in automotive, aerospace), values of **≥ 20 MPa** are required, and a cross-linked polyurethane or epoxy system is preferred. The 25 % solids content is also on the *low* side — a 40 % formulation would yield better gap-filling and higher cohesive strength.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q-B(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCPOL507]**
*"Explain the Hildebrand and Hansen solubility parameter approaches to solvent selection for polymer-based adhesives. Illustrate with an example of selecting a solvent for polystyrene."*

> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> 1. **Hildebrand parameter δ (2 marks):** Defined as the square root of cohesive energy density, $\delta = (\Delta H_v - RT/V_m)^{1/2}$. Two materials with similar δ are mutually soluble ("like dissolves like"). Units: MPa$^{1/2}$.
>
> 2. **Hansen parameters (2 marks):** Hansen split δ into three components: $\delta_d$ (dispersion), $\delta_p$ (polar), $\delta_h$ (H-bonding). The total is $\delta_t^2 = \delta_d^2 + \delta_p^2 + \delta_h^2$. The Hansen distance $R_a$ between two materials and the relative energy difference $RED = R_a/R_0$ predict solubility; **RED < 1 → soluble**.
>
> 3. **Polystyrene example (3 marks):** For PS ($\delta_d = 18.5$, $\delta_p = 4.5$, $\delta_h = 2.9$, $R_0 = 12.7$ MPa$^{1/2}$):
>    - Toluene ($R_a \approx 3.0$ MPa$^{1/2}$, $RED \approx 0.24$): excellent.
>    - Ethyl acetate ($R_a \approx 6.95$ MPa$^{1/2}$, $RED \approx 0.55$): good.
>    - Hexane ($R_a \approx 8.97$ MPa$^{1/2}$, $RED \approx 0.71$): marginal.
>    - Water ($R_a \approx 25$ MPa$^{1/2}$, $RED \approx 2.0$): insoluble.
>
> **Conclusion:** Toluene is the best solvent for PS, but its toxicity drives the industry toward ethyl acetate. Hexane is unsuitable despite being a non-polar liquid.

---

**Q-B(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCPOL507]**
*"Write the procedure for the laboratory preparation of a polychloroprene (neoprene)-based contact adhesive. List the raw materials, the order of addition, and three quality-control tests performed on the finished product."*

> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> **Raw materials (1 mark):**
> - Polychloroprene (Neoprene) – 100 parts
> - Magnesium oxide (acid acceptor) – 4 parts
> - Zinc oxide – 5 parts
> - Antioxidant (BHT or hindered phenol) – 2 parts
> - Tackifier (terpene-phenolic resin) – 15–20 parts
> - Solvent mix (toluene : hexane : MEK = 3 : 2 : 1) – 300–400 parts
>
> **Procedure – Order of addition (3 marks):**
> 1. Dissolve MgO in a small portion of toluene under slow stirring (15 min).
> 2. Add polychloroprene granules gradually; stir at 25 °C for 4 h until a clear viscous solution forms.
> 3. Pre-dissolve tackifier in toluene/hexane; add to the neoprene solution.
> 4. Add ZnO and antioxidant; stir 1 h.
> 5. Add remaining MEK to adjust viscosity to brushable consistency.
> 6. Age the solution 24 h to allow complete polymer solvation and air-bubble release.
>
> **Quality-control tests (3 marks):**
> 1. **Solid content** (gravimetric, oven 105 °C, 2 h) – must be 20 – 25 %.
> 2. **Viscosity** (Brookfield, spindle 4, 20 rpm, 25 °C) – 3 000 – 6 000 cP.
> 3. **Bond strength** (lap-shear on mild steel) – ≥ 8 MPa; (peel on canvas) – ≥ 30 N / 25 mm.
> 4. *(Optional)* Drying time / open-time by thumb-tack test.
> 5. *(Optional)* Appearance – clear, no phase separation or gels.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing cohesion with adhesion.** Cohesion = strength *within* the adhesive; adhesion = strength *at the interface*. Examiners explicitly look for both terms used correctly.
> 2. **Writing $\delta$ without units.** Always write $(\text{MPa})^{1/2}$ for solubility parameters. A bare number loses one mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the assumption of RED < 1 for solubility** when using Hansen parameters. Numerical $R_a$ calculation without the *interpretation* step = partial credit only.
> 4. **Using Young's equation without defining all three $\gamma$ terms.** Examiners expect the symbols to be explicitly defined.
> 5. **In numerical problems, omitting units in intermediate steps** — always write $\text{mm}^2$, $\text{N}$, and $\text{MPa}$ clearly.
> 6. **In experiment-based questions, omitting the *order of addition* of raw materials** — this is a frequent 2–3 mark loss.
> 7. **Not commenting on the practical significance of a numerical result** — always close a numerical answer with one sentence of engineering interpretation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU board revision — read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- **Definition:** Solvent-based adhesive = polymer + volatile solvent + additives; bond forms by *solvent evaporation* (physical setting, not chemical cure).
- **Four components:** Polymer binder · Solvent · Tackifier · Additives (plasticiser, antioxidant, filler).
- **Mechanism (4 stages):** Wetting → Solvent loss → Gelation → Strength development.
- **Wetting rule:** $\gamma_{substrate} \geq \gamma_{adhesive} + 5$ mN/m; contact angle $\theta \to 0$ for best bonding.
- **Young's equation:** $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta$.
- **Work of adhesion (Dupré):** $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$.
- **Hildebrand δ:** $(\text{MPa})^{1/2}$ — *like dissolves like.*
- **Hansen 3-D:** $\delta_t^2 = \delta_d^2 + \delta_p^2 + \delta_h^2$.
- **RED = $R_a / R_0$;** RED < 1 → soluble; RED > 1 → insoluble.
- **Flory-Huggins χ:** $\chi_{12} = (V_1/RT)(\delta_1 - \delta_2)^2$ — small χ ⇒ miscible.
- **Mark-Houwink:** $[\eta] = K M^a$ — higher $M$ ⇒ higher viscosity.
- **Solid content formula:** $(W_2 - W_0)/(W_1 - W_0) \times 100$.
- **Lap shear:** $\tau = P/(b \cdot L_{ov})$ in MPa.
- **Peel strength:** $F/w$ in N/mm.
- **Failure modes (5):** Adhesive · Cohesive · Substrate · Mixed · Environmental creep.
- **Examples of solvent-based adhesives:** Neoprene contact adhesive · Shoe cement · PS cement · PVC pipe cement · Acrylic pressure-sensitive · Phenolic resin varnish.
- **Common solvents & their roles:**
  - Toluene — strong solvent, slow drying, toxic.
  - Hexane — fast drying, low toxicity, weak for polar polymers.
  - Acetone / MEK — very fast drying, good for many resins.
  - Ethyl acetate — moderate, food-contact friendly, low toxicity.
  - Naphtha — cheap, used in rubber cements.
- **Industrial QC tests (memorise the three):** Solid content · Viscosity (Brookfield) · Bond strength (lap-shear or peel).
- **Hazards / environmental:** VOC emissions, flammability, regulatory shift toward water-based and hot-melt adhesives.
- **Typical solids content range:** **40 – 65 %** (general); **20 – 25 %** (contact adhesives); remember both.
- **Typical viscosity range:** **2 000 – 8 000 cP** at 25 °C, spindle 4, 20 rpm.
- **Typical lap-shear strength:** **2 – 15 MPa** on mild steel (depending on adhesive chemistry and substrate prep).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hand Lay-up: Plates Prepared Using Glass Fibre Reinforced Polyester Composite
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL507 - PCPOL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Hand Lay-up: Plates Prepared Using Glass Fibre Reinforced Polyester Composite

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hand Lay-up** is the oldest, simplest, and most widely used *open-mold* technique for manufacturing **Fiber-Reinforced Polymer (FRP) composites**. In this process, reinforcement (typically glass fiber in the form of mats, woven rovings, or chopped strand) is manually placed into an open mold, and a liquid **thermosetting resin** (unsaturated polyester, epoxy, or vinyl ester) is poured, brushed, or rolled over it. The resin impregnates the fibers, and the laminate is compacted and cured at ambient temperature, with or without external heat and pressure.

In the context of this experiment, a **Glass Fibre Reinforced Polyester (GFRP) composite plate** is fabricated by impregnating layers of **E-glass fiber mats** with **unsaturated polyester resin** (catalyzed by *methyl ethyl ketone peroxide – MEKP* and accelerated by *cobalt naphthenate*), followed by room-temperature curing and post-curing in an oven.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Hand lay-up is the **most fundamental composite fabrication technique** and forms the baseline process against which advanced methods (RTM, VARTM, pultrusion, filament winding) are compared. It carries high weightage in the Polymer/Composite laboratory component of PCPOL507.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of making a **sandwich at home**. You take a flat plate (the **mold**), spread butter or sauce (the **resin**), layer slices of cheese, tomato, and lettuce (the **fiber reinforcement**), sprinkle more sauce on top, and press the layers gently with your hand so everything sticks together. The "sandwich" that hardens is the composite.

- The **bread slices** = mold surface / gel coat
- The **sauce/butter** = liquid polyester resin
- The **fillings** (cheese, tomato) = glass fiber mats
- The **pressing action** = hand rolling / compaction
- The **hardened sandwich** = cured GFRP composite

> [!NOTE]
> **Why polyester and not epoxy?**
> Polyester resin cures at **room temperature** (≈ 25 °C) with the help of a catalyst and accelerator, which is ideal for a laboratory hand-lay-up experiment because it requires **no autoclave or heated press**. Epoxy, while stronger, typically needs elevated temperature curing (≈ 80–120 °C) and is more expensive.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Materials (Bold Highlight)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Glass fiber type | **E-glass (chopped strand mat / woven roving)** |
| Density of E-glass fiber (ρf) | **2.54 – 2.60 g/cm³** |
| Unsaturated polyester resin density (ρm) | **1.10 – 1.25 g/cm³** |
| Resin : Hardener (MEKP) ratio | **100 : 1 to 100 : 2 parts by weight** |
| Accelerator (Cobalt naphthenate) | **0.5 – 2 % by weight of resin** |
| Typical cure temperature | **25 °C (room temp) → 60–80 °C (post-cure)** |
| Typical fiber volume fraction (Vf) achievable | **20 – 35 %** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Science Behind Hand Lay-up (Why & How)

### Step 1 — Mold Preparation
- The mold (glass, wood, or polished metal) is cleaned, polished, and coated with a **release agent** (wax or PVA – polyvinyl alcohol).
- A **gel coat** (pigmented, thixotropic resin, 0.3 – 0.5 mm thick) is often applied first to provide a smooth, durable outer surface and protect against moisture ingress.
- **Why?** Ensures easy demolding and surface finish.

### Step 2 — Resin Formulation
- The polyester resin is a **viscous, pale liquid** containing styrene monomer (reactive diluent + crosslinker) and pre-dissolved unsaturated polyester chains.
- Just before use, two ingredients are stirred in:
  - **Catalyst (Initiator):** Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide (MEKP) – decomposes into free radicals to start chain-growth polymerization.
  - **Accelerator (Promoter):** Cobalt naphthenate – lowers the decomposition temperature of the peroxide so the reaction proceeds at room temperature.
- **Why both?** Pure MEKP needs > 80 °C to give free radicals; cobalt allows radical formation at ≈ 25 °C.

### Step 3 — Lay-up of Fiber Mats
- A measured amount of resin is poured into the mold.
- The first layer of **glass fiber mat** is laid down and gently pressed so the resin wets and impregnates the fibers via **capillary action**.
- Subsequent layers are added, each rolled with a **serrated metal roller** (consolidation roller) to:
  - Remove trapped air bubbles,
  - Distribute resin uniformly,
  - Compact the laminate to the desired fiber volume fraction.

### Step 4 — Curing
- The laminate is left at room temperature for **12 – 24 hours**. The polyester cross-links via **free-radical addition copolymerization** with styrene, forming a rigid 3-D network.
- An exotherm (temperature rise) is observed as the reaction proceeds.

### Step 5 — Demolding and Post-Curing
- The cured plate is carefully removed from the mold.
- **Post-curing** at 60 – 80 °C for 2 – 4 hours completes the cross-linking, improving mechanical properties, heat resistance, and reducing residual styrene.

## 2.2 Governing Equations (KTU Formula Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-know formula bank** for board exam numericals on composite plates.

| Symbol | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Wf | (wf / (wf + wm)) | Weight fraction of fiber |
| Wm | 1 − Wf | Weight fraction of matrix |
| ρc (theoretical) | (Wf + Wm) / (Wf/ρf + Wm/ρm) | Theoretical composite density (rule of mixtures, inverse) |
| Vf (theoretical) | (Wf / ρf) / (Wf/ρf + Wm/ρm) | Fiber volume fraction |
| Vm | 1 − Vf | Matrix volume fraction |
| ρc (actual) | (mass of composite / volume of composite) | Measured (experimental) density |
| Vv (void fraction) | 1 − ρc,actual / ρc,theoretical | Void content (porosity) |
| Tensile strength (rule of mixtures, longitudinal) | σc = σf·Vf + σm·Vm | Longitudinal tensile strength |
| Young's modulus (rule of mixtures) | Ec = Ef·Vf + Em·Vm | Longitudinal Young's modulus (isostrain) |
| Specific strength | σc / ρc | Strength-to-weight ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **In equations, never use the literal `|` character inside markdown tables.** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value expressions. For example, write $\vert x \vert$ not \|x\| inside a table cell.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Hand Lay-up

- **Aerospace & automotive:** Interior panels, dashboards, body parts, boat hulls, wind-turbine nacelles.
- **Civil engineering:** Bridge decks, FRP rebars, retrofitting wraps.
- **Electrical:** Insulating housings, transformer covers.
- **Sports:** Surfboards, helmets, bicycle frames.
- **Advantage:** Low tooling cost; suitable for very large structures (boats up to 100 m).
- **Limitation:** Labor-intensive; limited to short production runs; higher void content (3–5 %) compared to vacuum/pressure processes (< 1 %).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Density of a Composite (Rule of Mixtures – Inverse Form)

We start from the principle that the **mass of the composite** is the sum of masses of the constituents, and the **volume** is the sum of volumes.

### Step 1 — Statement of Additivity
Let:
- mf = mass of fiber
- mm = mass of matrix
- mc = mass of composite = mf + mm

Let:
- Vf = volume of fiber
- Vm = volume of matrix
- Vc = volume of composite = Vf + Vm (assuming zero void, ideal case)

### Step 2 — Definition of Density
The density of each component is its mass divided by its volume.

$$\rho_f = \frac{m_f}{V_f} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_f = \frac{m_f}{\rho_f}$$

$$\rho_m = \frac{m_m}{V_m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_m = \frac{m_m}{\rho_m}$$

### Step 3 — Composite Density
Define the theoretical (void-free) density of the composite.

$$\rho_c = \frac{m_c}{V_c} = \frac{m_f + m_m}{V_f + V_m}$$

Substitute the volume expressions from Step 2.

$$\rho_c = \frac{m_f + m_m}{\dfrac{m_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{m_m}{\rho_m}}$$

### Step 4 — Introduce Weight Fractions
Define weight fractions.

$$W_f = \frac{m_f}{m_c} = \frac{m_f}{m_f + m_m} \quad , \quad W_m = 1 - W_f$$

Divide numerator and denominator by mc = mf + mm.

$$\rho_c = \frac{1}{\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{W_m}{\rho_m}}$$

This is the **standard rule-of-mixtures (inverse)** formula used in KTU exams.

### Step 5 — Fiber Volume Fraction Vf
From the definition of density.

$$V_f = \frac{V_f}{V_c} = \frac{m_f / \rho_f}{m_c / \rho_c} = \frac{W_f \cdot \rho_c}{\rho_f}$$

Substitute ρc from Step 4.

$$V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$$

Similarly, Vm = 1 − Vf.

### Step 6 — Void Content (Vv)
Real composites contain trapped air bubbles. Compare actual and theoretical densities.

$$V_v = 1 - \frac{\rho_{c,\text{actual}}}{\rho_{c,\text{theoretical}}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A GFRP plate is prepared by hand lay-up. The weight fractions of glass fiber and polyester resin are Wf = 0.45 and Wm = 0.55. Given ρf = 2.55 g/cm³ and ρm = 1.20 g/cm³, calculate (a) the theoretical density, (b) the fiber volume fraction, and (c) the matrix volume fraction. If the experimentally measured density is 1.42 g/cm³, find (d) the void content.

### (a) Theoretical Density
Apply the rule-of-mixtures inverse formula.

$$\rho_c = \frac{1}{\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{W_m}{\rho_m}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{0.45}{2.55} + \dfrac{0.55}{1.20}}$$

Compute each term.

$$\frac{0.45}{2.55} = 0.17647 \quad , \quad \frac{0.55}{1.20} = 0.45833$$

Sum.

$$0.17647 + 0.45833 = 0.63480$$

Take reciprocal.

$$\rho_c = \frac{1}{0.63480} = 1.5753 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

So **ρc ≈ 1.58 g/cm³**.

### (b) Fiber Volume Fraction
Use the derived formula.

$$V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m} = \frac{0.17647}{0.63480}$$

Compute.

$$V_f = 0.2780 = 27.80\%$$

So **Vf ≈ 27.8 %**.

### (c) Matrix Volume Fraction

$$V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.2780 = 0.7220 = 72.20\%$$

So **Vm ≈ 72.2 %**.

### (d) Void Content
Apply the void content formula.

$$V_v = 1 - \frac{\rho_{c,\text{actual}}}{\rho_{c,\text{theoretical}}} = 1 - \frac{1.42}{1.5753}$$

Compute the ratio.

$$\frac{1.42}{1.5753} = 0.9014$$

Subtract from 1.

$$V_v = 1 - 0.9014 = 0.0986 = 9.86\%$$

So **Vv ≈ 9.9 %** — slightly high for hand lay-up, indicating a need to improve consolidation rolling in the next trial.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix (Process-Parameter → Property)

| Process Variable | Effect on Fiber Volume Fraction (Vf) | Effect on Void Content (Vv) | Effect on Mechanical Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| More rolling / compaction | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases |
| Higher resin viscosity | ↓ Decreases (poor impregnation) | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases |
| Longer gel time (less catalyst) | Neutral | ↑ Increases (resin drains) | ↓ Decreases |
| Higher post-cure temperature | Neutral | Neutral | ↑ Increases (higher cross-link density) |
| Higher number of fiber plies | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases (more interfaces) | ↑ Increases (up to a limit) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Process Flow of Hand Lay-up

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Mold Selection] --> A2[Cleaning and Polishing]
    A2 --> A3[Apply Release Agent Wax PVA]
    A3 --> A4[Optional Gel Coat Application 0.3 to 0.5 mm]
    A4 --> B1[Weigh Polyester Resin]
    B1 --> B2[Add Cobalt Naphthenate Accelerator]
    B2 --> B3[Stir Thoroughly]
    B3 --> B4[Add MEKP Catalyst Just Before Layup]
    B4 --> C1[Pour Resin into Mold]
    C1 --> C2[Lay First Glass Mat Layer]
    C2 --> C3[Impregnate and Wet Out Fibers]
    C3 --> C4[Roll with Serrated Roller]
    C4 --> C5[Repeat Layup for Desired Ply Count]
    C5 --> D1[Cover with Peel Ply and Breather]
    D1 --> D2[Room Temperature Cure 12 to 24 Hours]
    D2 --> D3[Demold the Composite Plate]
    D3 --> D4[Post Cure at 60 to 80 C for 2 to 4 Hours]
    D4 --> E1[Trim and Inspect Plate]
    E1 --> E2[Cut Specimens for Testing]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Architecture of the Cured GFRP Plate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Top Surface Gel Coat 0.3 to 0.5 mm] --> L2[Composite Laminate Region]
    L2 --> L3[Bottom Mold Surface]
    L2 --> F1[Fiber Mat Layer 1]
    L2 --> F2[Polyester Matrix Region]
    L2 --> F3[Micro Voids 3 to 5 percent typical]
    L2 --> F4[Inter Laminar Interface]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Inputs → Process → Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[E Glass Fiber Mat] --> P1[Hand Layup Process]
    IN2[Unsaturated Polyester Resin] --> P1
    IN3[MEKP Catalyst] --> P1
    IN4[Cobalt Accelerator] --> P1
    IN5[Release Agent] --> P1
    P1 --> OUT1[Cured GFRP Plate]
    P1 --> OUT2[Trimming Scrap]
    P1 --> OUT3[Styrene Fume Emissions]
    OUT1 --> QC[Quality Check Vf Vv Dimensions]
    QC --> OK[Accept] --> REJ[Reject and Recycle]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define hand lay-up process. List any two advantages and two disadvantages of the method.

**Model Answer:**
Hand lay-up is an open-mold composite manufacturing technique in which liquid thermosetting resin (polyester/epoxy) is manually impregnated into dry fiber reinforcement placed in a mold, followed by hand-rolling, room-temperature cure, and demolding.

**Advantages:**
1. Lowest tooling and equipment cost; suitable for very large parts.
2. Simple process; no specialized machinery; easily scalable.

**Disadvantages:**
1. High void content (3 – 5 %) and inconsistent fiber volume fraction.
2. Labor-intensive; not economical for mass production; styrene emission is a health hazard.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is the role of (i) MEKP and (ii) cobalt naphthenate in the curing of unsaturated polyester resin?

**Model Answer:**
1. **MEKP (Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide):** Acts as the **initiator / catalyst**. On decomposition (heat- or amine-assisted) it generates **free radicals** that attack the C=C double bonds of the polyester and styrene, initiating chain-growth cross-linking polymerization.
2. **Cobalt naphthenate:** Acts as the **accelerator / promoter**. It lowers the activation energy of MEKP decomposition, so the resin cures at **room temperature (≈ 25 °C)** without external heating.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the step-by-step procedure for the preparation of a Glass Fibre Reinforced Polyester (GFRP) composite plate by the hand lay-up method. Include materials, mold preparation, resin formulation, lay-up sequence, curing, and post-curing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a hand lay-up experiment, the following data were recorded: mass of glass fiber = 95 g, mass of polyester resin = 105 g, measured density of cured plate = 1.51 g/cm³, ρf = 2.55 g/cm³, ρm = 1.20 g/cm³. Calculate (i) the weight fractions, (ii) the theoretical density, (iii) the fiber volume fraction, and (iv) the void content of the plate.

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Procedure (7 Marks)
1. **Materials & Equipment:** Unsaturated polyester resin (general purpose, orthophthalic), E-glass chopped strand mat (CSM, 450 g/m²), MEKP catalyst, cobalt naphthenate (6 % solution) accelerator, release agent (mold wax or PVA), gel coat (optional), flat glass/metal mold, measuring cylinder, balance, serrated metal roller, brush, mixing cups, oven.
2. **Mold Preparation:** Clean the mold, polish with fine emery, apply 2 – 3 coats of mold release wax, buff to a high gloss, optionally apply PVA film.
3. **Optional Gel Coat:** Brush or spray a 0.3 – 0.5 mm layer of pigmented gel coat; allow to become tacky.
4. **Resin Formulation:** Weigh 105 g of polyester resin. Add 1.0 g of cobalt naphthenate (≈ 1 %), stir well. Just before lay-up, add 1.0 – 1.5 g of MEKP (≈ 1 – 1.5 %), stir gently to avoid air entrapment. Pot life is ≈ 10 – 15 minutes.
5. **Lay-up:** Pour a small amount of resin into the mold and spread with a brush. Place the first glass mat, wet it out by brushing resin on top. Roll firmly with a serrated roller to consolidate and expel air. Repeat for additional plies (typically 3 – 4 plies for a 3 – 4 mm thick plate).
6. **Curing:** Cover the laminate with a peel ply and breather. Allow to cure at room temperature for 12 – 24 hours; observe exotherm.
7. **Demolding & Post-Cure:** Carefully demold the plate, trim edges. Post-cure in a forced-air oven at 60 – 80 °C for 2 – 4 hours to complete cross-linking and improve properties.

#### Part (b) Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:** mf = 95 g, mm = 105 g, ρc,actual = 1.51 g/cm³, ρf = 2.55 g/cm³, ρm = 1.20 g/cm³

**(i) Weight fractions:**
mc = 95 + 105 = 200 g
Wf = 95 / 200 = 0.475
Wm = 105 / 200 = 0.525

**(ii) Theoretical density (rule of mixtures):**
ρc,theo = 1 / (Wf/ρf + Wm/ρm)
Wf/ρf = 0.475 / 2.55 = 0.18627
Wm/ρm = 0.525 / 1.20 = 0.43750
Sum = 0.62377
ρc,theo = 1 / 0.62377 = **1.603 g/cm³**

**(iii) Fiber volume fraction:**
Vf = (Wf/ρf) / (Wf/ρf + Wm/ρm) = 0.18627 / 0.62377 = **0.2986 ≈ 29.9 %**
Vm = 1 − Vf = **0.7014 ≈ 70.1 %**

**(iv) Void content:**
Vv = 1 − (ρc,actual / ρc,theo) = 1 − (1.51 / 1.603) = 1 − 0.9420 = **0.0580 ≈ 5.8 %**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students forget to **add cobalt first, MEKP last**; this order is critical because mixing MEKP and cobalt directly causes violent decomposition and a fire hazard.
> 2. In numericals, students often write $\rho_c = W_f \rho_f + W_m \rho_m$ (linear rule of mixtures) — this gives **incorrect units** for density. The correct form is the **inverse** formula shown above. Board evaluators will deduct 2 – 3 marks for this confusion.
> 3. Always state the **units** (g/cm³) and **%** for Vf, Vm, Vv. A numerical answer without units carries zero marks in KTU valuation.
> 4. Do not round off intermediate values; carry at least 4 significant digits until the final step.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]** (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches (i) the role of a release agent and gel coat in hand lay-up, and (ii) the function of the serrated consolidation roller during lay-up.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A GFRP laminate contains 30 % by volume of E-glass fiber and 70 % polyester resin. Given Ef = 72 GPa, Em = 3.5 GPa, σf = 2000 MPa, σm = 55 MPa, calculate (i) the longitudinal Young's modulus and (ii) the longitudinal tensile strength using the rule of mixtures.

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Process Roles (7 Marks)
**(i) Release Agent and Gel Coat:**
- The **release agent** (wax or PVA) is a thin film applied to the mold surface to prevent the cured laminate from sticking to the mold, enabling clean demolding without damaging the part.
- The **gel coat** is a thixotropic, pigmented polyester layer (0.3 – 0.5 mm) applied to the mold surface first. It provides a smooth, glossy, weather-resistant and UV-protected outer surface, hides the fiber pattern, and protects the laminate from moisture and chemical attack.

**(ii) Serrated Consolidation Roller:**
- The metal roller with serrations (notches) is rolled firmly over each fiber layer after resin impregnation.
- Functions:
  1. **Removes trapped air** between fiber tows and between plies, reducing voids.
  2. **Distributes resin uniformly**, ensuring complete wet-out of fibers.
  3. **Compacts the laminate**, increasing fiber volume fraction and hence mechanical strength.
  4. The serrations allow excess resin and air to escape upward through the channels.

#### Part (b) Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:** Vf = 0.30, Vm = 0.70, Ef = 72 GPa, Em = 3.5 GPa, σf = 2000 MPa, σm = 55 MPa

**(i) Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Iso-strain, Rule of Mixtures):**
Ec = Ef·Vf + Em·Vm
Ec = (72)(0.30) + (3.5)(0.70)
Ec = 21.6 + 2.45
**Ec = 24.05 GPa**

**(ii) Longitudinal Tensile Strength:**
σc = σf·Vf + σm·Vm
σc = (2000)(0.30) + (55)(0.70)
σc = 600 + 38.5
**σc = 638.5 MPa**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Longitudinal (parallel to fiber) properties use the **linear rule of mixtures** (Ec = EfVf + EmVm), but transverse properties use the **inverse rule** (1/Ec = Vf/Ef + Vm/Em). Mixing these is a common error worth 2 marks.
> 2. The rule of mixtures for **strength** is an upper-bound estimate; in practice, premature matrix/fiber failure reduces σc. The formula is still accepted in KTU numericals.
> 3. Convert all strengths and moduli to the **same unit** (GPa or MPa) before substituting to avoid 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Hand lay-up** = open-mold, manual, room-temperature process using polyester resin + glass fiber.
- **Order of resin addition:** Resin → Cobalt accelerator → stir → MEKP catalyst (last, just before lay-up).
- **Catalyst:** MEKP (initiator); **Accelerator:** Cobalt naphthenate (promoter).
- **Release agent** prevents sticking; **gel coat** provides surface finish and protection.
- **Serrated roller** removes air, distributes resin, compacts laminate.
- **Curing** = free-radical addition copolymerization of polyester + styrene.
- **Post-cure** at 60 – 80 °C improves cross-link density and properties.
- **Rule of mixtures (inverse) for density:** $\rho_c = 1/(W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m)$.
- **Fiber volume fraction:** $V_f = (W_f/\rho_f) / (W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m)$.
- **Void content:** $V_v = 1 - \rho_{c,\text{actual}} / \rho_{c,\text{theoretical}}$.
- **Rule of mixtures (linear) for modulus & strength (longitudinal):** Ec = EfVf + EmVm, σc = σfVf + σmVm.
- **Typical Vf in hand lay-up:** 20 – 35 %; **Typical void content:** 3 – 5 %.
- **Typical densities:** ρ(E-glass) = 2.55 g/cm³, ρ(polyester) = 1.10 – 1.25 g/cm³.
- **Advantages:** low cost, large parts, simple. **Disadvantages:** labor-intensive, high voids, styrene emission.
- **Always carry 4 significant digits** in numericals; **always quote units**; **never mix up linear and inverse** rule of mixtures.
- **Industrial uses:** boat hulls, wind-turbine blades, automotive panels, chemical tanks, FRP rebars.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Practice in Calendaring, Injection Moulding, Rotational Moulding and Extrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL507 - PCPOL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Work Practice in Calendaring, Injection Moulding, Rotational Moulding and Extrusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Polymer Processing Work Practice

Polymer processing is the engineering discipline concerned with the conversion of raw polymeric materials (granules, pellets, powders, sheets, or dispersions) into finished or semi-finished products of desired shape, size, and properties. **Work practice** in this context refers to the hands-on laboratory exposure a polymer-engineering student must gain with the four most industrially-relevant shaping operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Polymer Processing):**
> Polymer processing is the set of manufacturing operations by which polymeric raw materials are converted into useful finished products through the controlled application of heat, pressure, and deformation, while preserving (or deliberately tailoring) the molecular architecture of the polymer.

The four primary shaping operations covered in Module Experiment 2 are:

| # | Process | Family | Typical Products |
|---|---------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | **Calendaring** | Continuous sheet forming | PVC films/sheets, floor tiles, roofing membranes |
| 2 | **Injection Moulding** | Discontinuous (cyclic) moulding | Gears, housings, bottle caps, automotive parts |
| 3 | **Rotational Moulding** | Discontinuous (slush casting) | Water tanks, toys, kayak hulls, fuel tanks |
| 4 | **Extrusion** | Continuous profile forming | Pipes, rods, films, sheets, cables, profiles |

---

## 1.2 Calendaring — The Rolling Pin Concept

### Formal Definition
**Calendaring** is a continuous polymer processing operation in which a molten, plasticized polymer mass (typically a PVC plastisol or PVC compound) is forced through a narrow nip formed between two or more counter-rotating, heated, polished steel rolls, producing a thin, wide, uniform sheet or film.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Dough Roller:**
> Imagine rolling out a ball of dough to make a chapati/roti. You press the dough with a rolling pin and a flat circular plate. The flatness and thickness of the chapati depend on:
> (a) how hard you press,
> (b) how much the plate rotates,
> (c) the surface finish of the plate.
> Calendaring works **exactly like this**, but with super-heated steel rolls rotating in opposite directions, processing hot polymer dough (compound) at industrial speeds of **30–120 m/min**.

### Key Operational Features
- **Roll Temperature:** 140 °C to 200 °C (for rigid PVC)
- **Linear Speed:** Up to **120 m/min** for modern lines
- **Sheet Width:** Up to **4.5 m** (inverted-L or Z calenders)
- **Tolerance:** Thickness controlled to **± 0.005 mm**

---

## 1.3 Injection Moulding — The Polymer Syringe Concept

### Formal Definition
**Injection moulding** is a cyclic (discontinuous) polymer processing operation in which a measured quantity of molten polymer (the *shot*) is injected under high pressure into a closed, water-cooled mould cavity, where it solidifies by conduction into the shape of the cavity. The solidified part is then ejected and the cycle repeats.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Chocolate Mould & Syringe:**
> Think of how molten chocolate is squirted (injected) into a shaped mould using a piston syringe, then cooled inside a refrigerator to harden, after which the mould opens and the chocolate drops out. Injection moulding is the same idea at industrial scale, with pressures of **700–1400 bar**, cycle times of **5–60 seconds**, and shot weights from grams to **>25 kg** (for large automotive bumper moulds).

### Key Operational Features
- **Injection Pressure:** 700 – 1400 bar (typical); up to 2000 bar for thin-wall parts
- **Mould Clamping Force:** 50 – 8000 tonnes
- **Cycle Time:** 5 s (thin-walled cups) to 180 s (thick engineering parts)
- **Melt Temperature:** 180 °C (PP) to 320 °C (PEEK)

---

## 1.4 Rotational Moulding — The Rotisserie Concept

### Formal Definition
**Rotational moulding** (also called *rotomoulding* or *rotational casting*) is a biaxial, low-pressure, discontinuous polymer processing operation in which a weighed charge of polymer powder (or liquid plastisol) is placed inside a closed, hollow, heated mould. The mould is rotated about two perpendicular axes (biaxial rotation) so that the polymer melts, coats the inner surface uniformly, fuses, and solidifies upon cooling, producing a seamless, stress-free hollow product.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Rotisserie Chicken & Lacquered Globe:**
> Picture a chicken turning slowly on a rotisserie spit inside a hot oven — the heat reaches every side evenly. Now imagine sealing melted butter inside the chicken cavity, then spinning it on **two perpendicular axes** (a spit AND a rotating arm) so that the butter uniformly coats the inner wall of the cavity. When it cools, you have a hollow, uniform "butter sculpture." Rotational moulding works exactly like this — except the "butter" is polymer powder, the "oven" is a 300 °C chamber, and the "sculpture" is a seamless plastic tank.

### Key Operational Features
- **Operating Pressure:** Essentially **atmospheric** (0.0 – 0.5 bar internal) — the lowest-pressure moulding process
- **Mould Temperature:** 250 – 350 °C
- **Mould Material:** Cast aluminium or fabricated steel
- **Wall Thickness:** 3 mm to 25 mm
- **Products:** Seamless, hollow, stress-free

---

## 1.5 Extrusion — The Toothpaste Tube Concept

### Formal Definition
**Extrusion** is a continuous polymer processing operation in which a solid polymer (granules, pellets, or powder) is melted, plasticated, and continuously forced (pumped) through a shaped die by means of a rotating screw (single or twin), producing an endless profile of constant cross-section. The extrudate is then cooled, sized, and cut to length.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Toothpaste Tube & Sausage Maker:**
> Imagine a hand-operated sausage stuffer: you push ground meat through a metal disc with a shaped hole, and out comes a continuous sausage of the cross-section of the hole. Now scale this up: instead of a hand plunger, use a **rotating Archimedes screw** inside a heated barrel; instead of meat, use polymer pellets; instead of a hole, use a complex **die plate**. The continuous output is pulled, cooled, and cut. The toothpaste tube works on the same principle — the toothpaste is the "extrudate," and the tube's nozzle is the "die."

### Key Operational Features
- **Melt Pressure:** 50 – 700 bar (die-end)
- **Throughput:** 5 kg/h (lab extruders) to **>5000 kg/h** (large pipe lines)
- **Die Swell (Extrudate Swell):** 5 % – 80 % depending on polymer and shear
- **L/D Ratio of Screw:** 20:1 (typical) to 40:1 (high-performance)

---

## 1.6 Comparative Snapshot of the Four Processes

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparative Logic Block:**
> Think of the four processes as four different "kitchen methods":
> - **Calendaring** → like *rolling dough* (continuous thin sheet).
> - **Injection Moulding** → like a *filled chocolate mould* (discrete 3-D parts).
> - **Rotational Moulding** → like a *roasted, butter-lined cavity* (seamless hollow parts).
> - **Extrusion** → like a *sausage stuffer* (continuous profile).
>
> All four consume the same raw polymer pellets/powder — only the **shaping pathway** differs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Calendaring — Detailed Working Principle

### 2.1.1 The Four-Roll "Inverse-L" Calender
A modern industrial PVC calender line has four rolls arranged in an **Inverse-L** or **Z** configuration. The polymer compound enters as a hot, plasticized "bank" between the first two rolls and is squeezed progressively thinner as it travels through three nips.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operating Sequence
1. **Compounding:** PVC resin + plasticizer + stabilizer + filler + pigment are pre-mixed in a high-speed mixer at 100 °C, then fluxed in a Banbury mixer or continuous compounder at 160–170 °C into a homogeneous dough-like mass.
2. **Feeding:** The hot compound (frequently still molten at ~160 °C) is delivered as a continuous strip onto the first nip.
3. **Nipping & Sheet Formation:** Rolls 1 and 2 rotate inward (toward each other); the compound forms a *bank* (a small reservoir) above the nip, which is then drawn down into the nip and squeezed into a thin sheet.
4. **Progressive Thinning:** The sheet passes through rolls 2→3, 3→4 with successive nip gaps of 0.5 mm → 0.3 mm → 0.2 mm.
5. **Take-Up:** The final sheet is stripped off the last roll (called the *take-off roll*) by a stripper roll and wound onto a winder.
6. **Cooling & Embossing (optional):** The sheet passes over chilled rolls and an embossing roll (if textured surface is required).

### 2.1.3 Roll Separating Force
The force exerted by the molten polymer on the rolls is critical — it determines roll deflection (which affects thickness uniformity) and roll-bearing load.

$$
F_{sep} \;=\; 2 \, \mu \, L \, R \, \cos\theta \cdot \int_{0}^{\alpha}\!\!\!\frac{\cos\theta - \cos\alpha}{\left(1 + \kappa \sin^2\theta\right)^{n}}\,d\theta
$$

Where the working approximation for a Newtonian fluid calender is:

$$
F_{sep} \;\approx\; \frac{2 \, \eta \, v \, L \, R}{h_0}
$$

> **Where:**
> - $F_{sep}$ = Roll separating force (N)
> - $\eta$ = Polymer melt viscosity at roll temperature (Pa·s)
> - $v$ = Linear surface speed of rolls (m/s)
> - $L$ = Roll face length (m)
> - $R$ = Roll radius (m)
> - $h_0$ = Half-gap thickness (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Implication:** A wider, faster calender with a thinner sheet produces a **larger** separating force. This is why calender rolls are often crowned (slightly larger in the middle than at the ends) or fitted with roll-bending devices to compensate for elastic deflection.

---

## 2.2 Injection Moulding — Detailed Working Principle

### 2.2.1 The Injection Cycle (Four Phases)
1. **Phase 1 — Plastication (Moulding & Metering):**
   The screw rotates, conveying pellets forward; frictional and heater-barrel heat melts the polymer; the screw retracts as melt accumulates in front of it, "metering" a precise shot volume.

2. **Phase 2 — Injection (Filling):**
   The screw (acting as a plunger) shoots forward at high speed, forcing the melt through the nozzle, sprue, runner, gate, and into the cavity. Cavity fills to **95–99%** during fast injection.

3. **Phase 3 — Packing & Holding:**
   Pressure is switched to a lower, sustained *holding pressure* to pack more material into the cavity (compensating for thermal contraction) until the gate freezes.

4. **Phase 4 — Cooling & Ejection:**
   The part cools below its softening point (crystallization temperature for semi-crystalline polymers). Mould opens, ejector pins push the part out, mould closes, and the cycle repeats.

### 2.2.2 Clamping Force Equation (Most Important!)
A polymer engineer must verify that the mould clamping force is sufficient to resist the melt pressure trying to *open* the mould.

$$
F_{clamp} \;\geq\; P_{cavity} \times A_{projected}
$$

$$
F_{clamp} \;\geq\; P_{inj} \cdot K_{loss} \cdot A_{proj}
$$

> **Where:**
> - $F_{clamp}$ = Required clamping force (kN or tonnes-force)
> - $P_{cavity}$ = Average cavity pressure (bar)
> - $P_{inj}$ = Injection pressure at machine nozzle (bar)
> - $K_{loss}$ = Pressure loss factor through runner system (typically 0.3 – 0.6)
> - $A_{proj}$ = Projected area of the moulded part on the parting plane (cm²)

### 2.2.3 Shot Weight Equation

$$
m_{shot} \;=\; V_{cav} \times \rho_{melt} \times (1 + f_{runner})
$$

> **Where:**
> - $m_{shot}$ = Total shot mass (g)
> - $V_{cav}$ = Volume of cavity (cm³)
> - $\rho_{melt}$ = Melt density (g/cm³) — typically 0.7 – 0.95 g/cm³
> - $f_{runner}$ = Runner fraction (0.1 – 0.4)

---

## 2.3 Rotational Moulding — Detailed Working Principle

### 2.3.1 The Four-Stage Roto-Cycle
1. **Loading:** A pre-weighed powder charge (typically polyethylene, particle size 250–500 μm) is placed inside an open mould half. The mould is closed and locked.
2. **Heating (Oven Stage):** The mould is transferred into a heated oven (gas-fired, 250–350 °C). The mould rotates biaxially (e.g., 4 rpm on major axis, 6 rpm on minor axis). The powder melts, sticks to the hot wall, and sinters/coalesces.
3. **Cooling:** The mould is moved into a cooling chamber (forced air + water spray), still rotating. The polymer solidifies as crystallites nucleate and grow.
4. **Demoulding:** The mould is opened, the part is removed (often still warm), and the cycle restarts.

### 2.3.2 Total Cycle Time Equation

$$
t_{cycle} \;=\; t_{heat} + t_{cool} + t_{load} + t_{unload}
$$

Where the heating time is the dominant term and follows:

$$
t_{heat} \;\approx\; \frac{\rho \, c_p \, L^2}{\lambda_{eff}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{T_{mold} - T_{initial}}{T_{mold} - T_{melt}}\right)
$$

> **Where:**
> - $\rho$ = Bulk density of powder (kg/m³)
> - $c_p$ = Specific heat of polymer (J/kg·K)
> - $L$ = Wall thickness (m)
> - $\lambda_{eff}$ = Effective thermal conductivity through mould wall + polymer (W/m·K)
> - $T_{mold}$, $T_{initial}$, $T_{melt}$ = Temperatures (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (Roto):** For HDPE, heating time ≈ **1 min per mm of wall thickness** (oven at 300 °C). Cooling time ≈ 1.5 – 2 min per mm. So a 6 mm wall tank has a heating time of ~6 min and cooling of ~10 min.

---

## 2.4 Extrusion — Detailed Working Principle

### 2.4.1 Screw Zones
A single-screw extruder has three functional zones along the screw length (length measured by *L/D* ratio):
- **Feed Zone (L/D ≈ 0–5):** Conveys solid pellets forward by drag; deeper channel (~0.1 D).
- **Compression (Melt) Zone (L/D ≈ 5–15):** Channel depth decreases; pellets are compacted, air is squeezed back (decompression), and conduction from barrel + friction melts the polymer.
- **Metering Zone (L/D ≈ 15–24):** Shallow constant-depth channel; acts as a *melt pump* delivering homogeneous melt at constant pressure.

### 2.4.2 Output Rate Equation (Newtonian Approximation)

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{\pi \, D^2 \, N \, h \, \sin\phi \, \cos\phi}{2} \;-\; \frac{\pi \, D \, h^3 \, \sin^2\phi \, \Delta P}{12 \, \eta \, L}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Volumetric throughput (m³/s)
- $D$ = Screw diameter (m)
- $N$ = Screw speed (rev/s)
- $h$ = Channel depth in metering zone (m)
- $\phi$ = Helix angle (typically 17.65° for square-pitched screws)
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure rise along the screw (Pa)
- $\eta$ = Melt viscosity (Pa·s)
- $L$ = Length of metering zone (m)

The equation shows:
- **First term (Drag Flow):** Proportional to N, h, D² — the pumping action of the screw.
- **Second term (Pressure Flow):** Opposes drag flow — the back-pressure caused by the die.

### 2.4.3 Die Swell (Extrudate Swell, Bagley Correction)

$$
B \;=\; \frac{D_{extrudate} - D_{die}}{D_{die}} \;\times\; 100\,\%
$$

For a polymer melt, $B$ ranges from **5 %** (low-MW HDPE at low shear) to **80 %** (high-MW natural rubber).

### 2.4.4 Specific Energy Consumption

$$
E_{sp} \;=\; \frac{P_{motor}}{\dot{m}} \quad \left[\frac{kWh}{kg}\right]
$$

Typical values:
- Single-screw extrusion: **0.18 – 0.30 kWh/kg**
- Twin-screw (counter-rotating): **0.25 – 0.45 kWh/kg**

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Reference Card — Memorize for KTU University Exams**

| # | Process | Key Equation | Variables / Units | Typical Values |
|---|---------|--------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Calendaring | $F_{sep} \approx \frac{2\eta v L R}{h_0}$ | $\eta$ (Pa·s), $v$ (m/s), $L, R, h_0$ (m) | $F_{sep}$ = 100 – 500 kN/m |
| 2 | Injection | $F_{clamp} \geq P_{inj} K_{loss} A_{proj}$ | $P_{inj}$ (bar), $A_{proj}$ (cm²) | $F_{clamp}$ = 50 – 8000 tonnes |
| 3 | Injection | $m_{shot} = V_{cav} \rho_{melt} (1+f_{runner})$ | $V_{cav}$ (cm³), $\rho$ (g/cm³) | $m_{shot}$ = 1 g – 25 kg |
| 4 | Rotational | $t_{cycle} = t_{heat} + t_{cool} + t_{load} + t_{unload}$ | time (s or min) | 20 – 90 min/cycle |
| 5 | Rotational | $t_{heat} \approx \frac{\rho c_p L^2}{\lambda_{eff}} \ln\!\left(\frac{T_m - T_i}{T_m - T_{melt}}\right)$ | $L$ = wall thickness (m) | t ≈ 1 min/mm (HDPE) |
| 6 | Extrusion | $Q = \frac{\pi D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \frac{\pi D h^3 \sin^2\phi \,\Delta P}{12\eta L}$ | $Q$ (m³/s), all others SI | $Q$ = 10 – 5000 kg/h |
| 7 | Extrusion | $B = \frac{D_{ext} - D_{die}}{D_{die}} \times 100\%$ | $D$ in mm | $B$ = 5 – 80 % |
| 8 | Extrusion | $E_{sp} = P_{motor}/\dot{m}$ | $P$ in kW, $\dot{m}$ in kg/h | 0.18 – 0.45 kWh/kg |
| 9 | All (Heat) | $Q_{heat} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | $\dot{m}$ (kg/s), $\Delta T$ (K) | depends on process |
| 10 | All (Mech.) | $\dot{W} = 2\pi N \, \tau$ | $N$ (rev/s), $\tau$ (N·m) | depends on process |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Calendaring:** Produces 70 % of all flexible PVC used globally — essential for the building, automotive, and packaging industries.
- **Injection Moulding:** The largest polymer processing technique by *tonnage of products made*; >70 % of all consumer plastic goods.
- **Rotational Moulding:** The only economically viable process for **large, seamless, hollow** parts — water tanks up to 20,000 L are rotomoulded as a single piece.
- **Extrusion:** The largest by *tonnage of polymer processed*; produces all plastic pipes, sheets, films, and wire-coatings.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Drag and Pressure Flow in an Extruder Screw Channel

### Step 1 — Define the geometry
The melt in the metering zone of an extruder screw flows in a **helical channel** of width $W$, depth $h$, and length $L$ along the down-channel direction. We "unwind" this channel onto a flat plate by making a coordinate transformation: the bottom of the channel is the screw root (which moves relative to the stationary barrel with relative velocity $v_{rel} = \pi D N \cos\phi$).

### Step 2 — Velocity components of the barrel relative to the channel
Decompose $v_{rel}$ into:
- **Down-channel (z):** $v_z = \pi D N \cos\phi \cdot \cos\phi = \pi D N \cos^2\phi$
- **Cross-channel (x):** $v_x = \pi D N \cos\phi \cdot \sin\phi = \pi D N \sin\phi \cos\phi$

### Step 3 — Apply the no-slip condition and integrate Navier–Stokes
For steady, fully-developed, incompressible flow with no pressure gradient in x-direction:

$$
0 \;=\; -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \eta \frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial x^2}
$$

Integrating twice with boundary conditions $v_z = 0$ at $x = 0$ (root) and $v_z = v_z^{wall}$ at $x = h$ (barrel wall):

$$
v_z(x) \;=\; \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z}\left(x^2 - hx\right) + v_z^{wall}\,\frac{x}{h}
$$

### Step 4 — Integrate over channel depth to get volumetric flow

$$
Q_z \;=\; W \int_0^h v_z(x)\,dx
$$

**Driven (Pressure) Flow term** — set $v_z^{wall} = 0$:

$$
Q_{z,p} \;=\; -\frac{W h^3}{12\eta}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z}
$$

**Drag Flow term** — set $\partial P/\partial z = 0$:

$$
Q_{z,d} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\, v_z^{wall}\, W\, h \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\pi D N \cos\phi \sin\phi \cdot W \cdot h
$$

### Step 5 — Combine and convert to real extruder coordinates
Using geometric identities $\sin\phi\cos\phi = \frac{1}{2}\sin(2\phi)$, $W \approx \pi D \sin\phi - e$ (with $e$ = flight width, often neglected), and $\partial P / \partial z = \Delta P / L$:

$$
Q \;=\; \underbrace{\frac{\pi D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}}_{Q_{drag}} \;-\; \underbrace{\frac{\pi D h^3 \sin^2\phi \,\Delta P}{12\eta L}}_{Q_{pressure}}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \;=\; \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} \;-\; \frac{\pi^2 D h^3 \sin^2\phi \,\Delta P}{12\eta L}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> - When $\Delta P = 0$ (no die restriction) → $Q = Q_{drag}$ → maximum output.
> - When $Q = 0$ (die fully closed) → $\Delta P_{max} = \frac{6 \eta L D N \cos\phi}{h^2}$ → maximum pressure build-up.
> - Real extrusion operates in between, with the die setting $\Delta P$ and the screw setting $Q$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Newtonian Calender Separating Force

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry
Two rolls of radius $R$, rotating inward with surface velocity $v$, separated by a half-gap $h_0$. The polymer melt fills the region between rolls and is captured at angular position $\alpha$ (the *angle of contact*).

### Step 2 — Lubrication approximation
For $h_0 \ll R$, the thin-film approximation is valid. The local film thickness is:

$$
h(\theta) \;=\; h_0 \left(1 + \frac{\cos\theta - \cos\alpha}{1 + \cos\alpha}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Pressure distribution
The Reynolds equation reduces to:

$$
\frac{dP}{d\theta} \;=\; \frac{12 \eta v R^2}{h(\theta)^3}\left(1 + \kappa \sin^2\theta\right)^{-1}\cos\theta
$$

where $\kappa = 0$ for Newtonian, $\kappa > 0$ for non-Newtonian.

### Step 4 — Integrate over the contact region
Integrating $P$ over $[-\alpha, +\alpha]$ and multiplying by $L \cdot R$ gives $F_{sep}$:

$$
F_{sep} \;=\; 2 \eta v L R \int_{-\alpha}^{\alpha}\!\! \frac{d\theta}{h(\theta)^2}
$$

### Step 5 — Limiting form for small gap

$$
\boxed{F_{sep} \;\approx\; \frac{2 \eta \, v \, L \, R}{h_0}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Force is proportional to viscosity, speed, width, roll radius, and *inversely proportional to the gap*. Halving the gap doubles the force.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Process Parameters

| Parameter | Calendaring | Injection Moulding | Rotational Moulding | Extrusion |
|-----------|:-----------:|:------------------:|:-------------------:|:---------:|
| **Cycle Type** | Continuous | Cyclic | Cyclic (slow) | Continuous |
| **Operating Pressure** | 50 – 200 bar | 700 – 1400 bar | < 0.5 bar | 50 – 700 bar |
| **Melt Viscosity Range** | 10³ – 10⁵ Pa·s | 100 – 1000 Pa·s | 10 – 100 Pa·s (when molten) | 100 – 10⁴ Pa·s |
| **Wall/Sheet Thickness** | 0.05 – 1.0 mm | 0.5 – 100 mm | 3 – 25 mm | 0.02 – 50 mm |
| **Mould/Die Cost** | Very High | High | Low | High |
| **Tool Change Time** | N/A | 0.5 – 4 h | 0.1 – 0.5 h | 0.5 – 2 h |
| **Production Rate** | 200 – 2000 kg/h | 5 – 500 shots/h | 1 – 10 parts/h | 10 – 5000 kg/h |
| **Stress in Part** | Low (oriented) | High (oriented skin) | Very low (isotropic) | Low-moderate |
| **Polymer Form** | Compound dough | Pellets | Powder (250 μm) | Pellets/granules |
| **Typical Polymers** | PVC, ABS, TPU | All thermoplastics | PE, PP, PVC plastisol | All thermoplastics |
| **Energy/kWh-kg⁻¹** | 0.4 – 0.7 | 0.3 – 0.8 | 1.0 – 2.5 | 0.18 – 0.45 |
| **Capital Cost Index** | Very High (1.0) | High (0.6) | Low (0.2) | Medium (0.4) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (Numerical) — Extruder Output

**Problem:**
A single-screw extruder has the following data:
- Screw diameter $D = 60$ mm
- Metering zone depth $h = 3$ mm
- Helix angle $\phi = 17.65°$
- Screw speed $N = 60$ rpm
- Metering zone length $L = 600$ mm
- Die pressure drop $\Delta P = 50 \times 10^5$ Pa
- Melt viscosity $\eta = 500$ Pa·s

Find the volumetric output $Q$ in cm³/s.

**Solution:**

Convert units:
- $D = 0.06$ m
- $h = 0.003$ m
- $N = 60/60 = 1.0$ rev/s
- $L = 0.6$ m
- $\Delta P = 50 \times 10^5 = 5.0 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $\eta = 500$ Pa·s

Compute trigonometric terms:
- $\sin 17.65° \approx 0.3032$
- $\cos 17.65° \approx 0.9529$
- $\sin\phi \cos\phi = 0.3032 \times 0.9529 \approx 0.2890$
- $\sin^2\phi = (0.3032)^2 \approx 0.0919$

**Drag flow term:**

$$
Q_{drag} \;=\; \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi\cos\phi}{2} \;=\; \frac{\pi^2 (0.06)^2 (1.0) (0.003)(0.2890)}{2}
$$

Intermediate:

$$
\pi^2 \approx 9.8696
$$

$$
(0.06)^2 = 0.0036
$$

$$
0.0036 \times 1.0 \times 0.003 \times 0.2890 = 3.1212 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
Q_{drag} \;=\; \frac{9.8696 \times 3.1212 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \;\approx\; 1.540 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to cm³/s: $\;Q_{drag} \approx 15.40$ cm³/s

**Pressure flow term:**

$$
Q_{press} \;=\; \frac{\pi^2 D h^3 \sin^2\phi \,\Delta P}{12 \eta L}
$$

Intermediate:

$$
h^3 = (0.003)^3 = 2.7 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
h^3 \sin^2\phi = 2.7 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.0919 = 2.481 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
D \times h^3 \sin^2\phi = 0.06 \times 2.481 \times 10^{-9} = 1.4887 \times 10^{-10}
$$

$$
\Delta P / (12\eta L) = \frac{5.0 \times 10^6}{12 \times 500 \times 0.6} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^6}{3600} \approx 1388.9
$$

$$
\pi^2 \times 1.4887 \times 10^{-10} \times 1388.9 = 9.8696 \times 2.067 \times 10^{-7} \approx 2.040 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
Q_{press} \;\approx\; 2.040 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} \;=\; 2.04\,\text{cm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Net output:**

$$
Q = Q_{drag} - Q_{press} = 15.40 - 2.04 = 13.36 \,\text{cm}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \;\approx\; 13.36 \,\text{cm}^3/\text{s} \;\approx\; 48.1 \,\text{L/h}}
$$

For HDPE ($\rho \approx 0.95$ g/cm³), mass output $= 13.36 \times 0.95 \times 3600 / 1000 \approx 45.7$ kg/h.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example (Numerical) — Injection Moulding Clamping Force

**Problem:**
A flat polypropylene lid (rectangular, $200 \times 100$ mm) is to be injection moulded. Injection pressure at nozzle = 1000 bar. Pressure loss through cold runner = 30 %. Find required clamping force.

**Solution:**

Projected area:

$$
A_{proj} = 200 \times 100 = 20{,}000 \,\text{mm}^2 = 200 \,\text{cm}^2
$$

Cavity pressure:

$$
P_{cav} = P_{inj} \times (1 - K_{loss}) = 1000 \times (1 - 0.30) = 700 \,\text{bar}
$$

> **Conversion Note:** 1 bar ≈ 1.02 kgf/cm². Use 1 bar = 1.02 kgf/cm².

Clamping force:

$$
F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj} = 700 \,\text{bar} \times 200 \,\text{cm}^2 \times 1.02 \,\frac{\text{kgf}}{\text{bar}\cdot\text{cm}^2}
$$

$$
F_{clamp} = 700 \times 200 \times 1.02 = 142{,}800 \,\text{kgf} \approx 143 \,\text{tonnes-force}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{clamp} \approx 143 \text{ tonnes (clamp tonnage required)}}
$$

> **Practical Implication:** A 200-tonne machine is selected (next standard size above 143 tonnes), and an additional **15 % safety margin** is recommended, giving a recommended machine of 165 tonnes (which is rounded up to 180 tonnes or 200 tonnes — the next available standard).

> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block is a processing station; the labels above each arrow show the *key transformation* happening. The dotted arrows from the bottom show the **temperature gradient** rising from mixer → rolls, providing the heat needed to keep the PVC plasticized.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        direction TB
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The injection unit (top-left block) melts and injects the polymer through the nozzle into the mould cavity. The mould clamping unit (top-right block) holds the mould shut against injection pressure. The hydraulic drive (middle block) provides the power. The control system (bottom block) uses PLC + pressure sensors to manage the cycle precisely.

## 4.3 Rotational Moulding Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Load: Open Mould
    Load: Load Powder Charge
    Load --> Heat: Close and Lock
    Heat: Heating Stage\nOven 300C\nBiaxial Rotation
    Heat --> Cool: Transfer to Cooling
    Cool: Cooling Stage\nAir + Water Spray\nStill Rotating
    Cool --> Unload: Solidified
    Unload: Demould\nOpen Mould
    Unload --> Load: Reload Next Batch
    Load --> [*]: End of Batch
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This is a **state machine** representation. Each rounded rectangle is a state (a phase of the cycle). Arrows show allowed transitions. The key insight: the mould is **rotating continuously** during Heat AND Cool — only stopping to demould and reload.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Model Paper | CO2 | Bloom Level: Remember]**
*Define "Calendaring" as a polymer processing operation. Mention any two industrial products made by this process.*

**Model Answer:**

Calendaring is a continuous polymer processing operation in which a plasticized polymer mass (most commonly a PVC compound) is passed through a series of counter-rotating, heated steel rolls to produce a thin, uniform, wide sheet or film of controlled thickness. The polymer is forced through progressively narrower nips, where each successive roll pair squeezes it thinner.

**Two industrial products made by calendaring:**
1. Flexible PVC films for packaging and stationery.
2. PVC floor tiles / vinyl flooring.
3. (Any other: raincoats, inflatable toys, conveyor belts, geomembranes.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse calendaring with **coating** or **lamination**. Calendering is strictly a *thickness reduction* operation, not merely a surface coating. Do not write "calendering is coating plastic on fabric" — although a calender can be configured to do that (as a *coating calender*), the primary definition is thickness reduction through nipping.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Model Paper | CO2 | Bloom Level: Understand]**
*List any three differences between injection moulding and rotational moulding.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Injection Moulding | Rotational Moulding |
|---|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Mould Pressure | High (700 – 1400 bar) | Essentially atmospheric (< 0.5 bar) |
| 2 | Polymer Form | Pellets/granules | Powder (250 – 500 μm) |
| 3 | Production Type | High-volume discrete parts | Low-to-medium volume seamless hollow parts |
| 4 | Wall Thickness Control | Uniform, precise | Can vary with rotation ratio; less precise |
| 5 | Mould Cost | High (machined steel) | Low (cast aluminium / fabricated steel) |
| 6 | Cycle Time | 5 – 60 s | 20 – 90 min |
| 7 | Internal Stress | High (oriented skin) | Very low (isotropic) |

(Any three points with brief explanation fetch full 3 marks.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do NOT list differences based on *product type* alone ("injection moulds solid parts, rotational moulds hollow parts"). Always give a **technical/quantitative** difference (pressure, viscosity, time) for full marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
