### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper | Module 2 Experiment | CO2, CO3 | Bloom: Apply + Analyze]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat schematic the working of an **injection moulding machine**. Explain the four phases of the injection moulding cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks)** An injection moulding machine is to be selected to mould a polypropylene box of dimensions $300 \times 200 \times 80$ mm with a wall thickness of 2.5 mm. The cavity pressure is estimated as 600 bar. The sprue and runner system accounts for 25 % of the total shot volume. The density of molten PP is 0.85 g/cm³. Calculate:
(i) the total shot weight in grams,
(ii) the required clamping force in tonnes, given projected area of the part is $300 \times 200$ mm² = 600 cm².
(Take 1 bar = 1.02 kgf/cm²)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Working of an Injection Moulding Machine

**Schematic (must be drawn in exam):**
A standard horizontal injection moulding machine consists of two main units:
1. **Injection Unit** — Hopper → Barrel (with heater bands) → Reciprocating Screw + Non-Return Valve → Nozzle.
2. **Clamping Unit** — Fixed Platen → Stationary mould half → Moving mould half → Moving Platen → Tie Bars → Toggle or Hydraulic Clamp Mechanism.
3. A **hydraulic power pack** drives the screw and the clamp.
4. A **PLC controller** sequences the cycle.

**Working — Four Phases of the Cycle:**

**Phase 1 — Plastication (Moulding and Metering):**
The mould is closed and clamped. The screw rotates inside the heated barrel. Polymer pellets fed from the hopper are conveyed forward, melted by conduction from the barrel heaters and by viscous dissipation, and accumulated in front of the screw tip. The screw retracts under a controlled back-pressure, "metering" a precise shot volume. A non-return valve at the screw tip prevents back-flow.

**Phase 2 — Injection (Filling):**
The screw (acting as a hydraulic plunger) is driven forward at high velocity (50 – 500 mm/s). The molten polymer is injected through the nozzle, the sprue bushing, runners, gate, and into the mould cavity. Cavity pressure builds rapidly to **700 – 1400 bar** at the nozzle. The cavity is filled to 95–99 % during this fast-fill stage.

**Phase 3 — Packing and Holding:**
Once the cavity is filled, the pressure is switched to a lower *holding pressure* (typically 30–60 % of injection pressure). The screw continues to advance slowly, packing additional melt into the cavity to compensate for thermal shrinkage as the part cools. Packing continues until the gate freezes solid (gate freeze time = gate thickness² / thermal diffusivity × small factor).

**Phase 4 — Cooling and Ejection:**
The part continues to cool inside the mould (water circulating through cooling channels). Once the part's temperature drops below the ejection temperature (~85 °C for PP, ~60 °C for HDPE), the mould opens. Ejector pins push the solidified part out. The mould closes and clamps, and the cycle repeats.

> **Sequence Diagram (for answer):** *Close mould → Plastication → Injection → Packing/Holding → Cooling → Mould opens → Eject → Close mould.*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Numerical on Shot Weight and Clamp Force

**Given Data:**
- Box dimensions: $L = 300$ mm, $W = 200$ mm, $H = 80$ mm
- Wall thickness: $t = 2.5$ mm
- Cavity pressure: $P_{cav} = 600$ bar
- Runner system fraction: $f_{runner} = 0.25$
- Density of molten PP: $\rho_{melt} = 0.85$ g/cm³
- Projected area: $A_{proj} = 300 \times 200 = 60{,}000$ mm² = 600 cm²

**Step 1 — Calculate cavity wall volume (shell of a rectangular box):**

Outer volume:

$$
V_{out} = L \times W \times H = 30 \times 20 \times 8 = 4800 \,\text{cm}^3
$$

Inner volume (subtract wall thickness):

$$
V_{in} = (30 - 2 \times 0.25) \times (20 - 2 \times 0.25) \times (8 - 0.25)
$$

$$
V_{in} = 29.5 \times 19.5 \times 7.75 = 4458.94 \,\text{cm}^3
$$

Cavity volume (walls only):

$$
V_{cav} = V_{out} - V_{in} = 4800 - 4458.94 = 341.06 \,\text{cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate cavity mass:**

$$
m_{cav} = V_{cav} \times \rho_{melt} = 341.06 \times 0.85 \approx 289.9 \,\text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate total shot weight (cavity + runner + sprue):**

$$
m_{shot} = m_{cav} \times (1 + f_{runner}) = 289.9 \times 1.25 \approx 362.4 \,\text{g}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{shot} \approx 362.4 \,\text{g}}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate required clamping force:**

$$
F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \times 1.02 \,\frac{\text{kgf}}{\text{bar}\cdot\text{cm}^2}
$$

$$
F_{clamp} = 600 \times 600 \times 1.02 = 367{,}200 \,\text{kgf} \approx 367 \,\text{tonnes-force}
$$

Adding a **15 % safety margin**:

$$
F_{clamp, recommended} = 367 \times 1.15 \approx 422 \,\text{tonnes}
$$

Select the next standard machine size: **450 tonnes clamping force**.

$$
\boxed{F_{clamp} \approx 367 \,\text{tonnes (minimum required)}}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper | Module 2 Experiment | CO2, CO3 | Bloom: Apply + Analyze]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the working principle of a **single-screw extruder** with a neat labelled sketch. Explain the functions of the three zones of the screw.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An extruder is to deliver 25 kg/h of HDPE melt through a die. The screw diameter is 45 mm, channel depth in metering zone is 2.5 mm, helix angle is 17.65°, metering length is 500 mm, and screw speed is 70 rpm. The melt viscosity at operating conditions is 400 Pa·s. Estimate the die pressure drop $\Delta P$ and the resulting die swell $B$ (assume $D_{die} = 8$ mm, extrudate diameter measured as 8.6 mm).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Single-Screw Extruder

**Labelled Sketch (must be drawn):**
- Hopper at the rear, gravity-fed.
- Horizontal barrel with 3 to 5 heater bands.
- Inside the barrel: a helical screw supported by a thrust bearing at the rear.
- The screw is driven by a motor through a gearbox (constant or variable speed).
- A *breaker plate* + *screen pack* at the front of the barrel.
- An adapter connecting the barrel to the die.
- The die at the front, exiting the extrudate.

**Three Zones of the Screw and Their Functions:**

**1. Feed Zone (L/D = 0 to 5):**
- Channel depth is **maximum** (typically 0.1 D to 0.15 D).
- Function: **conveying solid pellets** forward from the hopper.
- The pellets are heated slightly by the barrel heater but remain essentially solid.
- Conveying occurs by drag — the friction between the pellets and the barrel wall (which is stationary) is low; the friction between the pellets and the screw root (which rotates) is higher. The ratio of these frictions determines the conveying efficiency. If the friction with the barrel is too high, the pellets simply rotate with the screw and do not move forward — this is called *solids conveying failure*.

**2. Compression (Melt) Zone (L/D = 5 to 15):**
- Channel depth **decreases progressively** from the feed-zone value to the metering-zone value (typically 0.02 D to 0.05 D).
- Function: **compacting, melting, and mixing** the polymer.
- The decreasing depth squeezes the solid bed against the hot barrel wall, increasing the contact area and improving heat transfer.
- The melt that forms along the barrel wall forms a melt film, which is dragged off by the pushing flight and accumulates in front of the solid bed.
- A *solid bed* of unmelted pellets moves forward through a *melt pool* — this is the *Tadmor melting model*.
- Compression ratio = (channel depth in feed zone) / (channel depth in metering zone); typically 2:1 to 4:1.

**3. Metering Zone (L/D = 15 to 24 or higher):**
- Channel depth is **constant and minimum**.
- Function: **pumping** the fully molten, homogeneous polymer against the die pressure.
- Acts as a *positive-displacement pump* — output depends on screw speed, channel depth, and die back-pressure.
- Ensures the melt entering the die has uniform temperature and pressure → uniform extrudate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often describe only "feed, melt, meter" without explaining the **physical phenomena** of solids conveying (friction ratio), melting (Tadmor model), and pumping (drag-flow + pressure-flow). Always mention at least one equation per zone for full marks.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Numerical on Die Pressure Drop and Die Swell

**Given Data:**
- Required mass output $\dot{m} = 25$ kg/h
- HDPE density $\rho_{melt} \approx 0.95$ g/cm³ = 950 kg/m³
- Screw diameter $D = 45$ mm = 0.045 m
- Channel depth in metering $h = 2.5$ mm = 0.0025 m
- Helix angle $\phi = 17.65°$
- Metering length $L = 500$ mm = 0.5 m
- Screw speed $N = 70$ rpm = 70/60 = 1.1667 rev/s
- Melt viscosity $\eta = 400$ Pa·s
- Die diameter $D_{die} = 8$ mm
- Extrudate diameter $D_{ext} = 8.6$ mm

**Step 1 — Convert mass output to volumetric output:**

$$
\dot{m} = 25 \,\text{kg/h} = \frac{25}{3600} \,\text{kg/s} = 6.944 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{6.944 \times 10^{-3}}{950} = 7.31 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to cm³/s: $Q = 7.31$ cm³/s (negligible difference for calculation).

**Step 2 — Compute drag-flow component:**

$$
Q_{drag} = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}
$$

$\sin 17.65° \approx 0.3032$, $\cos 17.65° \approx 0.9529$, product = 0.2890.

Intermediate:

$$
\pi^2 \approx 9.8696
$$

$$
D^2 = (0.045)^2 = 2.025 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m}^2
$$

$$
Q_{drag} = \frac{9.8696 \times (2.025 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1.1667 \times 0.0025 \times 0.2890}{2}
$$

Compute numerator piece by piece:

$$
2.025 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.1667 = 2.363 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
2.363 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.0025 = 5.906 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
5.906 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.2890 = 1.707 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
9.8696 \times 1.707 \times 10^{-6} = 1.685 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
Q_{drag} = \frac{1.685 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = 8.43 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute pressure-flow component (which equals $Q_{drag} - Q$):**

From $Q = Q_{drag} - Q_{press}$:

$$
Q_{press} = Q_{drag} - Q = 8.43 \times 10^{-6} - 7.31 \times 10^{-6} = 1.12 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\Delta P$:**

$$
Q_{press} = \frac{\pi^2 D h^3 \sin^2\phi \,\Delta P}{12 \eta L}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{Q_{press} \cdot 12 \eta L}{\pi^2 D h^3 \sin^2\phi}
$$

Compute $h^3 = (0.0025)^3 = 1.5625 \times 10^{-8}$ m³.

$\sin^2 17.65° = (0.3032)^2 = 0.0919$.

Denominator:

$$
\pi^2 \times D \times h^3 \times \sin^2\phi = 9.8696 \times 0.045 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.0919
$$

Step by step:

$$
9.8696 \times 0.045 = 0.4441
$$

$$
0.4441 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-8} = 6.939 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
6.939 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.0919 = 6.377 \times 10^{-10}
$$

Numerator:

$$
1.12 \times 10^{-6} \times 12 \times 400 \times 0.5 = 1.12 \times 10^{-6} \times 2400 = 2.688 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \frac{2.688 \times 10^{-3}}{6.377 \times 10^{-10}} = 4.215 \times 10^{6} \,\text{Pa} \approx 42.15 \,\text{bar}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta P \approx 42 \,\text{bar}}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Die Swell:**

$$
B = \frac{D_{ext} - D_{die}}{D_{die}} \times 100\% = \frac{8.6 - 8.0}{8.0} \times 100\% = 7.5\,\%
$$

$$
\boxed{B \approx 7.5\,\%}
$$

**Engineering Comment:** A die swell of 7.5 % is typical for HDPE at moderate shear rates. The die-pressure drop of 42 bar is well within the safe operating range (typical HDPE extrusion dies operate at 50–200 bar).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in KTU 2024 Exam (Module 2):**
> 1. **Calendaring:** Students write only "Calendaring is rolling of plastic" without mentioning **nip**, **bank**, **roll speed**, or **temperature profile** — losing up to 3 marks. Always mention the *bank formation above the nip*.
> 2. **Injection moulding:** Students forget to label the **non-return valve** at the screw tip. Examiners specifically look for it. Also, students often miss stating the **percentage of cavity fill** in the fast-injection phase (must state 95–99 %).
> 3. **Rotational moulding:** Common error: writing "rotomoulding is centrifugal casting." It is **NOT** centrifugal — there is no significant centrifugal force applied externally; the coating is purely **gravitational/surface-tension-driven** during biaxial rotation. Examiners deduct 2 marks if you write "centrifugal."
> 4. **Extrusion:** Students forget to mention **die swell** (extrudate swell) as a consequence of elastic recovery of the polymer melt — losing 2 marks on Part B derivations.
> 5. **Numerical:** Forgetting to add the **15–20 % safety margin** on clamping force. Examiners always expect this — a raw answer of 367 tonnes without a margin gets only **5 of 7 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam (Module 2 Experiment)**

**Calendaring:**
- ☐ Continuous operation, 4-roll L or Z calender for PVC.
- ☐ Molten polymer is squeezed through nips of decreasing gap (0.5 → 0.3 → 0.2 mm).
- ☐ Roll separating force $F_{sep} \approx \dfrac{2\eta v L R}{h_0}$.
- ☐ Products: flexible PVC films, floor tiles, conveyor belts.
- ☐ Operating temperature range: 140–200 °C for rigid PVC.

**Injection Moulding:**
- ☐ Cyclic (discontinuous) process; high pressure (700–1400 bar).
- ☐ Four phases: **Plastication → Injection → Packing/Holding → Cooling/Ejection**.
- ☐ Clamping force equation: $F_{clamp} \geq P_{inj} K_{loss} A_{proj}$.
- ☐ Shot weight: $m_{shot} = V_{cav} \rho_{melt} (1 + f_{runner})$.
- ☐ Mould pressure varies from ~1400 bar at nozzle to ~300 bar at cavity end.
- ☐ Always add **15 % safety margin** to calculated clamp tonnage.

**Rotational Moulding:**
- ☐ Low-pressure (< 0.5 bar), biaxial rotation, **NOT centrifugal**.
- ☐ Powder charge (250–500 μm particle size), typically LLDPE or HDPE.
- ☐ Four stages: **Load → Heat → Cool → Unload**.
- ☐ Cycle time dominated by heating: ~1 min/mm for HDPE.
- ☐ Only economical process for **large seamless hollow parts** (tanks up to 20,000 L).

**Extrusion:**
- ☐ Continuous process; helical screw in heated barrel.
- ☐ Three zones: **Feed (conveying) → Compression (melting) → Metering (pumping)**.
- ☐ Output: $Q = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure}$ (Newtonian approximation).
- ☐ Die swell (extrudate swell): $B = (D_{ext} - D_{die}) / D_{die} \times 100\%$.
- ☐ Specific energy: 0.18–0.45 kWh/kg for single-screw extrusion.
- ☐ Throughput range: 5 kg/h (lab) to 5000 kg/h (industrial pipe line).

**Cross-Process Comparisons to Remember:**
- ☐ Highest pressure → Injection Moulding (1400 bar).
- ☐ Lowest pressure → Rotational Moulding (atmospheric).
- ☐ Highest production rate (by mass) → Extrusion.
- ☐ Most economical for hollow parts → Rotational Moulding.
- ☐ Most economical for thin sheets → Calendaring.
- ☐ Most economical for discrete 3D parts → Injection Moulding.

**Numerical Must-Know Conversions:**
- ☐ 1 bar = 1.02 kgf/cm² = 0.1 MPa = 100 kPa.
- ☐ 1 tonne-force = 1000 kgf ≈ 9.81 kN.
- ☐ $\pi^2 \approx 9.8696$ (used in extruder equation).
- ☐ $\sin 17.65° \approx 0.3032$, $\cos 17.65° \approx 0.9529$ (helix angle for square-pitched screws).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visit to Factories Manufacturing Tyres and Non-Tyre Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL507 - PCPOL507 (Polymer Compounding & Processing Laboratory)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Visit to Factories Manufacturing Tyres and Non-Tyre Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Factory Visit to Tyre and Non-Tyre Product Manufacturing Units** is a structured industrial observational experiment under the KTU 2024 B.Tech (Polymer Engineering) laboratory curriculum, designed to expose students to real-world **rubber compounding**, **mixing**, **processing**, **moulding**, **curing (vulcanization)**, and **finishing** operations. The visit integrates theoretical knowledge of elastomer science with industrial process flow, machinery selection, and quality control protocols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Module Experiment 2):**
> *Students are required to visit at least one tyre manufacturing plant and one non-tyre rubber product manufacturing plant, observe and document the process flow from raw material reception to finished goods dispatch, and submit a comprehensive industrial visit report with process flow diagrams, machinery specifications, and safety observations.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Tyre Products** include: Two-wheeler tyres, four-wheeler tyres, truck/bus radial & bias tyres, OTR (Off-The-Road) tyres, aircraft tyres, and bicycle tyres.
> **Non-Tyre Products** include: Conveyor belts, hoses, rubber sheets, gaskets, O-rings, shoe soles, rubberised coir/coconut products, rubber rollers, rubber linings, and sports goods.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy: "The Rubber Kitchen"**
> Think of a tyre factory as a giant industrial kitchen:
> - The **raw rubber (NR/SBR/BR)** is the *flour* (base ingredient).
> - The **Banbury Mixer** is the *industrial dough kneader* (mixes everything).
> - The **carbon black & chemicals** are the *spices* (give strength, colour, durability).
> - The **extruder & calender** are the *rolling pin & pasta maker* (shape the dough into sheets/plies).
> - The **tyre building machine** is the *cake layering process* (stacks plies, beads, sidewalls).
> - The **curing press (autoclave)** is the *oven* (bakes the tyre via vulcanization).
> - The **final inspection** is the *quality tasting* before delivery.

Similarly, **non-tyre factories** are like specialized "bakeries" — instead of round tyres, they mould hoses (like extruding long noodles), belts (like flatbread), or seals (like cookies).

## 1.3 Industrial Visit Objectives (KTU-Defined)

| S. No. | Objective | Learning Outcome (CO) Mapping |
|:------:|-----------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Identify raw materials & storage protocols | CO1 – Polymer Raw Materials |
| 2 | Understand mastication, mixing & compounding | CO2 – Compounding Technology |
| 3 | Observe extrusion, calendering & moulding | CO3 – Processing Techniques |
| 4 | Document curing / vulcanization cycles | CO4 – Vulcanization Principles |
| 5 | Record quality control & testing stages | CO5 – Quality Assurance |
| 6 | Observe safety, effluent & waste management | CO6 – Industrial Safety |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Note:** The visit report carries weightage for the **internal continuous evaluation (CE)**. Marks are awarded for: Process Flow Diagram (30%), Machinery Identification (25%), Safety Observations (15%), QA/QC Documentation (15%), and Report Presentation (15%).

## 1.4 Standard Industrial Metrics to Observe

- **Mixing Cycle Time:** 4 – 6 minutes per batch (Banbury)
- **Batch Size:** 50 – 250 kg (depending on mixer capacity)
- **Curing Temperature:** 140°C – 180°C
- **Curing Pressure:** 100 – 200 kg/cm² (for tyres)
- **Curing Time:** 10 – 60 minutes (depending on size)
- **Specific Energy Input (SEI):** 0.3 – 0.6 kWh/kg (mixing benchmark)
- **Green Strength:** Critical unvulcanized strength of tyre components
- **Mooney Viscosity (ML 1+4 at 100°C):** Standard QC parameter for compound

> **Bold Constants for Memorization:**
> - **Mixing Dump Temperature: < 130°C** (to prevent scorch)
> - **Curing Temperature: 160 ± 5°C** (typical for passenger tyres)
> - **Tyre Components: 8 – 15 different compounds** in a single tyre

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tyre Manufacturing: Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### **Stage 1: Raw Material Reception & Storage**
- **Natural Rubber (NR)** is received in 33.5 kg bales (block rubber) or 111.25 kg bales (RSS grades).
- **Synthetic Rubbers (SBR, BR, IIR)** arrive in 25 – 35 kg bales.
- **Carbon Black** is delivered in powder form or as beads/pellets; stored in silos.
- Storage conditions: Temperature 20 – 30°C, Humidity < 70%, away from direct sunlight & ozone sources (motors, transformers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why control storage?** Rubber undergoes **oxidative degradation** and **ozone cracking**. Even a **0.1% ozone concentration** can cause visible cracking within hours.

### **Stage 2: Mastication**
- Mastication reduces **Mooney viscosity** and improves filler incorporation.
- Done in two ways:
  * **Mechanical Mastication** (Banbury/Open mill) – chain scission by shear.
  * **Chemical Mastication** – using peptizers like *pentachlorothiophenol (PCTP)* or *Renacit 7*.

**Mechanism:** Shear stress → polymer chain scission → reduced molecular weight → lower viscosity.

### **Stage 3: Compounding & Mixing (Banbury Mixer)**

The **Banbury Internal Mixer** is the heart of any rubber factory. The mixing sequence is critical:

**Step 1 (0–30 sec):** Add rubber → form band on rotors.

**Step 2 (30–90 sec):** Add carbon black, silica, oil → achieve carbon-black incorporation.

**Step 3 (90–150 sec):** Add activators (ZnO, stearic acid) and antioxidants.

**Step 4 (150–240 sec):** Add curatives (sulphur + accelerators) — *last* to prevent scorch.

**Step 5 (Dump at 100–130°C):** Discharge onto open mill or extruder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why add curatives LAST?** Premature addition causes **scorch** (premature vulcanization), wasting the entire batch. This is the **#1 cause of batch rejection** in rubber factories.

### **Stage 4: Component Preparation**

Tyres are **multi-component structures**. Each part uses a different compound:

| Component | Compound Type | Function |
|-----------|---------------|----------|
| **Tread** | High-abrasion SBR/BR blend | Road grip, wear resistance |
| **Sidewall** | NR-rich compound | Flexibility, weather resistance |
| **Inner Liner** | Halobutyl (IIR) | Air retention |
| **Carcass Ply** | NR + RFL-treated nylon/polyester | Load-bearing |
| **Belt (Steel)** | NR-rich with steel cords | Stability, puncture resistance |
| **Bead** | NR + steel wire reinforcement | Rim fit, sealing |
| **Apex / Filler** | High-hardness NR | Stiffness transition |
| **Cap Ply (Nylon)** | NR + nylon cords | High-speed stability |

**Process Equipment Used:**
- **Calendering** → produces thin rubber sheets (0.5 – 3 mm) for inner liner, sidewall.
- **Extrusion** → produces tread strips, sidewall profiles, bead apex.
- **Cord Calender (Let-off & Take-up)** → coats tyre cord fabric with rubber cement (RFL dip).

### **Stage 5: Tyre Building**

Two methods:
1. **Bead-to-Bead on Drum (Old method)** — used for bias tyres.
2. **Two-Stage Building (Modern)** —
   - **Stage 1:** Inner liner + carcass plies + sidewalls + beads → forms **"green tyre" inner part** on a flat drum.
   - **Stage 2:** Transfer the carcass to a second drum, add belts + tread → complete **green tyre**.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Tyre building is like making a layered *shawarma roll* — first the base, then the meat, then the bread on top, wrapped in a specific order.

### **Stage 6: Curing (Vulcanization)**

The **green tyre** is placed inside a **curing press (mould)**. Modern factories use:

- **Hydraulic Curing Press** with segmented moulds.
- **Bag-O-Matic (BOM)** system for truck tyres.
- **Autoclaves** for OTR/industrial tyres.

**Curing Parameters:**
- Temperature: **150 – 180°C**
- Pressure: **100 – 250 kg/cm² (10 – 25 MPa)**
- Time: **8 – 45 minutes** (passenger), **30 – 90 minutes** (truck), **hours** (OTR).

> **Bold Constant:** **Curing Temperature (T) vs Time (t) relationship — Equivalent Cure Concept:**
> 
> $$t_{eq} = t_{actual} \times 10^{\frac{T_{actual} - T_{ref}}{z}}$$
> 
> where $z$ = 8 to 10 (activation energy constant for rubber vulcanization).

### **Stage 7: Post-Curing Inspection**

- **Visual Inspection** for blisters, flash, mould defects.
- **X-ray Inspection** for internal defects, cord spacing.
- **Uniformity Testing** (force variation, conicity, ply steer).
- **Dynamic Balance Test** for high-speed tyres.
- **Sectioning Analysis** of sample tyres (lab test).

## 2.2 Non-Tyre Rubber Products Manufacturing

| Product | Manufacturing Process | Key Machinery |
|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Conveyor Belts** | Calendering + Vulcanization | Multi-roller calender, press cure / rotocure |
| **Rubber Hoses** | Extrusion + Braiding + Curing | Extruder, braiding machine, steam autoclave |
| **Rubber Sheets** | Calendering + Press Cure | Calender, hydraulic press |
| **O-Rings & Gaskets** | Compression Moulding / Injection Moulding | Compression press / injection moulding machine |
| **Rubber Shoes/Soles** | Compression / Injection Moulding | Direct moulded sole (DMS) machine |
| **Rubberised Coir** | Impregnation + Drying | Impregnation tank, oven, cutting machine |
| **Rubber Rollers** | Centrifugal Casting / Wrapping + Grinding | Centrifugal casting machine, CNC grinder |
| **Sports Goods (Balls)** | Moulding + Hand Stitching | Moulding press, winding machine |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Industrial Process Parameters)

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Significance |
|-----------|----------------------|-------|--------------|
| **Mooney Viscosity** | $ML_{1+4} \text{ at } 100°C$ | Mooney units (MU) | Compound processability |
| **Mixing SEI** | $\text{SEI} = \dfrac{\text{Energy consumed (kWh)}}{\text{Batch weight (kg)}}$ | kWh/kg | Mixing efficiency benchmark |
| **Cure Rate Index (CRI)** | $\text{CRI} = \dfrac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s2}}$ | min⁻¹ | Production speed indicator |
| **Equivalent Cure Time** | $t_{eq} = t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - T_{ref}}{z}}$ | minutes | Vulcanization kinetics |
| **Scorch Time (tₛ₂)** | Measured by MDR/Rheometer | minutes | Process safety window |
| **Optimum Cure (t₉₀)** | Time to reach 90% torque | minutes | Production cycle time |
| **Green Strength** | Tensile strength of unvulcanized compound | MPa | Building tack & integrity |
| **Hardness (Shore A)** | Indentation depth (1 – 100 scale) | Shore A | End-use flexibility |
| **Specific Gravity** | $\rho_{compound} = \dfrac{W_{air}}{W_{air} - W_{water}}$ | g/cm³ | Compound cost indicator |
| **Compression Set** | $\text{CS} = \dfrac{h_0 - h_2}{h_0 - h_1} \times 100$ | % | Sealing ability (O-rings) |
| **Abrasion Resistance (DIN)** | Volume loss per unit revolution | mm³ | Tyre tread durability |
| **Modulus at 300% (M300)** | Stress at 300% elongation | MPa | Reinforcement efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** When using `|` in any formula, write it as `\vert` in LaTeX tables to avoid markdown rendering issues. For example, use $\vert h_0 - h_2 \vert$ instead of $|h_0 - h_2|$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tyre technology** directly influences vehicle safety, fuel efficiency (rolling resistance), and EV range extension.
- **Non-tyre products** are critical in mining (conveyor belts), aerospace (seals), healthcare (syringe plungers), and oil & gas (O-rings for subsea equipment).
- Understanding industrial-scale processes helps polymer engineers optimize **cycle time**, **material cost**, and **defect rate** — directly impacting factory profitability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Process Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Curing Time Optimization (Equivalent Cure Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU Board often asks a numerical on vulcanization kinetics. Below is the **complete, exhaustive derivation** — do not skip any step.

### **Problem Statement (Model Question):**

A rubber compound is cured at **160°C** for **t₉₀ = 12 minutes** in the lab. In the factory, due to mould thermal lag, the actual curing temperature inside the tyre is **148°C**. Calculate the **equivalent cure time** required at the factory temperature, given the **activation energy constant z = 9**.

### **Step 1: Identify the Governing Equation**

The vulcanization kinetics follow an **Arrhenius-type relationship** between cure time and temperature:

$$
t_{eq} = t_{actual} \times 10^{\frac{T_{actual} - T_{ref}}{z}}
$$

This is derived from the fact that for a **first-order vulcanization reaction**, the rate constant $k$ is:

$$
k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}
$$

For an equivalent degree of cure:

$$
k_1 t_1 = k_2 t_2
$$

Taking logarithms (base 10), the ratio simplifies to the above **equivalent cure equation**.

### **Step 2: Define the Variables**

$$
T_{ref} = 160°C \quad (\text{Lab temperature})
$$

$$
t_{ref} = 12 \text{ minutes} \quad (\text{Lab cure time})
$$

$$
T_{actual} = 148°C \quad (\text{Factory temperature})
$$

$$
z = 9 \quad (\text{Activation energy constant})
$$

### **Step 3: Substitute into the Equation**

$$
t_{factory} = t_{ref} \times 10^{\frac{T_{factory} - T_{ref}}{z}}
$$

### **Step 4: Compute the Temperature Difference**

$$
\Delta T = T_{factory} - T_{ref} = 148 - 160 = -12°C
$$

### **Step 5: Compute the Exponent**

$$
\frac{\Delta T}{z} = \frac{-12}{9} = -1.3333
$$

### **Step 6: Compute the Power of 10**

$$
10^{-1.3333} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-0.3333}
$$

Since $10^{-0.3333} = \dfrac{1}{10^{0.3333}} = \dfrac{1}{2.1544} \approx 0.4642$:

$$
10^{-1.3333} = 0.1 \times 0.4642 = 0.04642
$$

### **Step 7: Compute the Factory Cure Time**

$$
t_{factory} = 12 \times 0.04642 = 0.557 \text{ minutes}
$$

### **Step 8: Interpret the Result**

At a lower temperature (148°C), the cure is **incomplete** in 12 minutes. The factory needs only **~0.56 minutes equivalent at 160°C** worth of cure. To achieve the **same degree of cure** at 148°C:

$$
t_{148} = 12 \div 0.04642 = 258.5 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Interpretation:** A **12°C drop in temperature** increases the required cure time by a factor of **~21.5×**. This is why mould temperature control is **critical** in tyre factories — even small temperature variations drastically affect production cycle time.

### **Step 9: Validate the Result Using Arrhenius Form**

The standard Arrhenius form with $E_a$ in J/mol can be cross-verified:

$$
\ln \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)
$$

For $z = 9$ and a 10°C temperature interval, the **activation energy** $E_a$ can be back-calculated:

$$
z = \frac{2.303 \times R \times T^2}{E_a}
$$

Solving: $E_a \approx 90$ kJ/mol (typical for sulphur vulcanization). ✓ Matches literature.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Tyre vs Non-Tyre Manufacturing

| Parameter | Tyre Manufacturing | Non-Tyre Manufacturing |
|-----------|--------------------|------------------------|
| **No. of Compounds Used** | 8 – 15 per tyre | 1 – 3 per product |
| **Mixing Equipment** | Intermix / Banbury (250 L+) | Open mill / small Banbury |
| **Processing Equipment** | Calender, Extruder, Tyre Building Machine | Moulding press, extruder, autoclave |
| **Cure Method** | Hydraulic press with segmented moulds | Compression / injection / autoclave |
| **Cure Time** | 10 – 90 min | 2 – 30 min |
| **Batch Size** | Large (industrial scale) | Small to medium |
| **Quality Checks** | X-ray, uniformity, dynamic balance | Hardness, tensile, compression set |
| **Skill Level Required** | Highly automated + skilled operators | Often manual / semi-automatic |
| **Examples in Kerala** | MRF (Kottayam), Ceat (Kochi area) | Various small-scale rubber units |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Calculation: Specific Energy Input (SEI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SEI is the industry-standard metric for evaluating mixing efficiency.** A typical tyre compound should have **SEI in the range 0.3 – 0.6 kWh/kg**.

### **Problem:** A Banbury mixer of 250 kg capacity consumes **120 kWh** to mix a single batch in **5 minutes**. Calculate the SEI.

**Step 1:** Identify given values:
- Batch weight $W = 250$ kg
- Energy consumed $E = 120$ kWh
- Cycle time $t = 5$ min

**Step 2:** Apply formula:

$$
\text{SEI} = \frac{E}{W} = \frac{120}{250} = 0.48 \text{ kWh/kg}
$$

**Step 3:** Verify against benchmark:
- Acceptable range: 0.3 – 0.6 kWh/kg ✓
- This is a **well-optimized mix**.

**Step 4:** Industrial utility:
- Higher SEI (>0.6) → over-mixed, polymer degradation, scorch risk.
- Lower SEI (<0.3) → under-mixed, poor dispersion, defects in finished product.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Mastication. Why is it carried out in rubber processing?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Mastication** is the process of mechanically working raw rubber (natural or synthetic) to reduce its **Mooney viscosity** and improve processability, before the addition of compounding ingredients.

**Reasons / Objectives:**

1. **Reduce Molecular Weight:** Mechanical shearing causes chain scission, reducing the polymer chain length and viscosity.
2. **Improve Filler Incorporation:** Lower viscosity allows better mixing of carbon black, silica, and other additives.
3. **Uniform Dispersion:** Ensures homogeneity in the final compound.
4. **Plasticize the Rubber:** Makes it soft and pliable for further processing.
5. **Improve Solubility & Wetting:** Better interaction between rubber and chemical additives.

> **Note:** In modern factories, mastication is minimized to avoid polymer degradation, and **peptizers** (chemical mastication aids) are used.

---

### **Question 2: What is the difference between a tyre and a non-tyre rubber product? Give two examples of each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Tyre Products | Non-Tyre Products |
|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Definition** | Pneumatic products used for vehicle wheels | All other rubber products excluding tyres |
| **Structure** | Multi-component, multi-compound | Mostly single-compound products |
| **Examples** | 1. Passenger car tyre  2. Truck/bus radial tyre | 1. Rubber conveyor belt  2. Hydraulic rubber hose |
| **End Use** | Transportation, mobility | Industrial, sealing, gasketing, sports |
| **Cure Method** | Press cure with segmented moulds | Press / autoclave / injection moulding |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A: Tyre Manufacturing Process (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Describe the complete manufacturing process of a pneumatic passenger car tyre, starting from raw material preparation to the final inspection. Include the role of each major machinery. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The manufacturing of a pneumatic passenger car tyre involves the following sequential stages:

**1. Raw Material Preparation:**
NR, SBR, BR, carbon black, silica, oil, ZnO, stearic acid, antioxidants, and curatives (sulphur + accelerators) are weighed as per the formulation. Each component has a specific batch weight tolerance of **±0.1%**.

**2. Mastication (if required):**
Raw rubber is masticated in a **Banbury mixer** or **open mill** to reduce its viscosity. Peptizers may be added to accelerate this process.

**3. Compounding (Banbury Mixer):**
The ingredients are added in a specific sequence to ensure proper dispersion:
- Step 1: Polymer addition
- Step 2: Carbon black + silica + oil
- Step 3: Activators + antioxidants
- Step 4: Curatives (LAST)

Dump temperature is controlled at **< 130°C** to prevent scorch.

**4. Component Preparation:**
- **Calendering** produces inner liner sheets, sidewall strips.
- **Extrusion** produces tread strips and bead apex.
- **Cord Calender** coats tyre cord fabric with RFL (resorcinol-formaldehyde-latex).

**5. Tyre Building (Two-Stage Process):**
- **Stage 1:** Inner liner + carcass plies + sidewalls + beads → green tyre inner.
- **Stage 2:** Belts + tread cap → complete green tyre.

**6. Curing (Vulcanization):**
The green tyre is placed in a **hydraulic curing press** with a segmented mould. Cure conditions: **160–180°C, 100–250 kg/cm², 8–30 min** for passenger tyres.

**7. Post-Cure Operations:**
- Flash removal, visual inspection, X-ray, uniformity test, dynamic balancing.
- Final marking with size, brand, and DOT certification.

---

**(b) Explain the various components of a pneumatic tyre with their functions and the type of rubber compound used. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Component | Function | Compound Type |
|-----------|----------|---------------|
| **Tread** | Road contact, wear resistance, traction | SBR + BR blend, high carbon black |
| **Sidewall** | Protects carcass, flexibility | NR-rich, weather-resistant |
| **Inner Liner** | Air retention | Chlorobutyl / Bromobutyl (IIR) |
| **Carcass Ply** | Load-bearing, structural support | NR + RFL-treated nylon/polyester |
| **Belt (Steel Belt)** | Stability, puncture resistance | NR + steel cord |
| **Bead** | Rim fit, sealing | NR + steel wire |
| **Apex** | Stiffness transition between bead and sidewall | High-hardness NR |
| **Cap Ply** | High-speed stability | NR + nylon cap |

> A typical **passenger car tyre** uses **8–12 different compounds**, each engineered for its specific location in the tyre.

---

### **Question B: Non-Tyre Rubber Product Manufacturing (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With the help of a process flow diagram, describe the manufacturing of a textile-reinforced rubber conveyor belt. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Conveyor belts** are multi-layered fabric-rubber composites used for material handling in mining, cement, and bulk industries.

**Process Steps:**

**1. Fabric Preparation:**
Polyester/nylon fabrics are woven into a strong base. Multiple plies are used depending on belt strength requirements.

**2. RFL (Resorcinol-Formaldehyde-Latex) Dipping:**
The fabric is dipped in an **RFL solution** to enhance rubber-to-fabric adhesion. This is the **most critical step** — poor adhesion leads to delamination failures.

**3. Drying and Heat Setting:**
The dipped fabric is passed through a **drying oven (150–200°C)** to remove moisture and set the fabric dimensions.

**4. Calendering (Rubber Coating):**
The fabric is coated with skim rubber compound in a **3-roll or 4-roll calender** at controlled thickness (0.5 – 3 mm).

**5. Belt Assembly (Lay-up):**
Multiple plies are laid up with intermediate rubber layers. The covers (top and bottom) are applied last.

**6. Vulcanization (Press Cure or Rotocure):**
- **Press Cure:** Hydraulic press, batch process.
- **Rotocure:** Continuous vulcanization — preferred for long, uniform belts.
- Cure temperature: **140 – 160°C**, time depends on belt thickness (1–2 min per mm).

**7. Finishing:**
- Edge trimming
- Width cutting to specification
- **QC Tests:** Tensile strength, adhesion strength (ply-to-ply), elongation, hardness.
- Marking and packing.

**Process Flow Diagram (Textual):**

`Fabric → RFL Dip → Drying Oven → Calender Coating → Ply Layup → Vulcanization (Rotocure) → Edge Cutting → QC → Packing`

---

**(b) Explain the manufacturing of rubber O-rings by compression moulding. List the quality control tests performed. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**O-rings** are circular rubber seals used in hydraulic, pneumatic, and static sealing applications.

**Manufacturing Process:**

**1. Compound Preparation:**
Typically based on **Nitrile (NBR)**, **FKM (Viton)**, or **Silicone** rubber, depending on the application (oil resistance, temperature resistance, etc.).

**2. Preforming:**
The compound is extruded into long cylindrical "slugs" or pellets of controlled weight.

**3. Moulding:**
The preform is placed in a **compression mould** with a single or multi-cavity design. The mould is closed and heated in a **hydraulic press**.

**Cure Parameters:**
- Temperature: **150 – 180°C**
- Pressure: **100 – 200 kg/cm²**
- Time: **3 – 10 minutes** (depending on size and compound)

**4. Deflashing:**
After moulding, the flash (excess rubber at the parting line) is removed by:
- Tumbling (cryogenic or ambient)
- Manual trimming
- Tumble deflashing with frozen CO₂ or liquid nitrogen

**5. Post-Cure (optional):**
Some compounds (e.g., FKM) require **post-curing at 200–250°C for 24 hours** to achieve final properties.

**Quality Control Tests:**

| Test | Standard | Purpose |
|------|----------|---------|
| **Hardness (Shore A)** | ASTM D2240 | Material consistency |
| **Tensile Strength** | ASTM D412 | Mechanical strength |
| **Elongation at Break** | ASTM D412 | Flexibility |
| **Compression Set** | ASTM D395 | Sealing ability under compression |
| **Specific Gravity** | ASTM D297 | Compound verification |
| **Visual Inspection** | In-house | Surface defects, flash |
| **Dimensional Check** | ISO 3601 | Tolerance verification |
| **Aging Test** | ASTM D573 | Long-term performance |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Tyre Components:** Students often swap **inner liner** and **carcass ply** functions. The **inner liner is HALOBUTYL (air retention)**, NOT natural rubber. (–2 marks)
>
> 2. **Wrong Sequence in Mixing:** Adding curatives first instead of last leads to **scorch**. Examiners specifically test mixing sequence. (–2 marks)
>
> 3. **Units Error in SEI/Cure Time:** Writing SEI in J/kg instead of kWh/kg, or cure time in seconds instead of minutes. (–1 mark)
>
> 4. **Skipping Process Flow Diagram:** For 14-mark questions, a **labelled block diagram is mandatory**. Skipping it costs at least 3 marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Safety Observations:** The visit report MUST include **personal protective equipment (PPE)**, **fire safety**, **chemical handling**, and **effluent treatment** observations. Marks are explicitly allocated.
>
> 6. **Generic Answers:** Writing textbook definitions without industrial context loses marks. Always mention **specific machinery names** (e.g., Banbury, Calender, BOM press) and **real numbers** (temperature, pressure, time).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Exam):**

### **Tyre Manufacturing:**
- ☐ A **passenger car tyre uses 8 – 12 different compounds**.
- ☐ **Inner liner = Halobutyl (IIR)** for air retention.
- ☐ **Curing temperature = 160 ± 5°C**, pressure = 100 – 250 kg/cm².
- ☐ **Mixing dump temperature < 130°C** to avoid scorch.
- ☐ Curatives (sulphur + accelerators) are added **LAST** in the mixing sequence.
- ☐ **Two-stage tyre building** is the modern standard.
- ☐ **Bead = Steel wire + NR**, gives rim fit and sealing.
- ☐ **Carcass ply = RFL-treated nylon/polyester** with NR skim.

### **Non-Tyre Manufacturing:**
- ☐ **Conveyor belt = Fabric + RFL + Rubber + Vulcanization**.
- ☐ **Hoses = Extrusion (inner) + Braiding + Cover extrusion + Steam cure**.
- ☐ **O-rings = Compression moulding** with multi-cavity moulds.
- ☐ **Rotocure = Continuous vulcanization** used for long belts.
- ☐ **Post-cure for FKM (Viton) O-rings at 200–250°C for 24 h.**

### **Industrial Safety & QA (KTU 2024 Emphasized):**
- ☐ Observe **PPE compliance** (gloves, masks, safety shoes, ear plugs).
- ☐ Note **effluent treatment plant (ETP)** for wastewater from washing & latex dipping.
- ☐ Document **carbon black dust control** (bag filters, cyclone separators).
- ☐ Identify **fire extinguishers, eye-wash stations, emergency exits**.
- ☐ Mention **ISO 9001 / IATF 16949 / BIS certifications** if displayed.

### **Critical Numerical Conversions:**
- ☐ **1 kg/cm² = 0.098 MPa ≈ 0.1 MPa**
- ☐ **1 inch = 25.4 mm** (tyre nomenclature)
- ☐ **Mooney Viscosity** standard: **ML₁₊₄ @ 100°C**
- ☐ **Scorch time (tₛ₂)** = time to reach 2 dN.m rise in torque
- ☐ **Optimum cure (t₉₀)** = time to reach 90% of maximum torque

### **Famous Tyre/Non-Tyre Industries in Kerala (Visit-Relevant):**
- ☐ **MRF Ltd., Kottayam** — Tyre manufacturing
- ☐ **CEAT, Tripunithura (Kochi area)** — Tyre manufacturing
- ☐ **ATC Tires, Kochi** — Tyre retreading
- ☐ Various small-scale units in **Ernakulam, Thrissur, Kottayam** — Conveyor belts, hoses, O-rings, rubber sheets, coir-rubber composites

> **Final Tip:** Always carry your **lab observation notebook** during the visit. Note down **machine names, capacities, temperature/pressure values, and process flow** — these form the backbone of your KTU evaluation report.
